blocks:
Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا (١)
O mankind! Be wary of your Lord who created you from a single soul, and created its mate from it, and from the two of them scattered numerous men and women. Be wary of Allah, in whose Name you adjure one another and [of severing ties with] blood relations. Indeed Allah is watchful over you. (1)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah, as indicated by its opening, is the exposition of the laws of marriage—such as the number of wives, the prohibited degrees of kinship for marriage, and so forth—and the laws of inheritance. It also contains other matters regarding the rulings of prayer (الصلاة), struggle (الجهاد), testimonies (الشهادات), trade (التجارة), and others, while also addressing the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب).

The contents of its verses testify that it is Medinan (مدنية), revealed after the Migration (الهجرة). Its apparent context suggests that it was revealed piecemeal (نجوما) rather than all at once, even though most of its verses do not lack a thematic connection with one another.

As for this verse itself, it is one of a group of subsequent verses addressing the state of orphans and women, serving as a preamble (التوطئة) to what will be explained regarding inheritance and the prohibited degrees of marriage. As for the number of wives mentioned in the third verse, although it is among the primary objectives of the Surah, it was mentioned in a secondary manner (في صورة التطفل) by drawing upon the introductory discourse occurring in the verse, as will be explained later.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمُ (O mankind! Be wary of your Lord) until His saying: وَنِساءً (and women). He intends to call them to the godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence that leads to fulfilling His commands and avoiding His prohibitions — of their Lord regarding themselves. They are humans united in their human reality (الحقيقة الإنسانية), without any difference therein between the man among them and the woman, the small and the great, the weak and the strong. This is so that the man among them does not wrong the woman, nor does their great one oppress the small in their society, to which Allah has guided them for the completion of their happiness. It also concerns the rulings and laws practiced among them, which He inspired them with to facilitate the path of their lives and to preserve their existence and survival, both individually and collectively.

From this, the subtle point (النكتة) of addressing the discourse to "mankind" (الناس) rather than specifically to "the believers" (المؤمنين) becomes apparent. Likewise, the reason for linking godwariness (التقوى) to "their Lord" (ربهم) rather than saying "Be wary of Allah" (اتَّقُوا اللهَ) or the like, is that the description by which they were mentioned—namely His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ (who created you from a single soul)—encompasses all of mankind without being exclusive to the believers. Furthermore, this is among the attributes of Lordship (الربوبية) which undertakes the matter of governance (التدبير) and perfection (التكميل), rather than being among the affairs of Divinity (الألوهية).

As for His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ (who created you from a single soul), the soul (النفس), as understood from the language, is the essence (العين) of a thing. It is said: "So-and-so came to me himself (نفسه) and his very self (عينه)," even if the origin of the specification of these two words—the soul (النفس) and the essence (العين)—for this meaning (that by which a thing is what it is) differs. The soul of a human is that by which a human is a human; it is the sum of the human spirit (الروح) and body (الجسم) in this worldly life, and the spirit alone in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — as established in the previous discussion regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتٌ (Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ [2:154]).

The apparent context is that what is meant by the "single soul" (النفس الواحدة) is Adam (عليه‌السلام), and by "its mate" (زوجها) is his wife. They are the parents of this existing progeny to which we belong and to whom we all trace our lineage, as is apparent from the Noble Quran, such as in His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها (He created you from a single soul, then made from it its mate [39:6]), and His saying, the Exalted: يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ كَما أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ (O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise [7:27]), and His saying, the Exalted, recounting the words of Iblis: لئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً (‘If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, all but a few.’ [18:62]).

As for the possibility raised by some exegetes that the "single soul" and its mate in the verse refer to the absolute category of human males and females—the pairs upon whom the continuation of progeny depends—such that the meaning would be: "He created every one of you from a human father and mother," with no difference between you in that regard; this would then be analogous to His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ (O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-wary among you. [49:13]), since its apparent meaning is the negation of any difference between individuals based on the birth of each from a pair of their kind: male and female.

However, this view contains an obvious flaw. Such an exegete has missed the clear distinction between the two verses—the verse in Surat al-Nisa and the verse in Surat al-Hujurat. The verse in al-Hujurat is in the context of explaining the unity of human individuals in terms of their human reality and negating any difference between them based on the fact that the formation of each of them traces back to a human father and mother; thus, none should be arrogant toward others, and honor is only through godwariness (التقوى). Conversely, the verse in al-Nisa is in the context of explaining the unity of human individuals in terms of their reality, and that despite their multiplicity as men and women, they were derived from a single origin and branched out from a single source, thus becoming many, as is apparent in His saying: وَبَثَّ مِنْهُما رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً (and scattered from them many men and women). This meaning, as you can see, does not suit the interpretation that the "single soul" and its mate refer to any absolute male and female producing human offspring. Furthermore, it does not align with the purpose of the Surah, as previously explained.

Regarding His saying: وَخَلَقَ مِنْها زَوْجَها (and created from it its mate), al-Raghib said: "The term mate (زوج) is applied to each of the two companions, male and female, among mating animals. It is also applied to every two companions, whether among animals or otherwise, such as a pair of boots or sandals. It is also applied to everything that is paired with another, whether similar to it or its opposite." He then said: "The word zawjatahu (his wife) is a weak linguistic usage." End of quote.

The apparent meaning of the phrase—namely His saying: وَخَلَقَ مِنْها زَوْجَها (and created from it its mate)—is an exposition of the fact that her mate is of her own kind through similarity (التماثل), and that all these scattered individuals trace back to two similar and resembling individuals. Thus, the particle min (من) denotes origin (نشوئية). The verse follows the same course as His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً (And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them, and He ordained affection and mercy between you. [30:21]), and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً (Allah has made for you mates from your own selves, and appointed for you children and grandchildren from your mates [16:72]), and His saying, the Exalted: فاطِرُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً وَمِنَ الْأَنْعامِ أَزْواجاً يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ (The Originator of the heavens and the earth, He made for you mates from your own selves, and mates of the cattle [too], multiplying you thereby. [40:11]). Similar to this is His saying: وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ (In everything We have created pairs [51:49]). Therefore, what is found in some commentaries—that the verse means the mate of this soul was derived from it and created from a part of it, in accordance with some reports stating that Allah created Adam's wife from one of his ribs—is something for which there is no evidence in the verse.

As for His saying: وَبَثَّ مِنْهُما رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً (and scattered from them many men and women), scattering (البث) is dispersal through agitation or the like. He, the Exalted, said: فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا (and become scattered dust [56:6]). From this comes the "scattering of grief" (بث الغم); for this reason, the word bath (بث) is sometimes used to mean grief, because it is something scattered that a person naturally spreads. He, the Exalted, said: قالَ إِنَّما أَشْكُوا بَثِّي وَحُزْنِي إِلَى اللهِ (He said, ‘I complain of my grief and sorrow only to Allah’ [12:86]), meaning my grief and sorrow.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that the existing human progeny traces back to Adam and his wife without anyone else sharing in this with them, as He said: "and scattered from them many men and women," and He did not say: "from them and from others." Two matters branch out from this:

First: That what is meant by His saying: رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً (many men and women) are the human individuals from their progeny, whether directly or through intermediaries. It is as if it were said: "and He scattered you from them, O mankind."

Second: That marriage in the first generation after Adam and his wife—meaning among their immediate children—occurred between brothers and sisters (the marriage of sons to daughters), since males and females were restricted to them at that time. There is no harm in this, for it is a legislative ruling (حكم تشريعي) belonging to Allah, the Glorified; He may permit it one day and forbid it another. He, the Exalted, said: وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ (Allah judges, and there is none to take back His judgment [13:41]), and He said: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ (Sovereignty belongs only to Allah [12:40]), and He said: وَلا يُشْرِكُ فِي حُكْمِهِ أَحَداً (and He does not associate anyone in His judgment [18:26]), and He said: وَهُوَ اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (And He is Allah, there is no god but Him. All praise belongs to Him in the first and the last, and to Him belongs the judgment, and to Him you will be brought back. [28:70]).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي تَسائَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحامَ (and be wary of Allah in whose name you adjure one another and the ties of kinship). What is meant by adjuring one another (التساؤل) is people asking one another by Allah; one says to his companion: "I ask you by Allah to do such and such," which is an oath (إقسام) by Him, the Exalted. Adjuring by Allah is an allusion to Him, the Exalted, being magnified in their view and beloved to them, for a person only swears by something he magnifies and loves.

As for His saying: وَالْأَرْحامَ (and the ties of kinship), its apparent meaning is that it is coordinated with the Majestic Name (Allah), meaning: "and be wary of the ties of kinship." It has also been suggested that it is coordinated with the position of the pronoun in His saying: بِهِ (by Him), which is the accusative case (النصب); it is said: "I passed by Zayd and Amran" (مررت بزيد وعمرا). This may be supported by the recitation of Hamzah: وَالْأَرْحامِ in the genitive case (الجر), coordinated with the attached genitive pronoun—though grammarians have weakened this view. In that case, the meaning would be: "Be wary of Allah, by whom you ask one another and by the ties of kinship," as one says to his companion: "I ask you by Allah and I ask you by the womb (kinship)." This is what has been said. However, the context and the habitual style of the Quran in its expositions do not suit this. For if وَالْأَرْحامَ were an independent relative clause (صلة) for "the One who" (الذي), and the estimated meaning was: "Be wary of Allah, the One by whom you ask by the ties of kinship," it would be devoid of a returning pronoun (الضمير), which is impermissible. If the whole phrase were a single relative clause for "the One who," it would imply an equalization between Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), and the ties of kinship in the matter of grandeur and might, which contradicts the etiquette of the Quran.

As for attributing godwariness (التقوى) to the ties of kinship just as it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, there is no harm in it, since the ties of kinship ultimately trace back to His making and His creation. Indeed, godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His punishment — has been attributed in His speech to other than Him, as in His saying: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (2:281), and His saying: وَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَافِرِينَ "and beware of the Fire which has been prepared for the faithless" (3:131), and His saying: وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لَّا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنكُمْ خَاصَّةً "And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively" (8:25).

In any case, this segment of the discourse is like a qualification after an absolute statement and a narrowing after an expansion relative to the segment preceding it—namely, His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا (O mankind, be wary) up to His saying: وَنِساءً (and women). The sum total of the first segment's meaning is: Be wary of Allah from the perspective of His Lordship (الربوبية) over you, and from the perspective of His creating and making you—the assembly of human individuals—from a single essence (سنخ) preserved within you and a preserved matter that multiplies as you multiply, which is the essential human species-nature (النوعية الجوهرية الإنسانية). The sum total of this segment's meaning is: Be wary of Allah from the perspective of His grandeur and might in your eyes (which is among the affairs of Lordship and its branches), and be wary of the uterine unity (الوحدة الرحمية) that He created among you (for the womb is a branch among the branches of unity and essentiality (السنخية) that permeates human individuals).

From here, the reason for repeating the command of godwariness (التقوى) a second time in the second sentence becomes clear; for the second sentence is, in reality, a repetition of the first with an added benefit, which is to convey complete concern for the matter of the ties of kinship.

The womb (الرحم) — the ties of kinship (الرحم) — the biological and social bond connecting individuals through common ancestry — was originally the woman's womb, which is the internal organ prepared for nurturing the zygote into a newborn. It was then used metaphorically for kinship (القرابة) through the relationship of the container and the contained, because relatives share in emerging from a single womb. Thus, the rahm is the relative, and arham are the relatives. The Noble Quran has paid great attention to the matter of the womb just as it has paid attention to the matter of the folk (القوم) and the nation (الأمة). For the womb is a small society just as the folk is a large society. The Quran has attended to the matter of society and considered it a reality (حقيقة) possessing properties and effects, just as it has attended to the individual human and considered him a reality possessing properties and effects derived from existence (الوجود). The Exalted said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هَذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا * وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْمَاءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا "It is He who merged the two seas: this one sweet and agreeable, and this one salty and bitter, and He placed between them a barrier and a forbidden obstruction. And it is He who created man from water, then He established for him lineage and kinship by marriage, and your Lord is all-powerful" (25:53-54). And the Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا "and made you nations and tribes that you may co-identify with one another" (49:13). And the Exalted said: وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ "and the blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah" (33:6). And the Exalted said: فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحَامَكُمْ "May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship?" (47:22). And the Exalted said: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَافًا خَافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ "Let those fear [the result of their own conduct] who, if they were to leave behind weak offspring, would be concerned for them" (4:9), among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا "Indeed Allah is watchful over you." The Watchful (الرقيب) — the All-Watchful (الرقيب) — the attribute of Allah as the One who constantly observes and preserves all things — is the Guardian (الحفيظ), and watchfulness (المراقبة) is preservation (المحافظة). It is as if it is derived from the neck (الرقبة) with the consideration that they used to guard the necks of their slaves, or that the watcher (الرقيب) would look out over the one he was watching by raising his neck and extending it. Watchfulness (الرقوب) is not absolute preservation, but rather the preservation of the actions of the watched—their movements and stillness—to rectify instances of defect and corruption or to record them. It is as if it is the preservation of a thing with careful attention through knowledge and witnessing (شهودا); hence it is used in the sense of guarding, waiting, caution, and monitoring. Allah, the Glorified, is Watchful (رقيب) because He preserves for the servants their deeds to recompense them. The Exalted said: وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ "and your Lord is watchful over all things" (34:21). And He said: اللَّهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَا أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ "Allah is watchful over them, and you are not their guardian" (42:6). And He said: فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ * إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ "So your Lord poured on them the whip of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush" (89:13-14).

In justifying the command of godwariness (التقوى) regarding the human unity (الوحدة الإنسانية) permeating its individuals and preserving its necessary effects by His being Watchful (رقيبا), there is the greatest warning and intimidation against opposition. By reflecting upon this, the connection becomes clear between the verses addressing the matter of transgression (البغي), injustice (الظلم), corruption in the earth (الفساد في الأرض), tyranny (الطغيان), and so on—and the threats and warnings contained therein—and this divine purpose, which is the protection of human unity from corruption and collapse.

(A Discourse on the Age of the Human Species and the Primitive Man)

Jewish history mentions that the age of this species does not exceed approximately seven thousand years. Rational consideration (الاعتبار) supports this; for if we were to suppose a male and a female as a single pair of this species, and we supposed them living for an average lifespan in an average temperament, in an average state of security, fertility, and prosperity, with the assistance of other factors and conditions affecting human life; then we supposed them having married, procreated, and reproduced in proportionate average conditions; then we made this same supposition consistent for the sons and daughters they produced according to what the average state provides in all of that—we would find that the number we first supposed, which was only two, would exceed one thousand in a single century (one hundred years). That is, for every soul, approximately five hundred souls are born in a hundred years.

Then, if we consider the factors opposing man in existence and the general calamities for his species—such as heat, cold, floods, earthquakes, drought, epidemics, plague, subsidences, collapses, mass killings, and other non-general misfortunes—and we give them the most generous share of this species, exaggerating that until we take annihilation as encompassing individuals at a ratio of nine hundred and ninety-nine to a thousand, such that only one remains out of every thousand every hundred years—meaning the factor of procreation every hundred years increases by one for every two, which is one in a thousand.

Then, if we ascend with the number first supposed by this measure to a period of seven thousand years (70 centuries), we find it has exceeded two and a half billion, which is the number of human souls today according to global statistics.

This rational consideration supports what has been mentioned regarding the age of the human species in the world. However, geologists (علم طبقات الأرض) have stated that the age of this species exceeds millions of years. They have discovered human fossils, remains, and artifacts that, according to their findings, date back more than five hundred thousand years. This is their position; however, they possess no evidence that can convince a person or satisfy the soul regarding a continuous, uninterrupted lineage between these past generations and ancient nations. It is possible that this species appeared on this earth, multiplied, grew, and lived, then became extinct, and that this cycle of appearance and extinction repeated several times, with our present lineage being the last of these cycles.

As for the Holy Qur’an, it does not explicitly address whether the appearance of this species is confined to this current cycle we are in, or if there were multiple cycles of which we are the last. However, one might sense an indication from His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً قالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيها مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيها وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّماءَ "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?’" (2:30), suggesting the precedence of another human cycle before this present one. This was previously alluded to in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse.

Indeed, some narrations (روايات) transmitted from the Imams of the Household (أهل البيت) عليه‌السلام establish many cycles for humanity before this current cycle, as will be discussed in the section on narrations.

(A Discussion on the Present Lineage Originating from Adam and His Spouse)

It has sometimes been suggested that the variation in skin colors among human individuals—primarily white, like the inhabitants of the temperate regions of Asia and Europe; black, like the people of Southern Africa; yellow, like the people of China and Japan; and red, like the American Indians—necessitates that the lineage of each color traces back to a different origin than the others. This is based on the premise that differences in color stem from differences in the nature of the blood (طبيعة الدماء). Accordingly, the primary progenitors for the sum of individuals would be no fewer than four pairs for the four colors.

It is also argued that the continent of America was discovered to have inhabitants who were separated from the humans residing in the Eastern Hemisphere by such a vast distance that it is neither expected nor probable that the two lineages meet at a single father and mother. Both arguments, as you can see, are flawed:

As for the issue of blood differing with color, modern natural sciences are based on the hypothesis of evolution (التطور) in species. Given this framework, how can one be certain that the difference in blood and color is not attributed to evolution occurring within this species? Scientists have asserted the occurrence of evolution in many animal species, such as horses, sheep, elephants, and others, and archaeological research has uncovered many remains that reveal this. Furthermore, scientists today do not grant this variation such significant weight.

As for the issue of human existence across the oceans, the human era, according to natural scientists, extends to millions of years, while recorded history (التاريخ النقلي) does not exceed six thousand years. If this is the case, what prevents the occurrence of events in pre-history that separated the American continent from the other continents? There are many geological remains indicating significant changes in the Earth's surface over the ages, such as the transformation of sea into land and vice versa, and plains into mountains and vice versa. Even greater than that is the shifting of the poles and the zones, as explained by the sciences of geology (طبقات الأرض), astronomy (الهيئة), and geography (الجغرافيا). Thus, nothing remains for this claimant except mere improbability (الاستبعاد).

As for the Qur’an, its apparent meaning (ظاهره)—which is close to being explicit (النص)—is that this present, witnessed human lineage traces back through its ascent to a male and a female who are the father and mother of all individuals. As for the father, Allah the Exalted has named him in His Book as Adam (آدم), and as for his spouse, He did not name her in His Book, but the narrations name her Eve (حواء), as in the existing Torah. The Exalted said: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ "He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid." (32:7-8). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then He said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was." (3:59). And the Exalted said: وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً قالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيها مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيها وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّماءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ قالَ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْماءَ كُلَّها "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know you do not know.’ And He taught Adam the Names, all of them..." (2:30-31). And the Exalted said: إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فقعوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay. So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration.’" (38:71-72). These verses, as you see, testify that the custom (سنة) of Allah in the continuation of this lineage is through the medium of the sperm-drop (النطفة), but He initiated it when He manifested it by creating him from dust, and that Adam was created from dust and that people are his children. Thus, the apparent meaning of the verses regarding the termination of this lineage at Adam and his spouse is beyond doubt, even if they are not impossible to interpret metaphorically (التأويل).

It has been suggested that what is meant by Adam in the verses of creation and prostration is a "generic Adam" (آدم النوعي) rather than a specific individual (الشخصي). In this view, "Adam" refers to absolute humanity in terms of its creation originating from the earth and its role in lineage and procreation. This is sometimes inferred from His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْناكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ "Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’" (7:11). It is argued that this is not devoid of an indication that the angels were only commanded to prostrate to the one whom Allah prepared for it through creation and formation, and the verse mentions that this applies to all individuals, not one specific human person, as He said: "Certainly We created you (plural), then We formed you (plural)." Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ "He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands?’" until He said: قالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ "He said, ‘I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’" until He said: قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ "He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them.’" (38:75-83), where He replaced the singular reference used initially with a plural one later.

This is refuted—in addition to being contrary to the apparent meaning of the verses we cited—by the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted, after recounting the story of Adam, the prostration of the angels, and the refusal of Iblis in Surah al-A'raf: يا بَنِي آدَمَ لا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ كَما أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ يَنْزِعُ عَنْهُما لِباسَهُما لِيُرِيَهُما سَوْآتِهِما "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you as he expelled your parents from paradise, stripping them of their garments to expose to them their nakedness." (7:27). The clarity of the verse regarding the individuality (شخصية) of Adam is something that should not be doubted.

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ قالَ أَأَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِيناً قالَ أَرَأَيْتَكَ هذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً "When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He said, ‘Shall I prostrate before one whom You have created of clay?’ He said, ‘Tell me, this one whom You have honored above me, if You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely bring his progeny under my sway, except for a few.’" (17:61-62). Also, the verse under discussion: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْها زَوْجَها وَبَثَّ مِنْهُما رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً "O mankind! Be wary of your Lord who created you from a single soul, and created its mate from it, and from the two of them scattered numerous men and women." (4:1), according to the explanation previously provided.

The verses, as you see, refuse to allow man to be called "Adam" in one sense and "the son of Adam" in another. Similarly, they refuse to attribute creation to dust in one sense and to the sperm-drop in another, especially in verses like: "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then He said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was." Otherwise, the verse's argument that the creation of Jesus was an exceptional creation (خلقة استثنائية) breaking the established habit (العادة الجارية) would not hold. Thus, the claim of a "generic Adam" is a form of negligence (تفريط), while the opposite extreme (إفراط) is the claim of some that saying more than one Adam was created is disbelief (كفر)—a view held by Zayn al-Arab among the Sunni scholars.

(A Discussion on Man Being an Independent Species, Not Transformed from Another)

The preceding verses suffice for this discussion, as they trace this current lineage, which continues through the sperm-drop, back to Adam and his spouse, and clarify that they were created from dust. Thus, humanity terminates at them, and they do not connect to another species that resembles or shares a genus with them; rather, they were originated (حدثا حدوثا).

What is common today among researchers of human nature is that the first human was an individual who evolved into a human. This specific hypothesis, although not accepted by everyone as a definitive conclusion—and they have raised many objections against it mentioned in books—the core hypothesis, which is that "man is an animal that transformed into a human," is something they have accepted and built their research of human nature upon.

They hypothesized that the Earth—being one of the planets—is a piece of the sun detached from it. It was in a state of ignition and fusion, then began to cool due to the dominance of cooling factors. Heavy rains fell upon it, floods flowed, and seas were formed. Then, aqueous and terrestrial compositions occurred, leading to the emergence of aquatic plants. Through the evolution of plants and their containment of the germs of life (جراثيم الحياة), fish and other aquatic animals emerged, then flying fish with dual lives (amphibians), then land animals, and finally man. All of this occurred through an evolution (تكامل) occurring in the terrestrial composition present in the previous stage, whereby the composition transforms in its form (صورة) to the subsequent stage: plants, then aquatic animals, then amphibians, then land animals, then man, in this order. All of this is based on the observed organized perfection in their structures—an order of stages moving from deficiency to perfection—and what experimentation provides in particular instances of evolution (التطور).

This is a hypothesis proposed to explain the properties and effects associated with these species, without any specific evidence establishing it or negating alternatives. It remains possible to hypothesize that these species are distinct (متباينة) without any evolutionary connection between them, and to limit evolution to the states (حالات) of these species rather than their essences (ذواتها). This is what experiments have actually addressed, for experiments have never observed an individual of one of these species transforming into an individual of another species, such as an ape into a human. Rather, they address some of these species in terms of their properties, concomitants, and accidents (أعراض).

The exhaustive investigation of this research is sought elsewhere. The purpose here is merely to point out that it is a hypothesis they proposed to explain related issues without definitive proof. Thus, the truth indicated by the Holy Qur’an—that man is a species separated from other species—is not contradicted by anything scientific.

(A Discussion on the Procreation of the Second Generation of Humans)

The first generation of humans—Adam and his spouse—procreated through marriage, giving birth to sons and daughters (brothers and sisters). Did these then procreate through marriage among themselves as brothers and sisters, or through some other way? The apparent generality of His saying, the Exalted: وَبَثَّ مِنْهُما رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً "...and from the two of them scattered numerous men and women" (4:1), according to the previously mentioned explanation, is that the existing human lineage terminates at Adam and his spouse without anyone else, male or female, sharing in that. The Qur’an mentions no source for the scattering (البث) except them. Had anyone else shared in this, He would have said: "and scattered from them and from others," or mentioned it with suitable wording. It is well known that the restriction of the origin of the lineage to Adam and his spouse necessitates the marriage of their sons to their daughters.

As for the ruling of its prohibition in Islam, and likewise in the previous religious laws (الشرائع) as is narrated, it is a legislative ruling (حكم تشريعي) that follows interests (مصالح) and harms (مفاسد), not a primordial/ontological ruling (تكويني) that is incapable of change. Its reins are in the hand of Allah, the Glorified; He does what He wills and decrees what He intends. It is possible that He permitted it one day due to the call of necessity, then prohibited it thereafter when the need ceased and because it would lead to the spread of indecency (الفحشاء) in society.

The claim that it is contrary to the primordial nature (الفطرة)—and that the religion Allah legislated for His prophets is a primordial religion (دين فطري), as the Exalted said: فأقم وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature (الفطرة) of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion." (30:30)—is a false claim. The primordial nature (الفطرة) does not negate it nor call to its opposite out of an innate aversion to this type of intimacy (intimacy between brother and sister). Rather, it loathes and negates it because it leads to the prevalence of indecency and vice, and the destruction of the instinct of modesty (غريزة العفة), thereby removing it from human society. It is well known that this type of contact and intimacy is categorized as immorality (الفجور) and indecency (الفحشاء) in global society today. However, in a society where, according to what Allah the Glorified created, there were only brothers and sisters, and the Divine Will was attached to their multiplication and scattering, this categorization does not apply.

The evidence that the primordial nature (الفطرة) does not negate it due to an instinctive aversion is its practice among the Magians for long ages (as history recounts), its legal prevalence in Russia (as is reported), and its prevalence through illicit means outside of legal marriage in Europe.

It is sometimes said that it contradicts natural laws—those that govern man before the formation of a righteous society for his well-being—for social interaction and familiarity within the domestic sphere nullify the instinct of passion (غريزة التعشق) and the natural inclination between brothers and sisters, as mentioned by some legal scholars.

The response is that this is rejected, as previously mentioned, and secondly, it is limited to cases where there is no dire necessity. Furthermore, it is specific to situations where positive (man-made) laws, which are non-natural, do not preserve the necessary welfare in society and ensure the happiness of its members; otherwise, most of the laws practiced and principles circulating in life today are non-natural.

(Discussion on Narrations)

In al-Tawhid, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "Perhaps you see that Allah has not created any humans other than yourselves? Nay, by Allah, He has created a million Adams; you are at the end of those Adams."

I say: Ibn Mitham, in the commentary of Nahj al-Balagha, narrated something similar from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), and al-Saduq also narrated it in al-Khisal.

In al-Khisal, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Indeed, Allah the Exalted created twelve thousand worlds—each world of them is greater than the seven heavens and the seven earths—no world among them sees that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has any world other than them."

In the same source, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has created seven worlds on the earth since He created it—they are not from the children of Adam; He created them from the surface of the earth (أديم الأرض)—so He settled them in it one after another, each with its world. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created Adam, the father of humanity, and created his progeny from him..." the tradition.

In Nahj al-Bayan by al-Shaybani, from Amr ibn Abi al-Miqdam, from his father, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'From what did Allah create Eve?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'What do these people say?'"

I said: "They say that Allah created her from one of Adam's ribs." He replied: "They have lied! Was Allah unable to create her from something other than his rib?" I asked: "May I be your ransom, from what did He create her?" He said: "My father informed me on the authority of his forefathers, who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, took a handful of clay and mixed it with His right hand—and both of His hands are right—and created Adam from it. A small portion of the clay remained, and from it He created Hawwa (Eve).'"

I say: Al-Saduq narrated a similar report from Amr. There are other narrations indicating that she was created from Adam's back, specifically his shortest rib on the left side; this is also mentioned in the Torah, in the second chapter of the Book of Genesis. While this notion does not inherently entail an impossibility, the Quranic verses are devoid of any indication toward it, as previously discussed.

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Imam al-Sajjad (عليه السلام) in a discourse with a man from the Quraysh, wherein he described the marriage of Habil to Luzā, the sister of Qabil, and the marriage of Qabil to Iqlima, the sister of Habil. The Qurayshi man asked him: "Did they then father children from them?"

He replied: "Yes." The Qurayshi said: "But this is the practice of the Magians today." He (عليه السلام) replied: "The Magians did that after Allah had prohibited it." Then he said to him: "Do not deny this, for these were the divine laws (شرائع الله) as they unfolded. Did Allah not create Adam's wife from him and then make her lawful for him? That was a law among their laws, and then Allah sent down the prohibition after that," to the end of the narration.

I say: What is mentioned in this tradition is what accords with the apparent meaning of the Book and rational consideration (الاعتبار). There are other narrations that contradict this, indicating that they married houris or jinn who were sent down to them, but you have already learned the truth regarding that.

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي تَسائَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحامَ (and be wary of Allah in whose name you adjure one another and the ties of kinship), it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "And be wary of the ties of kinship (الأرحام) lest you sever them."

I say: This interpretation is based on the recitation of the word in the accusative case (النصب).

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi: "They are the kinship ties (أرحام) of the people; indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, commanded that they be joined and He magnified them. Do you not see that He placed them alongside Himself?"

I say: His saying, "Do you not see..." etc., is an explanation of the reason for their magnification. What is meant by "placing them alongside Himself" is the conjunction occurring in the saying of the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي تَسائَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحامَ.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd extracted from Ikrimah regarding His saying: الَّذِي تَسائَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحامَ, who said: "Ibn Abbas said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: Allah the Exalted says: Join your kinship ties, for it is more lasting (أبقى) for you in the life of this world and better for you in your Hereafter.'"

I say: His saying, "for it is more lasting for you..." etc., is an allusion to what has been narrated extensively (مستفيضا): that joining kinship ties (صلة الرحم) increases one's lifespan, while severing them does the opposite. One may find support for the reason behind this in what will follow in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ — "Let those fear [the day] who, should they leave behind them weakly offspring, would be afraid on their account" (4:9).

It is also possible that what is meant by it being "more lasting" is that joining ties is more preservative of life in terms of its effect. This is because joining ties strengthens the pervasive unity (الوحدة السارية) among relatives, thereby fortifying the individual against factors hostile to his life and detrimental to his well-being, such as calamities, misfortunes, and enemies.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: "I heard the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) say: 'Indeed, one of you may become angry and not be satisfied until he enters the Fire. So, whichever man among you becomes angry with a relative (ذي رحمه), let him draw near to him; for when kinship (الرحم) touches kinship, it settles. Indeed, it is suspended from the Throne (العرش), making a sound like the clanging of iron (تنقضه انتقاض الحديد), and it cries out: O Allah, join him who joins me and sever him who severs me!—and that is the saying of Allah in His Book: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي تَسائَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحامَ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيباً (and be wary of Allah in whose name you adjure one another and the ties of kinship. Indeed Allah is watchful over you). And whichever man becomes angry while standing, let him cling to the earth immediately, for that will remove the defilement (رجز) of Satan.'"

I say: Kinship (الرحم), as you have learned, is the aspect of unity (جهة الوحدة) existing between human individuals by virtue of the connection of the matter of their existence through birth from a father and a mother, or one of them. It is a real, pervasive quality (جهة حقيقية سائرة) among relatives that has actual effects—both innate (خلقية) and moral (خلقية), spiritual and physical—which cannot be denied, even if opposing factors sometimes arise that weaken its effect or partially nullify it until it seems non-existent, though it never vanishes entirely.

In any case, kinship is among the strongest causes of natural cohesion (الالتيام الطبيعي) between members of a clan, possessing the highest potential for influence. Therefore, the results of kindness (المعروف) among relatives are stronger and more intense than those among strangers. Likewise, ill-treatment toward relatives has a more severe impact than toward strangers.

From this, the meaning of his (عليه السلام) saying becomes clear: "Whichever man among you becomes angry with a relative, let him draw near to him..." etc. For drawing near to a relative is an observance of the law of kinship and a strengthening of its side; through this, the kinship is alerted and stirred to its natural function, and its effect is renewed through the manifestation of compassion and love.

Similarly, his (عليه السلام) saying at the end of the narration: "And whichever man becomes angry while standing, let him cling to the earth..." etc. For if anger arises from the soul's recklessness (طيش) and impetuosity (نزق), its manifestation and boiling over are rooted in the soul's whims and Satan's making it heedless, diverting it toward flimsy, imaginary causes. By changing one's state from standing to sitting, the soul is diverted from one condition to a new one, allowing it to occupy itself with the new state and thus turn away from anger. This is because the human soul, by its primordial nature (الفطرة), is more inclined toward mercy than toward anger. For this very reason, some narrations mention a general change of state during anger, as in al-Majalis, from Imam al-Sadiq on the authority of his father (عليه السلام): that he mentioned anger and said: "Indeed, a man may become angry until he is never satisfied again, and thereby enters the Fire. So, whichever man becomes angry while standing, let him sit—for the defilement of Satan will leave him. And if he is sitting, let him stand. And whichever man becomes angry with a relative, let him go to him, draw near, and touch him; for when kinship touches kinship, it grows calm." I say: Its effect is perceptible and proven by experience.

His (عليه السلام) saying: "And indeed, it is suspended from the Throne, making a sound like the clanging of iron (تنقضه انتقاض الحديد)..." etc., means it produces a sound in it like that produced in iron by tapping. In al-Sihah: al-Inqad (الإنقاض) is a slight sound like tapping. We have previously mentioned in the discussion on the Pedestal (الكرسي) a general indication—the details of which will come in the discussion on the Throne (العرش)—that the Throne refers to the station of active, undifferentiated knowledge (العلم الإجمالي الفعلي) of events. It is the stage of existence where the scattered reins of events and the diverse causes and cosmic conditions meet. It alone moves the chains of various and scattered causes; that is, it relates to their pervasive spirit that moves them, just as the reins of a kingdom, despite its different regions, affairs, and forms, meet at the king's throne, and a single word issued from him moves the chains of active powers and stations in the kingdom, manifesting in every instance with the appropriate form and effect.

Kinship (الرحم), as you have learned, is a reality that is like a spirit flowing through the molds of individuals who are united by the bond of relationship. Thus, it is among the attachments of the Throne. When it is wronged or oppressed, it seeks refuge in that to which it is attached and seeks aid. This is his (عليه السلام) saying: "making a sound like the clanging of iron," which is one of the most exquisite metaphors. He likened what occurs in this state to the tapping upon iron, which produces a resonance (رنين) that, through vibration and oscillation, encompasses the entire mass of the iron, as in the striking of bells, bowls, and the like.

His (عليه السلام) saying: "and it cries out: O Allah, join him who joins me and sever him who severs me!" is a narration of the essence (فحوى) of its seeking refuge and aid. Numerous narrations state that joining kinship ties increases the lifespan and severing them cuts it short. It was mentioned in the discussion on the connection between actions and external events in the second volume of this book that the Director of this cosmic system steers it toward righteous goals and ends, and He never neglects this. If a part or parts of it become corrupt, He treats it either by rectification or by excision and removal. The one who severs kinship ties fights Allah in His creation; so if he is not rectified through reform, Allah truncates his life and cuts off his progeny. As for the fact that man today does not perceive this reality and its like, it is no wonder, for ailments have surrounded the body of humanity, becoming mixed, indistinguishable, and chronic. Thus, the senses find no respite through which they might be strong enough to perceive the pain and the torment.

Ayahs

وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بِٱلطَّيِّبِ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا (٢)
Give the orphans their property, and do not replace the good with the bad, and do not eat up their property [by mingling it] with your own property, for that is indeed a great sin. (2)
وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا۟ فِى ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ فَٱنكِحُوا۟ مَا طَابَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ مَثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ فَوَٰحِدَةً أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا۟ (٣)
If you fear that you may not deal justly with the orphans, then marry [other] women that you like, two, three, or four. But if you fear that you may not treat them fairly, then [marry only] one, or [marry from among] your slave-women. That makes it likelier that you will not be unfair. (3)
وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ صَدُقَـٰتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً ۚ فَإِن طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَن شَىْءٍ مِّنْهُ نَفْسًا فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيٓـًٔا مَّرِيٓـًٔا (٤)
Give women their dowries, handing it over to them; but if they remit anything of it of their own accord, then consume it as [something] lawful and wholesome. (4)
وَلَا تُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلسُّفَهَآءَ أَمْوَٰلَكُمُ ٱلَّتِى جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ قِيَـٰمًا وَٱرْزُقُوهُمْ فِيهَا وَٱكْسُوهُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (٥)
Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned you to manage: provide for them out of it and clothe them, and speak to them honourable words. (5)
وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا (٦)
Test the orphans when they reach the age of marriage. Then if you discern in them maturity, deliver to them their property. And do not consume it lavishly and hastily lest they should grow up. As for him who is well-off, let him be abstemious, and as for him who is poor, let him eat in an honourable manner. And when you deliver to them their property, take witnesses over them, and Allah suffices as reckoner. (6)

(Commentary)

The verses are a continuation of the introduction and preamble placed at the beginning of the Surah to clarify the laws of inheritance (المواريث) and the core regulations of marriage (التزويج), such as the number of wives and the identification of unmarriageable kin (المحارم). These two chapters are among the greatest and most significant chapters of the laws governing human society, having the most profound impact on the formation and preservation of society. Through marriage (النكاح), the status of human offspring—who are the constituent parts of society and the factors that form it—is determined. Inheritance (الإرث) pertains to the distribution of existing worldly wealth, upon which the structure of society is built for its livelihood and survival.

Within their exposition, the verses have addressed the prohibition of adultery (الزنى) and fornication (السفاح), and the prohibition of consuming wealth unjustly (أكل المال بالباطل) except through trade by mutual consent. At that point, two valuable foundations were established for the affairs of society in its most critical aspects: the matter of offspring and the matter of wealth.

From here, the reason for the attention given to the preamble—aimed at clarifying these laws that pertain to human social life (الاجتماع الإنساني) and are rooted in its very foundations—becomes apparent. Turning people away from what their community has grown accustomed to, what their thoughts have fused with, what their flesh has grown upon, what their ancestors died practicing, and what their descendants were raised upon, is exceedingly difficult.

Such is the nature of the laws mentioned at the beginning of this Surah. This becomes clear through a general reflection on the state of the human world at that time in general, and the state of the Arab world—their land being the site of the Quran’s revelation and the emergence of Islam—in particular, and on the manner in which the Quran was gradually revealed and the Islamic laws were manifested in their legislation (تشريعها).

(A Discourse on the First Ignorance)

The Quran calls the era of the Arabs immediately preceding the emergence of Islam "the Ignorance" (الجاهلية). This is nothing but an allusion to the fact that the governing principle among them then was ignorance (الجهل) rather than knowledge (العلم), and that what dominated them in everything was falsehood (الباطل) and the following of mere opinion (سفر الرأي) rather than truth (الحق). They were indeed as the Quran narrates of their affairs; the Exalted said: يَظُنُّونَ بِاللهِ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ ظَنَّ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ — "they entertained odd thoughts about Allah, thoughts of [the age of] ignorance" (3:154). He said: أَفَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ — "Do they seek the judgement of [the age of] ignorance?" (5:50). He said: إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ — "When the faithless harboured zealotry in their hearts, the zealotry of [the age of] ignorance" (48:26). And He said: وَلا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ الْأُولى — "and do not display your finery with the display of the former [age of] ignorance" (33:33).

The Arabs at that time were bordered to the south by Abyssinia, which was Christian; to the west by the Roman Empire, which was Christian; to the north by the Persians, who were Magians; and elsewhere by India and Egypt, both of which were pagan (وثنيتان). Within their own land were groups of Jews. Despite this, they—meaning the Arabs—were pagans, most of whom lived a tribal life. All of this produced for them a barbaric, nomadic society (اجتماعا همجيا بدويا) containing a mixture of Jewish, Christian, and Magian customs, while they were intoxicated by their ignorance. The Exalted said: وَإِنْ تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ — "If you obey most of those on the earth, they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. They follow nothing but conjectures and they do nothing but surmise" (6:116).

The clans—the nomads—despite their wretched and lowly standard of living, lived by raids (الغزوات), launching incursions (شن الغارات), and snatching whatever was in the hands of others, whether goods or honor. There was neither security (أمن) nor trust (أمانة) among them, nor peace (سلم) nor safety (سلامة). Authority belonged to the victor, and ownership to the one who laid his hand upon it.

As for the men, virtue among them consisted of shedding blood, the zealotry of ignorance (الحمية الجاهلية), pride, arrogance, following oppressors, violating the rights of the oppressed, mutual enmity, rivalry, gambling, drinking wine, adultery, and eating carrion, blood, and shriveled dates.

As for the women, they were deprived of the privileges of human society. They possessed no will over themselves, nor any agency over their actions. They possessed no inheritance. Men married them without any limit on number, as was the case among the Jews and some pagans. Despite this, they would display their finery (يتبرجن) and invite whom they loved to themselves. Adultery and fornication (السفاح) spread among them, even among the chaste married women. A strange instance of their public exposure was that they would sometimes perform the Pilgrimage (الحج) naked.

As for the children, they were attributed to their fathers but did not inherit if they were young; the adults took the entire inheritance. Part of the inheritance was the wife of the deceased. The young, both male and female, and the women were deprived.

However, if the deceased left a young child, he would inherit, but the powerful would take charge of the orphan's (اليتيم) affairs and consume his wealth. If the orphan was a girl, they would marry her and consume her wealth, then divorce her and let her go, leaving her with no wealth to sustain her and no suitor interested in marrying and providing for her. Dealing with the affairs of orphans was one of the most frequent occurrences among them due to the constant state of wars, raids, and incursions; naturally, killing was widespread among them.

Part of the misery of their children was that their ruined lands and barren, desert territories were prone to drought and famine. Thus, a man would kill his children for fear of penury (إملاق) (6:151). They would bury infant girls alive (يئدون البنات) (81:8), and one of the most loathsome things to a man was to be given the news of a female birth (43:17).

As for the state of government among them, although the peripheries of the Peninsula sometimes had kings ruling under the protection of the strongest and nearest neighbors—such as Iran for the northern regions, Rome for the western regions, and Abyssinia for the southern regions—the central towns like Mecca, Yathrib, Ta'if, and others lived in a state more akin to a republic, yet not quite. The clans in the desert, and even within the towns, were managed by the rule of their chiefs and elders, and sometimes the situation would transform into a monarchy.

This was the strange chaos (الهرج) that manifested in every group of them in a different color and appeared in every region of the Peninsula in a different form, alongside the bizarre customs and superstitious beliefs (الاعتقادات الخرافية) circulating among them. Add to that the affliction of illiteracy (الأمية) and the absence of teaching and learning in their towns, let alone among the clans and tribes.

All that we have mentioned of their conditions, actions, and the customs and traditions circulating among them is most clearly understood from the context of the Quranic verses and the addresses directed to them. Reflect upon the objectives sought by the verses and the explanations delivered to them in Mecca first, and then after the emergence and strength of Islam in Medina second; and on the descriptions by which it describes them, the matters for which it censures and blames them, and the prohibitions directed at them in their intensity and mildness. If you contemplate all of that, you will find the truth of what we have related to you. Moreover, history records all of this and addresses details we have not mentioned due to the conciseness and brevity of the noble verses on the matter. The briefest and most comprehensive word to convey the sum of these meanings is the Quran’s naming of this era as the "Age of Ignorance" (عهد الجاهلية), for it summarized in its meaning all these details. This was the state of the Arab world at that time.

As for the world surrounding them then—Rome, Persia, Abyssinia, India, and others—the Quran speaks of it in general terms. As for the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) among them—meaning the Jews and Christians and those associated with them—their societies were managed by despotic whims and the individual dictates of kings, chiefs, governors, and officials. They were naturally divided into two classes: a ruling class that did as it pleased, trifling with lives, honor, and wealth; and a ruled class that was enslaved and humiliated, having no security in wealth, honor, or life, and no freedom of will except what agreed with those above them. The ruling class had won over the religious scholars and the bearers of the law, becoming integrated with them. They held the hearts and minds of the common people in their hands. In reality, they were the rulers of people's religion and their worldly life; they ruled the religion as they wished through the tongues and pens of the scholars, and the worldly life through the whip and the sword.

The ruled class was also divided among themselves according to their strength in power and wealth, similar to the first division—"and people follow the religion of their kings"—into the classes of the affluent (المترفين) and the weak, the helpless, and the slaves. Likewise, into the master of the house and his subordinates among women and children. Similarly, into men who possessed freedom of will and action in all affairs of life, and women who were deprived of all that, being purely followers of men, serving them in what was desired of them without even the slightest independence.

The sum of these historical realities is evident from the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims.’" (3:64). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) included this in his letter to Heraclius, the Great of Rome, and it is said he also wrote it to the Great of Egypt, the Great of Abyssinia, the King of Persia, and to Najran.

Likewise, the saying of the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you." (49:13). And His saying regarding the recommendation to marry slave-girls and young women: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ — "You are all [descended] from one another. So marry them with the permission of their families" (4:25). And His saying regarding women: أَنِّي لا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "I do not let the work of any worker, male or female, go to waste. You are all [descended] from one another." (3:195), among other verses.

As for those other than the People of the Book—who were then the pagans and those associated with them—the situation among them was worse and more ominous than that of the People of the Book. The verses revealed in argumentation against them reveal the failure of their efforts and the loss of their bargain in all affairs of life and modes of happiness. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ إِنَّ فِي هذا لَبَلاغاً لِقَوْمٍ عابِدِينَ وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ قُلْ إِنَّما يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ آذَنْتُكُمْ عَلى سَواءٍ — "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’ There is indeed a message in this for a people who worship [Allah]. We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations. Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So will you submit?’ But if they turn away, say, ‘I have proclaimed [the truth] to you all alike...’" (21:105-109). And the Exalted said: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "This Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby, and whomever it may reach." (6:19).

(How did the Islamic Call emerge?)

The state of human society then (the Age of Ignorance) was as you have heard: people devoted to falsehood, with corruption and injustice dominating them in all affairs of life. Here is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد)—the true religion—seeking to make the truth sovereign and to grant it absolute authority over them, to purify their hearts from the stains of polytheism (الشرك), to sanctify their actions, and to reform their society after corruption had taken root in its roots, branches, interior, and exterior.

In short, Allah desires to guide them to the manifest truth; He does not desire to lay any hardship upon them, but He desires to purify them and to complete His blessing upon them. Thus, the falsehood they were upon and the word of truth He desired from them were at two opposite points and two conflicting poles. Was it then necessary to win over some of them and reform the rest of the people of falsehood through them, and then through some the others, out of eagerness for the truth to appear by any means possible—as it is said: "the importance of the end justifies the means even if prohibited"? This is the political conduct employed by politicians.

This type of conduct toward a goal rarely fails to reach its objectives in any field it is applied, except that it does not apply in the field of the manifest truth (الحق الصريح), which is the aim of the Islamic Call (الدعوة الإسلامية). For the end is the child of its premises and means; how can falsehood give birth to truth, or the diseased produce the sound, when the offspring is a composite taken from the two that bore it?

The aim and desire of politics is to reach power and dominance, to gain precedence, prominence, and distinction, and to enjoy benefits in whatever way happens, applying to any description of good or evil, truth or falsehood. It has no desire for the truth. But the true call seeks nothing but the true end; if it were to use falsehood as a means to it, that would be an endorsement and implementation of falsehood by it, thus becoming a false call rather than a true one.

This reality has prominent manifestations in the biography (سيرة) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the pure ones from his progeny (عليه السلام).

With this, his Lord commanded him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Quran was revealed in instances where they sought to entice him toward compromise or oily diplomacy (المداهنة)—even slightly—in the matter of religion. The Exalted said: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ وَلا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ وَلا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ — "Say, ‘O faithless ones! I do not worship what you worship, nor do you worship what I worship. Nor will I worship what you have worshipped, nor will you worship what I worship. To you your religion, and to me my religion.’" (Surah al-Kafirun). And the Exalted said, in a tone of threat: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ — "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly leaned towards them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death" (17:74-75). And the Exalted said: وَما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُداً — "nor do I take those who mislead as assistants." (18:51). And the Exalted said—which is a parable of broad meaning: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except scantily." (7:58).

Since truth does not mix with falsehood nor harmonize with it, Allah, the Glorified, when He burdened him with the weight of the Call, commanded him to use gentleness and gradualism (التدرج) in its affair, considering the Call itself, the one called, and that to which they are called, from three perspectives:

First: From the perspective of the true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) and the legislated laws (القوانين المشرعة) contained in the religion, which are meant to reform the affairs of human society and cut off the sources of corruption. It is exceedingly difficult to change people's beliefs, especially if they are rooted in character and actions, and customs have settled upon them, centuries have passed over them, ancestors have followed them, and descendants have been raised upon them. This is especially true if the word of religion and its call encompass all affairs of life and cover all human movements and stillness, inwardly and outwardly, at all times and for all persons, individuals, and societies without exception (as is the case with Islam). The mere conception of this is enough to stun the intellect, or it is a customary impossibility.

The difficulty and hardship of this matter in actions is greater than in beliefs. For man's familiarity, habit, and contact with action is older than with belief; it is more apparent to his senses and more favored by his desires and whims. Therefore, the Call manifested the true beliefs in its early stages as a whole, but the divine laws and statutes appeared gradually, ruling by ruling.

In short, the Call was gradual in presenting its hidden contents to the people so that natures would not recoil from receiving them, and souls would not be shaken by the arrangement of some parts of the Call upon others. This which we have mentioned is apparent to the reflecting researcher of these realities; for he finds the Quranic verses differing in their presentation of divine knowledge and legislated laws between their Meccan and Medinan periods. The Meccan verses call to universal principles (كليات) in which the discourse is summarized, while the Medinan—by which we mean those revealed after the Migration (الهجرة) wherever they were revealed—detail the discourse and provide the specifics of the laws whose universals and summaries preceded in the Meccan verses. The Exalted said: كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى إِنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى عَبْداً إِذا صَلَّى أَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ كانَ عَلَى الْهُدى أَوْ أَمَرَ بِالتَّقْوى أَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى أَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ بِأَنَّ اللهَ يرى — "Indeed man becomes rebellious when he considers himself without need. Indeed to your Lord is the return. Have you seen him who forbids a servant when he prays? Have you seen if he is on [the path of] guidance, or bids to God-consciousness? Have you seen if he denies and turns away? Does he not know that Allah sees?" (96:6-14). These verses were revealed at the beginning of the Message after the Prophethood, as alluded to in the verses of fasting in the second volume; they contain a summary of monotheism (التوحيد) and the Return (المعاد), and a summary of the matter of godwariness (التقوى) and worship (العبادة).

And the Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ — "O you enwrapped in your mantle! Arise and warn! And magnify your Lord!" (74:1-3), which are also among the verses revealed at the start of the Mission (البعثة). And the Exalted said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it is certainly a failure." (91:7-10). And the Exalted said: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى — "Felicitous is he who purifies himself, and celebrates the Name of his Lord, and prays." (87:14-15). And His saying: قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا إِلَيْهِ وَاسْتَغْفِرُوهُ وَوَيْلٌ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ — "Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So be steadfast in your devotion to Him and plead to Him for forgiveness.’ And woe to the polytheists—those who do not pay the zakat and who disbelieve in the Hereafter. As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be an everlasting reward for them." (41:6-8). These verses are also from those revealed in the early days of the Mission.

And the Exalted said: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ مِنْ إِمْلاقٍ نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَلا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَلا تَقْرَبُوا مالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّى يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزانَ بِالْقِسْطِ لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها وَإِذا قُلْتُمْ فَاعْدِلُوا وَلَوْ كانَ ذا قُرْبى وَبِعَهْدِ اللهِ أَوْفُوا ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ — "Say, ‘Come, I will tell you what your Lord has forbidden you: that you shall not ascribe any partners to Him, and you shall be good to your parents, and do not kill your children because of poverty—We provide for you and for them—and do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and do not kill the soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may exercise your reason. And do not approach the orphan’s property, except in the best [manner], until he comes of age. And observe fully the measure and the balance with justice. We do not task any soul except according to its capacity. And when you speak, be just, even if it were a near relative, and fulfill Allah’s covenant. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may take admonition. Indeed this is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may be God-conscious.’" (6:151-153).

Observe the context of the noble verses, how the discourse was summarized regarding the legal prohibitions first, and the legal commands second. It was summarized by gathering all under a description that even the common intellect does not recoil from accepting. For there is no hesitation regarding the foulness of indecencies (الفواحش) and the necessity of avoiding them, and refraining from them is within one's grasp. Likewise, congregating upon a straight path (صراط مستقيم) that ensures safety from division, weakness, and falling into destruction and ruin is something no one doubts by the judgment of instinct (الغريزة). Thus, in this Call, support was drawn from the instincts of those called. For that very reason, it mentioned the listed prohibitions in a detailed manner, such as being undutiful to parents and mistreating them, killing children out of poverty, killing the inviolable soul, and consuming the orphan's wealth, to the end of what was mentioned. For human instinctive emotions support the Call in its command due to their natural aversion to committing these crimes and sins. There are other verses that the reflector will encounter, seeing that the situation in them is similar to what we have mentioned in the verses we cited.

In any case, the Meccan verses are characterized by calling to summaries that were detailed afterward by the Medinan verses. Nevertheless, the Medinan verses themselves are not devoid of such gradualism; not all religious laws and statutes were revealed in Medina at once, but rather gradually and in stages (نجوما).

It suffices you to reflect on a sample of them that has been previously alluded to, which are the verses on the prohibition of wine. The Exalted said: وَمِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَالْأَعْنابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً — "and from the fruits of date palms and vines you derive intoxicants and good provision" (16:67).
