This verse is Meccan; it mentioned the matter of wine and remained silent about it, except for the allusion in His saying: وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً (good provision) that intoxicants are not part of "good provision." Then He said: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and sin...’" (7:33). This verse is also Meccan, explicitly prohibiting sin (الإثم), but it did not clarify that drinking wine is a sin, as a gentleness in the Call to abandon an evil habit to which their desires had attracted them, upon which their flesh had grown, and their bones had strengthened. Then He said: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما — "They ask you concerning wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for the people, but their sinfulness is greater than their profit.’" (2:219). This verse is Medinan, clarifying that drinking wine is part of the sin that the verse of al-A'raf prohibited. The tone of the verse—as you see—is one of gentleness and counsel. Then the Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَالْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلاةِ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُنْتَهُونَ — "O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them, so that you may be felicitous. Indeed Satan seeks to cast enmity and hatred among you through wine and gambling, and to hinder you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. Will you, then, desist?" (5:90-91). This verse is Medinan, concluding the matter of prohibition.

Similar to it is inheritance (الإرث). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) first established brotherhood (آخى) between his companions, and one brother inherited from the other in the beginning, preparing them for what Allah would later legislate regarding inheritance. Then the saying of the Exalted was revealed: وَأُولُوا الْأَرْحامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ — "and the blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants" (33:6). In this manner, most abrogating (الناسخ) and abrogated (المنسوخ) laws were revealed.

In all these instances and their like, the Call was gradual in manifesting and implementing the laws, adopting gentleness for the wisdom of preservation, ease of burden, and good reception with acceptance. The Exalted said: وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً — "We have sent the Quran in [discrete] parts so that you may read it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in stages." (17:106). Had the Quran been revealed to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) all at once, and then the Messenger explained the details of its statutes as assigned to him by the saying of the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ — "We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them" (16:44), and thus brought the explanation of all its knowledge of belief, ethics, universal laws of worship, and the laws governing transactions and politics, and so on—the understandings then would not have been able to conceive or carry them, let alone people accepting them, acting upon them, and having them rule over their hearts in their will and over their limbs and bodies in their actions. Thus, its revelation at intervals is what prepared for the religion the possibility of acceptance and taking root in the hearts. The Exalted said: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً — "The faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ So it is, that We may strengthen your heart with it, and We have recited it in a measured tone." (24:32). In the verse is an indication that He, the Glorified, was gentle with His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in revealing the Quran in stages, just as He was gentle with his community. Reflect upon that and contemplate it, and the end of the verse: "and We have recited it in a measured tone."

It is necessary to remember that the progression from summary to detail and the gradualism in delivering the laws to the people—out of gentleness, good upbringing, and consideration of interest—is different from oily diplomacy (المداهنة) and compromise, and this is apparent.

Second: Gradual conduct in terms of selecting those called and following an order among them. It is known that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent to all of humanity, without his call being restricted to one people over another, one place over another, or one time over another (the latter two actually refer back to the first). Certainly, the Exalted said: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Say, ‘O mankind! I am the Apostle of Allah to you all, [of Him] to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth.’" (7:158). And the Exalted said: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "This Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby, and whomever it may reach." (6:19). And the Exalted said: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ — "We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations." (21:107).

Furthermore, history narrates his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calling of the Jews—who are from the Children of Israel—the Romans, the Persians, the Abyssinians, and the Egyptians, and they are not from the Arabs. Among the famous people who believed in him were Salman, who was Persian; his caller to prayer (مؤذنه) Bilal, who was Abyssinian; and Suhayb, who was Roman. Thus, the universality of his prophethood (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his time is beyond doubt, and the previous verses include by their generality all times and places as well.

Moreover, the saying of the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ — "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a gradual revelation from One who is All-wise, All-laudable." (41:41-42), and the saying of the Exalted: وَلكِنْ رَسُولَ اللهِ وَخاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ — "but [he is] the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets" (33:40), indicate the universality of prophethood and its inclusion of all places and times as well. The detailed discussion of these verses is to be sought in their respective places in the commentary.

In any case, the prophethood is universal. One who reflects on the breadth of Islamic knowledge and laws, and the state of the world when Islam appeared—the darkness of ignorance and the filth of corruption and transgression—will not doubt the impossibility of confronting the world and combating polytheism and corruption all at once.

Rather, it was necessary in wisdom that the Call begin with some, and that those some be the people of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), then through the establishment of the religion in them, it would manifest to others, and so it was. The Exalted said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ — "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them." (14:4). And He said: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ فَقَرَأَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs, and had he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it." (26:198-199). The verses indicating the connection of the Call and warning to the Arabs do not indicate more than their being some of those to whom the Call and warning pertained. Likewise, the verses revealed in challenge (التحدي) through the Quran—if they contain what restricts the challenge to eloquence (البلاغة) alone—it is because eloquence is one of the aspects of the challenge through inimitability (الإعجاز). There is no evidence in that for the Arab nation being the only intended target of the Call. Yes, the language is intended independently for clarification, as passed in His saying: "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them." And His saying: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ — "We relate to you the best of narratives by revealing this Quran to you" (12:3). And His saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَتَنْزِيلُ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ — "It is indeed a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds. The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language." (26:192-195). Thus, the Arabic language is the most perfect manifestor of mental meanings and objectives; therefore, Allah, the Glorified, chose it for His August Book among all languages and said: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ — "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (43:3).

In short, Allah commanded him, after establishing the core of the Call, to begin with his kinsfolk, saying: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ — "Warn your nearest relations" (26:214). He obeyed His command, gathered his kinsfolk, called them to what he was sent for, and promised them that the first to respond to him would be his successor after him. Ali (peace be upon him) responded to him, so he thanked him for that, while the others mocked him, as recorded in the authentic narrations, history books, and biographies. Then people from his family joined him, such as Khadijah his wife, his uncle Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Ubaydah, and his uncle Abu Talib—according to what the Shi'a have narrated, and in his poetry are explicit statements and allusions to that (he only did not manifest his faith so as to be able to protect him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)).

Then Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to expand the Call to his people, as appears from His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى وَمَنْ حَوْلَها — "Thus have We revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it" (42:7). And His saying: لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ — "that you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before you, so that they may be guided." (32:3). And His saying: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "This Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby, and whomever it may reach." (6:19). This verse is among the evidences that the Call is not restricted to them, and that he only began with them out of wisdom and interest.

Then Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to expand the Call to the world, including all followers of religions and others, as indicated by the previous verses such as His saying: "Say, ‘O mankind! I am the Apostle of Allah to you all...’" and His saying: "but [he is] the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets," and others mentioned before.

Third: Adopting levels in terms of calling, guidance, and implementation, which are: calling by word, passive calling (الدعوة السلبية), and struggle (الجهاد).

As for calling by word, it is something understood from the entire Quran by necessity. Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to observe human dignity and good character in that; the Exalted said: قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ — "Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you. It has been revealed to me...’" (18:110). And He said: وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "and lower your wing to the faithful." (15:88). And He said: وَلا تَسْتَوِي الْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا السَّيِّئَةُ ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ — "Good and evil [conduct] are not equal. Repel [evil] with what is best. [If you do so,] behold, he between whom and you was enmity, will be as though he were a sympathetic friend." (41:34). And He said: وَلا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ — "and do not confer a favor to seek more [in return]" (74:6), among many other verses.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to use all arts of expression according to the differences in understanding and the capacities of individuals. The Exalted said: ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ — "Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best." (16:125).

As for passive calling, it is the believers' withdrawal from the disbelievers in their religion and actions, and the formation of an Islamic society that does not mix with the religion of others who do not monotheize Allah, the Glorified, nor with the actions of non-Muslims such as sins and other moral vices, except what the necessity of life requires of social interaction. The Exalted said: لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ — "To you your religion, and to me my religion." (109:6). And He said: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَمَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ وَلا تَطْغَوْا إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ — "So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not overstep the bounds. Indeed He sees best what you do. And do not incline toward the wrongdoers, lest the Fire should touch you, and you will have no friends besides Allah, then you will not be helped." (111:112-113). And He said: فَلِذلِكَ فَادْعُ وَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ وَقُلْ آمَنْتُ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ مِنْ كِتابٍ وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّنا وَرَبُّكُمْ لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ لا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمُ اللهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ — "So summon to this, and be steadfast, just as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires, and say, ‘I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down. I have been commanded to do justice among you. Allah is our Lord and your Lord. Our deeds belong to us and your deeds belong to you. There is no argument between us and you. Allah will gather us together, and toward Him is the return.’" (42:15). And the Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِّ — "O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection while they have defied the truth that has come to you..." until He said: لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ أَنْ تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوا إِلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ إِنَّما يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ قاتَلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ وَظاهَرُوا عَلى إِخْراجِكُمْ أَنْ تَوَلَّوهُمْ وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ — "Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion and did not expel you from your homes, that you deal with them kindly and equitably. Indeed Allah loves the equitable. Allah only forbids you in regard to those who made war against you on account of religion and expelled you from your homes and supported [others] in your expulsion, that you make friends with them, and whoever makes friends with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers." (60:1-9). The verses regarding disavowal (التبري) and withdrawal from the enemies of religion are many, and they—as you see—explain the meaning of this disavowal, its manner, and its specifics.

As for struggle (الجهاد), the discussion regarding it has preceded in the commentary on the verses of struggle in Surah al-Baqarah. These three levels are among the merits and prides of the Islamic religion. The first level is necessary in the latter two, as is the second in the third. It was part of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practice to call and advise in his battles before commencing them, as his Lord, the Glorified, commanded him, saying: "But if they turn away, say, ‘I have proclaimed [the truth] to you all alike...’"

Among the most slanderous words is what they have cast at Islam: that it is a religion of the sword rather than a religion of calling, despite the Book, the biography, and history testifying to it and illuminating it. But for whomever Allah has not made a light, there is no light for him.

Some of these critics are from the people of the Church, which for centuries had established within it a religious court that sentenced those who deviated from the religion to the fire, imitating the divine court on the Day of Resurrection. Its agents would roam the lands, bringing to it people they accused of apostasy, even for modern statements in physics and mathematics that were not held by the Scholastic philosophy (الفلسفة الإسكولاستيكية) that the Church was promoting.

I wonder, is the spreading of monotheism, the cutting off of the roots of paganism, and the purification of the world from the filth of corruption more important to the sound intellect, or the strangling of one who spoke of something like the movement of the earth or the denial of the Ptolemaic spheres, forcing his breath back into his chest? It was the Church that incited the Christian world against the Muslims in the name of a crusade against paganism, maintaining the Crusades for approximately two hundred years, destroying lands, annihilating millions of souls, and violating honors.

Others are not from the people of the Church but are claimants of civilization and freedom! These are the ones who ignite the fires of world wars and turn the world upside down whenever their whims suggest a slight danger to some of their material interests. Is the establishment of polytheism in the world, the degradation of morals, the death of virtues, and the surrounding of the earth and its inhabitants with omen and corruption more harmful, or the loss of authority over a few inches of land or the loss of a few measly coins?! Indeed, man is ungrateful to his Lord.

It pleases me to quote what some of the great researchers mentioned in this regard in one of his treatises; he (may Allah have mercy on him) said: "The means followed for social reform, achieving justice, tearing down oppression, and resisting evil and corruption are almost limited to three types:

  1. Means of calling and guidance through speeches, articles, books, and pamphlets. This is the noble plan alluded to by the Truth, may His majesty be exalted, in His saying: ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ, and His saying: ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ. This is the method Islam used at the beginning of the Mission..." until he said:

  2. Means of peaceful and passive resistance, such as demonstrations, strikes, economic boycotts, non-cooperation with oppressors, and not participating in their works and government. The proponents of this method do not permit the path of war, killing, and violence. This is alluded to by the saying of the Exalted: وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا فَتَمَسَّكُمُ النَّارُ, and لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ. In the Noble Quran are many verses pointing to this method. The most famous of those who called for this method and emphasized it were the Indian prophet Buddha, the Messiah (peace be upon him), the Russian writer Tolstoy, and the Indian spiritual leader Gandhi.

  3. War, revolution, and fighting.

Islam progresses through these three methods: "The first" is good advice and peaceful calling. If it does not succeed in repelling the oppressors and averting their corruption and despotism, then "the second" is peaceful or passive boycott and non-cooperation and non-participation with them. If that does not avail or benefit, then "the third" is armed revolution. For Allah is never pleased with oppression; rather, the one who is pleased and silent is a partner to the oppressor.

Islam is a creed (عقيدة). He who says that Islam spread its call by the sword and fighting has erred and overstepped. For Islam is faith and creed, and creed is not obtained by compulsion and coercion; it only submits to proof and evidence. The Glorious Quran proclaims this in several verses, including: لا إِكْراهَ فِي الدِّينِ قَدْ تَبَيَّنَ الرُّشْدُ مِنَ الْغَيِّ — "There is no compulsion in religion: rectitude has become distinct from error" (2:256).

Islam only used the sword and unsheathed the weapon against the oppressors who were not convinced by signs and proofs. It used force against those who stood as a stumbling block in the way of the call to truth. It prepared the weapon to repel the evil of the stubborn, not to bring them into the fold of Islam. The Truth, may His majesty be exalted, says: قاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّى لا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ — "Fight them until there is no more chaos" (2:193). Thus, fighting is only to repel chaos (الفتنة), not to embrace the religion and creed.

Islam does not fight out of whim or choice; rather, the enemies corner it, so it resorts to it out of necessity. It only takes from it by noble means; it prohibits in war and peace destruction, burning, poisoning, and cutting off water from enemies, just as it prohibits killing women, children, and prisoners, and recommends gentleness toward them and kindness to them regardless of their enmity and hatred for Muslims. It prohibits assassination in war and peace, and prohibits killing the elderly, the helpless, and those who did not initiate war. It prohibits attacking the enemy at night—"so proclaim [the truth] to them all alike"—and prohibits killing on suspicion and accusation, and punishment before the commission of a crime, among similar actions that honor and chivalry reject and which stem from meanness, cruelty, baseness, and savagery.

All those actions that the honor of Islam refused to commit even with enemies in all the battles and wars it had, have been committed in their most hideous forms and most terrifying types by the civilized nations in this age which they call the Age of Light. Yes, the Age of Light has permitted the killing of women, children, the elderly, and the sick, and night raids and night attacks with weapons and bombs on unarmed and peaceful civilians, and has permitted mass killing.

Did not the Germans in the Second World War send rocket bombs to London, destroying buildings and killing women, children, and peaceful residents?! Did not the Germans kill thousands of prisoners?! Did not the Allies in the past war send thousands of planes to Germany to destroy its cities?! Did not the Americans drop atomic bombs on Japanese cities?!

After the invention of modern means of destruction like rockets and atomic and hydrogen bombs, none but Allah knows what will befall the earth of torment, destruction, tragedies, and pains if a third world war occurs and the warring nations resort to using those means. May Allah guide man to the path of correctness and lead him to the straight path. End of quote.

The saying of the Exalted: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى أَمْوالَهُمْ — "Give the orphans their property..." to the end of the verse. It is a command to give the orphans their property, which is a preamble to the two subsequent phrases: وَلا تَتَبَدَّلُوا (and do not replace) etc., or the two phrases are like an explanation for this phrase. However, the justification at the end of the verse—because it refers back to the two phrases or the last phrase—supports that the first phrase is placed in the discourse as a preparation for the prohibition in the two subsequent phrases.

The core of the prohibition against harmful disposal of orphans' wealth, as previously explained, is a preamble and preparation for what will be mentioned of the laws of inheritance, and for what will be mentioned in the following verse of the law of marriage.

As for the saying of the Exalted: وَلا تَتَبَدَّلُوا الْخَبِيثَ بِالطَّيِّبِ — "and do not replace the good with the bad," meaning: do not replace the bad (الخبيث) of your wealth with the good (الطيب) of their wealth, such that they have good wealth with you, so you set it aside for yourselves and return to them its equivalent from your inferior wealth. It is possible that the meaning is: do not replace the consumption of the lawful with the consumption of the prohibited—as has been said—but the first meaning is more apparent. For it is evident that each of the two phrases—meaning His saying: وَلا تَتَبَدَّلُوا etc. and His saying: وَلا تَأْكُلُوا (and do not consume) etc.—is an exposition of a specific type of impermissible disposal, and His saying: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى etc. is a preparation for the exposition of both together. As for His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ حُوباً كَبِيراً — "Indeed that is a great sin," the word الحوب means sin (الإثم), both as a verbal noun and a noun.

The saying of the Exalted: وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا فِي الْيَتامى فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ — "If you fear that you may not deal justly with the orphans, then marry [other] women who seem good to you" (4:3). It was previously alluded to that the people of Ignorance among the Arabs—who were rarely free from wars, killings, assassinations, and raids, and among whom incidents of killing were frequent—had many orphans. The chiefs and the powerful among them would take orphan women and their wealth, marry them, and consume their wealth along with their own. Then they would not deal justly with them, and perhaps they would turn them out after consuming their wealth, so they became neglected and destitute, having no wealth to sustain them and no suitor interested in them to marry and provide for them. The Noble Quran severely denounced this evil habit and manifest injustice, and emphasized the prohibition of oppressing orphans and consuming their wealth, as in the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً إِنَّما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ ناراً وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيراً — "Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only ingest fire into their bellies, and soon they will enter the Blaze." (4:10), and the saying of the Exalted: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى أَمْوالَهُمْ وَلا تَتَبَدَّلُوا الْخَبِيثَ بِالطَّيِّبِ وَلا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَهُمْ إِلى أَمْوالِكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ حُوباً كَبِيراً — "Give the orphans their property, and do not replace the good with the bad, and do not consume their property along with your own property. Indeed that is a great sin." (4:2). Consequently, the Muslims became fearful for themselves—as has been said—and feared greatly, to the point that they removed the orphans from their homes for fear of being afflicted by their wealth and failing in their rights. Whoever kept an orphan with him would separate his portion of food and drink; if anything remained of their food, they would not approach it until it stayed and spoiled. They became distressed by this and asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about it and complained to him. Then there was revealed: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْيَتامى قُلْ إِصْلاحٌ لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ وَإِنْ تُخالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوانُكُمْ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ الْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِ وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ — "They ask you concerning the orphans. Say, ‘To promote their welfare is better; and if you integrate your affairs with theirs, they are your brethren. Allah knows the corrupt from the reformer, and were Allah to wish, He would have surely distressed you. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise.’" (2:220). Thus, He permitted them to shelter them and keep them for the sake of promoting their welfare, and to integrate with them, for they are their brethren. This clarified the matter for them and relieved their anxiety.

If you contemplate this and then return to His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا فِي الْيَتامى فَانْكِحُوا (And if you fear that you will not act justly towards the orphans, then marry...) [4:3] etc., which occurs immediately after His saying: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى أَمْوالَهُمْ (Give the orphans their property...) [4:2], it will become clear to you that the verse occupies a position of progression (الترقي) relative to the prohibition mentioned in the previous verse. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Be conscious of Allah regarding the matter of the orphans; do not exchange your worthless property for their good property, and do not consume their property along with your own. Furthermore, if you fear that you will not act justly (تقسطوا) toward the orphan girls among them, and your souls do not feel inclined to marry them, then leave them and marry other women who seem good to you, two, three, or four.

Thus, the conditional sentence—namely His saying: إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا فِي الْيَتامى فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ (If you fear that you will not act justly towards the orphans, then marry such women as seem good to you)—carries the meaning of our saying: "If the orphans do not seem good to you due to the fear of failing to be just, then do not marry them, and marry other women instead." Thus, His saying: فَانْكِحُوا (then marry) stands in the place of the actual apodosis (الجزاء الحقيقي). His saying: ما طابَ لَكُمْ (such as seem good to you) suffices instead of mentioning the description of the women—meaning the word "others." The phrase ما طابَ لَكُمْ (what seems good to you) was used rather than "whoever (من) seems good to you" as an allusion to the number that He will detail in His saying: مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ (two, three...) etc. Placing His saying: إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا (if you fear that you will not act justly) in the place of "the lack of the soul's inclination" is an instance of placing the cause (السبب) in the place of the effect (المسبب), while alluding to the effect in the apodosis with His saying: ما طابَ لَكُمْ (such as seem good to you). This is the point.

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse, as recorded in the lengthy commentaries (التفاسير), and they are many. Among them: that a man among them would marry four, five, or more, saying, "What prevents me from marrying as so-and-so married?" Then, when his wealth was exhausted, he would turn to the wealth of the orphan under his care. Thus, Allah forbade them from exceeding four so that they would not need to take the orphan's wealth unjustly (ظلما).

Another view: they were strict regarding the matter of orphans but were not strict regarding the matter of women; they would marry a great number of them and not act justly between them. So Allah, the Exalted, said: "If you fear the matter of orphans, then fear regarding women, and marry from one to four of them."

Another view: they used to feel a sense of sin (يتحرجون) regarding the guardianship of orphans and consuming their property. So He, the Glorified, said: "If you feel a sense of sin regarding that, then likewise feel a sense of sin regarding fornication (الزنا), and marry such women as seem good to you."

Another view: the meaning is that if you fear you will not act justly toward the orphan girl raised in your care, then marry such women as seem good to you from those whom Allah has made lawful for you among the orphans of your relatives, two, three, and four.

Another view: the meaning is that if you feel a sense of sin regarding eating with orphans, then feel a sense of sin regarding combining women and failing to be just between them; do not marry of them except those with whom you feel secure from injustice (الجور). These are the views they have mentioned, but you are perceptive enough to see that none of them align with the wording of the verse with such precision; therefore, one must resort to what we have previously presented.

His saying, the Exalted: مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ (two, three, and four). The patterns maf‘al (مفعل) and fu‘āl (فعال) in numbers indicate the repetition of the root (تكرار المادة). Thus, the meaning of mathnā, thulāth, and rubā‘ is "two by two," "three by three," and "four by four." Since the address is directed to the individuals of the people, and the coordinating "and" (الواو) of detail has been placed between mathnā, thulāth, and rubā‘—which indicates choice (التخيير)—the speech signifies that every one of the believers may take for himself two wives, or three, or four. Thus, in relation to the collective, they become "two by two," "three by three," and "four by four."

By this, and by the context of His saying afterward: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ (but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one, or [retain] those whom your right hands possess) [4:3], as well as the verse concerning the muḥṣanāt (chaste women), all of this refutes the idea that the verse means marrying two in a single contract, or three in a single contract, for example. It also refutes the idea that it means marrying two together, then two together, and so on—likewise for three and four—or that it means more than one man sharing a single wife. These are possibilities that the verse does not tolerate.

Furthermore, necessity (الضرورة) dictates that Islam does not permit the combining of more than four wives, nor the sharing of a single wife by more than one man.

Likewise, this refutes the possibility that the "and" (الواو) is for summation (الجمع), which would imply the permissibility of combining nine wives, because the sum of two, three, and four is nine. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "Combining in this sense is not possible at all; for if someone says, 'The people entered the city two by two, three by three, and four by four,' it does not necessitate the summation of the numbers such that their entry was nine by nine. Moreover, for this number (nine), there is a specific word designated, which is tis‘ (تسع); to deviate from it to mathnā, thulāth, and rubā‘ would be a kind of linguistic deficiency (العي)—and His speech is far exalted and sanctified above that."

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً (but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one)—meaning marry one and no more. Allah, the Exalted, conditioned this upon the "fear" (الخوف) of that, rather than "knowledge" (العلم), because knowledge in these matters—where the soul's self-deception (تسويل النفس) has a clear influence—is usually not attained, and thus the benefit would be lost.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ (or those whom your right hands possess)—these are the slave-girls. Whoever fears that he will not act justly toward them, he must marry one; and if he wishes to increase the number, he should turn to slave-girls, as the mandatory division of nights (القسم) was not legislated for slave-girls.

From here it becomes clear that the intent is not an incitement toward slave-girls by permitting injustice or transgression against them; for Allah does not love the wrongdoers (الظالمين), nor is He ever unjust to His servants. Rather, since the mandatory division of nights was not legislated for them, the matter of justice regarding them is easier. For this very subtle point (النكتة), the mention of "those whom your right hands possess" meant being content with taking them and approaching them by virtue of ownership (ملك اليمين) without marrying them in a way that reaches or exceeds the number. The issue of marrying them will be addressed in what follows in His saying: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً أَنْ يَنْكِحَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ فَمِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ مِنْ فَتَياتِكُمُ الْمُؤْمِناتِ (As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women, then [let them marry] from the believing maids whom your right hands possess...) [4:25].

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ أَدْنى أَلَّا تَعُولُوا (That is more appropriate, so that you may not make a deviation). Al-‘awl (العول) is deviation (الميل). That is, this method, as it has been legislated, is closer to ensuring that you do not deviate from justice nor transgress against them in their rights. It has been said that al-‘awl means "burden" (الثقل), but this is far-fetched both linguistically and in meaning.

The mention of this sentence, which contains the wisdom of the legislation (حكمة التشريع), indicates that the foundation of legislation in the laws of marriage is based on justice (القسط) and the negation of deviation and encroachment upon rights.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتُوا النِّساءَ صَدُقاتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً (And give the women their dowries as a gift) [4:4]. Al-ṣaduqah (الصدقة)—with either a ḍammah or fatḥah on the dāl—and al-ṣadāq refer to the dowry (المهر). Al-niḥlah (النحلة) is a gift (العطية) given without a price-exchange (مثامنة).

In the attribution of "dowries" (صدقات) to the pronoun "their" (هن), there is an indication that the ruling of the obligation of giving is based on what is customary among people in the traditions of marriage—namely, designating some wealth or anything of value as a dowry for them. It is as if it is given in exchange for the vulva (البضع), similar to the exchange of a price for a sold good. For it is customary among people that the one seeking and calling for marriage is the man—as will be discussed in the following scholarly inquiry—which is the proposal (الخطبة), just as the buyer takes the price to the seller to take his commodity. In any case, the verse confirms this ongoing custom among people.

Perhaps the possibility of imagining that the husband is not permitted to dispose of the dowry at all, even with the wife's consent, is what necessitated the inclusion of the condition in His saying: فإِنْ طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَنْ شَيْءٍ مِنْهُ نَفْساً فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيئاً مَرِيئاً (And if they remit anything of it to you out of their own accord, then consume it as a wholesome and pleasing [asset]) [4:4]. This is in addition to how conditioning the "consumption" (الأكل) upon the "soul's inclination" (طيب النفس) emphasizes the previous sentence containing the ruling, and indicates that the ruling is a legal status (وضعي) rather than a purely moral obligation (تكليفي).

Al-hanā’ (الهناء) is ease of digestion and natural acceptance, and it is used for food. Al-marī’ (المري) is from al-rayy (الري)—satisfaction of thirst—and it is for drink as al-hanī’ is for food. However, al-hanā’ is used for both food and drink together; but when it is said "wholesome and pleasing" (هنيئا مريئا), al-hanā’ is specific to food and al-rayy to drink.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً (Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned you for [your] support) [4:5]. Al-safah (السفه) is lightness of intellect (خفة العقل). The origin of its meaning was absolute lightness in that which is not supposed to be light. From this comes "the feeble rein" (الزمام السفيه), meaning one that agitates much, and "a feeble garment" (ثوب سفيه), meaning one of poor weave. Then it became dominant in describing lightness of the soul (خفة النفس), and it varied according to different aims and purposes. Thus, one is called "feeble-minded" (سفيه) for having light judgment in worldly matters, or for being a profligate (الفاسق) who is indifferent to the matters of his religion, and so on.

What appears at first glance from the verse is that it is a prohibition against excessive spending on the feeble-minded and giving them wealth beyond their necessary needs for sustenance. However, the occurrence of the verse within the context of the speech regarding the property of orphans—whose management and growth are overseen by guardians—is a determining context (قرينة معينة) that the "feeble-minded" (السفهاء) intended are the feeble-minded among the orphans. Furthermore, the phrase أَمْوالَكُمُ (your property) refers in reality to their property, attributed to the guardians by way of a certain consideration (عناية), as is also evidenced by His saying afterward: وَارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيها وَاكْسُوهُمْ (and provide for them out of it and clothe them). Even if the verse must indicate the matter of other feeble-minded people besides orphans, then "the feeble-minded" would be a general term encompassing both the orphan and the non-orphan, though the first view is more probable.

In any case, if the "feeble-minded" refers to the feeble-minded among the orphans, then His saying أَمْوالَكُمُ (your property) refers to the property of the orphans. It was only attributed to the addressed guardians by way of the consideration that the totality of wealth and resources existing in the world belongs to the totality of its inhabitants. Some individuals of society were only granted specific portions of it, and others other portions, for the sake of the general welfare (الصلاح العام) upon which the principle of ownership and exclusivity is built. Thus, people must realize this truth and know that they are a single society and all wealth belongs to their society. Every one of them must guard it and preserve it, and not let it be lost through the squandering of feeble-minded souls or the management of anyone who does not manage well, such as the minor or the insane. This attribution is like His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً أَنْ يَنْكِحَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ فَمِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ مِنْ فَتَياتِكُمُ (As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women, then [let them marry] from the believing maids whom your right hands possess...) [4:25]. It is well known that "the maids" (الفتيات) does not refer only to the slave-girls owned by the one who wishes to marry.

Thus, in the verse, there is an indication of a general ruling directed toward society: that society possesses a single personality (شخصية واحدة) and owns all the wealth by which Allah has established its backbone and made it a means of livelihood. It is incumbent upon society to manage it, improve it, place it in a position of growth, and derive sustenance from it in a moderate and economical manner, preserving it from loss and corruption. A branch of this principle is that guardians must take charge of the affairs of the feeble-minded; they must not give them their property such that they waste it by placing it where it ought not to be. Rather, they must withhold it from them, rectify its state, and grow it through earning, trade, and profit-seeking. They should provide for those feeble-minded from its fruits and growth, not from its principal, so that it does not gradually vanish and lead to the poverty and misery of the owner of the wealth.

From here it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: وَارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيها وَاكْسُوهُمْ (and provide for them out of it and clothe them) is that the feeble-minded person should be provided for within the wealth—meaning he should live from its growth, product, and profits—not from the wealth in a way that he begins consuming its principal while it remains stagnant without circulation, causing it to be completely exhausted. This is the subtle point (النكتة) in His saying فِيها (in it) rather than saying "from it" (منها), as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari.

It is not far-fetched to derive from the verse the generality of guardianship (الولاية) over those under legal restriction (المحجور عليهم), in the sense that Allah is not pleased with the neglect of their affairs. Rather, the Islamic society must take charge of their affairs. If there is one of the closest guardians, such as the father or grandfather, then the guardianship and direct management fall upon him. Otherwise, it is upon the legitimate government or the believers to undertake the matter according to the details mentioned in jurisprudence (الفقه).

(A Discourse on the Fact that All Wealth Belongs to All People)

This is a Quranic truth that serves as a foundation for important rulings and laws in Islam—namely, what this verse signifies: that wealth belongs to Allah in a real ownership (ملكا حقيقيا), which He has made a support (قياما) and livelihood for human society, without restricting it to one person over another in an unchangeable or unalterable endowment, or a gift that strips away the power of legislative disposal. Then, He permitted their exclusivity over that which He empowered the collective to possess, according to legislated proportions such as inheritance, acquisition, and trade, etc. He conditioned their disposal upon matters such as intellect (العقل), reaching maturity (البلوغ), and the like.

The established principle (الأصل الثابت) that must be observed and by which its branches are measured is that "the whole belongs to the whole." Private interests are only observed on the condition that the general interest (المصلحة العامة) belonging to society is preserved and not conflicted with. As for cases of conflict and disparity, the welfare of society is prioritized without hesitation.

From this fundamental principle (الأصل الأصيل) in Islam, many important branches are derived, such as the rulings of spending (الإنفاق) and most of the rulings of transactions (المعاملات), and others. Allah, the Exalted, has supported this in several places in His Book, such as His saying: خَلَقَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً (It is He who created for you all that is in the earth) [2:29]. We have provided some discourse related to this station in the inquiry into the verses of spending in Surah al-Baqarah; so let it be referred to there.

His saying, the Exalted: وَارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيها وَاكْسُوهُمْ وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَعْرُوفاً (and provide for them out of it and clothe them, and speak to them honorable words) [4:5]. The discourse on the meaning of "provision" (الرزق) has already been completed regarding His saying: وَتَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ (and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning) [3:27]. His saying: وَارْزُقُوهُمْ فِيها وَاكْسُوهُمْ (and provide for them out of it and clothe them) is like His saying: وَعَلَى الْمَوْلُودِ لَهُ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَكِسْوَتُهُنَّ (and their provision and clothing shall be upon the father) [2:233]. Thus, what is meant by provision (الرزق) is the food by which a human is nourished, and clothing (الكسوة) is what he wears to protect him from heat and cold. (However, the terms "provision" and "clothing" in the parlance of the Quran are like "clothing" and "maintenance" in our language)—they are used as a metonymy (كناية) for the totality of what fulfills a human's material and vital needs. Thus, other things a human needs, such as housing and the like, are included. Similarly, "eating" (الأكل) has a specific meaning according to its origin, but it is then used as a metonymy for absolute disposals, as in His saying: فَإِنْ طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَنْ شَيْءٍ مِنْهُ نَفْساً فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيئاً مَرِيئاً (And if they remit anything of it to you out of their own accord, then consume it as a wholesome and pleasing [asset]).

As for His saying: وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَعْرُوفاً (and speak to them honorable words), it is an ethical statement by which the matter of guardianship is rectified. For these people, even if they are feeble-minded and restricted from disposing of their wealth, they are not mute animals nor pasturing cattle. Rather, they are human beings who must be treated with human treatment; they should be spoken to as a human is spoken to, not with offensive speech, and they should be socialized with as a human is socialized with.

From here it appears that it is possible for His saying: وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَعْرُوفاً (and speak to them honorable words) to be a metonymy for good treatment and praiseworthy socialization that is not blameworthy, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً (and speak kindly to people) [2:83].

His saying, the Exalted: وَابْتَلُوا الْيَتامى حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغُوا النِّكاحَ فَإِنْ آنَسْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ (Test the orphans until they reach the age of marriage. Then if you discern...) [4:6] until His saying: أَمْوالَهُمْ (their property). Al-ibtilā’ (الابتلاء) is testing (الامتحان). What is meant by "reaching marriage" is reaching its time; thus, it contains an intellectual metaphor (مجاز عقلي). Al-īnās (الإيناس) is observation (المشاهدة), and it carries a shade of the meaning of familiarity (الألفة) since its root is uns (أنس). Al-rushd (الرشد) is the opposite of error (الغي), and it is the guidance toward the objectives of life. "Pushing" (دفع) the orphan's wealth to him is a metonymy for giving it to him and handing it over, as if the guardian pushes it toward him and distances it from himself; despite its common usage, it is a subtle metonymy.

His saying: حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغُوا النِّكاحَ (until they reach the age of marriage) is connected to His saying: وَابْتَلُوا (test). In this, there is an indication of continuity—that the guardian should begin testing him from the moment he begins to show discernment (التمييز) and becomes fit for testing, until he reaches the time of marriage and attains manhood. The nature of this ruling dictates this, for discerning maturity (إيناس الرشد) is not achieved by testing the child in one or two instances; rather, it must be repeated until discernment is achieved and it proceeds naturally over a long period until he reaches adolescence (الرهاق) and then marriage.

His saying: فَإِنْ آنَسْتُمْ (then if you discern) etc., is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: وَابْتَلُوا (test). The meaning is: Test them, and if you discern maturity in them, then hand over their property to them. The speech suggests that reaching marriage is like the necessitating factor (المقتضي) for handing over the wealth to the orphan and his independence in disposing of his own wealth, while maturity (الرشد) is a condition (شرط) for the validity of the disposal. Islam has detailed the consideration of the matter of maturity in a human being: it deemed the mere legal age—which is the age of marriage—sufficient for matters of worship (العبادات) and things like legal punishments (الحدود) and blood money (الديات). However, it conditioned the validity of financial disposals, acknowledgments (الأقارير), and the like—the detailed explanation of which is in jurisprudence—upon reaching marriage along with maturity. This is among the subtleties of its conduct in the stage of legislation. For neglecting the matter of maturity and disregarding it in financial disposals and the like would disrupt the order of social life for the class of orphans; the validity of their disposals and acknowledgments would lead corrupt individuals to deceive them and strip all means of life from their hands by the simplest means, using false words, lying promises, and deceptive transactions (المعاملات الغررية). Thus, maturity is indispensable as a condition in this type of matter. As for things like worship, the lack of need for such a condition therein is obvious. Likewise for things like legal punishments and blood money; for perceiving the ugliness of these crimes and sins and understanding the obligation to refrain from them does not require maturity. Rather, a human is capable of understanding that before it, and his state in that regard does not differ before maturity and after it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَأْكُلُوها إِسْرافاً وَبِداراً أَنْ يَكْبَرُوا (and do not consume it extravagantly and hastily lest they should grow up) [4:6] etc. Al-isrāf (الإسراف) is exceeding moderation in action. Al-bidār (البدار) is hastening toward something. His saying وَبِداراً أَنْ يَكْبَرُوا (and hastily lest they should grow up) carries the meaning of "for fear that they grow up" and thus prevent you from consuming. The omission of the negation or its equivalent before an (أن) is standard according to the grammarians. He, the Exalted, said: يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَضِلُّوا (Allah clarifies for you lest you should go astray) [4:176]—meaning so that you do not go astray or for fear that you go astray.

The contrast occurring between "consuming extravagantly" and "consuming hastily lest they grow up" indicates that consuming extravagantly is transgressing against their property without need or any trace of entitlement, but rather as an encroachment without concern. Consuming hastily is for the guardian to consume from it what is customarily considered a wage for his work therein, except that if the orphan grew up, he might prevent him from such consumption. All of this is forbidden unless the guardian is poor and has no choice but to occupy himself with earning to satisfy his hunger, or he works for the orphan and satisfies his necessary needs from his wealth. This, in reality, reverts to what a worker takes for trade, construction, and the like. This is what He mentioned with His saying: مَنْ كانَ غَنِيًّا (whoever is rich)—meaning he does not need to take from the orphan's wealth for his livelihood—فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ (let him be abstemious)—meaning let him seek the path of chastity and adhere to it, so he does not take from their property. وَمَنْ كانَ فَقِيراً فَلْيَأْكُلْ (and whoever is poor, let him consume) from it بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (honorably). Some commentators mentioned that the meaning is: "let him consume honorably from his own wealth, not from theirs," but this does not suit the detail given between the rich and the poor.

As for His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوالَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا عَلَيْهِمْ (And when you hand over their property to them, let there be witnesses over them) [4:6], it is a legislation for seeking witnesses at the time of handing over to solidify the matter and remove the trouble of dispute and contention. For it is possible that the orphan, after maturity and taking the wealth, might make a claim against the guardian. Then He concluded the whole with His saying: وَكَفى بِاللهِ حَسِيباً (and Allah suffices as a reckoner), linking the ruling to its original, primary source—namely, the origin of every ruling from His names and attributes, the Exalted. For since He, the Exalted, is a Reckoner (حسيبا), He would not leave the rulings of His servants without a precise reckoning, which is His firm legislation. This also serves to complete the Islamic religious upbringing; for Islam undertakes the upbringing of people on the basis of monotheism (التوحيد). For although seeking witnesses usually removes dispute and contention, it might sometimes fail due to a deviation in the witnesses' integrity (عدالتهم) or other various factors. However, the high, strong spiritual cause is the consciousness of Allah (تقوى الله), who suffices as a Reckoner. If the guardian, the witnesses, and the orphan to whom the wealth was handed over kept this meaning before their eyes, no dispute or contention would ever occur.

Observe the two verses and how they have excelled in exposition. They first explained the main issues of guardianship over the property of orphans and those under legal restriction, and its essentials: the manner of taking, preserving, growing, disposing, and returning; the time of taking and handing over; and solidifying its foundation by explaining the aspect of general welfare in all of that—which is that wealth belongs to Allah, who made it a support for the human being, as previously explained.

Secondly, the ethical principle (الأصل الأخلاقي) that trains the human being in accordance with these laws, which He, the Exalted, mentioned in His saying: وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَعْرُوفاً (and speak to them honorable words).

Thirdly, by building the whole upon the principle of monotheism, which governs with its unity over all practical and ethical rulings, and which remains effective in all cases even if the practical rulings and ethical instructions were assumed to be weak in terms of impact. This is what He mentioned with His saying: وَكَفى بِاللهِ حَسِيباً (and Allah suffices as a reckoner).

(A Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى أَمْوالَهُمْ (Give the orphans their property...) [4:2]—Ibn Abī Ḥātim narrated from Sa‘īd ibn Jubayr, who said: "A man from Ghaṭafān had a great deal of wealth belonging to an orphaned nephew of his. When the orphan reached maturity, he requested his wealth, but the man withheld it from him. So he brought the dispute to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the verse was revealed: وَآتُوا الْيَتامى أَمْوالَهُمْ (Give the orphans their property...)," the tradition continues.

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him): "It is not lawful for a man's water to flow into more than four wombs of free women."

In al-Kāfī, from him (peace be upon him): "If a man has combined four [wives] and divorces one of them, he shall not marry a fifth until the waiting period (عدة) of the woman he divorced has expired."

I say: The narrations in this chapter are many.

In al-‘Ilal, with its chain to Muḥammad ibn Sinān: that Imam al-Riḍā (peace be upon him) wrote to him—among the answers to his questions—regarding the reason for a man marrying four women and the prohibition of a woman marrying more than one: "Because if a man marries four women, the child is attributed to him. But if a woman had two husbands or more, it would not be known to whom the child belongs, as they are partners in her marriage. In that, there is the corruption of lineages, inheritances, and identities." Muḥammad ibn Sinān said: "And among the reasons regarding free women—and the permissibility of four women for one man—is that they are more numerous than men. So when He looked—and Allah knows best—Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ (then marry such women as seem good to you, two, three, or four). That is a measure ordained by Allah, the Exalted, to accommodate both the rich and the poor, so that a man may marry according to his capacity," the tradition continues.

In al-Kāfī, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him): in a tradition, he said: "And jealousy (الغيرة) is for men; for that reason, it was forbidden for a woman [to have anyone] except her husband, while four were permitted for the man. For Allah is too generous to afflict them with jealousy while permitting the man three others along with her."

I say: This is clarified by the fact that jealousy (الغيرة) is one of the praiseworthy morals and virtuous traits (الملكات الفاضلة). It is a change in a person's usual state and an inclination toward defense and retaliation when another encroaches upon something he respects for himself—whether religion, honor, or status—and believes it to be a matter of his dignity. This instinctive trait is not absent from any human being whatsoever; it is among the primordial natures (فطريات) of the human being. Islam is a religion built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة); it takes the matters dictated by the human primordial nature and moderates them by restricting them to what is beneficial for the human in his life, while removing from them what is unnecessary and contains aspects of defect and corruption—as in the acquisition of wealth, food, drink, clothing, marriage, and so on.

So, if it is assumed that Allah, the Glorified, has permitted for the man three others along with one woman—and the religion is built upon observing the judgment of the primordial nature—it follows that what appears of the state of women and their change toward men regarding the matter of co-wives is envy (حسد) on their part, not jealousy (غيرة). It will become even clearer in the upcoming inquiry on polygamy that this state is an accidental condition (حال عرضي) that has come upon them, not an instinctive, primordial one.

In al-Kāfī, with its chain from Zurārah from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him), who said: "A man may not take back what he gifts to his wife, nor a woman what she gifts to her husband, whether it has been compensated or not. Does Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, not say: وَلا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئاً (It is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them) [2:229]? And He said: فَإِنْ طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عن شَيْءٍ مِنْهُ نَفْساً فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيئاً مَرِيئاً (And if they remit anything of it to you out of their own accord, then consume it as a wholesome and pleasing [asset])? This applies to the dowry and the gift."

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from ‘Abdullāh ibn al-Qaddāḥ from Imam al-Ṣādiq from his father (peace be upon them), who said: "A man came to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, I have a pain in my stomach.' The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said to him: 'Do you have a wife?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Ask her to gift you something from her wealth with which she is pleased. Then buy honey with it, then pour rain-water upon it, then drink it. For I have heard Allah saying in His Book: وَنَزَّلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً مُبارَكاً (And We sent down from the sky blessed water) [50:9], and He said: يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بُطُونِها شَرابٌ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوانُهُ فِيهِ شِفاءٌ لِلنَّاسِ (there issues from their bellies a juice of diverse hues, wherein is a cure for the people) [16:69], and He said: فَإِنْ طِبْنَ لَكُمْ عَنْ شَيْءٍ مِنْهُ نَفْساً فَكُلُوهُ هَنِيئاً مَرِيئاً (And if they remit anything of it to you out of their own accord, then consume it as a wholesome and pleasing [asset]). You shall be cured, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.' He said: 'So he did that and was cured.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthūr from ‘Abd ibn Ḥumayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abī Ḥātim from him (peace be upon him). This is a subtle type of derivation, and its basis is the expansion of meaning. There are similar examples in the traditions narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), some of which we will mention in their appropriate places.

In al-Kāfī, from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): "Whenever I tell you something, ask me for its basis from the Book of Allah." Then he said in part of his discourse: "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله forbade idle talk (القيل والقال), the wasting of wealth (فساد المال), and excessive questioning (كثرة السؤال)." It was said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, where is this in the Book of Allah?" He said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: لا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْواهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ (There is no good in much of their secret talks, excepting him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people) [4:114], and He said: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ الَّتِي جَعَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ قِياماً (Do not give the feeble-minded your property, which Allah has assigned you for [your] support) [4:5], and He said: لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ (do not ask about things which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you) [5:101]."

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Yūnus ibn Ya‘qūb, who said: "I asked Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ (Do not give the feeble-minded your property). He said: '[It means] those whom you do not trust.'"

In the same source, from Ibrāhīm ibn ‘Abd al-Ḥamīd, who said: "I asked Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) about this verse: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ. He said: 'Everyone who drinks wine is feeble-minded (سفيه).'"

In the same source, from ‘Alī ibn Abī Ḥamzah from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَلا تُؤْتُوا السُّفَهاءَ أَمْوالَكُمُ. He said: 'They are the orphans; do not give them their property until you discern maturity in them.' I said: 'So how can their property be our property?' He said: 'If you are the one who will inherit from them.'"

In the Tafsīr of al-Qummī, from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): regarding the verse: "The feeble-minded are women and children. If a man knows that his wife is feeble-minded and corrupt, and his child is feeble-minded and corrupt, he should not give either of them authority over his wealth, which Allah has made a support (قياما) for him—meaning a livelihood," the tradition continues.

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are many. They support what we previously stated: that "feeble-mindedness" (السفه) has a broad meaning with various levels—such as the feeble-minded person under legal restriction, the child before he reaches maturity, the frivolous and impulsive woman, the wine-drinker, and generally anyone whom you do not trust. According to the difference of these instances, the meaning of "giving wealth" differs, as does the meaning of the attribution "your property." You must apply and consider this.

His saying in the narration of Ibn Abī Ḥamzah: "If you are the one who will inherit from them" is an allusion to what we previously stated: that all wealth belongs to society according to the primary principle, then to each of the individuals secondarily and for private interests. For the society's participation in wealth primarily is what necessitates its transfer from one person to another.

In al-Faqīh, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him): "The end of the orphan's state is nocturnal emission (الاحتلام), which is its peak. If he has an emission but maturity is not discerned in him, and he is feeble-minded or weak, his guardian should withhold his wealth from him."

In the same source, from him (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَابْتَلُوا الْيَتامى (Test the orphans) etc.—he said: "Discerning maturity (إيناس الرشد) is the preservation of wealth."

I say: The manner in which the verse indicates this has already been presented.

In al-Tahdhīb, from him (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: وَمَنْ كانَ فَقِيراً فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (and whoever is poor, let him consume honorably). He said: "That is a man who restrains himself from [other] livelihood; there is no harm in him consuming honorably if he is rectifying [affairs] for them. But if the wealth is little, he should not consume anything from it."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Aḥmad, Abū Dāwūd, al-Nasā’ī, Ibn Mājah, Ibn Abī Ḥātim, and al-Naḥḥās in his Nāsikh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar: "A man asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, saying: 'I have no wealth, but I have an orphan [under my care].' He said: 'Consume from the wealth of your orphan without being extravagant, nor wasteful, nor accumulating wealth [for yourself], and without protecting your own wealth with his wealth.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are many from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) and others. There are jurisprudential inquiries and traditions looking into them; whoever desires them should refer to the collections of hadith and books of jurisprudence.

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from Rifā‘ah from him (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Exalted: فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (let him consume honorably). He (peace be upon him) said: "My father used to say: 'It is abrogated (منسوخة).'"

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Abū Dāwūd and al-Naḥḥās both narrated in the Nāsikh, and Ibn al-Mundhir from the path of ‘Aṭā’ from Ibn ‘Abbās: "وَمَنْ كانَ فَقِيراً فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (and whoever is poor, let him consume honorably). He said: 'It was abrogated by: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً (Indeed, those who consume the property of orphans unjustly...) [4:10].'"

I say: The verse being abrogated does not suit the standard of abrogation (النسخ); for there is no verse among the noble verses whose relationship to this verse is that of an abrogating verse (الناسخة) to an abrogated one (المنسوخة). As for His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً (Indeed, those who consume the property of orphans unjustly...), its content does not contradict the content of this verse. For the "consumption" in this permitting verse is restricted by "honorably" (بالمعروف), while in that prohibiting verse it is restricted by "unjustly" (ظلما). There is no contradiction between permitting honorable consumption and prohibiting unjust consumption. Thus, the truth is that the verse is not abrogated, and the two narrations do not agree with the Book, notwithstanding their weakness.

In the Tafsīr of al-‘Ayyāshī, from ‘Abdullāh ibn al-Mughīrah from Imam Ja‘far ibn Muḥammad (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: فَإِنْ آنَسْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ رُشْداً فَادْفَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوالَهُمْ (Then if you discern maturity in them, hand over their property to them). He said: "He said: 'If you see them loving the Family of Muḥammad, then raise them a degree.'"

I say: This is an instance of applying the inner meaning (باطن) of the revelation. For the Imams of the religion are the fathers of the believers, and the believers are the orphans of knowledge when they are cut off from them. So if their attribution to them through love is correct, let them be raised a degree by being taught the true knowledge which is the inheritance of their fathers.

(A Scholarly Inquiry in Three Sections)

1 - Marriage is among the Objectives of Nature: The origin of the connection between man and woman is something that human—and even animal—nature (الطبيعة) demonstrates with its most eloquent expression. Islam is the religion of the primordial nature (الفطرة); thus, it necessarily permits it.

The matter of procreation and breeding, which is the desire of nature and the purpose of creation in this social union, is the sole reason and the original factor in casting this act into the mold of marriage (الازدواج) and bringing it out from absolute mixing for mating and copulation into the form of marriage (النكاح) and companionship. For this reason, you see that the animal in whose upbringing both parents participate—such as birds in brooding their eggs and feeding and raising their chicks, and such as the animal that needs a nest for birth and upbringing, where the females need the assistance of the males in building and guarding it—chooses marriage for this purpose. This is a type of companionship and exclusivity between the two mates, male and female. They connect at that time and participate in guarding the eggs of the females, managing them, and bringing out the chicks, and so on until the end of the period of raising the offspring. Then they separate, if they separate, then the marriage is renewed, and so on. Thus, the factor of marriage and union is procreation and the raising of offspring. As for extinguishing the fire of lust, or participating in vital activities such as earning, collecting wealth, managing food, drink, furniture, and running the house—these are matters outside the level of nature's purpose and creation; they are merely preparatory matters or resulting benefits.

From here it becomes clear that freedom and unbridled license for the two mates—such that each mate connects with someone other than their spouse wherever they want and whenever they want without restraint, like mute animals whose males mount the females wherever they find them, as is almost the current tradition among civilized nations today—as well as fornication (الزنا), especially the fornication of a married woman, [are all contrary to nature].

Likewise, the solidification of the occurring marriage and the prohibition of divorce and separation between the mates, and the abandonment of the husband to take another husband as long as life brings them together [is also contrary to nature].

Likewise, the abolition of procreation and the raising of offspring, and building marriage on the basis of participation in domestic life—as is customary today among advanced nations—and its equivalent, sending newborns to public institutions prepared for nursing and upbringing—all of that is contrary to the tradition of nature. The human being has been equipped with that which contradicts these modern traditions, as previously alluded to.

Yes, the animal in whose birth and upbringing there is no need for more than the mother carrying it, nursing it, and raising it in her company—there is no natural need in it for marriage, companionship, and exclusivity. This type of animal has the freedom of mating to the extent that it does not harm the purpose of nature regarding the preservation of the species.

Beware of imagining that departing from the tradition of creation and what nature requires is harmless after compensating for the occurring deficiencies with thought and reflection, along with the pleasures of life and luxury it contains. For that is among the greatest errors. For these natural structures—among which is the human structure—are compounds composed of many parts that necessitate, by each falling into its specific place according to its specific conditions, a state that is compatible with the purpose of nature and creation and suitable for the perfection of the species. This is like pastes and medicinal compounds that require parts with specific descriptions, quantities, weights, and conditions; if one of them deviates from its specific form in the slightest way, the effect is lost.

The human being, for example, is a natural, existential being with parts composed in a specific way that entails internal attributes and spiritual properties, which in turn result in actions and deeds. So if he diverts some of his actions and deeds from their natural place to another, that entails a deviation and change in his spiritual attributes and properties. Thereby, all properties and attributes deviate from the level of nature and the path of creation, and his connection to his natural perfection and the goal he seeks according to creation is nullified.

If we investigate the general calamities that encompass humanity today, which frustrate people's deeds and efforts to attain comfort and a happy life, and threaten humanity with collapse and destruction, we find that the strongest factor in them is the nullification of the virtue of God-consciousness (التقوى) and the dominance of recklessness, cruelty, severity, and greed over the souls of human societies. Its greatest causes and reasons are freedom, unbridled license, and neglect of the laws of nature in the matter of marriage and the raising of offspring. For the tradition of domestic society and the raising of offspring today kills the talents of compassion, mercy, chastity, life, and humility in the human being from the first moment he begins to discern until the end of his life.

As for compensating for these deficiencies with thought and reflection—how far-fetched that is! For thought, like other necessities of life, is a natural means that nature has taken as a tool to return what has departed and deviated from the path of nature and existence to it, not to nullify the effort of nature and creation and kill it with the very sword it gave to the human being to ward off evil from it. If thought—which is one of nature's means—is used to support what has been corrupted of nature's affairs, this means also becomes corrupt and deviant like other means. For this reason, you see that whenever the human being today rectifies, by the power of his thought, one of the general evils that threaten his society, it produces that which is more bitter and calamitous, and increases the prevalence and scope of the affliction and misery.

Yes, one of them might say: "The spiritual attributes called psychological virtues are remnants from the era of myths and savagery; they do not suit the life of the advanced human today—such as chastity (العفة), generosity (السخاء), modesty (الحياء), compassion (الرأفة), and truthfulness (الصدق). For chastity is a restriction of the soul's nature in what it desires without cause; generosity is a nullification of the human's effort in collecting wealth and the hardships he endured in the path of its acquisition, besides being a habituation of the poor to idleness in earning and extending his hand to the humiliation of begging; modesty is a bridle that bridles the human from demanding his rights and disclosing what is in his mind; compassion weakens the heart; and truthfulness does not suit daily life." This very speech is among the examples of the deviation we mentioned.

This speaker does not realize that these virtues in human society are among the necessary obligations which, if they were removed from their root, society would not live after them in a state of social union for even an hour.

For if these traits were removed, and every individual encroached upon the exclusive rights, property, and honor of every other individual; and no one was generous enough to bestow what the society needed; and no one felt ashamed of violating what he must observe of the laws; and no one was compassionate toward the helpless who have no sin in their helplessness, such as children and those like them; and everyone lied to everyone in everything he reports and promises, and so on—human society would disintegrate instantly.

So it behooves this speaker to know that these traits do not depart and will never depart from the world, and that human nature is clinging to them, preserving its life as long as it calls the human to social union. The whole matter—the entire matter—lies in organizing these attributes and moderating them such that they agree with the purpose of nature and creation in its call to the human toward the happiness of life. If the traits prevalent in advanced society today were virtues for humanity, moderated by what is appropriate of moderation, they would not have brought society to the point of corruption and destruction, and people would have settled in a state of security, comfort, and happiness.

Let us return to what we were discussing in the inquiry and say: Islam placed the matter of marriage in its natural place as we mentioned. It permitted marriage (النكاح) and prohibited fornication (الزنا) and debauchery (السفاح). It placed the bond of marriage on the basis of the permissibility of separation—which is divorce (الطلاق). It placed this bond on the basis of exclusivity in general, as we shall explain. It placed the contract of this union on the basis of procreation and upbringing. Among the famous prophetic traditions is his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Marry and procreate, so that you may multiply..." the tradition continues.

2 - The Dominance of Males over Females: Furthermore, reflection on the mating of animals shows that the males among them have a trace of dominance over the females in this regard. For we see that the male among them seems to see himself as the owner of the vulva, having authority over the female. For this reason, you see the males among them contending and fighting over the females, but not the reverse. The female does not rise against her like when the male inclines toward her, unlike the reverse. Likewise, what occurs among them similar to a proposal (الخطبة) only begins from the side of the males, not the females.
