This is only because nature perceives by instinct that the males in this act are like the active, superior party (الفاعل المستعلي) and the females are like the receptive, submissive party (القابل الخاضع). This meaning is different from what is observed of a type of obedience from the males to the females in observing what her soul inclines toward and her nature finds pleasurable; for that reverts to observing the aspect of passion and lust and increasing pleasure. As for the aforementioned type of dominance and superiority, it returns to the power of masculinity and the execution of what nature commands.

This meaning—namely, the necessity of strength and vigor for the class of males, and softness and receptivity for the class of females—is something that belief in exists, more or less, among all nations. It has even permeated various languages; thus, everything that is strong and difficult to lead is called "male" (ذكر), and everything soft and easily receptive is called "female" (أنثى). It is said: "male iron" (حديد ذكر), "a male sword" (سيف ذكر), "a male plant" (نبت ذكر), "a male place" (مكان ذكر), and so on.

This matter is ongoing in the human species, prevalent among different societies and diverse nations in general, even if it is perhaps not free from variation in increase and decrease.

Islam has considered this in its legislation. Allah, the Exalted, said: الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ (Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others...) [4:34]. Thus, it legislated the obligation of her responding to him if he calls her to intercourse, if it is possible for her.

3 - Polygamy: The matter of unity and plurality in what we observe of the types of animals is not clear. In those among which there is a domestic union, the females are singular and exclusive to the males, because the males are preoccupied with participating with them in managing the home and brooding and raising the chicks. Perhaps the ongoing state among them changes through artifice, management, and care—meaning through domestication and breeding—as is observed in the case of the rooster and hens, pigeons, and the like.

As for the human being, the practice of taking multiple wives was an ongoing tradition in most ancient nations, such as Egypt, India, China, Persia, and even Rome and Greece. For they would sometimes add to the single wife in the house a mistress who accompanied them. Indeed, among some nations, it did not end at a number at which it stopped, such as the Jews and the Arabs. A man among them would sometimes marry ten, twenty, or more. It has been mentioned that Solomon the King married hundreds of women.

Most of what occurred of polygamy was only in tribes and those who followed their way among the inhabitants of villages and mountains. For the head of the household among them had a strong need for gathering and a large number of members. They intended by that to multiply in sons through frequent procreation to make the matter of defense—which is among the necessities of their life—easier for them, and for that to be a means by which they seek leadership and nobility in their people, in addition to what frequent marriage brings of multiplying relatives through in-laws.

What some scholars mentioned—that the factor in polygamy in tribes and village folk was only the abundance of occupations and works among them, such as the work of carrying, transport, herding, agriculture, irrigation, hunting, cooking, weaving, and so on—is true in general. However, reflection on their spiritual attributes shows that these works are in the second degree of importance to them. What we mentioned is what the nomadic human intends primarily and essentially; just as the prevalence of claiming [sons] and adoption among them previously was among the branches of this purpose.

Furthermore, there was another fundamental factor in these nations for the prevalence of polygamy among them: the increase in the number of women over men to an extent that cannot be overlooked. For these nations, living by the way of tribes, were constant in wars, raids, slaughter, and assassination. Thus, killing would exhaust the men, and the number of women would increase over the men—an increase with which the need of nature is not fulfilled except through polygamy. This is the point.

Islam legislated marriage with one, and permitted multiplication up to four on the condition of being able to act with justice (القسط) between them, while rectifying all the objections directed toward plurality, as we shall point out. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ الَّذِي عَلَيْهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (And they have rights similar to those upon them, in an honorable manner) [2:228].

Objections have been raised against the ruling on polygamy (تعدد الزوجات):

First: That it produces harmful effects within society, for it strikes at the hearts of women in their emotions and disappoints their hopes. It extinguishes the fervor of love in their hearts, causing the sense of love to invert into a sense of revenge. Consequently, they neglect the affairs of the home, become sluggish in the upbringing of children, and confront men with the same ill-treatment they received. Thus, adultery (الزنا), fornication (السفاح), and betrayal in property and honor become widespread, and it is not long before society declines in the shortest possible time.

Second: That polygamy contradicts what is observed and apparent from the workings of nature. Statistics across nations and generations indicate that the two categories of male and female are approximately equal in number. Therefore, what nature has prepared is one for one, and anything contrary to that is contrary to the purpose of nature.

Third: That the legislation of polygamy encourages men toward gluttony and lust, and strengthens this power within society.

Fourth: That it diminishes the status of women in society by equating four of them to one man. This is an unjust valuation, even considering the perspective of Islam, which equated two women to one man in matters such as inheritance (الإرث) and testimony (الشهادة) and others. The logical necessity of this would be to permit marrying two of them, not more. Thus, permitting four is a departure from justice in any case, without justification. These objections are among those raised by Christians against Islam, or by those modernists who agree with them and champion the cause of equal rights for men and women in society.

The answer to the first objection is what has been mentioned more than once in previous discussions: that Islam established the structure of human society on the basis of the rational life (الحياة التعقلية) rather than the emotional life (الحياة الإحساسية). Therefore, what is followed in its view is rational benefit (الصلاح العقلي) in social norms, rather than what the senses desire or what emotions are drawn toward. This does not imply the killing of emotions and delicate feelings, nor the nullification of divine gifts and natural instincts (الغرائز الطبيعية). For it is established in psychological research that spiritual traits, emotions, and internal feelings vary in quantity and quality according to differences in upbringing and habit. Just as many manners and customs praised by Easterners, for example, are condemned by Westerners and vice versa, every nation differs from others in some of these aspects.

Religious upbringing in Islam places the Muslim woman in a position where her emotions are not pained by such matters. Indeed, the Western woman, having been accustomed for centuries to monogamy and having been indoctrinated with this generation after generation, has developed a psychological emotion in her soul that opposes polygamy. Evidence for this is the horrific indulgence that has become widespread among men and women in the "civilized" nations today.

Do their men not satisfy their lustful desires with whomever they desire and whoever desires them among their women—whether near relatives (محارم) or others, whether virgins or non-virgins, whether married or unmarried? This has reached such an extent that one can hardly find one person in a thousand who is safe from adultery, whether among men or women. They were not content with this until they fell into homosexuality to such a degree that few individuals are safe from it, until the matter reached a point where, about a year ago, they petitioned the British Parliament to legalize sodomy (اللواط) after it had become widespread among them unofficially. As for women, especially virgins and unmarried young women, the matter is even stranger and more horrific.

I wonder, then, how is it that women there do not grieve, nor feel constrained, nor are their hearts broken, nor do their emotions suffer when they witness all these scandals from their men? And how is it that a man’s emotions and feelings are not pained when he marries a young woman only to find her a non-virgin who has lost her virginity and shared her bed not with one or two men, but then he soon boasts among his peers that the lady is one for whom many men had desire and for whom dozens and hundreds competed? Is this not because these vices have been repeated among them and the tendency toward "freedom" has taken hold of their souls until it became a deep-rooted, familiar habit that emotions and feelings do not resist and souls do not denounce? It is simply that prevailing norms incline emotions and feelings toward what accords with them and not what opposes them.

As for what they mentioned regarding the necessity of women neglecting the management of the home, being sluggish in the upbringing of children, and the spread of adultery and betrayal, experience has shown the opposite. This ruling was practiced in the early days of Islam, and no one expert in history can claim that a stagnation occurred in the affairs of society because of it; rather, the matter was the reverse.

Furthermore, these women who are married in addition to the first wife in Islamic society and other societies that allow this—meaning the second, third, and fourth wives—only marry out of their own consent and desire. They are women from these very societies; men did not enslave them from other societies, nor did they bring them for marriage from outside this world. They desired such a marriage for social reasons. Thus, the nature of the female gender does not inherently reject the issue of polygamy, nor are their hearts pained by it. Rather, if such a thing exists, it is among the concomitants or accidental circumstances of the first marriage—meaning that when a woman is the sole wife of a man, she does not like another to enter upon her and her home for fear that her husband might incline away from her, or that another might rule over her, or that children might conflict, and so on. Thus, the lack of consent and the pain, where they exist, originate from an accidental state (being the sole wife) and not a natural instinct (الغريزة الطبيعية).

The answer to the second objection is that the argument based on nature’s equalization of the number of men and women is flawed in several respects.

Among them is that the matter of marriage does not rely solely on what they mentioned; rather, there are other factors and conditions for this matter. First, intellectual maturity and readiness for marriage come faster to women than to men. Women, especially in hot regions, are fit for marriage once they pass the age of nine, whereas men are generally not ready for it before the age of sixteen (which is what Islam considered for marriage).

Evidence for this is the prevailing custom among the young women of civilized nations; it is a rare exception for a girl to remain a virgin until the legal age of maturity. This is only because nature prepared her for marriage before it prepared men for it.

The consequence of this characteristic is that if we consider those born in a sixteen-year period among a people (assuming the number of males and females is equal), the number of men fit for marriage in the sixteenth year—which is the first year of fitness—would be those born in a single year (the first assumed year). Meanwhile, the women fit for marriage would be those born over seven years (from the first to the seventh year). If we consider those born over twenty-five years—the age of full strength for men—there would be men fit for marriage from ten birth years, and women from fifteen birth years. If we take the median ratio, nature’s working results in two women for every one man.

Second, statistics, as they mentioned, show that women have a longer lifespan than men. The consequence of this is that the rate of death prepares a number of women who have no men corresponding to them. (1)

Third: The capacity for procreation and reproduction lasts longer in men than in women. Most women reach menopause (يئسن من الحمل) at the age of fifty, while this capacity remains in men for many years after that. Sometimes the ability to father children remains in men until the end of a natural lifespan, which is one hundred years. Thus, the duration of a man’s fitness for procreation is approximately eighty years, which is double that of a woman, which is approximately forty. When this point is added to the previous one, it results in nature and creation permitting men to go beyond one wife to others. There is no meaning in preparing the power of procreation while preventing reproduction in a place whose nature allows it, for that is something the system of causes and effects (سنة العلل والأسباب) rejects.

Fourth: The destructive events that wipe out members of society, such as wars and battles, afflict men and annihilate them far more than women, as previously mentioned—this was the strongest factor for the prevalence of polygamy among tribes. These widows and defenseless women have no choice but to accept polygamy, or resort to adultery, or face the disappointment and nullification of the power placed in their natures.

This reality is supported by what occurred in West Germany a few months before the writing of these pages: the association of defenseless women expressed their distress at the loss of husbands and asked the government to allow them the Islamic law of polygamy so that any man who wished could marry more than one, thereby removing the calamity of deprivation. However, the government did not grant their request, and the Church refused to accept it, preferring the spread of adultery and the corruption of lineage resulting from it.

Another point is that the argument based on the equalization of the species’ nature between men and women in number—even ignoring what was mentioned—would only be valid if it were assumed that every man in society married more than one up to four women. However, nature does not allow for all men to be prepared for that, and it is naturally only possible for some of them, not all. Islam did not legislate polygamy as a mandatory obligation (فرض ووجوب) upon men; rather, it only permitted it for those among them who are able to maintain justice (القسط). The clearest evidence that this legislation does not necessitate hardship or corruption is that the practice of this norm among Muslims, as well as among other nations that allow it, did not result in the hardship of a shortage of women or their unavailability to men. On the contrary, the prohibition of polygamy in countries where it exists has left thousands of women deprived of husbands and domestic life, sufficing themselves with adultery.

Furthermore, the aforementioned argument—even disregarding the previous points—would only be valid if this ruling were not rectified and balanced by being restricted with conditions that remove the imagined drawbacks. Islam stipulated that any man who desires polygamy must establish justice (العدل) in consorting with them in a kind manner (بالمعروف), and in the division of time and intimacy. It imposed upon them their maintenance (النفقة) and then the maintenance of their children. It is not feasible to provide for four women, for example, and the children they bear, while fulfilling the condition of justice in consorting and other matters, except for some of those who possess means and capacity (أولي الطول والسعة) among the people, not all of them.

Moreover, there are religious legal ways to which a woman can resort to oblige the husband to restrict himself to her and forgo taking multiple wives.

The answer to the third objection: It is based on a lack of reflection upon the nature of Islamic upbringing and the objectives of this Sacred Law (الشريعة). The religious upbringing of women in the Islamic society approved by the religion—characterized by veiling (الستر), chastity (العفاف), modesty (الحياء), and lack of brazenness—develops the woman such that her desire for marriage is less than that of the man (despite the common claim that her desire is greater, argued from her natural excessive fondness for adornment and beauty). This is a matter that Muslim men who have married women raised on religious upbringing hardly doubt. The desire for marriage in the average man equals that of more than one woman, even two or three.

From another perspective, it is part of the care of this religion that deprivation in the necessary requirements of nature and the desires of the soul be removed. It considered that lust should not be bottled up in the man, nor should he be deprived of it, lest that drive him to transgression into vice and indecency. A single wife might be excused for nearly a third of the time of companionship, such as during the days of menstruation (العادة) and some days of pregnancy, childbirth, and nursing. The haste in fulfilling this instinctive need is a necessary consequence of what we have repeated in previous discussions of this book: that Islam builds society on the basis of the rational life rather than the emotional life. For a human to remain in a state of emotionality that invites indulgence in whims and evil thoughts—such as the state of celibacy and the like—is among the greatest dangers in the view of Islam.

From another perspective, one of the most important objectives of the Legislator of Islam is the multiplication of the progeny of Muslims and the cultivation of the earth at the hands of a Muslim society—a righteous cultivation that removes polytheism (الشرك) and corruption.

These aspects and their like are what Islam emphasized in legislating polygamy, rather than promoting lust or encouraging people to indulge in it. If these objectors were fair, they would see that the social norms known among those who build society on the basis of material enjoyment are more deserving of being accused of promoting indecency and encouraging gluttony than Islam, which builds society on the basis of religious felicity (السعادة الدينية).

Furthermore, permitting polygamy calms the storm of greed that is a concomitant of deprivation; for every deprived person is greedy, and the prohibited and confined person has no concern but to tear the veil of prohibition and confinement. A Muslim, even if he has only one wife, is at peace and ease of mind knowing that he is not prohibited from expanding the fulfillment of his desire should his soul one day feel constrained toward it. This is a type of calming for the impulsiveness of the soul and a protection (إحصان) for it against inclining toward indecency and violating prohibited honors.

Some Western researchers have been fair when they said: "No factor has worked more strongly in spreading adultery and indecency among Christian nations than the Church's prohibition of polygamy." (1)

The answer to the fourth objection: It is rejected. We have explained in some previous discussions when speaking about the rights (2) of women in Islam: that no religious or worldly norm, ancient or modern, has respected women or observed their rights with the full care that Islam has respected them, and we shall increase the clarity of this.

As for permitting polygamy for the man, it is not based on what was mentioned regarding the nullification of social weight, the killing of their rights, or the belittling of their position in life. Rather, it is based on aspects of benefit (المصالح) that have been previously explained.

A group of Western researchers, both men and women, have admitted the goodness of this Islamic legislation and the social evils and vital dangers present in its prohibition; whoever wishes may refer to the relevant sources.

The strongest point that opponents of the norm of polygamy among Western scholars have clung to—and embellished in the eyes of observers—is what is witnessed in the homes of Muslims, those homes containing multiple wives: two co-wives (ضرتان) or more. For these homes do not contain a righteous life or a pleasant living. No sooner do two co-wives settle in a house than they begin to envy one another, to the point that they have called envy "the disease of co-wives." At that point, all the delicate emotions and feelings with which women are naturally endowed—such as love, gentleness, tenderness, compassion, pity, sincerity, keeping secrets, loyalty, affection, mercy, and devotion toward the husband and his children from another wife and his home and all that pertains to him—turn into their opposites. The home, which is a place of rest (سكن) for the human to find relief from the daily toil of life and the pain of soul and body from the hardships of work and the effort of earning, turns into a battlefield where life, honor, property, and status are violated. Nothing is safe from anything else; the clarity of life becomes clouded, the pleasure of life departs, and in its place come hitting, insulting, cursing, slandering, backbiting, surveillance, cunning, and plotting. Children conflict and quarrel, and the matter may even lead to the wife attempting to destroy the husband, or children killing one another or their father. The kinship between them turns into blood feuds that result in bloodshed, the destruction of progeny, and the corruption of the home. Add to this what spreads from that into society of misery, corruption of character, cruelty, injustice, transgression, indecency, and the loss of security and trust—especially if the permissibility of divorce (الطلاق) is added to it. Thus, the permissibility of polygamy and divorce creates in society men who are mere "tasters" and sybarites, whose only concern is following lusts, greed, and the craving to take this one and leave that one, to raise one and lower another. This results in nothing but the wasting and misery of half of society, which is the category of women, and thereby the other half is corrupted.

This is the summary of what they mentioned. It is true, but it applies to Muslims, not to Islam and its teachings. When have Muslims acted according to the reality of what the teachings of Islam bestowed upon them, such that Islam should be blamed for the evils that followed their actions? For centuries, they have lacked a righteous government that raises people with noble religious teachings. Rather, the first people to tear the veils that the religion drew and to violate its laws and nullify its limits were the class of rulers and governors over the Muslims; and people follow the religion of their kings. If we were to begin narrating some of the prevailing biographies in the houses of kings and the scandals committed by the kings and governors of Islam since the religious government turned into absolute monarchy and sultanate, it would constitute a separate volume on its own. In short, if the objection is valid, it is valid against the Muslims in their choice for their homes of a type of social gathering that does not include the felicity of their living, and a type of policy they are unable to implement such that it does not deviate from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم). The blame for that returns to the men rather than the women and children, though every soul bears what it has earned of sin. This is because the conduct of these men and their sacrificing the felicity of themselves, their families, their children, and the clarity of their social atmosphere for the sake of their evil and ignorance is the root of all these social evils and the planting ground for all this destructive misery.

As for Islam, it did not legislate polygamy as an obligation or a duty upon every man. Rather, it looked into the nature of individuals and the accidental circumstances that might befall them, and it considered the definitive benefit in that (as detailed previously). Then it surveyed the evils of multiplication and its drawbacks and enumerated them; it then permitted polygamy to preserve the interest of human society, and restricted it with that which removes all these hideous evils—which is the man’s confidence that he will be equitable among them and just. So, whoever is confident in himself regarding that and is granted success for it, he is the one for whom the religion permitted polygamy. As for those who have no concern for the felicity of themselves, their families, and their children, and who have no sense of dignity except satisfying their bellies and private parts, and for whom the concept of a woman is nothing but that she is created for the sake of the man’s lust and pleasure—Islam has no part in them, and it is not permissible for them except to marry one, if even that were permissible for them in such a state.

Furthermore, in the root of the objection, there is a confusion between two distinct aspects in Islam: the aspect of legislation (التشريع) and the aspect of governance (الولاية).

To clarify: the criterion for judging the benefit or corruption in enacted laws and prevailing norms among researchers today is the satisfactory or unsatisfactory effects and results obtained from their application in societies, and the acceptance or rejection of them by societies in their existing actuality. I do not think they are unaware that a society might contain some norms, customs, and accidents that do not suit the ruling under discussion, and that the society must be equipped with what does not contradict the mentioned ruling or norm until it is seen what its outcome becomes. What remains of the effect, whether good or evil, benefit or harm? However, they consider in enacted laws what the society wants and demands with its present will and apparent thought, however it may be. So, what accords with their will and demands is the "sound law," and what contradicts that is the "unsound law."

Therefore, when they saw the Muslims wandering in the valleys of error, corrupt in their worldly life and their hereafter, they attributed what they observed among them of lying, betrayal, obscenity, the crushing of rights, the spread of transgression, the corruption of homes, and the disorder of society to the religious laws circulating among them. This was based on their assumption that the Islamic norm, in its application among people and its influence, is like all other social norms that are imposed upon people from accumulated feelings among them. They conclude from this that Islam is the generator of these social evils and from it arises this transgression and corruption (while among them is the most transgressive of transgression and the most obscene of obscenity, "and all the game is in the belly of the wild ass"). They argue that if it were a true religion and the laws enacted in it were good, containing the benefit and felicity of the people, they would have produced beautiful, felicitous effects in them and would not have turned into a calamity upon them!

But they confused the nature of the sound, reforming ruling with the nature of the corrupt, corrupting people. Islam is a collection of fundamental knowledge (معارف أصلية), ethical traits, and practical laws with proportionate sides and interconnected parts. If some of its parts are corrupted, it necessitates the corruption of the whole and its deviation in influence—like compound medicines and pastes that require for their healthy influence the integrity of their parts and a prepared, ready site for their entry and action. If some of its parts are corrupted, or if the conditions of use are not observed in the person using it, its descriptive influence is nullified, and it might even produce the opposite of its expected effect.

Suppose that the Islamic norm was not strong enough to reform the people and erase general vices and depravities due to the weakness of its legislative foundations; then why does the democratic norm not succeed in our Eastern lands as it does in European lands? And why is it that the more we delve into the path and toil, the more we exaggerate in retreating on our heels? No doubter doubts that vices and depravities today are more hardened and deep-rooted in us—while we are "civilized" and "enlightened"—than they were half a century ago when we were "barbaric." We have no share of social justice, the life of human rights, general high knowledge, or any social felicity except names we call and words we hear.

Can anyone offer an excuse for that except by saying that these satisfactory norms did not produce their effect because you do not act upon them and do not care to implement them? Why then is this excuse valid and effective for them, but not valid or effective for Islam?

And suppose that Islam—due to the weakness of its foundation (Allah forbid)—was unable to take hold in the hearts of people and penetrate fully into the depths of society, so its government did not last and it was unable to preserve its life in the Islamic society, and it was not long before it became forsaken. Why then did the democratic norm—which was a satisfactory, global norm—depart from Russia after the first Great World War, its traces being erased and replaced by the communist norm? Why did it turn into the communist norm after the second Great World War in the kingdoms of China, Latvia, Estonia, Lithuania, Romania, Hungary, Yugoslavia, and others, and it threatens the rest of the kingdoms and has penetrated them deeply?

And why is it that the communist norm—after it had lasted for nearly forty years, spread, and ruled over nearly half of human society, and its callers and guardians continued to boast of its virtue as the only pure legislation unclouded by the arbitrariness of despotism or the exploitation of democracy, and that the lands where it took root were the promised paradise—then these very callers and guardians did not hesitate, two years ago, to rise up to condemn the government of its sole leader (Stalin), who had held its imamate and leadership for thirty years? They clarified that his government was a government of arbitrariness, despotism, and enslavement in the form of communism. Inevitably, he had a great influence on the enactment of the prevailing laws, their implementation, and all that pertained to that. Thus, none of that emerged except from a despotic, enslaving will and an individual government that gives life to thousands and death to thousands, making some peoples happy and others miserable. Allah knows who will come after these and judge them just as they judged those before them.

The norms, manners, and customs prevailing in societies (both the sound and the corrupt) that then departed from them due to various factors—the strongest of which is the betrayal of their guardians and the weakness of the will of the individuals following them—are many, and can be found by anyone who refers to history books.

I wonder, then, what is the difference between Islam, insofar as it is a social norm, and these fluctuating, changing norms, such that the excuse is accepted for them but not for Islam? Indeed, the word of truth today is caught between a formidable Western power and an Eastern ignorance of imitation; no sky shades it and no earth carries it. In any case, one must be alerted by what we have detailed that the influence of a norm upon people or its lack thereof, as well as its remaining among people or its departure, is not entirely linked to its soundness or corruption such that it could be argued from that. Rather, other causes and factors have an influence in that. There is no norm among the norms prevailing among people in all stages and eras except that it produces results one day and becomes sterile another, and stays among people for a period of time and departs from them in another due to different factors working upon it. "And such days We alternate among the people, and so that Allah may know those who have faith and that He may take martyrs from among you."

In short, Islamic laws and the rulings within them differ in terms of foundation and approach from all other social laws prevailing among people. For the social laws they have differ with the change of eras and are replaced with the change of interests. But Islamic laws do not admit of difference or change—whether obligatory (واجب), prohibited (حرام), recommended (مستحب), disliked (مكروه), or permissible (مباح). However, regarding the actions that an individual of society may do or leave, and every disposal he may make or forgo—the guardian of the affair (والي الأمر) may command the people with them or forbid them from them, and dispose in that as if the society were an individual and the guardian himself were the thinking, willing soul.

So, if Islam had a guardian, he could prevent the people from these injustices they commit in the name of polygamy and other things, without the divine ruling of its permissibility changing. Rather, it would be a general executive determination (عزيمة إجرائية) for a benefit, similar to the determination of a single individual to forgo polygamy for a benefit he sees—not due to a change in the ruling, but because it is a permissible ruling (حكم إباحي) that he has the right to resolve to forgo.

(Another Supplemental Scholarly Discussion)

(On the Multiplicity of the Prophet's Wives)

Among the things they have objected to is the multiplicity of the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They said: "Polygamy is not free in itself from gluttony and submission to the call of lust; and he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was not content with what he legislated for his nation of four, until he exceeded it to nine women."

The issue is linked to many scattered verses in the Qur'an, and the discussion from every one of its aspects must be fully exhausted when speaking about the verse related to it. For this reason, we have deferred the detailed discourse to its appropriate places. We only point to that here with a summary indication.

We say: It is necessary to draw the attention of this objector to the fact that the story of the multiplicity of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wives is not of this simplicity (that he, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, went to extremes in the love of women until he brought the number of his wives to nine). Rather, his choice of those he chose from among them followed a specific path throughout his life. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married—the first time he married—Khadijah (may Allah be pleased with her) and lived with her, restricting himself to her for over twenty years (which is two-thirds of his noble life after marriage). Thirteen of those years were after his prophethood before the migration (الهجرة) from Makkah. Then he migrated to Madinah and began spreading the call and raising the word of the religion. He married women after her—among them the virgin and the non-virgin, the young and the old and the middle-aged. He continued in that for nearly ten years, then women were prohibited to him after that except those who were already in his marriage bond. It is well known that this conduct, with these specificities, does not accept being explained merely by the love of women, being infatuated with them, or being enamored with being near them. The beginning of this biography and its end contradict that.

Furthermore, we do not doubt, according to what we observe of prevailing habit, that one who is infatuated with women, enamored with their love, with being alone with them, and with longing for them, is drawn to adornment, a lover of beauty, enchanted by coquetry and allure, and yearning for youth, the prime of age, and the freshness of form. These characteristics also do not apply to his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) biography. For he married the non-virgin after the virgin, and the old woman after the young girl. He married Umm Salamah when she was elderly, and he married Zaynab bint Jahsh when her age at that time exceeded fifty, after he had married the likes of Aisha and Umm Habibah, and so on.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) gave his wives the choice between being provided for and released gracefully—which is divorce (الطلاق)—if they desired the world and its adornment, or being ascetic in the world and leaving adornment and beautification if they desired Allah, His Messenger, and the Abode of the Hereafter, as witnessed by the saying of the Exalted in the story: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَاجِكَ إِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا فَتَعَالَيْنَ أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ وَأُسَرِّحْكُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا وَإِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِنَاتِ مِنكُنَّ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا — "O Prophet! Say to your wives, ‘If you desire the life of the world and its glitter, come, I will provide for you and release you in a kindly manner. But if you desire Allah and His Apostle and the abode of the Hereafter, then Allah has indeed prepared for the virtuous among you a great reward.’" (Al-Ahzab: 28-29). This meaning also—as you see—does not apply to the state of a man enamored with the beauty of women and longing for union with them.

Therefore, nothing remains for the deep-searching researcher, if he is fair, but to explain the multiplicity of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marriages between the beginning of his affair and its end by other factors besides the factor of gluttony, lust, and amusement.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married some of these wives to gain strength and increase support and kinship; some of them to win over hearts and guard against certain evils; some of them to provide for her by maintenance and managing her livelihood, and so that it might be a prevailing norm among the believers in protecting widows and old women from poverty and loss; and some of them to establish a legislated ruling and implement it in practice to break the debased norms and false innovations (البدع) prevailing among the people—as in his marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh. She had been the wife of Zayd ibn Harithah, then Zayd divorced her. This Zayd used to be called the son of the Messenger of Allah by way of adoption (التبني), and the wife of the one called a son was, in their view, like the wife of a biological son, whom the father does not marry. So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married her, and the verses were revealed regarding her.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married for the first time after the death of Khadijah, Sawdah bint Zam'ah. Her husband had died after returning from the second migration to Abyssinia. This Sawdah was a believing migrant; had she returned to her family—who were disbelievers at that time—they would have persecuted her as they persecuted other believing men and women with restraint, killing, and coercion into disbelief.

He married Zaynab bint Khuzaymah after her husband, Abdullah ibn Jahsh, was killed at Uhud. She was among the noble, virtuous ladies in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية), called "the Mother of the Poor" (أم المساكين) due to her extensive goodness toward the poor and needy and her compassion for them. So he preserved her dignity by marrying her.

He married Umm Salamah, whose name was Hind. She was previously the wife of Abdullah Abu Salamah, the son of the Prophet's paternal aunt and his brother through nursing (أخيه من الرضاعة), and the first to migrate to Abyssinia. She was ascetic, virtuous, possessing religion and sound judgment. When her husband died, she was elderly and had orphans, so the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married her.

He married Safiyyah bint Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the chief of Banu al-Nadir. Her husband was killed on the day of Khaybar, and her father was killed with Banu Qurayzah. She was among the captives of Khaybar, so he chose her, emancipated her, and married her. He thereby protected her from humiliation and connected his lineage with the Children of Israel.

He married Juwayriyah, whose name was Barrah bint al-Harith, the chief of Banu al-Mustaliq, after the battle of Banu al-Mustaliq. The Muslims had captured two hundred households of them, including women and children. When he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married her, the Muslims said: "These are the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah; it is not appropriate to hold them captive," and they emancipated them all. Banu al-Mustaliq embraced Islam because of that and joined the Muslims to the last of them; they were a vast group, and that had a beautiful effect on the rest of the Arabs.

He married Maymunah, whose name was Barrah bint al-Harith al-Hilaliyyah. She was the one who gifted herself to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) after the death of her second husband, Abu Ruhm ibn Abd al-Uzza. So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) took her in marriage and wedded her, and the Qur'an was revealed regarding her.

He married Umm Habibah, whose name was Ramlah bint Abi Sufyan. She was the wife of Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh and migrated with him to Abyssinia in the second migration. Ubaydullah became a Christian there, while she remained firm upon Islam. Her father, Abu Sufyan, was gathering forces against Islam at that time, so the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married her and protected her.

He married Hafsah bint Umar; her husband, Khunays ibn Hudhafah, had been killed at Badr, and she remained a widow. And he married Aisha bint Abi Bakr, and she was a virgin.

Reflection upon these specificities, along with what was mentioned at the beginning of the discourse regarding the summary of his biography in his early and later years, and the asceticism and abandonment of adornment he practiced and his calling his wives to that, leaves no room for doubt in the mind of the reflector that his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marriage to those women he married was not like that of the rest of the general public. Add to that the sum of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) beneficent acts toward women, and the revival of what the centuries of ignorance and eras of barbarism had killed of their rights in life and diminished of their weight in human society. It is even narrated that the last thing he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) spoke was his recommendation regarding them to the community of men; he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The prayer, the prayer! And those whom your right hands possess; do not burden them with what they cannot bear. Allah, Allah, regarding women, for they are captives (عوان) in your hands..." the hadith.

His (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) conduct in being just among his wives, his good consorting with them, and his observing their side was among what was unique to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) (as a portion of it will come in the discussion of his biography in future sections, if Allah wills). The ruling of exceeding four was, like the continuous fast (صوم الوصال), among his unique characteristics (مختصاته) from which the nation was barred. These traits and their manifestation to the people are what prevented his enemies from objecting to him on that basis, despite their lying in wait for any opportunity against him.

Ayahs

لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ ۚ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (٧)
Men have a share in the heritage left by parents and near relatives, and women have a share in the heritage left by parents and near relatives, whether it be little or much, a share ordained [by Allah]. (7)
وَإِذَا حَضَرَ ٱلْقِسْمَةَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينُ فَٱرْزُقُوهُم مِّنْهُ وَقُولُوا۟ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (٨)
And when the division is attended by relatives, the orphans and the needy, provide for them out of it, and speak to them honourable words. (8)
وَلْيَخْشَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَـٰفًا خَافُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ فَلْيَتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (٩)
Let those fear [the result of mistreating orphans] who, were they to leave behind weak offspring, would be concerned on their account. So let them be wary of Allah, and let them speak upright words. (9)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا ۖ وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا (١٠)
Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only ingest fire into their bellies, and soon they will enter the Blaze. (10)

(Commentary)

This marks the beginning of the legislation of the laws of inheritance (أحكام الإرث) after the preliminary foundations that were established. A general explanation of the ruling on inheritance was previously provided as a sort of foundational rule (القاعدة) to announce that there is no permanent deprivation (حرمان) in inheritance once birth or kinship (القرابة) is established—a deprivation that was fixed for certain relatives and kin, such as the exclusion of minor heirs and women. Furthermore, there was an added warning against depriving orphans of their inheritance, as this entails the other heirs consuming their wealth unjustly (ظلماً), an act which Allah has strictly forbidden. Along with this, the issue of providing sustenance (رزق) for relatives, orphans, and the needy was mentioned, should they be present at the distribution of the estate (التركة) without being among those who inherit by right.

His saying, the Exalted: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ — "To the men belongs a share of what parents and kinsfolk leave" (4:7). The share (النصيب) is the portion or lot (الحظ والسهم). Its linguistic root is from nasb (النصب), meaning to set up or establish (الإقامة), because each portion during distribution is set up distinctly so that it does not mix with others. The estate (التركة) is what remains of the deceased's wealth after him, as if he leaves it behind and departs; its original usage was metaphorical (استعاري) and then it became commonplace. The kinsfolk (الأقربون) are the closest relatives. The choice of this specific word over terms like al-aqriba’ (الأقرباء) or ulu al-qurba (أولي القربى) is not without significance; it indicates that the criterion (الملاك) in inheritance is the proximity (أقربية) of the deceased to the heir, as will be discussed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً — "Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness" (4:11). Ordainment (الفرض) means cutting a hard object and separating one part from another; hence it is used in the sense of obligation (الوجوب) because its performance and the compliance with its command are cut and determined (مقطوعا معينا) without hesitation. Thus, the "ordained share" (النصيب المفروض) is that which is cut and specified.

The verse provides a universal ruling (الحكم الكلي) and legislates a new tradition (سنة) that was unfamiliar to the minds of those addressed. The law of inheritance as legislated in Islam had no prior precedent. Customs and traditions regarding the deprivation of several classes of heirs had become like a second nature (الطبيعة الثانية) among the people, provoking their souls and stirring false emotions if anything contrary reached their ears.

Islam prepared the way for this first by consolidating love for the sake of Allah and religious altruism (الإيثار) among the believers. The Prophet established brotherhood (عقد الإخوة) between the believers and then made inheritance between the two brothers, thereby abrogating (انتسخ) the previous custom of inheritance. The believers were thus uprooted from their old pride and tribalism (العصبية). Then, when the structure of the religion grew strong and its backbone was established, inheritance among blood relatives (أولي الأرحام) was legislated at a time when there was a sufficient number of believers who would respond to this legislation in the best possible manner.

From this introduction, it becomes clear that the context is one of explicit declaration (التصريح) and the removal of every imagined ambiguity by establishing the universal rule with His saying: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ. The ruling is absolute (مطلق), not restricted by any state, description, or anything else at all. Similarly, its subject—namely "the men" (الرجال)—is general (عام) and not specified by any connected clause; thus, minors possess a share just like adults.

Then He said: وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ — "and to the women belongs a share of what parents and kinsfolk leave." This is like the preceding clause, general without a trace of specification (تخصيص), encompassing all women without exception or restriction. He used the explicit noun in His saying: مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ — "of what parents and kinsfolk leave," even though the context allowed for a pronoun (الإضمار), in order to fulfill the requirement of explicit declaration and textual specification (التنصيص). Then He said: مِمَّا قَلَّ مِنْهُ أَوْ كَثُرَ — "whether it be little or much," as a further clarification that there is no room for negligence in any part of it due to its smallness or insignificance. Then He said: نَصِيباً مَفْرُوضاً — "an ordained share," which functions as a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "the share" because of the verbal noun meaning it carries. In terms of meaning, it is an emphasis upon emphasis and an increase in specifying that the portions are cut and determined, accepting neither confusion nor ambiguity.

This verse has been used as evidence for the universality of the law of inheritance regarding the estate of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and others, and for the invalidity of ta’sib (التعصيب) — the practice of giving the remainder of the estate to male agnates — in the ordained shares.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا حَضَرَ الْقِسْمَةَ أُولُوا الْقُرْبى — "And when the kinsfolk... are present at the division" (4:8). The apparent meaning of the verse is that their presence at the division means they witness the distribution of the estate when the heirs begin to divide it, not what some have mentioned—that it refers to their presence with the deceased when he makes a will and the like; this is obvious.

Accordingly, what is meant by "the kinsfolk" (أولي القربى) are the poor among them. This is also supported by their mention alongside orphans and the needy, and the tone of His saying: فَارْزُقُوهُمْ مِنْهُ وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلاً مَعْرُوفاً — "give them [something] out of it, and speak to them honourable words," which clearly suggests seeking mercy and kindness. The address would then be to the guardians of the deceased and the heirs.

There is a difference of opinion as to whether the "provision" (الرزق) mentioned in the verse is obligatory (الوجوب) or recommended (الندب), which is a jurisprudential (فقهي) discussion outside the scope of this book. Similarly, there is disagreement over whether the verse is decisive (محكمة) or abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of inheritance shares (آية المواريث). However, the relationship between the two verses is not one of contradiction, because the verse of inheritance shares specifies the fixed portions of the heirs, while this verse indicates others—either obligatorily or recommendably in general—without specifying a fixed share. Thus, there is no cause for abrogation, especially based on the view that the provision is recommended, as the verse is not devoid of an apparent indication of that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ — "Let those fear who, if they were to leave behind weak offspring, would be concerned for them" (4:9). Fear (الخشية) is the heart's being affected by what one dreads will occur, accompanied by a trace of veneration and awe. Rightness of speech (سداد القول) refers to it being correct and straight (صوابا مستقيما).

It is not unlikely that the verse is connected in some way to His saying: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ, because that verse includes the inheritance of minor orphans in its generality. Thus, this verse is framed as a threat (تهديد) to those who follow the path of depriving minor heirs of their inheritance. In that case, His saying: وَلْيَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً — "and let them speak upright words" — would be an allusion (كناية) to adopting the method of deprivation and acting upon it, thereby encroaching upon the rights of minor orphans. Alluding to an action (الفعل) by mentioning speech (القول) is common in language due to the frequent correlation between them, as in His saying: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْناً — "and speak kindly to people" (2:83). This is supported by describing the speech as "upright" (سديد) rather than "honourable" (معروف) or "gentle" (لين), for the apparent meaning of uprightness in speech is that it is something capable of being believed in and acted upon, not merely something by which people's dignity and sanctity are preserved.

In any case, the apparent meaning of His saying: الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ is that it is a parable (تمثيل) for mercy and compassion toward weak offspring who have no guardian to take charge of their affairs and repel humiliation and lowliness from them. The intimidation and threat derived from the verse are not restricted to those who actually have weak offspring at the moment, because of the word "if" (لو) in His saying: لَوْ تَرَكُوا — "if they were to leave." He did not say: "if they left their weak offspring." Rather, it is a parable intended to explain the situation. The meaning is: those whose attribute is such—meaning that in their hearts there is human mercy, tenderness, and compassion for weak offspring whose fathers have died, namely orphans. Those whose attribute is such are the people, especially the Muslims who are disciplined by the discipline of Allah and characterized by His character. Thus, the meaning returns to something like our saying: "Let people fear and let them be wary of Allah regarding the matter of orphans, for they are like their own orphans in that they are weak offspring for whom one must fear and whose affairs must be cared for; they should not be oppressed nor should their rights be encroached upon." The speech follows the pattern of our saying: "Whoever fears humiliation and degradation should occupy himself with earning," while everyone fears that.

The people in the verse were not commanded to show mercy or compassion, but rather to fear (الخشية) and be wary of Allah (اتقاء الله). This is nothing but a threat that what they have brought upon the orphans of others—by nullifying their rights and consuming their wealth unjustly—will befall their own orphans after them, and the calamities they inflicted upon them will rebound upon their own offspring after them.

As for His saying: فَلْيَتَّقُوا اللهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً — "so let them be wary of Allah and let them speak upright words," it has already been mentioned that the apparent meaning of "speech" (القول) is practical conduct (الجري العملي), and it is possible that it refers to an opinion (الرأي).

(A Discourse on the Rebound of Action upon its Doer)

Whoever wrongs an orphan regarding his wealth, his wrongdoing will surely return to the orphans among his descendants. This is one of the wondrous Quranic realities (الحقائق القرآنية), and it is a branch of what appears from His speech, the Exalted, that there is a connection between good and evil deeds and external events. Some discussion on this has already passed in the inquiry into the rulings of actions (أحكام الأعمال) in the second volume of this book.

People generally accept that a human being only reaps the fruit of his action, and that the virtuous, good person among men finds happiness in his life, while the wrongdoer and evil person does not wait long before tasting the evil consequence (وبال) of his deed. In the Holy Quran, there are verses that indicate this by their absolute nature, such as His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها — "Whoever acts righteously, it is for [the benefit of] his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its detriment" (41:46); and His saying: فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ — "So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (99:7-8). Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly shown us favour. Indeed if one is God-conscious and patient, Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste’" (12:90); and His saying: لَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ — "for him there is disgrace in this world" (22:9); and His saying: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ — "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned" (42:30)—among other verses indicating that the good and evil of an action have a kind of reflection and rebound (ارتداد) upon the doer in this world.

What first comes to our minds—habituated as they are to empirical thoughts (الأفكار التجربية) circulating in society—from these verses is that this reflection is only from the person's action to himself. However, there are verses indicating that the matter is broader than that, and that a person's action, whether good or evil, may return to him in his offspring and descendants. The Exalted said: وَأَمَّا الْجِدارُ فَكانَ لِغُلامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَكانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنْزٌ لَهُما وَكانَ أَبُوهُما صالِحاً فَأَرادَ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يَبْلُغا أَشُدَّهُما وَيَسْتَخْرِجا كَنزَهُما رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "As for the wall, it belonged to two boy orphans in the city, and there was a treasure under it belonging to them. Their father had been a righteous man. So your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure, as a mercy from your Lord" (18:82). The apparent meaning of the verse is that the righteousness (صلاح) of their father had an influence on what Allah willed as a mercy for them. And the Exalted said: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ.

Accordingly, the matter of the rebound of action is broader and more general; a blessing (النعمة) or a calamity (المصيبة) may befall a person because of what his own hands earned or what the hands of his fathers earned.

Contemplation (التدبر) on His speech, the Exalted, guides one to the true reason for this. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on supplication (الدعاء) in the second volume of this book, regarding His saying: وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي — "When My servants ask you about Me" (2:186), that His speech indicates that everything that befalls a person from His side, the Exalted, is only due to a request (مسألة) he asked of his Lord. The preliminaries he establishes and the causes he enters into are a request from him for the events and effects that result from them. The Exalted said: يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work" (55:29). And the Exalted said: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها — "And He gave you all that you had asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34). He did not say "if you enumerate it [what you asked]," because among what they asked for is that which is not a blessing, and the context is one of reminding them of blessings and blaming them for ingratitude; therefore, He mentioned some of what they asked for, which is the blessing.

Furthermore, what a person does for himself and inflicts upon others of good or evil, he accepts it for the one he inflicts it upon, who is a human being like himself. This is nothing but his accepting it for himself and asking for it for his own person, for there is nothing there but humanity (الإنسانية). From here, it becomes clear to a person that if he does good to someone, he has only asked Allah for that good for himself—an answered prayer and a request not rejected. And if he does evil to someone or wrongs him, he has only sought that for himself and accepted it for his own soul. What he accepts for the children and orphans of others, he accepts for his own children and asks for them, whether good or evil. The Exalted said: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works" (2:148). Its meaning is: take the lead in good works so that your direction may be good.

The sharing in blood and the unity of the womb (وحدة الرحم) makes the pillar of lineage—which is the progeny (العترة)—a single thing. Thus, any state that occurs to one side of this unity, and any calamity that descends upon one of its parts, has only occurred and descended upon its core, and it is reckoned against all parts. A portion of the discussion on the womb (الرحم) has already passed at the beginning of this Surah.

It has become clear through this explanation that how a person treats others or the offspring of others must inevitably rebound upon himself or turn back upon his own offspring, unless Allah wills otherwise. We made this exception because in existence there are factors and aspects that are limitless, which human enumeration cannot encompass. It is possible that factors and causes operate there of which we are unaware or have not discovered, necessitating the contrary, as hinted at by His saying: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (30:30).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً إِنَّما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ ناراً — "Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only consume fire into their bellies" (4:10). It is said: "he ate it" and "he ate it into his belly," and they both carry the same meaning, except that the second expression is more explicit. The verse, like the one before it, is connected to the content of His saying: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ, and it is an intimidation and a deterrent for people against encroaching upon the rights of orphans in inheritance.

The verse is among those that indicate the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) — the ontological transformation of human actions into their corresponding heavenly or hellish forms in the hereafter — as mentioned in the first volume of this book regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً ما — "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable" (2:26). Perhaps this is what was intended by those exegetes who said that His saying: إِنَّما يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ ناراً is a statement of reality (الحقيقة) rather than metaphor (المجاز). Based on this, the objection raised by some exegetes is not valid: that the word "they consume" (يَأْكُلُونَ) was intended for the present state rather than the future, due to the evidence of the conjunction of His saying: وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيراً — "and they shall enter the Blaze" — which is a verb preceded by the particle of futurity (حرف الاستقبال). They argued that if the literal meaning of eating were intended—and its time is the Day of Resurrection—it would have been necessary to say: "they will consume fire into their bellies and enter the Blaze." Thus, they concluded that the truth is that the metaphorical meaning is intended, and that in consuming the orphan's wealth, they are like one who consumes fire into his belly. This conclusion is a result of overlooking the meaning of the embodiment of deeds.

As for His saying: وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيراً, it is an allusion to the punishment of the Hereafter. Al-Sa‘ir (السعير) is one of the names of the Fire of the Hereafter. It is said: "he entered the fire" (صلى النار), meaning he was burned by it and suffered its torment.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ: People have differed regarding this verse according to two views: one of them is that it is decisive (محكمة) and not abrogated, and this is what is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

I say: It is narrated from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim that it is abrogated by His saying, the Exalted: يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ — "Allah enjoins you concerning your children" (4:11). There is no basis for this. It has become clear in the preceding commentary that the verse is a universal explanation of the law of inheritance, and there is no contradiction between it and the other decisive verses of inheritance such that it could be said to be abrogated by them.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Ikrimah regarding the verse: He said: "It was revealed concerning Umm Kulthum and the daughter of Umm Kuhlah—or Umm Kuhlah—and Tha‘labah ibn Aws and Suwayd, who were from the Ansar. One of them was her husband and the other was the paternal uncle of her child. She said: 'O Messenger of Allah, my husband died and left me and his daughter, but we were not given any inheritance from his wealth.' The uncle of her child said: 'O Messenger of Allah, she does not ride a horse, nor does she repel an enemy, and she is earned upon but does not earn.' Then was revealed: لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ."

I say: In some narrations from Ibn Abbas, it is said that it was revealed concerning a man from the Ansar who died and left two daughters. His two cousins came, who were his ‘asabah (male agnates). His wife said: "Marry them"—and they were unattractive—so they refused. She brought the matter to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the verses of inheritance were revealed. There is no harm in the multiplicity of these occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), as has been mentioned repeatedly.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا حَضَرَ الْقِسْمَةَ أُولُوا الْقُرْبى: People have differed regarding this verse according to two views: one of them is that it is decisive and not abrogated. He said: "And it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام)." In Nahj al-Bayan by al-Shaybani, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (عليهما السلام).

I say: In some narrations, it is said that it is abrogated by the verse of inheritance shares, but it has already been stated in the preceding commentary that it is not susceptible to abrogation.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu ‘Abdillah and Abu al-Hasan (عليهما السلام): "Indeed, Allah has promised two punishments regarding the wealth of the orphan: as for one of them, it is the punishment of the Hereafter, the Fire; as for the other, it is the punishment of this world—His saying: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ فَلْيَتَّقُوا اللهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً." He said: "He means by that: let him fear that I should replace him in his offspring just as he did to these orphans."

I say: The like of it is narrated in al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and in al-Ma‘ani from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

In the same source, from ‘Abd al-A‘la, the client of the family of Sam, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said spontaneously: "Whoever wrongs, Allah will set over him one who wrongs him, or over his offspring, or over the offspring of his offspring." He said: "I thought to myself and said: 'He wrongs, yet it is set over his offspring and the offspring of his offspring?' He said to me before I spoke: 'Indeed, Allah says: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ فَلْيَتَّقُوا اللهَ وَلْيَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Qatadah, who said: "It was mentioned to us that the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Fear Allah regarding the two weak ones: the orphan and the woman. He made him an orphan, then He enjoined [care] for him; He tested him and tested [others] through him.'"

I say: The reports concerning the consumption of the orphan's wealth and that it is a destructive major sin (كبيرة موبقة) are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة) through the chains of both sects.

Ayahs

يُوصِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ ۖ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۚ فَإِن كُنَّ نِسَآءً فَوْقَ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثَا مَا تَرَكَ ۖ وَإِن كَانَتْ وَٰحِدَةً فَلَهَا ٱلنِّصْفُ ۚ وَلِأَبَوَيْهِ لِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ مِمَّا تَرَكَ إِن كَانَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلَدٌ وَوَرِثَهُۥٓ أَبَوَاهُ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلثُّلُثُ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُۥٓ إِخْوَةٌ فَلِأُمِّهِ ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِى بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ لَا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعًا ۚ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١)
Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females, and if there be [two or] more than two females, then for them shall be two-thirds of what he leaves; but if she be alone, then for her shall be a half; and for each of his parents a sixth of what he leaves, if he has children; but if he has no children, and his parents are his [sole] heirs, then it shall be a third for his mother; but if he has brothers, then a sixth for his mother, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred]. Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is likelier to be beneficial for you. This is an ordinance from Allah. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (11)
۞ وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ مَا تَرَكَ أَزْوَٰجُكُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّهُنَّ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَهُنَّ وَلَدٌ فَلَكُمُ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْنَ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصِينَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۚ وَلَهُنَّ ٱلرُّبُعُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُمْ إِن لَّمْ يَكُن لَّكُمْ وَلَدٌ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ وَلَدٌ فَلَهُنَّ ٱلثُّمُنُ مِمَّا تَرَكْتُم ۚ مِّنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ تُوصُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ ۗ وَإِن كَانَ رَجُلٌ يُورَثُ كَلَـٰلَةً أَوِ ٱمْرَأَةٌ وَلَهُۥٓ أَخٌ أَوْ أُخْتٌ فَلِكُلِّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱلسُّدُسُ ۚ فَإِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَكْثَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فِى ٱلثُّلُثِ ۚ مِنۢ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ يُوصَىٰ بِهَآ أَوْ دَيْنٍ غَيْرَ مُضَآرٍّ ۚ وَصِيَّةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٢)
For you shall be a half of what your wives leave, if they have no children; but if they have children, then for you shall be a fourth of what they leave, after [paying off] any bequest they may have made or any debt [they may have incurred]. And for them [it shall be] a fourth of what you leave, if you have no children; but if you have children, then for them shall be an eighth of what you leave, after [paying off] any bequest you may have made or any debt [you may have incurred]. If a man or woman is inherited by siblings and has a brother or a sister, then each of them shall receive a sixth; but if they are more than that, then they shall share in one third, after [paying off] any bequest he may have made or any debt [he may have incurred] without prejudice. [This is] an enjoinment from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing. (12)
تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٣)
These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He shall admit him to gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success. (13)
وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٤)
But whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle and transgresses the bounds set by Allah, He shall make him enter a Fire, to remain in it [forever], and there will be a humiliating punishment for him. (14)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ — Allah enjoins you concerning your children: for the male shall be the like of the share of two females (4:11). Enjoining (الإيصاء) and recommending (التوصية) refer to a covenant (العهد) and a command (الأمر). Al-Raghib stated in Mufradat al-Quran: "The bequest (الوصية) is to direct another toward that which they should act upon, coupled with an exhortation (وعظ)." End quote.

The shift from the term "sons" (الأبناء) to "children" (الأولاد) indicates that the ruling of one share and two shares is specific to the direct offspring of the deceased without an intermediary. As for the children of children and those below them, their ruling is the ruling of the person through whom they are connected. Thus, the daughter of a son receives two shares and the son of a daughter receives one share if there is no one in a prior rank to precede them, just as the ruling for the children of brothers and sisters is the ruling of the person through whom they are connected. As for the term "son" (الابن), it does not necessarily negate an intermediary, just as "father" (الأب) is more general than "progenitor" (الوالد).

Regarding His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: آباؤُكُمْ وَأَبْناؤُكُمْ لا تَدْرُونَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ لَكُمْ نَفْعاً — Your parents and your children—you do not know which of them is closer to you in usefulness (4:11), it will be explained later that there is a specific rhetorical purpose (عناية خاصة) necessitating the choice of the term "sons" (الأبناء) over "children" (الأولاد).

As for His saying: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ — for the male shall be the like of the share of two females, the selection of this expression contains an allusion to the invalidation of the practice of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) regarding the prohibition of women from inheriting. It is as if He made the inheritance of the female an established and known matter and then informed that the male receives twice her share; or He made her share the primary basis in legislation (التشريع) and made the male's inheritance dependent upon it, known by relation to it. Had it not been for this, He would have said: "for the female is half the share of the male," but then it would not convey this meaning, nor would the context harmonize with it—as you can see. This is what some scholars have mentioned, and it is sound. This is perhaps supported by the fact that the verse does not explicitly and independently address any shares except those of women; even when it mentions the shares of men, it is in conjunction with the mention of their shares, as in the following verse and the verse at the end of the Surah.

In short, His saying: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females) serves as an explanation (تفسير) for His saying: يُوصِيكُمُ اللهُ فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ (Allah enjoins you concerning your children). The definite article (اللام) in "the male" (الذكر) and "the two females" (الأنثيين) is for defining the genus (تعريف الجنس); meaning that the genus of the male is equivalent in share to two females. This only occurs when there are both male and female heirs together, so the male receives twice the share of the female. He did not say: "for the male is like the two shares of the female" or "twice the share of the female," so that the speech might indicate the share of two females when they are alone, preferring conciseness (الإيجاز), as will be explained.

In any case, if the heirs consist of males and females, each male receives two shares and each female receives one share, regardless of their number.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ كُنَّ نِساءً فَوْقَ اثْنَتَيْنِ فَلَهُنَّ ثُلُثا ما تَرَكَ — But if they be women, more than two, then for them is two-thirds of what he leaves (4:11). The appearance of this statement after His saying: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females) indicates that it is based on a suppressed (محذوف) conjunctive clause. It is as if it were said: "This is if they are women and men; but if they be women..." etc. This is common in usage, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَتِمُّوا الْحَجَّ وَالْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا اسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ الْهَدْيِ — Complete the hajj and the ‘umrah for Allah’s sake, and if you are prevented, then [offer] such sacrificial animal as may be feasible (2:196), and His saying: أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ فَمَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ — [Fasting is for] a certain number of days. But if any of you is ill or on a journey, then [let it be] a [similar] number of other days (2:184).

The pronoun in "if they be" (كن) refers to the "children" (الأولاد) in His saying: فِي أَوْلادِكُمْ (concerning your children). The feminization of the pronoun is due to the feminization of the predicate (الخبر). The pronoun in His saying: تَرَكَ (he leaves) refers to the deceased (الميت), known from the context of the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كانَتْ واحِدَةً فَلَهَا النِّصْفُ — and if she be one, then for her is the half (4:11). The pronoun refers to the "child" (الولد) understood from the context, and its feminization is in consideration of the predicate. What is meant by "the half" (النصف) is half of what he leaves; thus, the definite article (اللام) replaces the possessive construction (المضاف إليه).

He did not mention the share of two females because it is understood from His saying: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females). For if a male and a female are together, the share of the female is one-third according to the verse, and the share of the male is two-thirds, which is the share of two females. Thus, the share of two females is two-thirds. This amount is understood from the speech in general (إجمالا), though it is not inherently definitive for understanding, as it would not contradict a case where it might have been said afterward: "and if they be two, then for them is the half or the whole," for example. However, it is determined by the silence regarding this share and the explicit statement in His saying: "But if they be women, more than two," for it suggests an intentionality in omitting the mention of the share of two females.

Furthermore, the fact that their share is two-thirds is what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله acted upon, and the practice has continued from his time صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله until our time among the scholars of the Ummah, except for what has been narrated of the disagreement of Ibn Abbas.

This is the best of the views in justifying the omission of an explicit mention of the share of two females. Al-Kulayni (may Allah have mercy on him) said in al-Kafi: "Allah made the share of two females two-thirds by His saying: لِلذَّكَرِ مِثْلُ حَظِّ الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ (for the male shall be the like of the share of two females).
