وَٱلَّـٰتِى يَأْتِينَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ فَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنكُمْ ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّىٰهُنَّ ٱلْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا (١٥)
Should any of your women commit an indecent act, produce against them four witnesses from yourselves, and if they testify, detain them in [their] houses until death finishes them, or Allah decrees a course for them. (15)
وَٱلَّذَانِ يَأْتِيَـٰنِهَا مِنكُمْ فَـَٔاذُوهُمَا ۖ فَإِن تَابَا وَأَصْلَحَا فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ تَوَّابًا رَّحِيمًا (١٦)
Should two among you commit it, chastise them both; but if they repent and reform, let them alone. Indeed Allah is all-clement, all-merciful. (16)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفاحِشَةَ (As for those of your women who commit indecency) until His saying: مِنْكُمْ (from among you). It is said: "he came to it" (أتاه) and "he brought it" (أتى به), meaning he performed it. Indecency (الفاحشة) is derived from "fuhsh" (الفحش), which refers to repulsiveness (الشناعة); thus, it is a repulsive way of acting. Its usage has become common for adultery or fornication (الزنا). In the Quran, it has been applied to sodomy (اللواط), or to both sodomy and lesbianism (السحق) together, in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفاحِشَةَ ما سَبَقَكُمْ بِها مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ "You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you!" (Al-Ankabut: 28).

It appears that what is intended here is adultery or fornication (الزنا), as mentioned by the majority of commentators. They narrated that when the verse of flogging (الجلد) was revealed, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله mentioned that flogging is the "way" (السبيل) that Allah has ordained for them if they commit adultery. This is supported by the apparent meaning of the verse, which indicates that this ruling would eventually be abrogated (ينسخ), as the Exalted says: أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلاً "or Allah ordains a way for them." Furthermore, it has not been transmitted that the legal punishment (الحد) for lesbianism (السحق) was abrogated by anything else, nor that this specific punishment was ever carried out against anyone committing it. His saying: أَرْبَعَةً مِنْكُمْ "four [witnesses] from among you" indicates that the number refers to men.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِي الْبُيُوتِ "then detain them in [their] houses" until the end of the verse. He made detention (الإمساك) — perpetual confinement — contingent upon the testimony (الشهادة), not upon the mere occurrence of the indecency, even if it were known, as long as witnesses have not testified to it. This is among the favors of Allah, the Glorified, upon the community (الأمة) in terms of indulgence (السماحة) and overlooking (الإغماض).

The ruling is permanent confinement, based on the context of the limit mentioned in the speech, namely His saying: حَتَّى يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ "until death takes them." However, He did not express it using terms for imprisonment (الحبس) or jail (السجن), but rather as "detaining them in the houses" (الإمساك لهن في البيوت). This is also a clear instance of facilitation (التسهيل) and indulgent overlooking. His saying: حَتَّى يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلاً "until death takes them or Allah ordains a way for them" means a path to release and salvation from this permanent detention.

The use of the alternative "or" (الترديد) suggests that it was expected that this ruling would be abrogated. And so it was, for the ruling of flogging (الجلد) abrogated it. It is a matter of necessity that the ruling applied to adulteresses in the latter period of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله life, and practiced by Muslims after him, was flogging rather than detention in houses. Thus, the verse—assuming it indicates the ruling for adulteresses—is abrogated by the verse of flogging, and the "way" (السبيل) mentioned therein is undoubtedly flogging.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّذانِ يَأْتِيانِها مِنْكُمْ فَآذُوهُما "And the two of you who commit it, punish them." The two verses are consistent in their content, and the pronoun in His saying: يَأْتِيانِها "commit it" definitely refers back to "indecency" (الفاحشة). This supports the view that both verses were revealed to explain the ruling on adultery (الزنا). Accordingly, the second verse completes the ruling of the first; the first only addressed the ruling for women, while the second explains the ruling for both [the man and the woman], which is punishment (الإيذاء). Thus, the combined meaning of the two verses is the ruling for both the male and female adulterer: their punishment, and the detention of the women in houses.

However, this does not align well with His saying afterward: فَإِنْ تابا وَأَصْلَحا فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُما "then if they repent and reform, let them alone." For "letting them alone" (الإعراض) does not fit with perpetual confinement. Therefore, it must be said that what is meant by "letting them alone" is ceasing the punishment (الإيذاء), while the confinement remains as it was.

For this reason, it has sometimes been said—following what appears in some narrations (which we will cite)—that the first verse explains the ruling of adultery for the married (الثيب), while the second is directed at the ruling for the unmarried (الأبكار), and that the "punishment" (الإيذاء) intended for the unmarried is confinement, followed by their release upon repentance and reform. However, two issues remain: first, the reason for specifying the first verse for the married and the second for the unmarried without any linguistic evidence; and second, the reason for mentioning only the female adulterer in the first verse while mentioning both together in the second: وَاللَّذانِ يَأْتِيانِها مِنْكُمْ "And the two of you who commit it."

It has been attributed to the commentator Abu Muslim that the first verse explains the ruling for lesbianism (السحق) between women, and the second verse explains the ruling for sodomy (اللواط) between men, and that neither verse is abrogated.

The falsity of this is evident. Regarding the first verse, it is due to what we mentioned in the discussion of His saying: وَاللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفاحِشَةَ مِنْ نِسائِكُمْ "As for those of your women who commit indecency." As for the second verse, it is because it is established in the Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition — that the legal punishment (الحد) for sodomy is death. It is authentically reported from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: "Whoever among you performs the act of the people of Lot, kill the actor and the one to whom it is done." This is either an original ruling that was never abrogated, or a ruling that abrogates the ruling of the verse. In either case, his [Abu Muslim's] view is invalidated.

It is possible to say regarding the meaning of the two verses—considering the apparent meaning (الظاهر) that first comes to mind, the surrounding context (القرائن), and the aforementioned problems in the meanings others have suggested—and Allah knows best: that the verse contains the ruling for the adultery of "fortified" or married women (المحصنات). This is indicated by the verse's specific mention of women to the exclusion of men. The use of "women" (النساء) to refer to wives is common in the language, especially when attributed to men, as in His saying: "your women" (نسائكم). The Exalted said: وَآتُوا النِّساءَ صَدُقاتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً "Give women their dowries as an unconditional gift" (An-Nisa: 4), and He said: مِنْ نِسائِكُمُ اللَّاتِي دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَ "from your women with whom you have consummated marriage" (An-Nisa: 23).

Based on this, the initial, temporary ruling for them was detention in houses, and then stoning (الرجم) was legislated for them. This is not an abrogation (نسخ) of the Book by the Sunnah, as al-Jubba'i argued. For abrogation is only the removal of a ruling that appeared, according to the evidence, to be eternal. This ruling, however, is coupled with that which suggests it is temporary and will cease upon its expiration, which is His saying: أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلاً "or Allah ordains a way for them," due to its apparent meaning that there is a ruling they will eventually come to know. Even if this were called abrogation, there would be no harm in it, for it does not involve the corruption that follows from the abrogation of the Book by the Sunnah; the Quran itself indicates that the ruling will be lifted when its term ends, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is the clarifier of the intended meanings of the Noble Quran.

The second verse contains the ruling for adultery without "fortification" or marriage (الإحصان), which is punishment (الإيذاء), whether that meant confinement, striking with sandals, verbal reproach, or otherwise. According to this, the verse is abrogated by the verse of flogging in Surah al-Nur. As for the narration stating that the verse contains the ruling for the unmarried (الأبكار), it is from the solitary reports (الآحاد) and is furthermore disconnected (مرسلة), weakened by its lack of a complete chain (الإرسال). Allah knows best, though this view is not without its own weaknesses.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تابا وَأَصْلَحا فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُما "then if they repent and reform, let them alone." The conditioning of repentance (التوبة) upon reform (الإصلاح) is to realize the true nature of repentance and to clarify that it is not merely a word or a fleeting state.

(Discussion on Narrations)

In al-Safi, from the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفاحِشَةَ (As for those of your women who commit indecency), he said: "The verse is abrogated, and the 'way' (السبيل) refers to the legal punishments (الحدود)."

In the same source, from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: He was asked about this verse and said: "It is abrogated." It was asked: "How was it?" He said: "When a woman committed adultery and four witnesses stood against her, she was placed in a house; she was not spoken to, nor sat with, and she was brought her food and drink until she died or Allah ordained a way for them." He said: "He made the 'way' flogging and stoning."

It was asked: "And His saying: وَاللَّذانِ يَأْتِيانِها مِنْكُمْ (And the two of you who commit it)?" He said: "It means the unmarried (البكر)—if she commits the indecency that this married woman (الثيب) committed. فآذوهما (punish them)?" He said: "She is confined." (The hadith).

I say: The account—namely that the ruling applied to them at the beginning of Islam was detention in houses until death—is narrated through several chains of the Ahl al-Sunnah from Ibn Abbas, Qatadah, Mujahid, and others. It is narrated from al-Suddi that confinement in houses was the ruling for married women (الثيبات), and the punishment mentioned in the second verse was the ruling for young women and youths who had not married. You have already seen what ought to be said regarding this matter.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا ٱلتَّوْبَةُ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِن قَرِيبٍ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٧)
[Acceptance of] repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah will accept, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (17)
وَلَيْسَتِ ٱلتَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ قَالَ إِنِّى تُبْتُ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ وَلَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٨)
But [acceptance of] repentance is not for those who go on committing misdeeds: when death approaches any of them, he says, ‘I repent now.’ Nor is it for those who die while they are faithless. For such We have prepared a painful punishment. (18)

(Commentary)

The content of these two verses is not without connection to the two verses preceding them, for those concluded with the mention of repentance (التوبة). It is possible that these two were revealed along with those two; nevertheless, these two verses contain an independent meaning in themselves, representing one of the sublime Islamic truths and lofty Quranic teachings—namely, the reality of repentance (التوبة), its status, and its ruling.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ — Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly (4:17). Repentance (التوبة) is "returning" (الرجوع). It is a return from the servant to Allah, the Glorified, through remorse (الندامة) and turning away from the rejection of servitude (العبودية); and it is a return from Allah to the servant through mercy (الرحمة), by granting him the success (التوفيق) to return to his Lord or by forgiving his sin. It has been mentioned repeatedly that a single instance of repentance from the servant is flanked by two instances of repentance from Allah, the Glorified, according to what the Holy Quran indicates.

This is because repentance from the servant is a righteous deed (حسنة) that requires strength, and all righteous deeds are from Allah, and all strength belongs to Allah. Thus, from Allah comes the facilitation of causes (توفيق الأسباب) so that the servant is able to repent and succeeds in turning away from plunging into the depths of distance and returning to his Lord. Then, if he is granted the success to repent and return, he requires—in order to be purified from these stains (الألواث), to have these filthinesses (القذارات) removed, and to enter and settle within the courtyard of proximity (ساحة القرب)—another return from his Lord toward him through mercy, affection, pardon, and forgiveness.

These two returns from Allah, the Glorified, are the two repentances flanking the servant's repentance and return. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا — then He turned to them [clemently] so that they might repent (9:118); this is the first repentance. And the Exalted said: فَأُولئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ — it is they to whom I shall turn [clemently] (2:160); this is the second repentance. Between these two repentances from Him, the Exalted, lies the repentance of the servant, as you have heard.

As for His saying: عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ (upon Allah for those), the particles ‘ala (على) and al-lam (اللام) signify benefit and harm, as in our saying: "The turn of fortune was for (li) Zayd against (‘ala) ‘Amr," or "The race was won by (li) so-and-so over (‘ala) so-and-so." The reason these particles signify harm and benefit is that ‘ala conveys the meaning of superiority (الاستعلاء), while al-lam conveys the meaning of ownership (الملك) and entitlement (الاستحقاق). It follows that meanings involving two parties—where one benefits and the other is harmed, such as war, combat, dispute, and the like—result in one being the victor and the other the vanquished. The meaning of ownership applies to the victor, and the meaning of superiority applies to the vanquished. The same applies to similar concepts, such as the meaning of influence between the affected and the influencer, or the meaning of a covenant or promise between the promisor and the promisee, and so on. Thus, it becomes clear that the use of ‘ala and al-lam to signify harm and benefit is a matter arising from the context of usage, not from the inherent meaning of the words themselves.

Since the success of repentance is due to a promise Allah made to His servants—whereby He made it obligatory upon Himself for their sake according to that promise—He said here: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ (Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance). Thus, it is incumbent upon Him, the Exalted, to accept the repentance of His servants. However, this is not in the sense that anyone else has the right to obligate Him or charge Him with a duty—whether that "other" is called intellect (العقل), the nature of the matter (نفس الأمر), reality (الواقع), truth (الحق), or anything else; He is far exalted and sanctified above that. Rather, it is because He, the Exalted, promised His servants that He would accept the repentance of the repentant among them, and He does not break His promise. This is the meaning of the necessity of accepting repentance being "upon Allah" (على الله) in what is necessary, and it is also the meaning of the necessity of everything that is necessary for Allah to do.

The apparent meaning of the verse is, firstly, that it is for the purpose of explaining the matter of repentance that belongs to Allah—meaning His return, the Exalted, with mercy toward His servant—rather than the repentance of the servant, even if the matter of the servant's repentance is clarified thereby by way of necessity. For when Allah's repentance is completed in its conditions, it is inseparable from the completion of the conditions of the servant's repentance. This point—that the verse is in the position of explaining Allah's repentance—requires no further clarification.

Secondly: It explains the matter of repentance in a general sense, whether the servant repents from polytheism (الشرك) and disbelief (الكفر) through faith (الإيمان), or repents from disobedience (المعصية) to obedience (الطاعة) after having faith. The Quran calls both matters "repentance" (التوبة). The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَحْمَةً وَعِلْماً فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ — Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord, You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge! So forgive those who repent and follow Your way’ (40:7). He means "those who believe" (للذين آمنوا) by the context of the beginning of the speech; thus, He called faith "repentance." And the Exalted said: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ — then He turned to them [clemently] (9:118).

The evidence that the intended meaning is repentance in its general sense—whether from polytheism or disobedience—is the generalization found in the following verse: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ (But repentance is not...), for it addresses the state of both the disbeliever and the believer together. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ (commit evil) includes the state of both the believer and the disbeliever. The disbeliever, like the profligate believer (المؤمن الفاسق), is among those who commit evil out of ignorance—either because disbelief is an act of the heart, and "act" (العمل) is more general than the acts of the heart and the limbs, or because disbelief is not devoid of evil acts of the limbs. Thus, the meaning of الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ (those who commit evil out of ignorance) refers to the disbeliever and the profligate when they are not obstinate (معاندين) in their disbelief and disobedience.

As for His saying, the Exalted: بِجَهالَةٍ (out of ignorance), ignorance (الجهل) is the opposite of knowledge (العلم) in its essence. However, when people observed in themselves that they perform each of their ongoing actions out of knowledge and will, and that the will only arises from some love or desire—whether the act is something that ought to be done according to the view of the rational people in society or something that ought not to be done—those with a discerning intellect (عقل مميز) among them do not embark upon an evil act that is censured by the rational. Thus, they concluded that whoever commits these censured evils due to a psychological whim (هوى نفساني) or a carnal or angry impulse, the face of knowledge has become hidden from him, and his discerning intellect—which judges between the beautiful and the ugly, the praised and the censured—has vanished from him, and whim has overcome him. At that point, his state regarding his knowledge and will is called "ignorance" (جهالة) in their convention. Although, upon precise examination, it is a type of knowledge, since the knowledge he possesses regarding the ugliness and blameworthiness of the act did not influence him to refrain from falling into ugliness and depravity, it is treated as non-existent. Thus, he is "ignorant" in their view. They even call a young person of little experience "ignorant" because of the dominance of whim and the emergence of raw emotions and feelings over his soul. For this reason, you see that they do not call the state of one who commits evils "ignorance" if he was not moved by whim or emotion during the commission of the evil; rather, they call it obstinacy (عناد), intentionality (عمد), and the like.

It is thus clear that "ignorance" (الجهالة) in the context of actions is performing the act out of whim and the emergence of lust or anger without obstinacy against the truth. One of the characteristics of this act arising from ignorance is that when the storm of the faculties subsides and the flame of lust or anger is extinguished—either by the commission of the evil, the occurrence of an obstacle, the passage of time, or the weakening of the faculties through old age or temperament—the person returns to knowledge, the ignorance vanishes, and remorse (الندامة) becomes apparent. This is in contrast to an act arising from obstinacy, intentionality, and the like; since the cause of its occurrence was not the transgression of one of the faculties, emotions, or psychological inclinations, but rather something they call "wickedness of essence" (خبث الذات) and "depravity of the primordial nature" (رداءة الفطرة), it does not vanish with the subsiding of the transgression of the faculties and inclinations, whether quickly or slowly. Rather, it generally persists as long as life persists, without being followed by prompt remorse, unless Allah wills.

Yes, it may happen that the stubborn, obstinate person turns back from his obstinacy, stubbornness, and superiority over the truth, humbles himself before the truth, and enters into the humility of servitude. In their view, this reveals that his obstinacy was actually out of ignorance. In reality, every disobedience is an "ignorance" on the part of the human being. Based on this, there remains no instance of the "obstinate" except for one who does not return from his evil deed until the very end of his life and health.

From here, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ (and then repent promptly) becomes clear. That is, the one who commits evil out of ignorance does not remain devoted to his way, adhering to it throughout his life without hope of turning toward godwariness (التقوى) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), as the stubborn, obstinate person persists. Rather, he returns from his act "promptly" (من قريب). What is meant by "promptly" is a recent time or a near period, which is before the appearance of the signs of the Hereafter and the arrival of death.

Every stubborn, obstinate person in his actions, when he witnesses the evil consequences of his deeds and the bane of his acts, his soul compels him toward remorse and disavowal of his act. However, in reality, he is not remorseful out of his nature or the guidance of his primordial nature; rather, it is a trick his evil soul devises to escape the bane of the act. The proof for this is that if he happens to escape that specific bane, he returns a second time to the evil deeds he was upon. The Exalted said: وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — Were they to be sent back, they would surely return to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars (6:28).

The evidence that "promptly" (القريب) in the verse refers to what is before the appearance of the sign of death is His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ (But repentance is not...) until His saying: قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ (he says, ‘I repent now’).

Accordingly, His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ (and then repent promptly) is a metaphor (كناية) for the negligence that leads to the loss of opportunity.

It becomes clear from what has passed that both conditions—namely His saying: بِجَهالَةٍ (out of ignorance) and His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ (and then repent promptly)—are restrictive (احترازيان). The first is intended to mean that one does not commit evil out of obstinacy and superiority over Allah. The second is intended to mean that the person does not delay repentance until the presence of his death out of laziness, lethargy, and procrastination. For repentance is the return of the servant to Allah, the Glorified, through servitude; thus, His repentance, the Exalted, is the acceptance of this return. Servitude has no meaning except within the worldly life, which is the sphere of choice (الاختيار) and the abode of obedience and disobedience. With the rising of the sign of death, there is no choice through which obedience or disobedience can proceed. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ أوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً — the day when some of your Lord’s signs come, its faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some good in its faith (6:158). And the Exalted said: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا قالُوا آمَنَّا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ وَكَفَرْنا بِما كُنَّا بِهِ مُشْرِكِينَ فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ وَخَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْكافِرُونَ — Then, when they sighted Our punishment, they said, ‘We believe in Allah alone, and we disbelieve in what we used to associate with Him.’ But their faith did not benefit them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent that has passed among His servants—and there the faithless were the losers (40:84-85), among other verses.

In short, the meaning returns to the fact that Allah, the Glorified, only accepts the repentance of the sinning disobedient one if he did not commit the disobedience out of arrogance (استكبارا) toward Allah such that the spirit of return and self-abasement before Allah is nullified in him, and if he did not neglect and overlook the matter of repentance to the point that it leads to the loss of opportunity by the presence of death.

It is possible that His saying: بِجَهالَةٍ (out of ignorance) is an explanatory condition (قيد توضيحي), and the meaning would be: "for those who commit evil," and that only happens out of ignorance on their part, for it is a risking of the soul and an exposure to a painful punishment; or it only happens out of their ignorance of the reality of the disobedience and the perils that result from it. This would necessitate that His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ (and then repent promptly) is an indication of what is before death, not a metaphor for negligence in the matter of repentance. For whoever performs the disobedience out of arrogance and does not submit to the authority of Lordship would, on this assumption, be excluded by His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ, not by His saying: بِجَهالَةٍ. Based on this, it would not be possible for His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ to be a metaphor for negligence and lethargy—so understand that. Perhaps the first view is more in accord with the apparent meaning of the verse.

Some have mentioned that the meaning of His saying: ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِنْ قَرِيبٍ is that repentance is realized in a time close to the moment the disobedience occurred according to convention (عرفا), such as the time of finishing the commission of the disobedience or what is conventionally considered connected to it, rather than extending until the presence of death as mentioned.

This is invalid because it invalidates the meaning of the following verse. For the two verses are in the position of explaining a universal rule (ضابط كلي) for Allah's repentance—meaning His acceptance of the servant's repentance—as indicated by the restriction (الحصر) found in His saying: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ (Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those...). The second verse explains the cases in which repentance is not accepted, and only two cases are mentioned in the verse: the repentance of the evildoer who is negligent in repenting until the presence of death, and the repentance of the disbeliever after death. If the accepted repentance were only that which is conventionally considered close and connected to the time of the disobedience, there would be other instances of unaccepted repentance that were not mentioned in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكانَ اللهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً — it is they to whom Allah shall turn [clemently], and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise (4:17). The use of the demonstrative pronoun for the distant (أولئك) is not without an indication of the elevation of their status and the greatness of their matter. Similarly, His saying: يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ (commit evil out of ignorance) indicates a leniency in accounting for their sins, contrary to what is in the second verse: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ (But repentance is not for those who commit [many] evils...).

The choice to conclude the speech with His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً (and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise), rather than saying "and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful," is to indicate that the opening of the door of repentance is due to His knowledge, the Exalted, of the state of the servants and what their weakness and ignorance lead them to, and due to His wisdom (حكمته) which necessitates establishing what is required for the perfection of the system and the rectification of affairs. He, the Exalted, due to His knowledge and wisdom, is not deceived by the outward appearance of states; rather, He tests the hearts. He is not moved by plot or deception. Thus, the repentant among the servants must repent with a true repentance so that Allah may respond to him with a true response.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ — But repentance is not for those who commit [many] evils (4:18). The non-repetition of His saying: عَلَى اللهِ (upon Allah), despite it being intended, carries a subtle allusion to the cutting off of special mercy and divine care from them. Similarly, expressing "evils" (السَّيِّئاتِ) in the plural form indicates a concern with accounting for their evils and preserving them against them, as previously indicated.

The restriction of His saying: يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ (commit [many] evils) by His saying: حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ (until, when death visits one of them)—which signifies the continuity of the action—is either because negligence in hastening toward repentance and procrastinating it is in itself a continuous and repeated disobedience, or because it is in the position of persisting in the act, or because negligence in the matter of repentance is usually not devoid of the repetition of disobediences of the same genus as the committed disobedience or similar to it.

In His saying: حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ (until, when death visits one of them), rather than saying "until death comes to them," there is an indication of the disdain for the matter and the belittling of it. That is, until the matter of repentance becomes this trivial and this easy, such that people do what they desire and choose what they wish without concern, and whenever the occurrence of death presents itself to one of them, he says: "I repent now." Thus, the perils of sins and the destruction of opposing the divine command are repelled by a mere word repeated by their tongues or a thought that occurs to their minds at the end of the matter.

From here, the meaning of restricting His saying: قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ (he says, ‘I repent’) with His saying: الْآنَ (now) becomes clear. It signifies that the presence of death and the witnessing by this speaker of the authority of the Hereafter are what compel him to say "I repent," whether he mentions it or not. The meaning is: "I am repentant because I have witnessed the true death and the true recompense." He, the Exalted, said regarding its like, narrating from the guilty on the Day of Resurrection: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [confessing], ‘Our Lord, we have seen and heard; so send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we are [now] convinced’ (32:12).

This is a repentance that is not accepted from its possessor because despair of the worldly life and the terror of the transition are what forced him to regret his act and resolve to return to his Lord. But it is not the time for return, where there is no worldly life and no practical choice.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ — nor for those who die while they are faithless (4:18). This is another instance of the non-acceptance of repentance: the person who persists in disbelief and then dies while he is a disbeliever. Allah does not turn to him, for his faith—which is his repentance—does not benefit him on that day. It has been repeated in the Holy Quran that there is no salvation with disbelief after death, and that they are not answered even if they ask. The Exalted said: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَبَيَّنُوا فَأُولئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنَا التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُولئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ خالِدِينَ فِيها لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ — excepting those who repent, and reform, and make things clear. It is they to whom I shall turn [clemently], and I am the All-clement, the All-merciful. Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless—it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind. They will remain in it [forever]; their punishment shall not be lightened, nor shall they be respited (2:160-162). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَنْ يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمْ مِلْءُ الْأَرْضِ ذَهَباً وَلَوِ افْتَدى بِهِ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ — Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless, a world of gold shall not be accepted from any of them should he offer it for ransom. There is a painful punishment for them, and they will have no helpers (3:91). The negation of helpers is a negation of intercession (الشفاعة) for them, as previously discussed in the commentary on the verse in the third volume of the book.

The restriction of the sentence by His saying: وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ (while they are faithless) indicates repentance for the sinning believer if he dies upon disobedience without arrogance or negligence. For repentance from the servant—in the sense of his return to elective servitude—even if its subject is removed by death as previously mentioned, yet repentance from Him, the Exalted—in the sense of returning with forgiveness and mercy—can be realized after death through the intercession of the intercessors. This in itself is among the evidences that the two verses are intended to explain the state of Allah's repentance toward His servants, not the state of the servant's repentance toward Allah except by consequence.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — For such We have prepared a painful punishment (4:18). The demonstrative pronoun indicates their distance from the courtyard of proximity and honor. Preparation (الاعتاد) is the same as al-i‘dad (الإعداد) or the promise (الوعد).

(A Discourse on Repentance)

Repentance (التوبة) in its full meaning mentioned in the Quran is among the true teachings exclusive to this Heavenly Book. For repentance in the sense of faith after disbelief and polytheism, although it was prevalent in other divine religions like the religion of Moses and Jesus (peace be upon them), it was not from the perspective of analyzing the reality of repentance and extending it to faith, but rather under the name that it is faith.

It even appears from the principles upon which they built the independent Christian religion that repentance is of no benefit and that it is impossible for a human being to benefit from it, as shown by what they cited in justifying the crucifixion and atonement; this was previously quoted in the discussion on the creation of the Messiah in the third volume of this book.

Furthermore, the matter of the Church later drifted into excess regarding repentance to the point where they were selling indulgence papers and trading in them, and the religious authorities would forgive the sins of the disobedient for what they confessed to them! However, the Quran analyzed the state of the human being according to the call addressed to him and the guidance related to him. It found him—with respect to the perfection, dignity, and happiness necessary for him in his afterlife with Allah, the Glorified, which he cannot do without in his elective journey toward his Lord—to be utterly poor in his essence, empty-handed in himself. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُ — O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient (35:15). And He said: وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلا نَفْعاً وَلا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَلا حَياةً وَلا نُشُوراً — they do not control any benefit or harm for themselves, nor do they control death, or life, or resurrection (25:3).

Thus, he is situated in the descent of misery, the decline of distance, and the isolation of destitution, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ — We certainly created man in the best of forms; then We relegated him to the lowest of the low (95:4-5). And His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وارِدُها كانَ عَلى رَبِّكَ حَتْماً مَقْضِيًّا ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيها جِثِيًّا — There is none of you but will come to it: a [matter that is a] must, a settled decree of your Lord. Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees (19:71-72). And His saying: فَلا يُخْرِجَنَّكُما مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقى — so let him not expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable (20:117).

If this is the case, then his arrival at the station of dignity and his settling in the abode of happiness depend on his turning away from the descent of misery and the decline of distance he is in, and uprooting himself from it by returning to his Lord.

This is his repentance toward Him in the root of happiness, which is faith (الإيمان), and in every branch of happiness, which is every righteous deed (عمل صالح)—meaning repentance and returning from the root of misery, which is polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, the Glorified, and from the branches of misery, which are the evil deeds after polytheism. Thus, repentance—in the sense of returning to Allah and stripping away the stains of distance and misery—is that upon which settling in the abode of dignity through faith depends, as well as enjoying the various types of blessings of obedience and proximity. In other words, proximity to Allah and the abode of His dignity depends on repentance from polytheism and from every disobedience. The Exalted said: وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ — and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous (24:31). Thus, repentance in the sense of returning to Allah encompasses both types of repentance; indeed, it encompasses them and others, as will come, if Allah wills.

Furthermore, since the human being is poor in himself, possessing no good or happiness for himself except through his Lord, he is in need in this return also of a care from his Lord for his affair, and an assistance from Him in his state. His return to his Lord through servitude and destitution requires a return from his Lord toward him through success (التوفيق) and assistance. This is Allah's repentance toward His servant that precedes the servant's repentance toward his Lord, as He, the Exalted, said: ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا — then He turned to them [clemently] so that they might repent (9:118). Likewise, the return to Allah, the Glorified, requires His acceptance through the forgiveness of sins and purification from filthiness and the stains of distance. This is the second repentance from Allah, the Glorified, which follows the servant's repentance toward his Lord, as He, the Exalted, said: فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ (it is they to whom Allah shall turn [clemently]).

If you reflect with true reflection, you will find that the multiplicity in Allah's repentance only occurs when measured against the servant's repentance. Otherwise, it is a single repentance, which is the return of Allah, the Glorified, toward His servant with mercy. This occurs at the time of the servant's repentance as a return toward him before it and after it. Sometimes it occurs without any repentance from the servant, as previously inferred from His saying: وَلَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ (nor for those who die while they are faithless), and that the acceptance of intercession for the sinning servant on the Day of Resurrection is among the instances of repentance. From this category is His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الشَّهَواتِ أَنْ تَمِيلُوا مَيْلاً عَظِيماً — Allah desires to turn to you [clemently], but those who follow their passions desire that you should deviate in a great deviation (4:27).

Likewise, since proximity and distance are relative, it is possible for distance to be realized within the station of proximity by comparing some of its positions and stages to others. At that point, the meaning of repentance applies to the return of some of the near ones among the righteous servants of Allah from the position they are in to a higher position closer to their Lord. This is evidenced by what He, the Exalted, narrates of the repentance of the Prophets, while they are infallible (معصومون) by the text of His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَتَلَقَّى آدَمُ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَلِماتٍ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ — Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him [clemently] (2:37). And His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْراهِيمُ الْقَواعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْماعِيلُ — As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael... until His saying: وَتُبْ عَلَيْنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ — and turn to us [clemently]. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful (2:127-128). And His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Moses (peace be upon him): سُبْحانَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers’ (7:143). And His saying, the Exalted, addressing His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَاستَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكارِ — So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. Plead for forgiveness for your sin, and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening (40:55). And His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ تابَ اللهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنْصارِ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ فِي ساعَةِ الْعُسْرَةِ — Allah has certainly turned [clemently] to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers who followed him in the hour of difficulty (9:117).

This general repentance from Allah, the Glorified, is what is indicated by the absolute sense of many verses of His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: غافِرِ الذَّنْبِ وَقابِلِ التَّوْبِ — forgiver of sin and acceptor of repentance (40:3). And His saying, the Exalted: يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ — It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants (42:25), and others.

In summary of what has passed: First, the spreading of mercy from Allah, the Glorified, upon His servant to forgive his sins and remove the darkness of disobediences from his heart—whether polytheism or what is below it—is a repentance from Him, the Exalted, toward His servant. And the return of the servant to his Lord for the forgiveness of his sins and the removal of his disobediences—whether polytheism or otherwise—is a repentance from him toward his Lord.

It thus becomes clear that it is necessary in the true call to attend to the matter of disobediences just as one attends to the root of polytheism, and to invite toward absolute repentance, which encompasses repentance from polytheism and repentance from disobediences.

Second: Allah's repentance toward His servant—whether the initial or the subsequent—is a grace (فضل) from Him, like all other blessings He bestows upon His creation, without any compulsion or obligation imposed upon Him, the Exalted, from anyone else. The meaning of the necessity of accepting repentance being upon Him, the Exalted, rationally is nothing other than what is indicated by the likes of His saying: وَقابِلِ التَّوْبِ (acceptor of repentance), and His saying: وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ (and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful), and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُحبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ — Indeed Allah loves the penitent (2:222), and His saying: فَأُولئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ (it is they to whom Allah shall turn [clemently]), among the verses containing His description, the Exalted, as accepting repentance, and those inviting toward repentance, calling for seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) and turning in penitence (الإنابة), and others that include the promise of acceptance either explicitly or implicitly. And Allah, the Glorified, does not break His promise.

From here, it becomes clear that Allah, the Glorified, is not compelled to accept repentance; rather, to Him belongs the sovereignty without exception—He does what He wills and judges as He intends. Thus, He may accept what He accepts of repentance according to what He promised, and reject what He rejects of it, as is apparent in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَنْ تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ — Indeed those who turn faithless after their [acknowledgement of] faith and then advance in faithlessness, their repentance will never be accepted (3:90). It is possible that His saying, the Exalted, is of this category: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلاً — Indeed those who believe and then turn faithless, then believe, then turn faithless, and then advance in faithlessness, Allah will never forgive them, nor will He guide them to any way (4:137).

Among the strange things said in this regard is the statement of some concerning His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Pharaoh's drowning and his repentance: حَتَّى إِذا أَدْرَكَهُ الْغَرَقُ قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائِيلَ وَأَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — until, when the drowning overtook him, he said, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit [to Him].’ [He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you were disobedient before and were among the agents of corruption?’ (10:90-91).

He said, in essence: The verse does not indicate the rejection of his repentance, nor is there anything in the Quran that indicates his eternal destruction. It is unlikely for one who reflects on the vastness of Allah's mercy and its precedence over His wrath to permit that He, the Exalted, would reject one who sought refuge at the door of His mercy and dignity, abased and destitute, with disappointment and despair. If one of us, having adopted innate human ethics of generosity, bounty, and mercy, would have mercy on such a person who truly regrets his past evil deeds, then what of Him who is the Most Merciful of the merciful, the Most Generous of the generous, and the Succor of those who seek help?

This is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ (But repentance is not for those who commit [many] evils until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now’). It has already been mentioned that remorse at that time is a false remorse that the human being is driven to manifest by his witnessing the bane of the sin and the descent of the affliction.

If every remorse were repentance and every repentance were accepted, it would be refuted by His saying, the Exalted, narrating the state of the guilty on the Day of Resurrection: وَأَسَرُّوا النَّدامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ — and they will hide their remorse when they sight the punishment (34:33), among many other verses narrating their remorse for what they did and their request to return to the world to act righteously, and the response to them that if they were sent back, they would return to what they were forbidden, and that they are indeed liars.

Beware of imagining that the path the Holy Quran followed in analyzing repentance—as previously clarified—is a mental analysis of no consequence in the realm of realities. This is because research into the chapter of human happiness, misery, righteousness, and corruption yields nothing else. For if we consider the state of the ordinary human being in society—based on what we see of the influence of education and upbringing—we find him in himself devoid of social righteousness and corruption, capable of both. Then, if he wishes to adorn himself with the ornament of righteousness and clothe himself in the garment of social godwariness, that is not possible for him except by the coincidence of causes for his exit from the state he is in. This corresponds to the first repentance from Allah, the Glorified, in the chapter of spiritual happiness. Then, his extraction and the turning of his soul away from the squalor of the state he is in and the bond of stagnation and neglect is a repentance corresponding to the repentance of the servant in our discussion. Then, the vanishing of the form of corruption and the attribute of vileness dominating his heart until the attribute of perfection and the light of righteousness settle within it—for the heart cannot contain both righteousness and corruption together—this corresponds to the acceptance of repentance and forgiveness in our discussion. Likewise, in the stage of social righteousness in which the human being proceeds by his primordial nature, all the rulings and effects that the religion considered in the chapter of repentance apply, proceeding according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which Allah originated mankind.

Third: Repentance—as inferred from the sum of the aforementioned verses and others—is a reality with an influence on the human soul in terms of rectifying it and preparing it for the human righteousness in which lies the happiness of his world and his hereafter. In other words, repentance only benefits—if it benefits—in removing the psychological evils that drag the human being toward every misery in his first and second lives and prevent him from settling upon the throne of happiness. As for the legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) and religious laws, they remain as they are; they are not removed from him by repentance, just as they are not removed from him by disobedience.

Yes, perhaps some rulings are linked to it and are removed by repentance according to the interests of the legislation. This is different from repentance being a remover of one of the rulings. The Exalted said: وَالَّذانِ يَأْتِيانِها مِنْكُمْ فَآذُوهُما فَإِنْ تابا وَأَصْلَحا فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُما إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ تَوَّاباً رَحِيماً — And the two of you who commit it, punish them both; but if they repent and reform, let them alone. Indeed Allah is All-clement, All-merciful (4:16). And the Exalted said: إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً أَنْ يُقَتَّلُوا أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوا أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ أَوْ يُنْفَوْا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ ذلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْيٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَلَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهِمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — Indeed the recompense of those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle, and strive to cause corruption on the earth, is that they shall be slain or crucified, or have their hands and feet cut off from opposite sides, or be banished from the land. That is a disgrace for them in this world, and in the Hereafter there is a great punishment for them; excepting those who repent before you capture them. So know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful (5:33-34), and others.

Fourth: The criterion for which repentance was legislated—as clarified from what has passed—is to escape the destruction of sin and the ruin of disobedience, as it is the means of success and the precursor to attaining happiness, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللهِ جَمِيعاً أَيُّهَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful, so that you may be felicitous). Among its benefits, in addition to that, is that it preserves the spirit of hope from being extinguished or stagnating. For the human being's vital journey does not remain upright except through balanced fear and hope, so that he is repelled from what harms him and attracted to what benefits him; otherwise, he would perish. The Exalted said: قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ — Say [that Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. And turn to your Lord in penitence...’ (39:53-54). The human being, as we know from his instinct, continues to have vigor in his active spirit and seriousness in resolve and effort as long as his deal in the marketplace of life has not lost. When it appears to him that his work is lost, his effort is disappointed, and his wish is nullified, despair overcomes him, and the pillars of his action collapse. He may even turn his face away from his path, despairing of success and disappointed in attainment and felicity. Repentance is the only medicine that treats his disease and revives his heart when it has approached destruction and ruin.

From here, the fallacy of what might be imagined—that the legislation of repentance and the call to it is an incitement to disobedience and an encouragement to abandon obedience—becomes apparent. For if a person is certain that Allah accepts his repentance if he commits any disobedience, this would not leave an effect in his soul other than increasing his audacity to violate Allah's sanctities and immerse himself in the depths of disobediences and sins, knocking on the door of every disobedience intending to sin and then repent.

The reason for its fallacy is that repentance was legislated—in addition to the fact that adornment with honors depends on the forgiveness of sins—to preserve the attribute of hope and its good influence. As for what was mentioned regarding it necessitating that a person intends every disobedience with the intention to disobey and then repent, he has missed the fact that repentance with this description does not realize the reality of repentance. For it is an uprooting (انقلاع) from the disobedience, and there is no uprooting in this person's action. The proof for this is that he was resolved upon it before the disobedience, during the disobedience, and after the disobedience. There is no meaning to remorse (remorse being repentance) before the act is realized. Rather, the sum of the act and the repentance in such disobediences is taken as a single act intended with a single intention of plot and deception to deceive the Lord of the worlds, and the evil plot only besets its authors.

Fifth: Disobedience—which is the evil position of the human being—has an evil effect on his life. One does not repent from it or return from it except with knowledge and certainty of its evil. This is inseparable from remorse (الندم) for its occurrence first. Remorse (الندم) is a specific internal affection from the evil act. Upon the settling of this, the return depends on some righteous acts that are contrary to that evil, indicating the return and repentance second.

To this return all that is considered legally among the etiquettes of repentance, such as remorse, seeking forgiveness, engaging in righteous deeds, and uprooting from the disobedience, among other things mentioned in the narrations and addressed in the books of ethics.

Sixth: Repentance—which is the elective return from evil to obedience and servitude—is only realized in the sphere of choice, which is the worldly life, the level of choice. As for where the servant has no choice in selecting either of the two paths of righteousness and corruption, happiness and misery, there is no place for repentance there, as has been clarified.

Of this category is repentance regarding the rights of people (حقوق الناس). It only rectifies what relates to the rights of Allah, the Glorified. As for the evil related to the rights of people, which requires their satisfaction for its removal, it is not remedied by it at all. For Allah, the Glorified, respected people through rights He established for them in their wealth, honor, and lives, and He considered transgression against any of them in any of that as injustice and aggression. Far be it from Him to strip them of anything He established for them without a crime committed by them, such that He Himself would do what He forbids and wrong them thereby. He, the Mightiest of speakers, said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً — Indeed Allah does not wrong mankind in any way (10:44).

However, Islam—which is the repentance from polytheism—erases every previous evil and past consequence related to the branches, as indicated by his saying (peace be upon him): "Islam cancels what preceded it." By this, the absolute verses indicating the forgiveness of all evils are interpreted, such as His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ — Say [that Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. And turn to your Lord in penitence and submit to Him...’ (39:53-54).

Also of this category is the repentance of one who established an evil precedent or led people astray from the path of truth. Narrations have been recorded stating that upon him is the like of the burdens of those who acted upon it or strayed from the truth. For the reality of return is not realized in such cases, because the disobedient one initiated an event therein that has effects that remain as long as it remains, and he is unable to remove them, unlike the cases where the disobedience does not exceed what is between him and his Lord, exalted is His Name.

Seventh: Although repentance erases the evils it erases—as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ جاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ فَانْتَهى فَلَهُ ما سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللهِ — So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists, he shall keep what is past, and his matter will rest with Allah (2:275) according to the previous explanation in the second volume of this book—indeed, the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلاً صالِحاً فَأُوْلئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً وَمَنْ تابَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَإِنَّهُ يَتُوبُ إِلَى اللهِ مَتاباً — excepting those who repent, believe, and act righteously: such, Allah will replace their evil deeds with good deeds, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. And whoever repents and acts righteously, he indeed turns to Allah with a [true] conversion (25:70-71), especially considering the second verse, is that repentance by itself or joined with faith and righteous deeds necessitates the changing of evil deeds into good deeds. Nevertheless, avoiding the evil is better than committing it and then erasing it through repentance. For Allah, the Glorified, clarified in His Book how disobediences, whatever they may be, ultimately lead to satanic whisperings. Then He described the sincere ones (المخلصين)—who are protected from the slip of disobediences and the stumble of evils—with that which no praise mentioned for others equals. The Exalted said: قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ قالَ هذا صِراطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ — He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all, except Your exclusive servants among them.’ He said, ‘This is a straight path [leading] to Me. Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them...’ (15:39-42). And the Exalted said, narrating from Iblis also in the story: وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ — and You will not find most of them to be grateful (7:17).

These people are exclusive to the station of honorary servitude (العبودية التشريفية), an exclusivity in which no other righteous repentant ones share with them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Faqih, it is narrated that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said in the last sermon he delivered: من تاب قبل موته بسنة تاب الله عليه — "Whoever repents a year before his death, Allah turns to him." Then he said: إن السنة لكثيرة ومن تاب قبل موته بشهر تاب الله عليه — "Indeed, a year is a long time; and whoever repents a month before his death, Allah turns to him." Then he said: وإن الشهر لكثير ومن تاب قبل موته بيوم تاب الله عليه — "Indeed, a month is a long time; and whoever repents a day before his death, Allah turns to him." Then he said: وإن اليوم لكثير ومن تاب قبل موته بساعة تاب الله عليه — "Indeed, a day is a long time; and whoever repents an hour before his death, Allah turns to him." Then he said: وإن الساعة لكثيرة من تاب وقد بلغت نفسه هذه ـ وأهوى بيده إلى حلقه تاب الله عليه — "Indeed, an hour is a long time; whoever repents when his soul has reached this"—and he pointed with his hand to his throat—"Allah turns to him."

Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ حَتَّى إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ (But repentance is not for those who commit [many] evils until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now’). He said: ذلك إذا عاين أمر الآخرة — "That is when he beholds the matter of the Hereafter."

I say: The first narration was recorded in al-Kafi with a chain of transmission from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and it is narrated through Sunni paths, and there are other narrations with the same meaning.

The second narration interprets the verse and interprets the narrations regarding the non-acceptance of repentance at the presence of death, stating that what is meant by the presence of death is the knowledge of it and the witnessing of the signs of the Hereafter, at which point there is no repentance. As for one who is ignorant of the matter, there is no obstacle to the acceptance of his repentance. Similar to it are some of the narrations that follow.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said: إذا بلغت النفس هذه ـ وأهوى بيده إلى حنجرته ـ لم يكن للعالم توبة ، وكانت للجاهل توبة — "When the soul reaches this"—and he pointed with his hand to his throat—"there is no repentance for the one who knows, but there is repentance for the one who is ignorant."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, recorded by Ahmad, al-Bukhari in his Tarikh, al-Hakim, and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Dharr: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إن الله يقبل توبة عبده أو يغفر لعبده ما لم يقع الحجاب — "Indeed, Allah accepts the repentance of His servant or forgives His servant as long as the veil has not fallen." It was asked: "And what is the falling of the veil?" He said: تخرج النفس وهي مشركة — "The soul departs while it is polytheistic."

In the same source, recorded by Ibn Jarir from al-Hasan, who said: It reached me that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إن إبليس لما رأى آدم أجوف قال : وعزتك لا أخرج من جوفه ما دام فيه الروح ـ فقال الله تبارك وتعالى : وعزتي لا أحول بينه وبين التوبة ما دام الروح فيه — "Indeed, when Iblis saw that Adam was hollow, he said: 'By Your might, I shall not leave his interior as long as the spirit is in him.' So Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: 'By My might, I shall not come between him and repentance as long as the spirit is in him.'"

In al-Kafi, narrated from ‘Ali al-Ahmasi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said: والله ما ينجو من الذنوب إلا من أقر بها — "By Allah, none is saved from sins except one who confesses them." He said: And Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) said: كفى بالندم توبة — "Remorse is sufficient as repentance."

In the same source, through two paths from Ibn Wahb, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) saying: إذا تاب العبد توبة نصوحا أحبه الله تعالى فستر عليه — "When the servant repents with a sincere repentance, Allah the Exalted loves him and thus conceals [his sins] for him." I asked: "And how does He conceal for him?" He said: ينسي ملكيه ما كانا يكتبان عليه ـ ثم يوحي الله إلى جوارحه وإلى بقاع الأرض : أن اكتمي عليه ذنوبه فيلقى الله حين يلقاه ـ وليس شيء يشهد عليه بشيء من الذنوب — "He makes his two angels forget what they used to write against him. Then Allah reveals to his limbs and to the tracts of the earth: 'Conceal his sins for him.' Thus, he meets Allah when he meets Him, and there is nothing to testify against him regarding any of the sins."

In the same source, narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said: يا محمد بن مسلم ذنوب المؤمن إذا تاب عنها مغفورة له ـ فليعمل المؤمن لما يستأنف بعد التوبة والمغفرة ـ أما والله إنها ليست إلا لأهل الإيمان — "O Muhammad ibn Muslim, the sins of the believer, when he repents from them, are forgiven for him. So let the believer act for what he commences after repentance and forgiveness. Indeed, by Allah, it is only for the people of faith." I asked: "What if he returns to sins after repentance and seeking forgiveness, and then returns to repentance?" He said: يا محمد بن مسلم أترى العبد المؤمن يندم على ذنبه ـ فيستغفر الله منه ويتوب ثم لا يقبل الله توبته؟ — "O Muhammad ibn Muslim, do you think the believing servant regrets his sin, seeks Allah's forgiveness for it, and repents, and then Allah does not accept his repentance?" I asked: "What if he does that repeatedly—he sins and then repents and seeks forgiveness?" He said: كلما عاد المؤمن بالاستغفار والتوبة عاد الله تعالى عليه بالمغفرة ، وإن الله غفور رحيم يقبل التوبة ، ويعفو عن السيئات فإياك أن تقنط المؤمنين من رحمة الله — "Whenever the believer returns with seeking forgiveness and repentance, Allah the Exalted returns to him with forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful; He accepts repentance and pardons evil deeds. So beware of making the believers despair of the mercy of Allah."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Abu ‘Amr al-Zubayri from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى — Indeed I am all-forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then follows guidance (20:82). He said: لهذه الآية تفسير يدل على ذلك التفسير ـ أن الله لا يقبل من عبد عملا إلا لمن لقيه بالوفاء منه بذلك التفسير ، وما اشترط فيه على المؤمنين — "For this verse there is an interpretation that indicates that interpretation—that Allah does not accept an act from a servant except from one who meets Him with fulfillment of that interpretation and what was stipulated therein for the believers." And he said: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ — "meaning every sin the servant committed. Even if he was knowledgeable of it, he is 'ignorant' when he risked his soul in the disobedience of his Lord." And He said regarding that, narrating the saying of Joseph to his brothers: هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جاهِلُونَ — ‘Did you know what you did to Joseph and his brother, when you were ignorant?’ (12:89). Thus, he attributed ignorance to them because of their risking their souls in the disobedience of Allah.

I say: The narration is not without some disturbance in the text. It appears that what is meant by the beginning is that the act is only accepted if the servant fulfills it and does not violate it; thus, repentance is only accepted if it acts as a deterrent and prohibition against the sin, even for a time. And his saying: "And He said: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ..." is a new statement by which he intended to clarify that His saying: بِجَهالَةٍ (out of ignorance) is an explanatory condition, and that in absolute disobedience there is ignorance according to one of the two previous interpretations. This latter part has also been narrated in al-Majma‘ from him (عليه السلام).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَرِثُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ كَرْهًا ۖ وَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا۟ بِبَعْضِ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِينَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ ۚ وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِن كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (١٩)
O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women forcibly, and do not press them to take away part of what you have given them, unless they commit a gross indecency. Consort with them in an honourable manner; and should you dislike them, maybe you dislike something while Allah invests it with an abundant good. (19)
وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمُ ٱسْتِبْدَالَ زَوْجٍ مَّكَانَ زَوْجٍ وَءَاتَيْتُمْ إِحْدَىٰهُنَّ قِنطَارًا فَلَا تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أَتَأْخُذُونَهُۥ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٢٠)
If you desire to take a wife in place of another, and you have given one of them a quintal [of gold], do not take anything away from it. Would you take it by way of calumny and flagrant sin?! (20)
وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُۥ وَقَدْ أَفْضَىٰ بَعْضُكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَأَخَذْنَ مِنكُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٢١)
How could you take it back, when you have known each other, and they have taken from you a solemn covenant? (21)
وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ مَا نَكَحَ ءَابَآؤُكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَمَقْتًا وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا (٢٢)
Do not marry any of the women whom your fathers had married, excluding what is already past. That is indeed an indecency, an outrage and an evil course. (22)

(Commentary)

This is a return to the matter of women by mentioning other regulations concerning them. Alongside this, the verses contain His saying: وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسى أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَيَجْعَلَ اللهُ فِيهِ خَيْراً كَثِيراً — "And consort with them in a good manner; and if you should be averse to them, it may be that you are averse to something and Allah creates in it much good." (4:19). Indeed, this is a Quranic foundation for the social life of women.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ — "O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you..." up to His saying: كَرْهاً — "...forcibly." (4:19). The people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — according to history and narration — used to count the wives of the deceased as part of the inheritance (التركة), provided the woman was not the mother of the heir. Thus, they would inherit them along with the estate. One of the heirs would cast a garment over the wife of the deceased and thereby inherit her. If he wished, he would marry her without a dower (مهر), but rather by right of inheritance; and if he was averse to marrying her, he would detain her with him. Then, if he wished, he would marry her off to someone else and benefit from her dower, or if he wished, he would constrain her (عضلها) and prevent her from marriage, keeping her detained until she died so that he might inherit her if she possessed wealth.

Although the apparent meaning of the verse is that it forbids a practice circulating among them—which is the inheritance of women that we mentioned—and is thus intended to deter them from this evil custom as some exegetes have stated, His saying at the end of the sentence, كَرْهاً — "forcibly," does not align with that, whether it is taken as an explanatory (توضيحي) or a restrictive (احترازي) qualification.

For if it were an explanatory qualification, it would imply that this inheritance always occurs against the will of the women, which is not the case, as is obvious. And if it were a restrictive qualification, it would imply that the prohibition only applies when the inheritance is against the women's will, but not when it is with their consent, which is also not the case.

Rather, "force" (الكره) is a reality that manifests in the constraint (العضل) from marriage out of greed for their inheritance, always or mostly, after seizing them through inheritance. Thus, it appears that the verse is in the position of deterring this inheritance against their will. As for marrying them by right of inheritance, that which addresses its prohibition is the saying of the Exalted that follows: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married..." (4:22). As for marrying them off to others and taking their dower, this is forbidden by verses such as the saying of the Exalted: وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ — "...and to women belongs a share of what they have earned." (4:32). All of this is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ — "...there is no sin upon you in what they may do with themselves in an honorable manner." (2:234).

As for His subsequent saying: وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا — "and do not constrain them that you may take away..." (4:19) etc., this is different from the aforementioned constraint from marriage to take wealth through inheritance. This is because the concluding phrase, لِتَذْهَبُوا بِبَعْضِ ما آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ — "...that you may take away part of what you have given them," indicates that what is meant is taking away part of the dower that the constraining husband had given, rather than wealth she owned through other means. In short, the verse forbids the inheritance of women's wealth against their will, rather than the inheritance of their persons; thus, the attribution of inheritance to "women" (النساء) is by assuming "wealth" (الأموال) as the elided object, or it is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا — "and do not constrain them that you may take away..." up to His saying: مُبَيِّنَةٍ — "...manifest." (4:19). This is either coordinated with His saying: تَرِثُوا — "that you should inherit," with the implied meaning being: "nor that you should constrain them"; or it is a prohibition coordinated with His saying: لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ — "it is not lawful for you," because the latter carries the meaning of a prohibition. Constraint (العضل) is prevention, restriction, and harshness. Indecency (الفاحشة) is a loathsome way of conduct, and its usage is frequent regarding adultery (الزنا). Manifest (المبينة) means that which is clear (المتبينة). It has been narrated from Sibawayh that abana (أبان), istabana (استبان), bayyana (بين), and tabayyana (تبين) all carry the same meaning, being both transitive and intransitive.

The verse forbids restricting them in any manner of restriction so that they are forced to relinquish part of the dower to dissolve the marriage bond and escape the hardship of life. Restricting them with this intention is forbidden for the husband, unless the wife commits a manifest indecency (فاحشة مبينة); in that case, he may constrain her and restrict her so that she separates from him by paying a ransom (البذل). This verse does not contradict the other verse regarding ransom: وَلا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئاً إِلَّا أَنْ يَخافا أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللهِ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيما حُدُودَ اللهِ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما فِيمَا افْتَدَتْ بِهِ — "It is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them, unless the couple fears that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds; so if you fear they may not maintain Allah’s bounds, there is no sin upon them in what she may give to ransom herself." (2:229). Rather, it is a specification (تخصيص). This verse specifies the verse of al-Baqarah to the case of committing indecency. As for the ransom mentioned in the verse of al-Baqarah, it occurs based on mutual consent, so this verse is not specified by it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ — "And consort with them in a good manner" (4:19) until the end of the verse. The "good manner" (المعروف) is that which people recognize in their society without denying or being ignorant of it. Since the command to consort is qualified by it, the meaning is the command to consort with them in the manner recognized among those being commanded.

The consorting that men recognize and practice among themselves is that each one of them is a constitutive part of society, equal to other parts in forming the human society for the purpose of cooperation and general mutual support of the species. Thus, a duty is directed toward each of them to strive to the best of his ability for what the society needs; he acquires what benefits him, gives what he can dispense with, and takes what he requires. If one of the parts of society were treated otherwise, it would be nothing but oppression by nullifying his independence as a part, making him a follower who is utilized without receiving anything equivalent in return; and this is the exception.

Allah, the Exalted, has clarified in His Book that all people—men and women—are branches of a single human root, and parts and portions of a single human nature. Society, in its formation, is in need of these just as it is in need of those, on an equal footing, as He, the Exalted, said: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "...you are all [members] of one another." (4:25).
