This does not contradict the fact that each of the two groups is characterized by a specific trait, such as men being characterized by toughness and strength (الشدة والقوة) as a type, and women being characterized by tenderness and emotion (الرقة والعاطفة) by nature. For human nature, in both its ontological and social life, requires the manifestation of toughness and strength just as it requires the flow of affection and mercy. Both traits are manifestations of the general attraction and repulsion within human society.

Thus, the two groups are balanced in weight and impact, just as the individuals within the group of men are equal in weight and influence in this composite structure, despite their differences in natural and social affairs—such as strength and weakness, knowledge and ignorance, shrewdness and dullness, youth and old age, leadership and subordination, being served and serving, nobility and baseness, and so on.

This is the judgment that springs from the taste of a moderate society that follows the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — without deviation. Islam has straightened the pillars of human society and corrected its crookedness; thus, there is no escape from the application of the rule of equality in consorting, which we express as social freedom (الحرية الاجتماعية). The freedom of women is like that of men, and its reality is that a human being, qua human, possesses thought and will, and has the right to choose what benefits him over what harms him, being independent in his choice. Then, when he enters society, he has the right to choose what he chooses—so long as it does not conflict with the happiness of human society—being independent in that without being prevented or forced to follow another without choice.

This, as you have realized, does not contradict the specialization of certain classes or certain individuals within a single class with privileges, or their deprivation of privileges—such as the specialization of men in Islam with the judiciary, governance, and struggle (الجهاد), and the obligation of providing maintenance (النفقة) for women upon men, and so on. Similarly, the deprivation of non-pubescent children from the validity of their acknowledgments and transactions, and the fact that religious obligations are not directed toward them, and the like. All of these are specificities of laws that apply to classes and individuals of society based on their differing weights within society, after they all share in the fundamental human social weight, the criterion of which is that everyone is a human being possessing thought and will.

These specificities are not unique to the sacred law of Islam; rather, they are found in all civil laws, and indeed in all human customs, even the primitive ones, to a greater or lesser extent. The comprehensive word for all these meanings is the saying of the Exalted: وَعاشِرُوهُنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ — "And consort with them in a good manner," as has been clarified.

As for His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ فَعَسى أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَيَجْعَلَ اللهُ فِيهِ خَيْراً كَثِيراً — "...and if you should be averse to them, it may be that you are averse to something and Allah creates in it much good" (4:19), it is of the nature of presenting a known matter in the form of something doubtful and possible, to avoid awakening the instinct of prejudice in the addressee. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلِ اللهُ وَإِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلى هُدىً أَوْ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ قُلْ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا أَجْرَمْنا وَلا نُسْئَلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — "Say, ‘Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah! Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error.’ Say, ‘You will not be questioned about our crimes, nor shall we be questioned about what you do.’" (34:24-25).

For human society at that time (the era of the Quran's revelation) did not place women in their true human position, and was averse to her entering society as a constitutive part. Rather, the societies standing at that time were divided between those who considered them external parasites whose existence is utilized, and those who considered them humans deficient in humanity, like children and the insane, except that they would never reach full humanity; thus, they must always live under subordination and domination. Perhaps His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ كَرِهْتُمُوهُنَّ — "if you should be averse to them," where He attributed the aversion to their persons rather than to their marriage, is an allusion to this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ أَرَدْتُمُ اسْتِبْدالَ زَوْجٍ مَكانَ زَوْجٍ — "And if you desire to replace a wife with [another] wife..." (4:20) until the end of the verse. Replacement (الاستبدال) is the seeking of a substitute. It is as if it means placing one wife in the position of another, or it is of the nature of inclusion (تضمين) meaning establishing one woman in the place of another through replacement. For this reason, He combined His saying أَرَدْتُمُ — "you desire" with اسْتِبْدالَ — "replacement," even though replacement already contains the meaning of desire and seeking. Accordingly, the meaning is: "And if you desire to establish one wife in the place of another through replacement."

Slander (البهتان) is that which bewilders (بهت) a person, i.e., makes him confused. Its usage is predominant regarding lies in speech. Originally, it is a verbal noun (مصدر), and it has been used in the verse for the action, which is the taking of the dower. In the verse, it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the act of taking, as is His saying: إِثْماً — "sinfully." The interrogation is rhetorical/denunciatory (إنكاري).

The meaning is: If you desire to divorce some of your wives and marry others in their place, do not take anything from the dower you have given them, even if what you gave them was a great amount of wealth (قنطاراً) and what you take is very little.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَيْفَ تَأْخُذُونَهُ وَقَدْ أَفْضى بَعْضُكُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ — "How can you take it back when you have already gone in to each other..." (4:21) until the end of the verse. The interrogation is for expressing wonder (تعجيب). Al-Ifda’ (الإفضاء) is connection through contact, and its root is al-fada’ (الفضاء) meaning vastness.

Since this taking is only through transgression and injustice, and its context is one of connection and union, this necessitated the validity of wonder; for the spouses, due to the intimacy and proximity necessitated by marriage, become like a single person. It is wondrous that a single person would wrong himself and hurt it, or that some of his parts would hurt others.

As for His saying: وَأَخَذْنَ مِنْكُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً — "...and they have taken from you a solemn covenant" (4:21), it appears that what is meant by the "solemn covenant" (الميثاق الغليظ) is the bond that the man solidified through the contract (العقد) and the like, one of the concomitants of which is the dower that is named at the time of marriage and which the woman is entitled to from the man.

It has been said that what is meant by the solemn covenant is the pledge taken from the man for the woman to "retain her honorably or release her kindly," as mentioned by Allah, the Exalted. It has also been said that what is meant is the ruling of lawfulness (الحلية) established by the Sharia in marriage. It is not hidden that both views are far-fetched in relation to the wording of the verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Hashim ibn Abdillah, from al-Sirri al-Bajali, who said: I asked him about His saying: وَلا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ لِتَذْهَبُوا بِبَعْضِ ما آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ — "and do not constrain them that you may take away part of what you have given them." He said: He related some words and then said: "As the Nabataeans say: when the garment is cast upon her, he has constrained her (عضلها), so she cannot marry another. This used to be in the Age of Ignorance."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً — "O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women forcibly." He said: "In the Age of Ignorance, during the early period of Islam among the Arab tribes, if a man's close relative died leaving a wife, the man would cast his garment over her and thus inherit her marriage by the dower of his relative—which he had given her. He would inherit her marriage just as he inherited his wealth. When Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died, Muhsin ibn Abi Qays cast his garment over his father's wife, who was Kubayshah bint Ma'mar ibn Ma'bad, and inherited her marriage. Then he left her, neither entering upon her nor spending on her. So she came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died and Muhsin his son inherited my marriage; he does not enter upon me, nor does he spend on me, nor does he release me so that I may join my family.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Return to your house; if Allah reveals anything regarding your matter, I will inform you.' Then there was revealed: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً وَمَقْتاً وَساءَ سَبِيلاً — 'Do not marry women whom your fathers married, except what has already passed. Indeed it is an indecency and an outrage and an evil course.' (4:22). So she joined her family. There were women in Medina whose marriage had been inherited just as Kubayshah's marriage was inherited, except that they were inherited by the sons. So Allah revealed: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً."

I say: The end of the narration is not free from some confusion in meaning. This story and the revelation of the verses concerning it have also been reported in several Sunni narrations, except that the narrations—or most of them—mention the revelation of His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا in the story. You have already seen in the preceding commentary that the context does not support this.

Nevertheless, the occurrence of the story and the connection of the verses in some way to it and to the custom prevalent among them at the time of revelation is beyond doubt. Thus, what is to be relied upon is what we presented in the preceding commentary.

In al-Majma': Regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِينَ بِفاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ — "...unless they commit a manifest indecency." He said: It is better to interpret the verse as referring to every act of disobedience (معصية). He said: And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Shaybani: "Indecency" (الفاحشة) means adultery (الزنا); that is, if the man discovers an indecency from her, he may take a ransom (الفدية). And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir extracted from Jabir that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Fear Allah regarding women, for you have taken them by the trust of Allah, and made their private parts lawful by the word of Allah. Your right over them is that they do not allow anyone you dislike to tread upon your beds. If they do that, then strike them with a strike that is not injurious. And their right over you is their provision and clothing in a good manner."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Umar that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O people, women are like captives (عوان) with you; you have taken them by the trust of Allah and made their private parts lawful by the word of Allah. You have a right over them, and of your right over them is that they do not allow anyone to tread upon your beds, nor should they disobey you in what is good. If they do that, then they are entitled to their provision and clothing in a good manner."

I say: That which clarifies the meaning of these narrations has already preceded.

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَخَذْنَ مِنْكُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً — "...and they have taken from you a solemn covenant." He said: "The covenant (الميثاق) is the word by which the marriage was contracted."

In al-Majma', he said: "The solemn covenant is the pledge taken from the husband at the time of the contract—to retain her honorably or release her kindly." He said: "And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

I say: This meaning is narrated from several of the early exegetes such as Ibn Abbas, Qatadah, and Abu Mulaykah. The verse does not reject this, considering that it is a ruling that can truly be called a covenant taken from men for women, although it is more apparent that what is meant is the contract performed at the time of marriage.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Zubayr ibn Bakkar extracted in al-Muwaffaqiyyat from Abdillah ibn Mus'ab who said: Umar said: "Do not increase the dowers of women beyond forty ounces (أوقية); whoever increases it, I shall cast the surplus into the public treasury (بيت المال)." A woman said: "That is not for you to do." He said: "And why?" She said: "Because Allah says: وَآتَيْتُمْ إِحْداهُنَّ قِنْطاراً — '...and you have given one of them a great amount [of wealth]...' (4:20)." So Umar said: "A woman is correct and a man has erred."

I say: It was also narrated by Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir from Abd al-Rahman al-Sulami, and also by Sa'id ibn Mansur and Abu Ya'la with a good chain from Masruq; in it, "four hundred dirhams" is mentioned instead of "forty ounces." It was also narrated by Sa'id ibn Mansur and Abd ibn Humayd from Bakr ibn Abdillah al-Muzani. The narrations are close in meaning.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ikrimah regarding His saying: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married." He said: "It was revealed concerning Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat, who succeeded to Umm Ubayd bint Damrah who was under his father al-Aslat; and concerning al-Aswad ibn Khalaf who succeeded to the daughter of Abi Talhah ibn Abd al-Uzza ibn Uthman ibn Abd al-Dar, who was with his father Khalaf; and concerning Fakhtah daughter of al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib ibn Asad, who was with Umayyah ibn Khalaf, so Safwan ibn Umayyah succeeded to her; and concerning Manzur ibn Rabab who succeeded to Mulaykah daughter of Kharijah, who was with his father Rabab ibn Sayyar."

In the same source, Ibn Sa'd extracted from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi who said: "When a man died leaving a wife, his son had more right to her to marry her if he wished—provided she was not his mother—or to marry her off to whomever he wished. When Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat died, his son Muhsin stood up and inherited his wife's marriage, but he did not spend on her nor did he give her anything from the inheritance. So she came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and mentioned that to him. He said: 'Return, perhaps Allah will reveal something concerning you.' Then there was revealed: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكاَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ and there was revealed: لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً."

I say: That which indicates this has already preceded from the narrations of the Shi'a.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Abbas who said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance used to forbid what Allah forbade except for the father's wife and combining two sisters. So Allah revealed: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ and أَنْ تَجْمَعُوا بَيْنَ الْأُخْتَيْنِ — '...and that you should marry two sisters together...'" (4:23). I say: There are other reports with the same meaning.

Ayahs

حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُمْ وَعَمَّـٰتُكُمْ وَخَـٰلَـٰتُكُمْ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأَخِ وَبَنَاتُ ٱلْأُخْتِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ وَأَخَوَٰتُكُم مِّنَ ٱلرَّضَـٰعَةِ وَأُمَّهَـٰتُ نِسَآئِكُمْ وَرَبَـٰٓئِبُكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى فِى حُجُورِكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّـٰتِى دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَإِن لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ دَخَلْتُم بِهِنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَحَلَـٰٓئِلُ أَبْنَآئِكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلَـٰبِكُمْ وَأَن تَجْمَعُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلْأُخْتَيْنِ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ سَلَفَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٢٣)
Forbidden to you are your mothers, your daughters and your sisters, your paternal aunts and your maternal aunts, your brother’s daughters and your sister’s daughters, your [foster-]mothers who have suckled you and your sisters through fosterage, your wives’ mothers, and your stepdaughters who are under your care [born] of the wives whom you have gone into—but if you have not gone into them there is no sin upon you—and the wives of your sons who are from your own loins, and that you should marry two sisters at one time—excluding what is already past; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful— (23)
۞ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۖ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَأُحِلَّ لَكُم مَّا وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُم مُّحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ ۚ فَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْهُنَّ فَـَٔاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا تَرَٰضَيْتُم بِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْفَرِيضَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٢٤)
and married women, excepting your slave-women. This is Allah’s ordinance for you. As to others than these, it is lawful for you to seek [temporary union with them] with your wealth, in wedlock, not in license. For the enjoyment you have had from them thereby, give them their dowries, by way of settlement, and there is no sin upon you in what you may agree upon after the settlement. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (24)
وَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنكُمْ طَوْلًا أَن يَنكِحَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ فَمِن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن فَتَيَـٰتِكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِكُم ۚ بَعْضُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ فَٱنكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ وَءَاتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ مُحْصَنَـٰتٍ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحَـٰتٍ وَلَا مُتَّخِذَٰتِ أَخْدَانٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ ٱلْعَنَتَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَأَن تَصْبِرُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٥)
As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women, then [let them marry] from what you own, from among your faithful slave-women. Your faith is best known [only] to Allah; you are all [on a] similar [footing]. So marry them with their masters’ permission, and give them their dowries in an honourable manner—[such of them] as are chaste women, not licentious ones or those who take paramours. But should they commit an indecent act on marrying, there shall be for them [only] half the punishment for free women. This is for those of you who fear falling into fornication; but it is better that you be continent, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (25)
يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٦)
Allah desires to explain [the laws] to you, and to guide you to the customs of those who were before you, and to turn toward you clemently, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (26)
وَٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ أَن تَمِيلُوا۟ مَيْلًا عَظِيمًا (٢٧)
Allah desires to turn toward you clemently, but those who pursue their [base] appetites desire that you fall into gross waywardness. (27)
يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ ۚ وَخُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ضَعِيفًا (٢٨)
Allah desires to lighten your burden, for man was created weak. (28)

(Commentary)

These are definitive verses (آيات محكمة) enumerating the prohibitions of marriage and those women who are lawful to marry. The preceding verse, which clarified the prohibition of marrying those whom one's fathers had married, although part of this group in terms of content, was placed among the previous verses because its apparent context (سياق) was a continuation of the preceding discourse, even though in meaning it is annexed to these verses.

In short, this set of verses encompasses the clarification of every marital prohibition without exception or restriction. This is evident from the saying of the Exalted after enumerating the prohibitions: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these" (4:24). For this reason, the people of knowledge did not differ in using this verse to prove the prohibition of the son’s daughter, the daughter’s daughter, and the father’s or mother’s mother. Likewise, they used His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married" (4:22) to prove the prohibition of the grandfather’s wife. From this, the Quranic perspective in identifying "sons" and "daughters" according to legislation (التشريع) is derived, as will be discussed, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهاتُكُمْ وَبَناتُكُمْ وَأَخَواتُكُمْ وَعَمَّاتُكُمْ وَخالاتُكُمْ وَبَناتُ الْأَخِ وَبَناتُ الْأُخْتِ — "Forbidden to you are your mothers, your daughters, your sisters, your paternal aunts, your maternal aunts, and your brother’s daughters and sister’s daughters" (4:23). These are the prohibited women based on lineage (النسب), and they comprise seven categories: 1. The Mother (الأم): Anyone to whom a person’s lineage is connected through birth, whether she gave birth to him directly or through an intermediary, such as the father’s mother or the mother’s mother, and upwards. 2. The Daughter (البنت): Anyone whose lineage is connected to the person because she was born from him, such as a daughter from his own loins without an intermediary, or the son’s daughter and the daughter’s daughter, and downwards. 3. The Sister (الأخت): Anyone whose lineage is connected to the person by virtue of both being born from the same father, the same mother, or both, without an intermediary. 4. The Paternal Aunt (العمة): The father’s sister, and likewise the grandfather’s sister from the father’s or mother’s side. 5. The Maternal Aunt (الخالة): The mother’s sister, and likewise the grandmother’s sister from the father’s or mother’s side.

The intended meaning of prohibiting "mothers" and the subsequent categories is the prohibition of marrying them, as indicated by the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of the expression and the suitability between the ruling and the subject (مناسبة الحكم والموضوع). This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ وَالدَّمُ — "Forbidden to you are carrion and blood" (5:3), meaning the eating of them; and His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّها مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Then it shall be forbidden to them" (5:26), meaning the dwelling in that land. This is a common rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

However, this interpretation does not align with what follows in His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "except those whom your right hands possess" (4:24), for that is an exception regarding sexual intercourse (الوطء) rather than the marriage bond (علقة النكاح), as will be explained. The same applies to His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "that you seek them with your wealth in wedlock, not in license" (4:24), as will be discussed. Thus, the truth is that the omitted word (المقدر) is that which conveys the meaning of sexual intercourse rather than the marriage bond. It was not explicitly mentioned out of refinement (تأدباً) and to safeguard the tongue, as is the custom of the speech of the Exalted.

The address is specifically directed to men, rather than saying "their sons are forbidden to them [women]," or "there is no marriage between a woman and her son," because the seeking and the proposal (الخطبة) naturally occur from the side of men only.

Directing the address to the collective while linking the prohibition to the collective—such as "mothers" and "daughters"—indicates exhaustive distribution (الاستغراق في التوزيع). That is, "forbidden to every man among you is his mother and his daughter," for there is no meaning in prohibiting the whole group to the whole group, nor in prohibiting every mother and daughter to every man, for example, as that would lead to the prohibition of the essence of marriage itself. Thus, the verse means that every man is forbidden from marrying his mother, his daughter, his sister, and so on.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُمَّهاتُكُمُ اللَّاتِي أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ وَأَخَواتُكُمْ مِنَ الرَّضاعَةِ — "and your mothers who have suckled you and your sisters through suckling" (4:23). This begins the clarification of prohibitions due to cause (السبب), which are seven. Six of them are in this verse, and the seventh is contained in His saying: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married" (4:22).

The verse, by its context, indicates the establishment of motherhood and daughterhood between a woman and the one she has suckled, as well as sisterhood between a man and his sister through suckling, as it presents this as an established fact. Thus, suckling (الرضاعة) creates lineage-like bonds according to legislation (التشريع). This is something unique to the Islamic Sharia, as will be pointed out.

It has been authentically reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in what both groups [Sunni and Shia] have narrated that he said: إن الله حرم من الرضاعة ما حرم من النسب — "Indeed, Allah has forbidden through suckling what He has forbidden through lineage." The implication is that the prohibition through suckling extends to the same categories as the lineage prohibitions, which are seven: the mother, the daughter, the sister, the paternal aunt, the maternal aunt, the brother’s daughter, and the sister’s daughter.

As for what constitutes suckling and the conditions for it to establish prohibition—in terms of quantity, quality, duration, and the associated rulings—these are clarified in jurisprudence (الفقه), and discussing them is outside the scope of this book. As for His saying: وَأَخَواتُكُمْ مِنَ الرَّضاعَةِ — "and your sisters through suckling," it refers to sisters who become related to a man because his mother suckled them with his father's milk, and so forth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُمَّهاتُ نِسائِكُمْ — "and the mothers of your wives" (4:23). This applies whether the wives (النساء)—meaning spouses—have been consummated with or not. When "women" (النساء) is annexed to "men," it refers to spouses in general. The proof for this is the subsequent restriction in His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ نِسائِكُمُ اللَّاتِي دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَ — "from your wives with whom you have consummated; but if you have not consummated with them..." (4:23).

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ — "and your stepdaughters who are under your care" (4:23) until His saying: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ — "there is no sin upon you" (4:23). Stepdaughters (الربائب) is the plural of rabibah (الربيبة), which is the daughter of a man's wife from another husband. She is called so because the management of the affairs of the children who are with the woman falls to her husband; thus, he is the one who fosters and raises her (yarubbuha) in the prevailing custom, even if it is not always the case.

Similarly, the stepdaughter being "under the care" (في حجر) of the husband is based on the common case, even if it does not always happen. Therefore, it has been said that His saying: اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ — "who are under your care" is a descriptive restriction based on the common case (قيد مبني على الغالب). Thus, the stepdaughter is prohibited whether she is under the care of her mother's husband or not; the restriction is explanatory (توضيحي) rather than exclusionary (احترازي).

It is possible to say that His saying: اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ — "who are under your care" points to what can be understood from the wisdom of legislating prohibitions (حكمة تشريع الحرمة) in lineage and causal relations, as will be discussed. This wisdom is the established and stable intermingling between a man and these categories of women, and the frequent companionship between them in homes and houses. Were it not for the ruling of perpetual prohibition, it would be impossible to avoid the occurrence of indecency (الفحشاء) merely by prohibiting adultery (الزنا), as will be explained.

Thus, His saying: اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ — "who are under your care" indicates that stepdaughters, because they are usually under your care and in your company, share with the other categories the underlying rationale (الملاك) and wisdom of the prohibition.

In any case, His saying: اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ — "who are under your care" is not an exclusionary restriction that limits the prohibition, such that a stepdaughter would be lawful to her stepfather if she were not under his care—such as an adult daughter whose mother a man marries. The proof for this is the explicit counter-implication (المفهوم) in His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ — "but if you have not consummated with them, then there is no sin upon you" (4:23). Here, He mentioned the removal of the restriction of consummation because consummation is a factor in the prohibition. If being "under the care" were like it, it would have been necessary to mention its removal as well, which is obvious.

His saying: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ — "there is no sin upon you," meaning in marrying them, was omitted for the sake of brevity because the context indicates it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحَلائِلُ أَبْنائِكُمُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلابِكُمْ — "and the wives of your sons who are from your loins" (4:23). Wives (الحلائل) is the plural of halilah (الحليلة). It is said in al-Majma': "Wives (الحلائل) is the plural of halilah, which means 'one who is made lawful' (محللة), derived from halal (الحلال). The male is called halil (حليل), and its plural is ahillah (أحلة), like aziz and a'izzah. They are so named because it is lawful for each to have intimacy with the other. It is also said to be from hulul (الحلول) because each of them 'alights' (yahullu) with the other, meaning they occupy the same bed." End quote.

The intended meaning of sons (الأبناء) is anyone connected to the person through birth, whether directly or through a son or daughter. Restricting it with His saying: الَّذِينَ مِنْ أَصْلابِكُمْ — "who are from your loins" is to exclude the wife of one who is called a son through adoption (التبني) rather than birth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ تَجْمَعُوا بَيْنَ الْأُخْتَيْنِ إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ — "and that you should marry two sisters together, except what has already passed" (4:23). This refers to the prohibition of marrying the wife's sister as long as the wife is alive and remains under the bond of marriage. This is the most concise and beautiful expression for conveying the intended meaning. The absolute nature of the speech refers to combining them in marriage at the same time. Thus, there is no barrier to a man marrying one sister and then marrying the other after the divorce or death of the first. The proof for this is the definitive practice (السيرة القطعية) among Muslims, continuous from the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

As for His saying: إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ — "except what has already passed," it is like its preceding counterpart in His saying: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married, except what has already passed" (4:22). It refers to what was practiced among the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) regarding combining two sisters. The intent is to clarify the pardon for what had passed of their practice of combining two sisters before the revelation of this verse, but not if any of that existed at the time of revelation through a prior marriage. The verse indicates its prohibition because it would be an actual combining of two sisters. This is also indicated by what was previously mentioned regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for His saying: وَلا تَنْكِحُوا ما نَكَحَ آباؤُكُمْ — "Do not marry women whom your fathers married," where the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) separated sons from their fathers' wives after the revelation of the verse, even though the marriage had occurred before its revelation.

Lifting the prohibition—which is permissibility—from a past marriage that is no longer an actual occurrence, and pardoning it in terms of the completed act itself, would be futile and without effect. However, it is not without benefit regarding the remaining effects of the act, such as the purity of birth (طهارة المولد) and the recognition of kinship resulting from procreation, and the like.

In other words, there is no meaning in directing prohibition or permissibility toward a past marriage where two sisters were combined if, for example, they both died, or one of them died, or divorce occurred for both or one of them. However, it is correct to remove the invalidation and prohibition from such a marriage in consideration of the children it produced, by ruling on the purity of their birth and the existence of kinship between them and their biological fathers and other paternal relatives, which affects inheritance, marriage, and other matters.

Accordingly, His saying: إِلَّا ما قَدْ سَلَفَ — "except what has already passed" is an exception from the ruling in consideration of its legal effects, not in consideration of its original attachment to an act that ended before the legislation. From this, it becomes clear that the exception is connected (متصل) rather than disconnected (منقطع), as some commentators have mentioned.

It is possible that the exception refers back to all the clauses mentioned in the verse, rather than being specific to His saying: وَأَنْ تَجْمَعُوا بَيْنَ الْأُخْتَيْنِ — "and that you should marry two sisters together." For although the Arabs did not commit these prohibitions except for combining two sisters, and did not practice marrying mothers, daughters, and the rest mentioned in the verse, there were other nations that married various categories of prohibited relatives, such as the Persians, the Romans, and other civilized and uncivilized nations at the time of the revelation of the verses, despite their differences. Islam considers the validity of the marriages of non-Muslim nations practiced among them according to their own creeds; thus, it rules on the purity of their birth and recognizes the validity of their kinship after they enter the religion of truth. However, the first view is more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً — "Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (4:23) is a justification (تعليل) referring back to the exception. This is one of the instances where forgiveness (المغفرة) is attached to the external effects of actions rather than to sins and acts of disobedience.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "and married women, except those whom your right hands possess" (4:24). Married women (المحصنات), with a fathah on the sad, is a passive participle from ihsan (الإحصان), which means "fortification" or "prevention." From it comes hisn hasin (الحصن الحصين), meaning a "fortified stronghold." It is said: "the woman is ahsanat (أحصنت)" if she is chaste, thus guarding herself and preventing herself from indecency. The Exalted said: الَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَها — "who guarded her chastity" (66:12), meaning she was chaste. It is also said: "the woman is uhsinat (أحصنت)"—in both active and passive forms—if she is married, for her husband or the marriage itself "fortifies" her from anyone other than her husband. It is also said: "the woman is ahsanat (أحصنت)" if she is free (حرة), for that prevents others from possessing her person or prevents her from adultery, as that was prevalent among slave-girls.

It is apparent that the intended meaning of married women (المحصنات) in the verse is the second meaning—that is, married women—rather than the first or third. This is because what is prohibited among women other than the fourteen categories enumerated in the two verses is only the marriage of those already married. There is no prohibition regarding other women, whether they are chaste or otherwise, and whether they are free or owned. Thus, there is no reason to interpret "married women" in the verse as "chaste women" when the ruling of prohibition is not specific to the chaste, and then resort to restricting the verse to marriage. Nor is there reason to interpret the term as "free women" when the ruling for slave-girls is the same, and then resort to restricting it to marriage. Such an approach is not accepted by a sound disposition.

Thus, the intended meaning of married women (المحصنات) is women who are married and under the bond of marriage. This is conjoined to the position of "your mothers." The meaning is: "and forbidden to you is every married woman among women as long as she is married and has a husband."

Accordingly, His saying: إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "except those whom your right hands possess" (4:24) is a removal of the prohibition from married slave-girls, as reported in the Sunnah: that the master of a married slave-girl has the right to come between his slave-girl and her husband, then have intimacy with her after a period of purification (استبراء), and then return her to her husband.

As for what some commentators have mentioned—that the meaning of His saying: إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "except those whom your right hands possess" is "except those chaste women whom you possess through marriage or through ownership of the person," where "possession" means the possession of enjoyment and the authority for intimacy—this is problematic. Firstly, it depends on interpreting "married women" as "chaste women" rather than "married women," and you have seen the flaw in that. Secondly, it is customary in the Quran to use this expression for a different meaning, which is the ownership of the person (ملك الرقبة) rather than the authority for utilization and the like.

Similarly, what others have mentioned—that the meaning of "those whom the right hands possess" is captive slave-girls if they have husbands among the disbelievers—and supported this with what was narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: that the verse was revealed regarding the captives of Awtas, where the Muslims captured women of the polytheists who had husbands in the Abode of War (دار الحرب). When the verse was revealed, the caller of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) announced: "Behold, pregnant women shall not be approached until they give birth, nor those who are not pregnant until they have been purified."

In addition to the weakness of the narration, this is a specification (تخصيص) of the verse without a specifier. Thus, the conclusion is what we have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: كِتابَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ — "[This is] Allah’s ordinance for you" (4:24). That is, adhere to the ruling of Allah that is written and decreed for you. Commentators have mentioned that His saying: كِتابَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ — "[This is] Allah’s ordinance for you" is in the accusative case as an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb. The estimation is: "Allah has written a writing (ordinance) for you," then the verb was omitted and the infinitive was annexed to its subject and put in its place. They did not take the word alaykum (عليكُم) as a verbal noun (اسم فعل) because grammarians state that it is weak in its action and its object cannot precede it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these" (4:24). The apparent use of the relative pronoun ma (ما), which typically refers to non-rational beings, as well as the demonstrative pronoun dhalikum (ذلكم), which refers to a singular masculine, and His subsequent saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ — "that you seek them with your wealth," indicates that the intended meaning of the relative and demonstrative pronouns is the omitted word in His saying: "Forbidden to you are your mothers," to which the prohibition is attached—namely, sexual intimacy or what is of that nature. The meaning is: "And lawful to you of intimacy with them is what is other than what has been mentioned to you," which is intimacy through marriage with those not counted among the fifteen categories, or through ownership. In this case, the substitution (بدلية) of His saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ — "that you seek them with your wealth" for His saying: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these" is perfectly sound.

Strange interpretations have been reported from commentators regarding this sentence of the verse. Some said the meaning of His saying: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these" is: "Lawful to you are those beyond the prohibited relatives among your kin." Others said the meaning is: "Lawful to you are those besides the five, which are the four [wives] and fewer, that you seek with your wealth by way of marriage." Others said the meaning is: "Lawful to you are those beyond these among those whom your right hands possess." Others said the meaning is: "Lawful to you are those beyond the prohibited relatives and the addition to the four, that you seek with your wealth through marriage or ownership."

These are weak views for which there is no evidence from the wording of the verse. Furthermore, they share the assumption that the word ma (ما) in the verse refers to rational beings, for which there is no necessity, as you have just seen. Moreover, the verse is in the position of clarifying the prohibited forms of intimacy with women in terms of the categories of women, not in terms of the number of wives. Thus, there is no reason to impose the meaning of number upon the verse. The truth is that the sentence is in the position of clarifying the permissibility of intimacy with women other than the categories enumerated in the two preceding verses, whether through marriage or ownership.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "that you seek them with your wealth in wedlock, not in license" (4:24) is a substitute (بدل) or an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying: ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "beyond these." Through it, the legislated path for intimacy with women and having intercourse with them is clarified. This is because what is encompassed by His saying: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these" consists of three instances: marriage (النكاح), ownership (ملك اليمين), and license (السفاح)—which is adultery. Thus, He clarified by His saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ — "that you seek them with your wealth" etc., the prohibition of license and restricted the lawfulness to marriage and ownership. Then He considered "seeking with wealth"—which in marriage is the dower (المهر) and the wage (الأجرة), a pillar of its pillars, and in ownership is the price (الثمن), the common way of possessing slave-girls. Thus, the meaning of the verse results in something like: "It is lawful for you, regarding categories other than those enumerated, to seek intercourse with women and intimacy with them by spending your wealth on the wages of the married women among them in marriage without license, or spending it on the price of maidservants and slave-girls."

From this, it becomes clear that the intended meaning of wedlock (الإحصان) in His saying: مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "in wedlock, not in license" is the fortification of chastity (إحصان العفة) rather than the fortification of marriage or the fortification of freedom. This is because the intended meaning of "seeking with wealth" in the verse is more general than what pertains to marriage or ownership, and there is no evidence to restrict it to marriage such that "fortification" would be interpreted as the fortification of marriage. The intended meaning of the fortification of chastity is not the avoidance of intimacy with women—as that would contradict the context—but rather what stands in opposition to license (السفاح), meaning the transgression into indecency in any way, by restricting the self to what Allah has made lawful and restraining it from what Allah has forbidden of the natural ways of direct enjoyment, the desire for which has been placed in the human primordial nature (الفطرة).

From what we have presented, the invalidity of what some have mentioned becomes clear: that His saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ — "that you seek them with your wealth" is with the estimation of the lam of purpose (لام الغاية) or what conveys its meaning, the estimation being "so that you may seek" or the intention "to seek."

This is because the content of His saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا — "that you seek" is, in a way, the very thing intended by His saying: ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "beyond these," not something subsequent to it or intended for its sake, which is obvious.

Likewise, what appears from the speech of some—that the intended meaning of license (المسافحة) is the absolute spilling and pouring of water (semen) without intending the purpose for which Allah, the Glorified, placed this innate, natural, lustful drive in man, which is the goal of establishing a household and producing offspring and children; and by contrast, wedlock (الإحصان) would be permanent marriage whose purpose is procreation and reproduction.

I see this speaker only as one who has confused the methods of inquiry, mixing the inquiry into the rationale of the ruling (ملاك الحكم)—called the wisdom of legislation (حكمة التشريع)—with the inquiry into the ruling itself. Consequently, he was forced into implications that he cannot possibly uphold.

One of the two inquiries—the inquiry into the rationale—is rational (عقلي), while the other—the inquiry into the legal ruling and its subject, object, conditions, and barriers—is linguistic (لفظي), following the linguistic clarification from the Legislator in its breadth and narrowness. We do not doubt that all legislated rulings follow real interests and rationales, and the ruling of marriage, which is also one of them, follows in its legislation a real interest and a real rationale, which is procreation and reproduction. We know that the system of creation and origination intended for the human species a specific survival through the survival of individuals as long as Allah wills. Then, this goal was facilitated by equipping the human constitution with the reproductive system, which separates parts of it to nurture and form a new human who succeeds the old one, thus extending the chain of the species without interruption. This system was further facilitated for work and production by placing the lustful power through which one of the two groups—male and female—longs for the other, and each is attracted to its companion through intercourse and intimacy. Then, this was completed by the intellect (العقل), which prevents the corruption of this path to which the system of creation calls.

While the system of creation achieves its command and finds its purpose, which is the survival of the species, we do not find that every individual instance of these direct connections between male and female, nor their various types, always leads to the purpose of creation. Rather, they are a common preliminary. Not every marriage leads to the appearance of a child, nor does every reproductive act, nor does every desire for this act produce this effect. Not every man or every woman, nor every marriage, leads by necessary guidance to the "tasting" and then the procreation; all these are common occurrences.

Thus, the constitutional equipment calls man to marriage seeking offspring through the path of lust, and the intellect placed within him adds to that the caution and preservation of the self from indecency, which corrupts the happiness of life, destroys the foundation of households, and cuts off the lineage.

This composite interest—namely, the interest of procreation and security from the creeping of indecency—is the common rationale (الملاك الغالبي) upon which the legislation of marriage in Islam is built. However, the "commonality" is among the rulings of the rationale, whereas the rulings legislated for their subjects do not accept anything but permanence.

It is not permissible to say that marriage or intimacy follow, in their permissibility, the aforementioned purpose and rationale in their presence or absence. Thus, it would not be that no marriage is permissible except with the intention of procreation, nor would the marriage of the sterile be permissible, nor the marriage of the old woman who no longer menstruates, nor the marriage of the young girl, nor the marriage of the adulterer, nor intimacy with a pregnant woman, nor intimacy without ejaculation, nor marriage without establishing a household, and so on.

Rather, marriage is a legislated tradition (سنة مشروعة) between the two groups of male and female, having permanent rulings. This legislated tradition was intended to preserve a general, common interest, as you have seen. Thus, there is no meaning in making a legislated tradition dependent on the realization of the rationale in its presence or absence, or in prohibiting those individuals or rulings through which the rationale is not realized.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فريضة — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries, as an obligation" (4:24). The pronoun in His saying: بِهِ — "from it" refers back to what is indicated by His saying: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these," which is intimacy or what conveys its meaning. Thus, ma (ما) would be for timing (توقيتية), and His saying: مِنْهُنَّ — "from them" would be connected to His saying: اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ — "you have had enjoyment." The meaning would be: "Whenever you have had enjoyment through intimacy with them, give them their wages as an obligation."

It is also possible for ma (ما) to be a relative pronoun (موصولة), with "you have had enjoyment" being its relative clause, and the pronoun in "from it" referring back to the relative pronoun, and His saying "from them" being an explanation of the relative pronoun. The meaning would be: "And those women from whom you have had enjoyment, etc."

The sentence—namely, His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ — "For the enjoyment you have had" etc.—is a derivation (تفريع) from the preceding speech—due to the presence of the fa (ف)—a derivation of the part from the whole or the particular from the universal, without doubt. This is because the preceding speech—namely, His saying: أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "that you seek them with your wealth in wedlock, not in license"—as previously explained, encompasses what is in marriage and ownership. Thus, the derivation of His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries" from it is the derivation of a part from the whole or the derivation of some particular categories from the universal category.

This type of derivation occurs frequently in the speech of the Exalted, such as His saying, Mighty is the Speaker: أَيَّاماً مَعْدُوداتٍ فَمَنْ كانَ مِنْكُمْ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى سَفَرٍ — "[For] a certain number of days. But if any of you is ill or on a journey" (2:184); and His saying: فَإِذا أَمِنْتُمْ فَمَنْ تَمَتَّعَ بِالْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى الْحَجِ — "And when you are secure, whoever enjoys the Hajj with the ‘Umrah" (2:196); and His saying: لا إِكْراهَ فِي الدِّينِ قَدْ تَبَيَّنَ الرُّشْدُ مِنَ الْغَيِّ فَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالطَّاغُوتِ وَيُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ — "There is no compulsion in religion: rectitude has become distinct from error. So one who disbelieves in the Rebel and believes in Allah" (2:256), among others.

The intended meaning of the "enjoyment" (الاستمتاع) mentioned in the verse is the marriage of temporary enjoyment (نكاح المتعة) without doubt. This is because the verse is Medinan, revealed in Surah al-Nisa in the first half of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) era after the Migration (الهجرة), as most of its verses testify. This marriage—namely, temporary marriage—was circulating among them and practiced by them during this period of time without doubt—and reports have unanimously accepted this—whether Islam was the one that legislated it or not. The essence of its existence among them, within the sight and hearing of the Prophet, is beyond doubt. Its name was this name, and it was not expressed except by this term. Thus, there is no escape from His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ — "For the enjoyment you have had from them" being interpreted as this meaning and understood as such. This is just as all other traditions, customs, and practices circulating among them during the era of revelation by their known and familiar names: whenever a verse was revealed addressing a ruling related to any of those names—by confirmation, rejection, command, or prohibition—it was necessary to interpret the names mentioned therein according to their named meanings, without interpreting them according to their original linguistic meanings.

This is like the Pilgrimage (الحج), trade (البيع), usury (الربا), profit (الربح), spoils (الغنيمة), and all that is of this nature. No one could claim that the intended meaning of "the Pilgrimage to the House" is its linguistic meaning of "intending" it, and so on. Likewise, the legal subjects brought by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) which then became widely used until they were known by their legal names—such as prayer (الصلاة), fasting (الصوم), alms (الزكاة), the Pilgrimage of Enjoyment (حج التمتع), and others—there is no room, after the naming is established, to interpret their terms occurring in the Noble Quran according to their original linguistic meanings once the legal reality (الحقيقة الشرعية) or the reality established by the practitioners of the law (المتشرعية) has been realized in them.

Thus, it is determined that the "enjoyment" mentioned in the verse must be interpreted as temporary marriage (نكاح المتعة) because it circulated by this name among them on the day the verse was revealed. Whether we say that temporary marriage was abrogated after that by the Book or the Sunnah, or we do not, is another matter.

The gist of the matter is that what is understood from the verse is the ruling of temporary marriage. This is what has been narrated from the early commentators among the Companions (الصحابة) and the Successors (التابعين), such as Ibn Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud, Ubayy ibn Ka'b, Qatadah, Mujahid, al-Suddi, Ibn Jubayr, al-Hasan, and others. This is the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

From this, the invalidity of what some have mentioned in the interpretation of the verse—that the intended meaning of "enjoyment" is marriage, for establishing the marriage bond is a seeking of enjoyment from her—becomes clear. Some have also mentioned that the sin and ta in istamta'tum (استمتعتم) are for emphasis, meaning "you have enjoyed."

This is because the prevalence of temporary marriage (by this name) and its being well-known among them leaves no room for this linguistic meaning to occur to the minds of the listeners.

Furthermore, this meaning—even if assumed correct and the meaning of "seeking" were applicable to the context, or if istamta'tum meant "you have enjoyed"—does not align with the recompense (الجزاء) consequent upon it, namely His saying: فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ — "give them their dowries." For the dower becomes obligatory merely by the contract (العقد); it does not depend on the enjoyment itself, nor on the seeking of enjoyment that applies to the proposal, the execution of the contract, playfulness, intimacy, and so on. Rather, half of it becomes obligatory by the contract and the other half by consummation.

Moreover, the verses revealed before this verse have already fully clarified the obligation of giving the dower in all its circumstances. Thus, there is no reason to repeat the clarification of the obligation. This is like His saying, the Exalted: وَآتُوا النِّساءَ صَدُقاتِهِنَّ نِحْلَةً — "Give the women their dowries as an out-and-out gift" (4:4); and His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ أَرَدْتُمُ اسْتِبْدالَ زَوْجٍ مَكانَ زَوْجٍ وَآتَيْتُمْ إِحْداهُنَّ قِنْطاراً فَلا تَأْخُذُوا مِنْهُ شَيْئاً — "If you desire to replace a wife with another, and you have given one of them a quintal [of gold], do not take anything back from it" (4:20); and His saying, the Exalted: لا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنْ طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ ما لَمْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً وَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ عَلَى الْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُ وَعَلَى الْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُ — "There is no sin upon you if you divorce women while you have not yet touched them or settled a dowry for them; yet provide for them—the well-to-do according to his capacity, and the lowly according to his capacity" (2:236)—until He said—: وَإِنْ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ وَقَدْ فَرَضْتُمْ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً فَنِصْفُ ما فَرَضْتُمْ — "And if you divorce them before you have touched them, and you have already settled a dowry for them, then [pay them] half of what you have settled" (2:237).

As for the possibility raised by some that the verse—namely His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries, as an obligation"—is intended for emphasis, it is refuted by the fact that the context of the verses just cited, especially the context of the end of His saying: "If you desire to replace..." etc., is more intense and emphatic in tone than this verse. Thus, there is no reason for this one to be an emphasis of those.

As for abrogation (النسخ), it has been said: the verse is abrogated by the verse in al-Mu'minun: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ ، فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ — "and who guard their private parts, (5) except from their spouses or those whom their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy; (6) but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors" (23:5-7). It has also been said it is abrogated by the verse of the waiting period (العدة): يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَ — "O Prophet! When you divorce women, divorce them at [the commencement of] their waiting period" (65:1) and وَالْمُطَلَّقاتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنْفُسِهِنَّ ثَلاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ — "Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity" (2:228), since the separation of spouses is only through divorce and a waiting period, and these are not in temporary marriage. It has also been said: it is abrogated by the verses of inheritance: وَلَكُمْ نِصْفُ ما تَرَكَ أَزْواجُكُمْ — "To you belongs half of what your wives leave" (4:12), since there is no inheritance in temporary marriage. It has also been said: it is abrogated by the verse of prohibition: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهاتُكُمْ وَبَناتُكُمْ — "Forbidden to you are your mothers and your daughters" (4:23), for it is regarding marriage. It has also been said: it is abrogated by the verse of number: فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ — "then marry of women such as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four" (4:3). It has also been said: it is abrogated by the Sunnah, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) abrogated it in the year of Khaybar, or the year of the Conquest, or during the Farewell Pilgrimage. It has also been said: temporary marriage of women was permitted and then prohibited twice or thrice, and the final state upon which the ruling settled is prohibition.

As for the abrogation by the verse in al-Mu'minun, it is invalid because that verse is Meccan while the verse of temporary marriage is Medinan, and a Meccan verse cannot abrogate a Medinan one. Furthermore, the claim that temporary marriage is not "marriage" and the woman in it is not a "spouse" is rejected; suffice it to look at what has occurred in the prophetic reports and in the words of the predecessors among the Companions and Successors calling it "marriage." The objection regarding the necessity of inheritance, divorce, and so on will be answered later.

As for the abrogation by other verses such as the verse of inheritance, the verse of divorce, and the verse of number, the relationship between them and the verse of temporary marriage is not one of abrogation (الناسخ والمنسوخ), but rather one of the general and the specific (العام والمخصص) or the absolute and the restricted (المطلق والمقيد). For the verse of inheritance, for example, encompasses all spouses, whether permanent or temporary, and the Sunnah specifies it by excluding some of its individuals—namely the temporary spouse—from its generality. The same applies to the verse of divorce and the verse of number, which is obvious. Perhaps the claim of abrogation arose from a failure to distinguish between these two relationships.

Yes, some scholars of the principles of jurisprudence (الأصوليون) held that if a specific ruling (خاص) is followed by a general one (عام) that contradicts it in affirmation or negation, the general abrogates the specific. However, this view, besides being weak as clarified in its proper place, does not apply to the case at hand. This is because the verses of divorce (which are general) are in Surah al-Baqarah, which is the first Medinan surah revealed before Surah al-Nisa which contains the verse of temporary marriage. Likewise, the verse of number is in Surah al-Nisa, preceding the verse of temporary marriage. Similarly, the verse of inheritance occurs before the verse of temporary marriage in a single, continuous context in one surah. Thus, the specific—namely the verse of temporary marriage—is later than the general in any case.

As for the abrogation by the verse of the waiting period, its invalidity is even clearer, for the ruling of the waiting period applies to the temporary wife just as it does to the permanent wife, even if they differ in duration. Thus, it also results in specification rather than abrogation.

As for the abrogation by the verse of prohibition, it is among the strangest things said in this regard. Firstly, because the entire discourse indicating the prohibition and that indicating the ruling of temporary marriage is a single, narrated speech with harmonized parts and connected portions; how then can it be conceived that the beginning of the speech indicates temporary marriage and then the end of the speech abrogates its beginning? Secondly, because the verse is neither explicit nor apparent in forbidding non-permanent marriage in any way; rather, it is in the position of clarifying the categories of women prohibited to men, and then clarifying the permissibility of intimacy with others through marriage or ownership. Temporary marriage is a marriage, as previously stated, so there is no relationship of contradiction between the two that would lead to abrogation.

Yes, it has been said: His saying, the Exalted: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "And lawful to you are [all women] beyond these, that you seek them with your wealth in wedlock, not in license," since it restricted the lawfulness of women to the dower and to "wedlock" (إحصان) without license, and there is no "wedlock" in temporary marriage—which is why the man in a temporary marriage is not stoned if he commits adultery because he is not considered "fortified" (محصن)—this refutes that temporary marriage is intended by the verse.

However, the response to this is what was previously mentioned: that the intended meaning of "wedlock" (الإحصان) in His saying: مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — "in wedlock, not in license" is the fortification of chastity rather than the fortification of marriage, because the speech itself encompasses ownership just as it encompasses marriage. Even if it were granted that the intended meaning of "wedlock" is the fortification of marriage, the matter would return to specifying the stoning for the adultery of the "fortified" person by excluding the temporary spouse from it according to the Sunnah, not the Book, for the ruling of stoning is not mentioned in the Book at all.

As for the abrogation by the Sunnah, besides the invalidity of this type of abrogation in principle—as it contradicts the mutawatir reports commanding that reports be checked against the Book and that what contradicts it be discarded, and that one must return to the Book—this will be discussed in the traditional discourse (البحث الروائي).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً أَنْ يَنْكِحَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ — "As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women" (4:25). Affordance (الطول) means wealth or an increase in capability, and both meanings suit the verse. The intended meaning of married women (المحصنات) here is free women (الحرائر), by the evidence of its being contrasted with "maidservants" (الفتيات). This itself testifies that the intended meaning is not "chaste women," otherwise it would not have been contrasted with maidservants, but with them and with the unchaste. Nor is the intended meaning "married women," for a contract cannot be made with them. Nor is it "Muslim women," otherwise there would be no need for the restriction "faithful" (المؤمنات).

The intended meaning of His saying: فَمِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "then [let them marry] from what your right hands possess" (4:25) is what is possessed by the right hands of the believers other than the one who desires the marriage. Otherwise, a person's marriage to his own slave-girl is invalid and not legislated. Ownership has been attributed to the believers, and among them is the one desiring marriage, yet they are treated as one without difference due to their unity in religion and the unity of their interests and benefits, as if they were a single person.

In restricting both the free women and the maidservants to "faithful" (المؤمنات), there is an indication of the impermissibility of marrying non-believing women, whether from the People of the Book or polytheists. There is a continuation of this discourse that you will encounter, if Allah the Almighty wills, at the beginning of Surah al-Ma'idah.

The summary of the verse's meaning is that whoever among you is unable to marry faithful free women due to his inability to bear the burdens of the dower and maintenance, he may marry faithful maidservants without feeling constrained by the lack of capability for free women and exposing himself to the dangers of indecency and the path of misery.

The intended meaning of this marriage is permanent marriage. The verse is in the context of concession (التنزل)—that is, "if you cannot do this, then you can do that." The discourse in the case of concession was restricted to some individuals of the original case—namely, permanent marriage, which is one of the types of permissible marriage—because permanent marriage is what is naturally recognized and determined in the view of a person desiring to establish a household, produce offspring, and leave behind a child. Temporary marriage is a religious facilitation through which Allah lightened the burden for His servants for the sake of closing the path of indecency and cutting off the roots of corruption.

Presenting the discourse according to the common or well-known aspect that first comes to mind—especially in the position of legislating rulings and laws—is frequent and common in the Noble Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ شَهدَ مِنْكُمُ الشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ ، وَمَنْ كانَ مَرِيضاً أَوْ عَلى سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ — "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it, and as for him who is ill or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days" (2:185), even though the excuse is not limited to illness and travel. And His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضى أَوْ عَلى سَفَرٍ أَوْ جاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغائِطِ أَوْ لامَسْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا ماءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيداً طَيِّباً — "And if you are ill or on a journey, or when one of you comes from the privy, or you have touched women, and you cannot find water, then make your ablution on clean ground" (4:43). The excuses and the restrictions of the speech, as you see, are built upon the common and well-known, among other verses.

This is according to what they mentioned regarding interpreting the verse as permanent marriage. This does not necessitate—by virtue of its containing the meaning of concession and expansion—the restriction of the preceding verse to permanent marriage, nor that His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَ — "For the enjoyment you have had from them" was not presented to clarify the ruling of temporary marriage, as some have imagined. This is because this concession and expansion occur with both its sides (the original and the conceded) within this very verse—namely, His saying: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً — "As for those of you who cannot afford" etc.

Furthermore, the verse by its wording does not refuse to be interpreted as absolute marriage, encompassing both permanent and temporary, as will become clear by discussing the rest of its clauses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمانِكُمْ بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "Allah knows your faith best; you are one from another" (4:25). Since faith, which is taken as a factor in the ruling, is a matter of the heart for which there is no way to know its reality through [ordinary] means, and this might suggest a dependency on the impossible or the difficult and cause distress to the legally responsible (المكلفين), He, the Exalted, clarified that He is the one who knows the faith of His believing servants. This is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that they are only commanded to proceed according to the apparent means indicating faith, such as the two testimonies (الشهادتين), entering the community of Muslims, and performing the general religious duties. Thus, the outward appearance of faith is the criterion, not its inward reality.

In guiding these legally responsible individuals who are unable to marry free women toward marriage with slave-girls, there is another deficiency and shortcoming in terms of its being accepted and influential. This is because people generally see a sense of lowliness and meanness in the status of the enslaved class of male and female slaves, and a kind of humiliation and brokenness. This causes them to feel contracted and their souls to recoil from intermingling and socializing with them, especially through marriage, which is a vital partnership and a blending of flesh and blood.

Thus, He, the Glorified, pointed out by His saying: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "you are one from another" a clear truth through which this false delusion is dispelled. For the slave is a human being just as the free person is a human being; they do not differ in that by which a human becomes a possessor of human qualities. They only differ by a series of established rulings through which human society is stabilized in its production of people's happiness. These distinctions carry no weight with Allah; what carries weight is godwariness (التقوى), through which there is nobility with Allah. Thus, it is not fitting for the believers to be affected by such delusional thoughts that distance them from the realities of knowledge encompassing their happiness and success. For departing from the level of the straight path, even if it is minor at its beginning, continues to distance man from the path of guidance until it leads him into the valleys of destruction.

From this, it becomes clear that the order occurring at the beginning of the verse in the form of a condition and concession—namely, His saying: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً أَنْ يَنْكِحَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ فَمِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ — "As for those of you who cannot afford to marry faithful free women, then [let them marry] from what your right hands possess"—only follows the course of nature and custom in speech. It is not an obligation upon the believers to follow this order in the sense that the permissibility of marrying a slave-girl depends on the lack of capability to marry a free woman. Rather, because people, according to their natures, follow this path, He addressed them that if they were unable to marry free women, they could proceed to marry maidservants without hesitation, while simultaneously reminding them that the free and the slave are of one species, some of whose individuals return to others.

From this, the invalidity of what some have mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: وَأَنْ تَصْبِرُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ — "and that you be patient is better for you" (4:25) also becomes clear: that the meaning is "your patience from marrying slave-girls while remaining chaste is better for you than marrying them because of the humiliation, meanness, and degradation involved." This is because His saying: بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ — "you are one from another" categorically contradicts that.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ — "So marry them with the permission of their masters" (4:25) until His saying: أَخْدانٍ — "paramours" (4:25). The intended meaning of married women (المحصنات) here is chaste women (العفائف), for marriage cannot occur with those who already have husbands. The intended meaning of prostitutes (المسافحات) is what stands in opposition to "takers of paramours." Paramours (الأخدان) is the plural of khidn (الخدن), which means a friend; it is used for both male and female, singular and plural. It was brought in the plural form to explicitly indicate multiplicity, for one who takes a friend for indecency is not satisfied with one or two, as the soul does not stop at a limit if it is obeyed in what it desires.

In view of this contrast, some have said: the intended meaning of license (السفاح) is open adultery, and taking a paramour is secret adultery. Taking a paramour was common among the Arabs, even among free men and women, and was not blamed, despite their condemnation of open adultery for those other than slave-girls.

Thus, His saying: فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ — "So marry them with the permission of their masters" is a guidance toward marrying maidservants on the condition that it be with the permission of their masters (الموالي), for the reins of their affairs are in the hands of the masters alone. He expressed them as "their folk" (أَهْلِهِنَّ) following what is necessitated by His previous saying: "you are one from another"; thus, the maidservant is one of the folk of her master's house, and her master is her folk.

The intended meaning of "giving them their dowries in an honorable manner" is the full payment of their marriage dowers, and giving the dowries to them means giving them to their masters. He has guided toward giving in an honorable manner without stinginess, procrastination, or harm.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفاحِشَةٍ فَعَلَيْهِنَّ نِصْفُ ما عَلَى الْمُحْصَناتِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ — "Then if they are married and commit indecency, they shall be liable to half the punishment of free women" (4:25). Uhsinna (أُحْصِنَّ) was read with a dammah on the hamzah in the passive voice, and ahsanna (أَحْصَنَّ) with a fathah on the hamzah in the active voice, which is more likely.

If "fortification" (الإحصان) in the verse refers to the fortification of marriage, then its inclusion in the condition is merely because the subject of the preceding discourse was their marriage. This is because the slave-girl is punished with half the punishment of the free woman if she commits adultery, whether she is fortified by marriage or not, without the fortification adding anything further to her case.

However, if it refers to the fortification of Islam, as has been said—and supported by the reading with the fathah on the hamzah—the meaning is completed without extra burden: they are liable, if they commit adultery, to half the punishment of free women, whether they have husbands or not.

The intended meaning of punishment (العذاب) is flogging (الجلد) rather than stoning (الرجم), because stoning does not accept being halved. This is the evidence that the intended meaning of "married women" (المحصنات) is free women who are not married, as mentioned at the beginning of the verse. The lam is for reference (للعهد). Thus, the meaning of the verse in summary is that if faithful maidservants commit indecency—which is adultery—they are liable to half the prescribed punishment (الحد) of free women who are not married, which is fifty lashes.

It is possible that the intended meaning of "fortification" (الإحصان) here is the fortification of chastity (عفة). The explanation for this is that female slaves in those days were not free to engage in whatever actions their souls desired, due to their obligation to follow the commands of their masters—especially regarding indecency (الفاحشة) and immorality (الفجور). Indecency among them—if it occurred—was often by the command of their masters for the purpose of exploitation and seeking gain from their honor, as suggested by the prohibition in His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً "Do not compel your slave girls to prostitution if they intend to keep chaste" (An-Nur: 33). Thus, their pursuit of immorality and engagement in indecency by making it a habit and a livelihood was, in many cases, by the command of their masters, without them having the capacity to refuse or rebel. If the masters did not compel them toward immorality, then the believing women among them would be upon the outward piety of Islam and the chastity of faith. In such a case, if they commit an indecency, they shall be liable to half the punishment of the fortified [free] women. This is His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا أُحْصِنَّ فَإِنْ أَتَيْنَ بِفاحِشَةٍ "Then when they are fortified [by marriage or chastity], if they commit an indecency..." etc.

From here it becomes clear that there is no "implied opposite" (مفهوم) for this conditional clause according to this meaning. This is because if they are not fortified and do not remain chaste, they would be compelled by their masters and acting under their orders. This is similar to how there is no implied opposite for His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُكْرِهُوا فَتَياتِكُمْ عَلَى الْبِغاءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّناً "Do not compel your slave girls to prostitution if they intend to keep chaste" (An-Nur: 33); for if they do not intend to keep chaste, the subject of their compulsion by the masters does not exist due to their own consent to it. So understand this.
