His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ الْعَنَتَ مِنْكُمْ "That is for those of you who fear falling into sin." The term العنت (distress) refers to hardship, severity, and ruin. What is intended here is adultery (الزنا), which is the result of a person falling into the hardship of intense lust (الشبق) and the strain of the desire for marriage, in which lies the ruin of man. The reference [in "that"], as has been said, is to the marriage of slave girls mentioned in the verse. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: وَأَنْ تَصْبِرُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ "And that you be patient is better for you" is that being patient and refraining from marrying female slaves, or refraining from adultery, is better for you. It is also possible that "that" refers to the obligation of marrying female slaves or the obligation of marriage in general, if either could be inferred from the preceding context of the verse; and Allah knows best.

In any case, the fact that patience is better—if the intended meaning is patience from marrying female slaves—is due to the rights of their masters and the status of their children, as detailed in jurisprudence (الفقه). If the intended meaning is patience from adultery, it is because patience involves the refinement of the soul (تهذيب النفس) and the preparation of the faculty of godwariness (ملكة التقوى) within it by abandoning the following of its whims in adultery, whether without marriage or with it. And Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful; He erases the traces of evil thoughts from the souls of His God-conscious servants through His forgiveness and encompasses them with His mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ "Allah desires to clarify for you" until the end of the verse, is an explanation and an indication of the ultimate purpose for legislating the preceding rulings in the three verses, and the benefits that result from them if acted upon. So His saying: يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ means the rulings of His religion which contain the rectification of your worldly life and your hereafter, and the knowledge and wisdom contained therein. Based on this, the object of His saying: لِيُبَيِّنَ (to clarify) is omitted to indicate the magnificence of its matter and the greatness of its status. It is also possible that His saying: لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ (to clarify for you) and His saying: وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ (and to guide you) are both competing for the object in His saying: سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ (the traditions of those).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ "and to guide you to the traditions of those who were before you" means the ways of life of the previous prophets and righteous nations who conducted themselves in the worldly life according to the pleasure of Allah, thereby attaining the happiness of this world and the hereafter. What is intended by their "traditions" (سنن) in this sense is their traditions in general, not their traditions in all their details and specificities. Thus, it cannot be objected that some of their rulings are precisely what these verses abrogate—such as the marriage of brothers to sisters in the tradition of Adam, or the combining of two sisters in the tradition of Ya‘qub (peace be upon him), as he (peace be upon him) combined the two sisters Leah, the mother of Judah, and Rachel, the mother of Joseph, according to some reports.

There is another meaning that has been suggested: that what is intended is guidance to the traditions of all those who came before, whether they were on the truth or on falsehood. That is: "We have clarified for you all the previous traditions, truth and falsehood alike, so that you may have insight and thus adopt the truth from them and leave the falsehood."

This is a plausible meaning, except that "guidance" (الهداية) in the Qur'an is not typically used in this sense. Rather, it is used to mean "conveying to the truth" or "the will for the truth," as in His saying: إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes" (Al-Qasas: 56), and His saying: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً "Indeed We have guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful" (Al-Insan: 3). It is more in accord with the style of the Qur'an to express such meanings with terms like "clarification" (التبيين) and "narrating" (القصص) and the like.

However, if His saying "clarifies" and His saying وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ (and guides you) are made to compete for His saying: سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ (the traditions of those before you), and His saying وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ (and turns to you in forgiveness) also refers back to it, the meaning would result in: Allah clarifies for you the traditions of those before you, guides you to the truth among them, and turns to you in forgiveness regarding the falsehood of them with which you were afflicted. This would be a valid interpretation, as the preceding verses mention the traditions of those who came before, both the truth and the falsehood among them, and the turning in forgiveness for the false traditions of the past.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ "and turn to you in forgiveness, and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." The توبة (turning in forgiveness) mentioned here is His returning to His servant with blessing and mercy, the legislation of the Sharia, the clarification of the truth, and the guidance to the straight path—all of this is a "turning" (توبة) from Him, the Glorified, just as the acceptance of the servant's repentance and the removal of the traces of disobedience is a "turning."

The conclusion of the speech with His saying: وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise) is so that it refers back to all the segments of the verse. Had the intention been for it to refer only to the last segment, it would have been more appropriate, outwardly, to say: وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (and Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ الَّذِينَ "And Allah desires to turn to you in forgiveness, while those who follow their lusts desire..." etc. The repetition of the mention of His turning in forgiveness to the believers is to indicate that His saying: وَيُرِيدُ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الشَّهَواتِ أَنْ تَمِيلُوا مَيْلاً عَظِيماً "while those who follow their lusts desire that you should deviate with a great deviation" only contrasts with the last of the three segments in the previous verse. For if His saying: وَيُرِيدُ الَّذِينَ "while those who follow..." were joined to the previous verse without the repetition of His saying: وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ "And Allah desires...", it would have implied a contrast with the meaning of all the segments, and the meaning would certainly have been lost.

What is intended by the "great deviation" (الميل العظيم) is the violation of these divine limits mentioned in the verses by committing forbidden acts, nullifying the effects of lineage and causes, permitting adultery, and preventing the adherence to the upright tradition that Allah has established.

His saying, the Exalted: يُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُخَفِّفَ عَنْكُمْ وَخُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ ضَعِيفاً "Allah desires to lighten your burden, and man was created weak." The fact that man is weak (ضعيفاً) is because Allah has instilled in him the appetitive powers (القوى الشهوية) which constantly contend with him regarding the objects of desire to which they are attached, and urge him to indulge in them. Thus, Allah bestowed His favor upon them by legislating the lawfulness of that which breaks the intensity of their lust through the permission of marriage, whereby the distress of hardship is removed, as He said: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ "and lawful for you are those beyond these" (An-Nisa: 24), which refers to marriage and "what your right hands possess." Thus, He guided them by that to the traditions of those before them, and increased the lightening of the burden for them by legislating temporary marriage (نكاح المتعة), as it does not carry the costs of permanent marriage and the heavy obligations that follow it, such as the dower (صداق), maintenance (نفقة), and other such things.

It has been said that what is intended here is the permission to marry female slaves in case of necessity as a lightening of the burden. However, this is countered by the fact that marrying female slaves in case of necessity was practiced among them before Islam, albeit with dislike and blame. What these verses innovated was the means to negate this dislike and aversion by clarifying that the female slave, like the free woman, is a human being—there is no difference between them—and that slavery (الرقية) does not necessitate the loss of the person's worthiness for companionship and social interaction.

The outward meaning of the verses—which cannot be denied—is that the address in them is directed toward the believers of this Ummah. Therefore, the lightening mentioned in the verse is a lightening for this Ummah, and what is intended by it is what we have mentioned.

Accordingly, the justification of the lightening with His saying: وَخُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ ضَعِيفاً "and man was created weak"—despite it being a common description among all nations, this Ummah and those before them—while the lightening is specific to this Ummah, is of the nature of mentioning the general requirement (المقتضي العام) while remaining silent about that which completes its effect. It is as if it were said: "We have lightened the burden for you because the general weakness in the human species is a cause requiring lightening were it not for an impediment; however, impediments continued to prevent the actualization of the lightening and the expansion of mercy in other nations until the turn reached you, so the mercy encompassed you and its effects appeared in you. Thus, the ruling of the aforementioned cause emerged, and the ruling of lightening was legislated for you, whereas the previous nations were deprived of that," as indicated by His saying: رَبَّنا وَلا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنا إِصْراً كَما حَمَلْتَهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِنا "Our Lord, do not lay upon us a burden as You laid it upon those before us" (Al-Baqarah: 286), and His saying: هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ "He has chosen you and has not placed any hardship for you in religion" (Al-Hajj: 78).

From here it becomes clear that the subtle point in this general justification is the clarification of the manifestation of the completion of human blessings in this Ummah.

(Narrative Discussion)

From the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): إن الله حرم من الرضاعة ما حرم من النسب — "Indeed, Allah has forbidden through fosterage (الرضاعة) what He has forbidden through lineage (النسب)." And from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): الرضاع لحمة كلحمة النسب — "Fosterage is a kinship like the kinship of lineage."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Malik and Abd al-Razzaq narrated from Aisha that she said: "Among what was revealed of the Qur'an were ten known fosterings—then they were abrogated by five known ones—then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) passed away while they were among what was recited of the Qur'an."

I say: It is narrated therein from her something similar through other paths, and these are among the narrations of alteration (التحريف) which are rejected due to their contradiction with the Book.

In it, Abd al-Razzaq, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated through two paths from Amr ibn Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: إذا نكح الرجل المرأة ـ فلا يحل له أن يتزوج أمها دخل بالابنة أو لم يدخل ، وإذا تزوج الأم فلم يدخل بها ـ ثم طلقها فإن شاء تزوج الابنة — "If a man marries a woman, it is not lawful for him to marry her mother, whether he has consummated the marriage with the daughter or not. But if he marries the mother and does not consummate the marriage with her, then divorces her, he may marry the daughter if he wishes."

I say: This meaning is narrated through Shi'i paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), and it is their school of thought, and it is what is inferred from the Book as mentioned in the previous commentary.

It has been narrated through Sunni paths from Ali (عليه السلام) that there is no harm in marrying the mother of the wife before consummating the marriage with the daughter, and that she is in the position of the stepdaughter (الربيبة); and that the stepdaughter, if she is not under the care of her mother's husband, her marriage is not forbidden to him. These are matters refuted by what is narrated from them (عليه السلام) through Shi'i paths.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Mansur ibn Hazim who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) when a man came and asked him about a man who married a woman and she died before he consummated the marriage with her—could he marry her mother? Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: 'A man from among us did that and did not see any harm in it.' I said: 'May I be your ransom, the Shi'a only take pride in the judgment of Ali (عليه السلام) regarding this in the Mashyakha (1) where Ibn Mas'ud gave a fatwa that there was no harm in it.'"

(1) Perhaps the correct word is al-Shamkhiyya, as in some Sunni reports it was a man from Banu Shamkh, or the correct is "regarding the Shamkhiyya woman" about whom Ibn Mas'ud gave the fatwa.

"Then he came to Ali (عليه السلام) and asked him, and Ali (عليه السلام) said to him: 'From where does he take it?' He said: 'From the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَرَبائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِكُمُ اللَّاتِي دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ فَإِنْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا دَخَلْتُمْ بِهِنَّ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ "and your stepdaughters who are under your care [born] of your wives whom you have gone into—but if you have not gone into them, there is no sin upon you" (An-Nisa: 23).' Ali (عليه السلام) said: 'Indeed, this one is excepted [conditioned] and that one is absolute (مرسلة).’ Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) then said to the man: 'Do you not hear what this one narrates from Ali (عليه السلام)?'"

"When I stood up, I regretted it and said: 'What have I done? He says: "A man from among us did it and saw no harm in it," and I say: "Ali (عليه السلام) judged regarding it!"' I met him after that and said: 'May I be your ransom, the matter of the man—what I said was a slip from me, so what do you say regarding it?' He said: 'O Shaykh, you tell me that Ali (عليه السلام) judged regarding it, and then you ask me what I say regarding it?'"

I say: The story of his judgment (عليه السلام) regarding the fatwa of Ibn Mas'ud, as narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from the Sunan of al-Bayhaqi and others, is that a man from Banu Shamkh married a woman and did not consummate the marriage with her—then he saw her mother and she pleased him—so he sought a fatwa from Ibn Mas'ud, who ordered him to leave her and then marry her mother. He did so and she bore him children. Then Ibn Mas'ud came to Medina and was told: "It is not permissible." When he returned to Kufa, he said to the man: "She is forbidden to you, so leave her."

However, the saying was not attributed therein to Ali (عليه السلام); rather it mentioned that he asked the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about it. In one wording: that he asked Umar about it. In some narrations: he was informed that it is not as he said, and that the condition is only regarding stepdaughters.

In al-Istibsar, with its chain to Ishaq ibn Ammar from Ja'far from his father: "That Ali (عليه السلام) used to say: 'Stepdaughters are forbidden to you along with the mothers with whom you have consummated the marriage—whether in your care or not, it is the same. And mothers [of wives] are absolute (مبهمات), whether he has consummated with the daughters or not; so forbid and leave absolute what Allah has left absolute.'"

I say: It has been attributed to him (عليه السلام) in some narrations through Sunni paths the conditioning of being "under care" (الحجور) for the prohibition of stepdaughters, but the narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) refute this, and it is what accords with what is inferred from the verse as previously mentioned.

The مبهمات (absolute/unconditioned) is from al-buhma, which is for a thing to be of a single color not mixed with another color and not varying in its color. By this term, those categories of forbidden women whose prohibition of marriage is absolute and unconditioned are named: they are mothers, daughters, sisters, paternal aunts, maternal aunts, brother's daughters, sister's daughters, those through fosterage, mothers of wives, and the wives of sons.

In it, with its chain to Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "I asked him about a man who has a slave girl and has relations with her—is it permissible for him to marry her daughter? He said: 'No, she is as Allah the Exalted said: وَرَبائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُمْ "and your stepdaughters who are under your care."'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Awn who said: "I heard Abu Salih al-Hanafi say: 'Ali (عليه السلام) said one day: "Ask me." Ibn al-Kawwa said: "Inform me about the daughter of a sister through fosterage, and about two slave girls who are sisters." He said: "Indeed, you are wandering in a wilderness; ask about what concerns you or benefits you." Ibn al-Kawwa said: "We only ask you about what we do not know; as for what we know, we do not ask you about it." Then he said: "As for the two slave sisters, one verse made them lawful and another verse made them forbidden; I do not make it lawful nor do I make it forbidden, and I do not do it, nor does anyone from my household."'"

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Mu'ammar ibn Yahya ibn Salim who said: "We asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about what people narrate from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) regarding things he would neither command nor forbid except for himself and his children. I said: 'How can that be?' He said: 'One verse made it lawful and another verse made it forbidden.' We said: 'Is it that one of them abrogated the other, or are they both decisive (محكمتان) and should be acted upon?' He said: 'He clarified for them when he forbade himself and his children.' We said: 'What prevented him from clarifying that to the people?' He said: 'He feared he would not be obeyed; for if the Commander of the Faithful's feet had been firmly established, he would have established the entire Book of Allah and the entire Truth.'"

I say: The narration reported from him (عليه السلام) is the same as what was reported from him through Sunni paths, as narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi and others from Ali ibn Abi Talib who said regarding two slave sisters: "One verse made them lawful and another verse made them forbidden; I neither command nor forbid, I neither make lawful nor make forbidden, and I do not do it, nor does my household."

It is also narrated therein from Qabisah ibn Dhu'ayb: "That a man asked him (عليه السلام) about that, and he said: 'If any part of the matter were up to me, and I found someone doing that, I would make him an example [of punishment].'"

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) say: 'If a person has two slave sisters and has relations with one of them, then he wishes to have relations with the second, it is not appropriate for him to have relations with the other until the first leaves his ownership—he either gifts her or sells her. If he gifts her to his son, it suffices him.'"

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ "and married women, except what your right hands possess" (An-Nisa: 24). He said: 'It is when a man commands his slave who is married to his slave girl, saying to him: "Withdraw from your wife and do not approach her," then he keeps her from him until she menstruates, then he touches her. When she menstruates after his touching her, he returns her to him without a [new] marriage.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ibn Muskan from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams] (عليهما السلام) regarding the saying of Allah: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ. He said: 'They are those who have husbands, except what your right hands possess—if you married your slave girl to your young slave, you may take her from him whenever you wish.' I said: 'What if he married her to someone other than his slave?' He said: 'He has no right to take her until she is sold. If he sells her, her private parts come into the hand of another; if the buyer wishes, he separates them, and if he wishes, he lets them remain.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi—who graded it hasan—and Ibn Majah narrated from Fayruz al-Daylami: "That he embraced Islam while he was married to two sisters. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to him: 'Divorce whichever of them you wish.'"

In it, Ibn Abd al-Barr narrated in al-Istidhkar from Iyas ibn Amir who said: "I asked Ali ibn Abi Talib, saying: 'I have two sisters among what my right hand possesses; I took one of them as a concubine and she bore me children, then I desired the other, so what should I do?' He said: 'Manumit the one you were having relations with, then you may have relations with the other.'"

Then he said: "Indeed, what is forbidden to you from what your right hand possesses is what is forbidden to you in the Book of Allah from free women, except for the number—or he said: except for four. And what is forbidden to you through fosterage is what is forbidden to you in the Book of Allah through lineage."

I say: It was narrated through other paths besides this one from him.

In the Sahihs of al-Bukhari and Muslim, from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'A woman and her paternal aunt shall not be combined [in marriage], nor a woman and her maternal aunt.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated through paths other than these two from Sunni paths, but what is narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt is contrary to that, and the Book supports it.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tayalisi, Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tahawi, Ibn Hibban, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent an army on the day of Hunayn to Awtas. They met an enemy and fought them, prevailing over them and taking captives. Some of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) felt uneasy about having relations with them because of their polytheist husbands. So Allah revealed regarding that: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ "and married women, except what your right hands possess," meaning: except what Allah has given you as spoils. Thus, we deemed their private parts lawful by that."

I say: This was also narrated from al-Tabarani from Ibn Abbas.

In it, Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Ikrimah: "That this verse in Surah an-Nisa: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ إِلَّا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ was revealed regarding a woman called Mu'adhah. She was married to an old man from Banu Sadus called Shuja' ibn al-Harith. She had a co-wife who had borne Shuja' male children. Shuja' went to bring provisions for his family from Hajar. A cousin of Mu'adhah passed by her and she said to him: 'Carry me to my family, for there is no good in this old man.' He carried her and went with her. This coincided with the old man's return. He went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah and the best of the Arabs, I went out seeking food for her in Rajab, but she turned away and acted deceitfully... she saw a young man sitting on a saddle, and she and he had a desire.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Ali, Ali! If the man has uncovered a garment with her, then stone her; otherwise, return the wife to the old man.' Malik ibn Shuja' and the son of her co-wife went and sought her, brought her back, and she stayed in her house."

I say: It has been mentioned repeatedly that such reported occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), especially those related to parts and segments of verses, are applications (تطبيقات) by the narrators and not actual causes.

In al-Faqih, al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ النِّساءِ. He said: "They are those who have husbands." It was asked: "وَالْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ" (and the chaste women from those who were given the Book before you). He said: "They are the chaste ones (العفائف)."

I say: Al-Ayyashi also narrated it from him (عليه السلام).

In al-Majma', regarding His saying: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً (And whoever among you cannot afford)—meaning whoever among you does not find wealth. He said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام)."

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said: "It is not appropriate for a free man to marry a slave girl today; that was only when Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ مِنْكُمْ طَوْلاً. And الطول (affordance) is the dower (المهر), and the dower of a free woman today is the dower of a slave girl or less."

I say: Wealth is one of the instances of "affordance" (الطول) as previously mentioned, and the narration does not indicate more than dislike (الكراهة).

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Abu al-Abbas al-Baqbaq who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): 'Can a man marry a slave girl without the knowledge of her people?' He said: 'That is adultery (زنا); indeed Allah the Exalted says: فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ "so marry them with the permission of their people."'"

In it, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr who said: "I asked al-Rida (عليه السلام): 'Can one perform mut'ah with a slave girl with the permission of her people?' He said: 'Yes, indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: فَانْكِحُوهُنَّ بِإِذْنِ أَهْلِهِنَّ.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from one of the two [Imams] (عليهما السلام) who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah regarding slave girls: فَإِذا أُحْصِنَّ (Then when they are fortified)—what is their fortification? He said: 'He consummates with them.' I said: 'If he has not consummated with them, is there no prescribed punishment (حد) upon them?' He said: 'Yes [there is].'"

In it, from Hariz who said: "I asked him about the fortified one (المحصن), and he said: 'The one who has that which suffices him.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Qays from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) judged regarding male and female slaves that if one of them commits adultery, they are to be lashed fifty lashes—whether they are Muslim, disbeliever, or Christian—and they are neither stoned nor exiled."

In it, with its chain to Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding a slave who slanders a free person. He said: "He is lashed eighty [lashes]; this is from the rights of the people. As for what is from the rights of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, he is struck half the punishment."

I said: "What are the rights of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic?" He said: "If he commits adultery or drinks wine; these are among the rights for which half the punishment is struck."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Burayd al-Ijli from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding a slave girl who commits adultery. He said: "She is lashed half the punishment, whether she had a husband or not."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The مسافحات (openly lewd) are those who are public with adultery, and those who take أخدان (secret lovers) are those who have a single secret companion. The people of the Jahiliyya used to forbid what was public of adultery and permit what was hidden, saying: 'As for what is public, it is meanness; as for what is hidden, there is no harm in it.' So Allah revealed: وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ 'Do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward' (Al-An'am: 151)."

I say: The narrations regarding the preceding meanings are numerous; we have limited ourselves to a small sample.

(Another Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about mut'ah (temporary marriage). He said: 'It was revealed in the Qur'an: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً وَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما تَراضَيْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْفَرِيضَةِ "For those of them whose company you have enjoyed, give them their dowries as an obligation; and there is no sin upon you in what you may agree among yourselves after the obligation" (An-Nisa: 24).'"

In it, with its chain to Ibn Abi Umayr from someone who mentioned it from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "It was revealed: 'For those of them whose company you have enjoyed until a specified term (إلى أجل مسمى), give them their dowries as an obligation.'"

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated this recitation from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), and the general mass of scholars (الجمهور) narrated it through numerous paths from Ubayy ibn Ka'b and Abdullah ibn Abbas, as will follow. Perhaps what is intended by such narrations is to indicate the intended meaning of the verse rather than the literal revelation.

In it, with its chain to Zurarah who said: "Abdullah ibn Umayr al-Laythi came to Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) and said to him: 'What do you say regarding the mut'ah of women?' He said: 'Allah made it lawful in His Book and upon the tongue of His Prophet; so it is lawful until the Day of Resurrection.' He said: 'O Abu Ja'far, one like you says this, while Umar forbade it and prohibited it?'

He said: 'Even if he did so.' He said: 'I seek refuge for you with Allah from that—that you make lawful something that Umar made forbidden.'

He said: 'Then you are upon the saying of your companion, and I am upon the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Come, let us invoke the curse of Allah upon each other (ملاعنة) that the truth is what the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said, and that falsehood is what your companion said.' Abdullah ibn Umayr then approached and said: 'Would it please you that your women, daughters, sisters, and cousins do so?' Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) turned away from him when he mentioned his women and cousins."

In it, with its chain to Abu Maryam from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "Mut'ah was revealed in the Qur'an and the Sunnah was established regarding it by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In it, with its chain to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah who said: "I heard Abu Hanifa asking Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) about mut'ah. He said: 'Which of the two mut'ahs are you asking about?' He said: 'I asked you about the mut'ah of Hajj—so inform me about the mut'ah of women: is it true?' He said: 'Glory be to Allah! Have you not read the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً?' He said: 'By Allah, it is as if it were a verse I have never read before.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "Jabir ibn Abdullah said regarding the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that they went on a campaign with him and he made mut'ah lawful for them and did not forbid it. Ali used to say: 'Had it not been for what Ibn al-Khattab—meaning Umar—preceded me in, none would commit adultery except a wretched person (1).' Ibn Abbas used to say: 'For those of them whose company you have enjoyed until a specified term, give them their dowries as an obligation,' and these people disbelieve in it, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) made it lawful and did not forbid it."

(1) In one version: "except the most wretched."

In it, from Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding mut'ah. He said: "This verse was revealed: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً وَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما تَراضَيْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْفَرِيضَةِ. He said: 'There is no harm in you increasing her [dower] and her increasing you [in time] when the term between you ends.' He says: 'I seek your lawfulness for another term with her consent.' And she is not lawful for another until her waiting period (العدة) expires, and her waiting period is two menstruations."

From al-Shaybani, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيما تَراضَيْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْفَرِيضَةِ. From Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليهما السلام) that they both said: "It is for him to increase her in the dower, and for her to increase him in the term."

I say: The narrations regarding the preceding meanings are widely transmitted (مستفيضة) or mutawatir (متواترة) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). We have only cited a portion of them, and whoever wishes to see them all should refer to the compendiums of Hadith. (1)

(1) Reports regarding the recitation: "until a specified term."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Mut'ah of women was in the beginning of Islam. A man would arrive in a town where he had no one to manage his affairs or guard his property, so he would marry a woman for the duration he expected to finish his need, so she would look after his property and manage his affairs. He used to recite: 'For those of them whose company you have enjoyed until a specified term.' It was abrogated by: مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ 'being chaste and not lewd' (An-Nisa: 24). And the 'fortification' (الإحصان) was in the hand of the man; he could keep her whenever he wished and divorce her whenever he wished."

In Mustadrak al-Hakim, with its chain to Abu Nadrah who said: "I recited to Ibn Abbas: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً. Ibn Abbas said: 'For those of them whose company you have enjoyed until a specified term.' I said: 'We do not recite it like that.' Ibn Abbas said: 'By Allah, Allah revealed it like that.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from him and from Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir narrated from Qatadah who said: "In the recitation of Ubayy ibn Ka'b: 'For those of them whose company you have enjoyed until a specified term.'"

In the Sahih of al-Tirmidhi, from Muhammad ibn Ka'b from Ibn Abbas who said: "Mut'ah was only in the beginning of Islam. A man would arrive in a town where he had no acquaintance, so he would marry a woman for the duration he expected to stay, so she would guard his property and manage his things. Until the verse was revealed: إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ 'except from their spouses or what their right hands possess' (Al-Mu'minun: 6). Ibn Abbas said: 'So every private part besides these two is forbidden.'"

I say: The implication of the report is that it was abrogated in Mecca because the verse is Meccan.

In Mustadrak al-Hakim, from Abdullah ibn Abi Mulaykah: "I asked Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) about the mut'ah of women. She said: 'Between me and you is the Book of Allah.' He said: 'And she recited this verse: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ "and who guard their private parts, (5) except from their spouses or what their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy" (Al-Mu'minun: 5-6). So whoever seeks beyond what Allah has married to him or what he possesses has transgressed.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, (1) Abu Dawud in his al-Nasikh, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Nahhas narrated through the path of Ata from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً. He said: "It was abrogated by: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ 'O Prophet! When you divorce women, divorce them at [the onset of] their waiting period' (At-Talaq: 1), and الْمُطَلَّقاتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنْفُسِهِنَّ ثَلاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ 'Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity' (Al-Baqarah: 228), and اللَّائِي يَئِسْنَ مِنَ الْمَحِيضِ مِنْ نِسائِكُمْ إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ فَعِدَّتُهُنَّ ثَلاثَةُ أَشْهُرٍ 'As for those of your women who have passed the age of menstruation, if you are in doubt, their waiting period shall be three months' (At-Talaq: 4)."

(1) A group of reports indicating the abrogation of the verse of mut'ah by the Book.

In it, Abu Dawud in his al-Nasikh, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Nahhas, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib who said: "The verse of inheritance abrogated mut'ah."

In it, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ibn Mas'ud who said: "Mut'ah is abrogated; it was abrogated by divorce, charity (alms), the waiting period, and inheritance."

In it, Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ali who said: "Ramadan abrogated every fast, and Zakat abrogated every charity, and divorce, the waiting period, and inheritance abrogated mut'ah, and the sacrifice (الضحية) abrogated every slaughter."

In it, Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, and Muslim narrated from Sabrah al-Juhani (2) who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) permitted us the mut'ah of women in the year of the conquest of Mecca. I and a man from my people went out; I had an advantage over him in beauty, and he was close to being ugly. Each of us had a cloak; my cloak was worn out, while my cousin's cloak was new and fresh. When we were in the upper part of Mecca, a young girl like a long-necked young camel met us. We said: 'Would you like one of us to perform mut'ah with you?' She said: 'What will you two offer?' Each of us spread his cloak. She began looking at the two men. When my companion saw her, he said: 'This one's cloak is worn out, and my cloak is new and fresh.' She said: 'And this one's cloak is not bad.' Then I performed mut'ah with her. We did not leave until the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade it."

(2) A group of reports indicating the abrogation of mut'ah by the Sunnah.

In it, Malik, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Majah narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib: "That the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade the mut'ah of women on the day of Khaybar, and the eating of the meat of domestic donkeys."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and Muslim narrated from Salamah ibn al-Akwa' who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) granted us a concession in the mut'ah of women in the year of Awtas for three days—then he forbade it after that."

In the commentary of Ibn al-Arabi on the Sahih of al-Tirmidhi, from Isma'il from his father from al-Zuhri: "That Sabrah narrated that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade it in the Farewell Pilgrimage." Abu Dawud narrated it and said: "Abd al-Aziz ibn Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz narrated it from al-Rabi' ibn Sabrah from his father: he mentioned therein that it was in the Farewell Pilgrimage after the ihlal (de-consecration), and that it was for a known term." Al-Hasan said: "It was in the Umrah of Fulfillment (عمرة القضاء)."

In it, from al-Zuhri: "That the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) combined mut'ah in the campaign of Tabuk."

I say: The narrations, as you see, differ in identifying the time of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibition: some say it was before the Hijra; some say it was after the Hijra by the revelation of the verses of marriage, divorce, the waiting period, and inheritance; or by the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibition in the year of Khaybar; or the time of the Umrah of Fulfillment; or the year of Awtas; or the year of the Conquest; or the year of Tabuk; or after the Farewell Pilgrimage. Therefore, it has been interpreted as the prohibition being repeated numerous times, and that each of the narrations speaks of one of those times. However, the majesty of some of its narrators like Ali, Jabir, and Ibn Mas'ud—along with their constant accompaniment of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and their expertise in the significant and minor details of his biography—makes it unlikely that his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibitions would be hidden from them.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ali who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade mut'ah; it was only for those who did not find [means]. When marriage, divorce, the waiting period, and inheritance between husband and wife were revealed, it was abrogated."

In it, al-Nahhas narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he said to Ibn Abbas: "You are a man wandering in a wilderness; indeed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade mut'ah."

In it, al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abu Dharr who said: "Mut'ah was only made lawful for the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for three days—then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade it."

In the Sahih of al-Bukhari, from Abu Jamrah who said: "Ibn Abbas was asked about the mut'ah of women and he granted a concession for it. A client (مولى) of his said to him: 'That was only when women were few and the situation was severe.' Ibn Abbas said: 'Yes.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bayhaqi narrated from Umar that he gave a sermon and said: "What is the matter with men who marry this mut'ah, while the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has forbidden it? No one who has married it shall be brought to me except that I will stone him."

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and Muslim narrated from Sabrah who said: "I saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) standing between the Corner and the Door saying: 'O people, I had permitted you in enjoyment (istimta'); behold, Allah has forbidden it until the Day of Resurrection. So whoever has anything of them with him, let him release her, and do not take anything from what you have given them.'"

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from al-Hasan who said: "By Allah, mut'ah was only for three days—the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) permitted it for them; it was not before that nor after." (1)

(1) A group of reports indicating the saying of some companions, successors, and exegetes regarding the permissibility of mut'ah.

In the Tafsir of al-Tabari, from Mujahid: "فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ" (For those of them whose company you have enjoyed)—he said: "It means the marriage of mut'ah."

In it, from al-Suddi regarding the verse: "This is mut'ah: a man marries a woman with a condition until a specified term. When the period ends, he has no way over her, and she is free from him, and she must ensure the emptiness of her womb (تستبرئ). There is no inheritance between them; neither of them inherits from the other."

In the Sahihs of al-Bukhari and Muslim, and narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Abi Shaybah from Ibn Mas'ud who said: "We used to go on campaigns with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and our women were not with us. We said: 'Should we not castrate ourselves?' He forbade us from that and granted us a concession to marry a woman for a cloak until a term. Then Abdullah recited: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ 'O you who have faith! Do not forbid the good things that Allah has made lawful to you' (Al-Ma'idah: 87)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Nafi' that Ibn Umar was asked about mut'ah and he said: "It is forbidden." It was said to him: "Indeed Ibn Abbas gives fatwas for it." He said: "Why did he not speak out about it during the time of Umar?"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and al-Bayhaqi narrated through the path of Sa'id ibn Jubayr who said: "I said to Ibn Abbas: 'What have you done? The travelers have gone with your fatwa, and the poets have spoken regarding it.' He said: 'And what did they say?' I said: 'They said:

I say to the Shaykh when his sitting lasted long: O companion, do you have interest in the fatwa of Ibn Abbas? Do you have interest in a concession of the limbs, a friendly one, who will be your dwelling until the departure of the people?'

He said: 'إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ (Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return). No, by Allah, I did not give a fatwa with this, nor did I intend this. I did not make it lawful except for the one in dire necessity (المضطر), and I did not make lawful of it except what Allah made lawful of carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine.'"

In it, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated through the path of Ammar, the client of al-Sharid, who said: "I asked Ibn Abbas about mut'ah: is it lewdness (سفاح) or marriage (نكاح)? He said: 'Neither lewdness nor marriage.' I said: 'Then what is it?' He said: 'It is mut'ah as Allah said.' I said: 'Does it have a waiting period?' He said: 'Its waiting period is one menstruation.' I said: 'Do they inherit from each other?' He said: 'No.'"

In it, Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated through the path of Ata from Ibn Abbas who said: "May Allah have mercy on Umar; mut'ah was nothing but a mercy from Allah with which He showed mercy to the Ummah of Muhammad. Had it not been for his prohibition of it, none would have needed adultery except a wretched person. He said: 'It is that which is in Surah an-Nisa: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ until such and such—of the term for such and such.' He said: 'There is no inheritance between them. If it seems good to them to agree after the term, then yes; and if they separate, then yes. There is no marriage between them.' And he informed that he heard Ibn Abbas: that he views it now as lawful."

In the Tafsir of al-Tabari, and narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abd al-Razzaq and Abu Dawud in his al-Nasikh from al-Hakam: "That he was asked about this verse: is it abrogated? He said: 'No.' And Ali said: 'Had it not been that Umar forbade mut'ah, none would commit adultery except a wretched person.'" (1)

(1) A group of reports indicating Umar's prohibition of mut'ah.

In Sahih Muslim, from Jabir ibn Abdullah who said: "We used to perform mut'ah for a handful of dates and flour for days during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Abu Bakr, until Umar forbade it in the matter of Amr ibn Hurayth."

I say: This was also transmitted from Jami' al-Usul by Ibn al-Athir, Zad al-Ma'ad by Ibn al-Qayyim, Fath al-Bari by Ibn Hajar, and Kanz al-Ummal.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Malik and Abd al-Razzaq narrated from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr that Khawlah bint Hakim entered upon Umar ibn al-Khattab and said: "Indeed Rabi'ah ibn Umayr performed mut'ah with a woman of mixed parentage and she became pregnant from him." Umar ibn al-Khattab went out dragging his cloak in alarm, saying: "This is mut'ah; had I preceded [with a prohibition] regarding it, I would have stoned [him]."

I say: This was also transmitted from al-Shafi'i in the book al-Umm and al-Bayhaqi in al-Sunan al-Kubra.

From Kanz al-Ummal, from Sulayman ibn Yasar from Umm Abdullah daughter of Abi Khaythama: "That a man arrived from Syria and stayed with her. He said: 'Indeed celibacy has become severe for me—so find me a woman to perform mut'ah with.' She said: 'I directed him to a woman—he made a contract with her and they had just witnesses testify to that. He stayed with her for as long as Allah willed him to stay. Then he left. Umar ibn al-Khattab was informed of that. He sent for me and asked me: "Is what I have been told true?" I said: "Yes." He said: "When he arrives, inform me." When he arrived, I informed him. He sent for him and said: "What carried you to do what you did?" He said: "I did it with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—then he did not forbid us from it until Allah took him—then with Abu Bakr and he did not forbid us from it until Allah took him—then with you and you did not innovate any prohibition for us regarding it." Umar said: "By the One in Whose hand is my soul—had I preceded with a prohibition, I would have stoned you. Make it clear so that marriage is distinguished from lewdness."'"

In Sahih Muslim and Musnad Ahmad, from Ata: "Jabir ibn Abdullah arrived for Umrah, so we came to him at his house. The people asked him about things, then they mentioned mut'ah. He said: 'We performed mut'ah during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), Abu Bakr, and Umar.' In the wording of Ahmad: 'Until it was in the end of the caliphate of Umar (may Allah be pleased with him).'"

From the Sunan of al-Bayhaqi, from Nafi' from Abdullah ibn Umar: "That he was asked about the mut'ah of women. He said: 'It is forbidden. Behold, if Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) had caught anyone in it, he would have stoned him with stones.'"

From Mir'at al-Zaman by Ibn al-Jawzi: "Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) used to say: 'By Allah, no man who permits mut'ah shall be brought to me except that I will stone him.'"

In Bidayat al-Mujtahid by Ibn Rushd, from Jabir ibn Abdullah: "We performed mut'ah during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Abu Bakr—and half of the caliphate of Umar—then Umar forbade the people from it."

In al-Isabah, Ibn al-Kalbi narrated: "That Salamah ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf al-Jumahi performed mut'ah with Salma, the client of Hakim ibn Umayyah ibn al-Awqas al-Aslami. She bore him a child and he denied her child. That reached Umar, so he forbade mut'ah."

From Zad al-Ma'ad, from Ayyub: "Urwah said to Ibn Abbas: 'Do you not fear Allah, granting a concession in mut'ah?' Ibn Abbas said: 'Ask your mother, O Urayyah!' Urwah said: 'As for Abu Bakr and Umar, they did not do it.' Ibn Abbas said: 'By Allah, I do not see you stopping until Allah punishes you! We narrate to you from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and you narrate to us from Abu Bakr and Umar!'"

I say: The mother of Urwah was Asma bint Abi Bakr; al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam performed mut'ah with her and she bore him Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr and Urwah.

In al-Muhadarat by al-Raghib: "Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr taunted Abdullah ibn Abbas for his making mut'ah lawful. He said to him: 'Ask your mother how the incense burners glowed between her and your father?' He asked her and she said: 'I did not give birth to you except in mut'ah.'"

In Sahih Muslim, from Muslim al-Qurri who said: "I asked Ibn Abbas about mut'ah and he granted a concession for it. Ibn al-Zubayr used to forbid it. He said: 'This is the mother of Ibn al-Zubayr narrating that the Messenger of Allah granted a concession for it—so enter upon her and ask her.' He said: 'We entered upon her and she was a large, blind woman. She said: "The Messenger of Allah granted a concession for it."'"

I say: The context of the situation narrated testifies that the question was about the mut'ah of women, and the other narrations also explain it as such.

In Sahih Muslim, from Abu Nadrah who said: "I was with Jabir ibn Abdullah when someone came to him and said: 'Ibn Abbas and Ibn al-Zubayr have differed regarding the two mut'ahs.' Jabir said: 'We performed them both with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), then Umar forbade us from them, so we did not return to them.'"

I say: Al-Bayhaqi narrated it in the Sunan as reported. This meaning was narrated in Sahih Muslim in three places with different wordings. In some of them (Jabir said): "When Umar stood up, he said: 'Indeed Allah used to make lawful for His Messenger whatever He willed by whatever He willed. So complete the Hajj and Umrah as Allah commanded, and cease from the marriage of these women. No man who has married a woman until a term shall be brought to me except that I will stone him.'"

This meaning was narrated by al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan, in Ahkam al-Qur'an by al-Jassas, in Kanz al-Ummal, in al-Durr al-Manthur, in the Tafsir of al-Razi, the Musnad of al-Tayalisi, and in the Tafsir of al-Qurtubi, from Umar: that he said in a sermon: "Two mut'ahs were during the time of the Messenger of Allah (عليه السلام), and I forbid them both and punish for them: the mut'ah of Hajj and the mut'ah of women."

I say: This sermon of his is among those matters upon which the transmitters of reports are in agreement; they have relayed it as an accepted fact, as found in the Tafsir of al-Razi, al-Bayan wa al-Tabyin, Zad al-Ma'ad, Ahkam al-Quran, al-Tabari, Ibn Asakir, and others.

It is narrated in al-Mustabin by al-Tabari, from Umar, that he said: "Three things were present during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which I forbid and for which I shall punish: the mut'ah of Hajj, the mut'ah of women, and [the phrase] 'hasten to the best of deeds' (حي على خير العمل) in the adhan."

In the Tarikh of al-Tabari, from Imran ibn Sawadah, who said: "I prayed the morning prayer with Umar, and he recited Subhan (Surah al-Isra) and another Surah with it. Then he finished, and I stood with him. He asked, 'Do you have a need?' I said, 'A need.' He said, 'Then follow me.' I followed him, and when he entered, he gave me permission to enter. I found him on a bedstead with nothing upon it. I said, 'I have some advice.' He said, 'Welcome to the advisor, morning and evening.' I said, 'Your community finds fault with you in four matters.' He placed the head of his whip against his chin and the bottom of it on his thigh, then said, 'Speak.' I said, 'They mention that you have forbidden the umrah during the months of Hajj, whereas the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not do that, nor did Abu Bakr (رضي الله عنه), and it is lawful.' He said, 'It is lawful? If they were to perform umrah in the months of Hajj, they would see it as sufficient for their Hajj, and it would be like a fledgling leaving its shell in its year, leaving their Hajj barren, while Hajj is a splendor among the splendors of Allah. You have spoken truly.'

I said, 'And they mention that you have forbidden the mut'ah of women, while it was a concession (رخصة) from Allah; we would contract mut'ah for a handful [of grain or dates] and part after three [days].' He said, 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) permitted it in a time of necessity (ضرورة), then the people returned to a state of ease. Thereafter, I did not know of any Muslim who practiced it or returned to it. Now, whoever wishes may marry for a handful and part after three by divorce (طلاق). You have spoken truly.'

He said: I said, 'And you have manumitted the slave-girl if she gives birth to the child of her womb without her master's manumission.' He said, 'I joined one sanctity to another sanctity, and I intended nothing but good, and I seek Allah's forgiveness.' I said, 'And they complain of your harshness toward the subjects and your rough conduct.' He unsheathed his whip and wiped it until he reached its end, then said, 'I was the companion of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)'—and he had accompanied him in the expedition of Qarqarat al-Kadr—'By Allah, I graze them until they are satiated, and I water them until they are quenched. I prod the slow ones, restrain the unruly ones, defend my vessel, pace my steps, gather the straying ones, catch up with the lagging ones, increase the scolding, decrease the striking, brandish the staff, and push with the hand. Were it not for that, I would have been excused.'

He said: This reached Mu'awiyah, who said, 'By Allah, he was knowledgeable regarding their subjects.'"

I say: Ibn Abi al-Hadid also narrated it in Sharh Nahj al-Balaghah from Ibn Qutaybah.

These are several narrations regarding the matter of the mut'ah of women. The contemplative and investigative observer will see the disparity and contradiction within them. The researcher gains nothing from their contents except that Umar ibn al-Khattab, during the days of his caliphate, forbade it and prohibited it based on an opinion he held regarding the stories of Amr ibn Hurayth and Rabi'ah ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf al-Jumahi. As for the report of its abrogation (النسخ) by the Book or the Sunnah, you have already seen that it amounts to nothing substantial. Furthermore, some narrations refute others in all their contents, except for the fact that Umar ibn al-Khattab was the one who prohibited it, enforced the ban, and established its prohibition and the penalty of stoning (حد الرجم) for whoever practiced it—this is the first point.

Secondly, it was a practiced tradition (سنة) during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in general, by his permission—either as an endorsement (إمضاء) or an original legislation (تأسيس). It was practiced by those of his companions for whom fornication (السفاح) is inconceivable, such as Jabir ibn Abdillah, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, and Asma bint Abi Bakr, who gave birth to Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr through it.

Thirdly, among the companions (الصحابة) and the followers (التابعين) were those who held it to be permissible, such as Ibn Mas'ud, Jabir, Amr ibn Hurayth, and others, as well as Mujahid, al-Suddi, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, and others.

This gross discrepancy between the narrations is what led the scholars of the majority (الجمهور)—after their disagreement regarding the origin of its permissibility or prohibition—to disagree regarding the nature of its prohibition and the manner of its ban, leading them to various strange opinions, sometimes reaching fifteen different views.

The issue has several facets of inquiry, but we are only concerned with some of them. There is a theological (كلامي) debate between the two sects, the Sunnis and the Shi'a; another legal (فقهي) branch of inquiry looking at the ruling of the matter in terms of permissibility and prohibition; and another exegetical (تفسيري) inquiry regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries, as an obligation" (Surah al-Nisa 4:24). Does its meaning signify the legislation of mut'ah marriage? And after establishing its indication of that, is it abrogated by any of the verses, such as the verse in al-Mu'minun or the verses of marriage, prohibition, divorce, the waiting period (العدة), and inheritance? Is it abrogated by a Prophetic tradition? And assuming its legislation, was it legislated as an initial ruling (حكماً ابتدائياً) or an endorsement of an existing practice (حكماً إمضائياً)? And so forth.

This third type of inquiry is what we pursue in this book. A summary of the argument has already been presented in the preceding commentary (البيان), and we shall now add further clarification by drawing attention to some of what has been said regarding the verse's indication of mut'ah marriage and its establishment as a tradition, which contradicts what was mentioned in the previous commentary.

One of them said, after insisting that the verse was only revealed to explain the payment of the dower (المهر) in permanent marriage: "The Shi'a have held that the verse refers to mut'ah marriage, which is marrying a woman for a specific term, such as a day, a week, or a month, for example. They argued for this using an anomalous reading (قراءة شاذة) narrated from Ubayy, Ibn Mas'ud, and Ibn Abbas (رضي الله عنهم), and by the reports and traditions narrated regarding mut'ah."

He said: "As for the reading, it is anomalous and not established as Quran. It has been previously stated that what is authentically narrated of this sort as solitary reports (آحاداً), the addition therein is of the nature of interpretation (التفسير); it is the understanding of its author, and the understanding of a companion is not a proof (حجة) in religion, especially when the composition and style reject it, as is the case here. For the one seeking enjoyment through temporary marriage does not intend chastity (الإحصان) rather than profligacy (المسافحة); rather, his primary intention is profligacy. Even if there were some kind of chastity for himself and a restraint from moving about in a state of adultery, there would be no chastity whatsoever for the woman who rents herself out every period of time to a different man, becoming as it is said:

Like a ball struck by mallets, Received by man after man."

I say: As for his claim that they argued for this using the reading of Ibn Mas'ud and others, anyone who refers to their words will see that they did not use it as a definitive, authoritative proof. How could they, when they do not consider anomalous readings to be a proof, even those narrated from their own Imams? How could they argue with what they do not consider a proof against those who also do not consider it a proof? Is this not a mockery?!

Rather, it is an argument based on the statement of those companions who read it as such, insofar as it is a statement from them that this is the intended meaning of the verse—whether that was a technical reading for them or an interpretation indicating that they understood this from the wording of the verse.

This benefits them from two angles: First, that a number of companions held the same view as these interlocutors, and it was held—according to what is narrated—by a vast multitude of the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the followers. One who wishes to verify this may refer to the relevant sources.

Second, that the verse indicates this, and the reading of these companions proves it, just as what is narrated from them regarding the abrogation of the verse also proves that they accepted its indication of mut'ah marriage to the point that they saw it as abrogated or narrated its abrogation. These are numerous narrations, several of which have been mentioned. Thus, the Shi'a benefit from the narrations of abrogation just as they benefit from the aforementioned anomalous reading, without necessarily holding the anomalous reading to be a proof, nor are they required to say that abrogation occurred. Rather, they benefit from all of this in terms of the indication that these reciters and narrators saw the verse as referring to mut'ah marriage.

As for his saying: "especially when the composition and style reject it, as is the case here," his words imply that he defined profligacy (المسافحة) as merely the spilling and pouring of water—taking it from its linguistic root—and then made it a matter contingent upon intention. This forced him to conclude that temporary marriage with the intention of satisfying desire and spilling water is profligacy (سفاح), not marriage (نكاح). He overlooked that the linguistic root of marriage (النكاح) is also sexual intercourse (الوقاع). In Lisan al-Arab, al-Azhari said: "The root of nikah in the speech of the Arabs is coitus (الوطء)." The consequence of his path would be that marriage is also profligacy, which would invalidate the contrast between marriage and profligacy.

Furthermore, the consequence of saying that the intention to spill water makes temporary marriage profligacy is that permanent marriage with the intention of satisfying desire and spilling water would also be profligacy. Would any Muslim man be pleased to issue such a legal opinion? If he says: "There is a difference between permanent and temporary marriage in this regard, for permanent marriage is by its nature established for the purpose of chastity through union, producing offspring, and forming a household, unlike temporary marriage," then this is mere obstinacy. For all the benefits that result from permanent marriage—such as protecting the soul from adultery, guarding against the confusion of lineages, producing offspring and children, and establishing a household—can also result from temporary marriage. It is distinguished by providing a kind of ease and relief for this community, whereby one who cannot afford permanent marriage due to poverty, inability to provide for a wife, being in a foreign land, or other various factors that prevent him from permanent marriage, may protect himself.

Likewise, everything that results from temporary marriage—which he counted as the criterion for profligacy—such as the intention to spill water and satisfy desire, is permissible to occur in permanent marriage. The claim that permanent marriage is by nature established for the aforementioned benefits, while mut'ah marriage is by nature established for these subsequent harms—assuming they are harms—is a patently false claim.

If he says: "Since mut'ah marriage is profligacy, it is adultery (زنا) which contrasts with marriage," the response is: The profligacy (السفاح) which he defined as spilling water is more general than adultery, and it may include permanent marriage, especially if it is with the intention of spilling water.

As for his saying: "Even if there were some kind of chastity for himself, etc.," this is a strange statement. I wonder, what is the distinguishing difference between the man and the woman in this regard, such that the man practicing mut'ah can protect himself from adultery through it, while it is not valid for the woman to have this intention? Is this not mere conjecture?

As for the poetry he recited in a factual inquiry aimed at uncovering a religious truth upon which important, vital, worldly, and otherworldly effects depend—whether mut'ah marriage is prohibited or permissible—what use is poetry? It is a fabric of imagination; falsehood is more familiar to it than truth, and error is closer to it than guidance.

Why did he not recite it following the narrations mentioned earlier, especially following the statement of Umar in the narration of al-Tabari: "Now, whoever wishes may marry for a handful and part after three by divorce"?

Does this criticism have any target other than Allah and His Messenger regarding the original legislation of this type of marriage, whether as an original institution or an endorsement, when it was undoubtedly practiced among Muslims in the early days of Islam, within the sight and hearing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?

If he says: "He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only permitted it due to necessity (الضرورة), such as the widespread poverty and destitution among the general Muslims, and the occurrence of expeditions," as appears from some of the preceding narrations, we say: Given the admission of its prevalence in early Islam among the people and its fame by the name "mut'ah marriage" and "istimta'" (enjoyment), there is no choice but to acknowledge the verse's indication of its permissibility due to its absolute (إطلاق) nature, and the lack of suitability of any verses or narrations to abrogate it. Thus, the claim that its permissibility was lifted is an interpretation (تأول) of the verse's indication without evidence.

Even if we grant that its permissibility was by permission from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for the sake of necessity, we ask: Was this necessity during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) more severe and greater than it was after him, especially during the time of the Rightly Guided Caliphs, when the armies of the Muslims were marching to the east and west of the earth with thousands upon thousands of warriors? What is the difference between the beginning of Umar's caliphate and its end in terms of the transformation of this necessity—from poverty, expeditions, being far from home, and so on? What is the difference between one necessity and another?

Is the necessity that makes it permissible today, in the current state of Islam, more severe and greater, or was it so during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the first half of the era of the Rightly Guided Caliphs? General poverty has cast its shadow over the lands of the Muslims; the colonial governments, the dominant and arrogant powers, and the tyrants among the leaders of the Muslims have sucked every drop of milk from their udders and harvested both the green and the dry of their crops.

Desires have appeared in all their forms, adorned in their finest and most beautiful attire, calling toward their commission with the most eloquent invitation. The matter continues to intensify, and the affliction encompasses the lands and the souls. Lewdness (الفحشاء) has spread among the classes of the educated youth, the soldiers, and the factory workers, who constitute the majority of the human masses and the souls of the inhabited world.

No doubter doubts, nor will they ever doubt, that the necessity driving them into the lewdness of adultery, sodomy, and every lustful abandonment is primarily the inability to provide for a household, and temporary, transient occupations that prevent establishing a home and permanent marriage due to being in a foreign land, service, study, and the like. Why then did these necessities make mut'ah marriage permissible in the early days of Islam—when they were fewer and lighter by comparison—but they do not suffice for permissibility in other than that era, when the affliction has surrounded us and the trial has become great?

Then he said: "Furthermore, it contradicts what is established in the Quran in this sense, such as the saying of the Almighty describing the believers: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ — 'and who guard their private parts, (5) except from their spouses or those whom their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy; (6) but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors' (Surah al-Mu'minun 23:5-7), meaning those who exceed what Allah has made lawful for them toward what He has forbidden them. These verses do not contradict the verse we are interpreting—meaning His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ—rather, they are in the same sense, so there is no abrogation. The woman contracted in mut'ah is not a wife (زوجة), such that she would have rights over the man similar to those he has over her in a fair manner, as Allah the Exalted said. It has been narrated from the Shi'a themselves that they do not give her the rulings of a wife and its requirements; they do not count her among the four whom a man is permitted to combine without fear of injustice; rather, they permit a man to contract mut'ah with many women. They do not say that the adulterer who has practiced mut'ah is to be stoned, as they do not consider him chaste (محصناً). This is a definitive admission from them that the saying of the Exalted regarding those seeking enjoyment: مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ — 'being chaste and not fornicating' (Surah al-Nisa 4:24) does not apply to him. This is a blatant contradiction on their part."

It was narrated from them by some exegetes: "The woman contracted in mut'ah has no inheritance, no maintenance (نفقة), no divorce, and no waiting period. In short, the Quran is far from this view, and there is no evidence in this verse, nor even a semblance of evidence for it at all."

I say: As for his saying: "Furthermore, it contradicts what is established in the Quran in this sense, etc.," its gist is: The verses of al-Mu'minun: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ restrict lawfulness to spouses (الأزواج), and the woman in mut'ah is not a spouse. Thus, the verses prevent the lawfulness of mut'ah first, and prevent the inclusion of His saying: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ for her second.

As for the claim that the verses forbid mut'ah, he has ignored the fact that these verses are Meccan (مكية), while mut'ah was practiced after the Hijrah in general. Was the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) permitting what the Quran had forbidden by his allowance of mut'ah? His (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) word is a proof by the text of the Quran, so this would lead to a contradiction within the Quran itself. Or was his permission an abrogation of the verses of prohibition: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ etc., and then the Quran or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prohibited it again, thereby reviving the verses after their death and strengthening them after their abrogation? This is something he does not say, nor has any Muslim said it, nor can it be said.

This in itself is an excellent witness that the woman in mut'ah is a wife, that mut'ah is a marriage, and that these verses indicate that mut'ah is a form of marrying. Otherwise, it would have had to be abrogated by the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) concession. Thus, the verses are a proof for the permissibility of mut'ah, not its prohibition.

In another formulation: The verses of al-Mu'minun and al-Ma'arij: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ are stronger in their indication of the lawfulness of mut'ah than other verses. It is agreed upon among them that these verses are decisive (محكمة), not abrogated, and they are Meccan. It is necessary according to the reports that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) permitted mut'ah. Were the woman in mut'ah not a wife, the concession would necessarily have been an abrogation of the verses, yet they are not abrogated. Therefore, mut'ah is a legislated spousal relationship. Once the indication of the verses for its legislation is established, what is claimed regarding the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prohibition of it is also invalid, as it would contradict the verses and necessitate their abrogation, while it is known that they are not abrogated by consensus.

In any case, the woman in mut'ah—contrary to what he mentioned—is a wife, and mut'ah is a marriage. It is sufficient for you to note what occurred in the narrations we cited, where it is named in the speech of the companions and the followers as "mut'ah marriage" (نكاح المتعة), even in the speech of Umar ibn al-Khattab in the narrations containing his prohibition, such as the narration of al-Bayhaqi from Umar in his sermon, and the narration of Muslim from Abu Nadrah. Even what occurred from his wording in the narration of Kanz al-Ummal from Sulayman ibn Yasar: "Make it clear so that marriage (النكاح) may be known from profligacy (السفاح)," for its meaning is that mut'ah is a marriage that is not clearly distinguished from profligacy, and that you must distinguish it from it, so bring a marriage that is clear and distinct from it. The evidence for this is his saying: "Make it clear."

In short, the fact that mut'ah is a marriage and that the woman in mut'ah is a wife in the parlance of the Quran and the speech of the predecessors (السلف) among the companions and those who followed them among the followers is something that should not be doubted. The two terms (marriage and marrying) only became restricted to permanent marriage after Umar's prohibition and the cessation of the practice among the people. Thus, no instance remained for the application of the two terms except permanent marriage, so it became the first thing that comes to mind from the word, like other technical religious terms (الحقائق المتشرعة).

From here, the collapse of what he mentioned thereafter becomes apparent. His saying: "It has been narrated from the Shi'a themselves that they do not give her the rulings of a wife and its requirements, etc.," prompts the question: What is meant by "wife"?

As for "wife" in the parlance of the Quran, they do give her its rulings without exception. As for "wife" in the parlance of the practitioners of the law—as mentioned—known in jurisprudence (الفقه), they do not give her its rulings, and there is no problem in that.

As for his saying: "This is a definitive admission from them that the saying of the Exalted: مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ does not apply to him—meaning the adulterer who has practiced mut'ah—and this is a blatant contradiction on their part," the response is that we mentioned in the commentary on the verse previously that its apparent meaning, due to its inclusion of slave-girls (ملك اليمين), is that chastity (الإحصان) refers to the chastity of refraining from forbidden acts (إحصان التعفف), not the chastity of marriage (إحصان الازدواج). Even if we grant that chastity refers to the chastity of marriage, the verse includes mut'ah marriage. As for the non-stoning of the adulterer who has practiced mut'ah (bearing in mind that stoning is not a Quranic ruling), it is only due to an explanation or a specification (تخصيص) from the Sunnah, like other rulings of the spousal relationship such as inheritance, maintenance, divorce, and the waiting period.

To clarify this: if the verses of rulings were intended in a general, non-specific sense (على الإهمال) because they were revealed at the origin of legislation, then the restrictions that occur to them are explanations without being specifications or qualifications. If they were generalities (عمومات) or absolute statements (إطلاقات), then the explanations provided in the Sunnah are specifications (مخصصات) or qualifications (مقيدات) without the problem of contradiction. The reference for this is the science of the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه).

These verses—meaning the verses of inheritance, divorce, and maintenance—like other verses, are not devoid of specification and qualification, such as inheritance and divorce in the case of an apostate woman, divorce upon the appearance of defects that permit the annulment of the contract, and maintenance in the case of recalcitrance (النشوز). Thus, let them be specified by mut'ah. The explanations that exclude mut'ah from the ruling of inheritance, divorce, and maintenance are specifications or qualifications. The restriction of the terms "marrying," "marriage," "chastity," and the like to permanence is from the perspective of technical religious usage (الحقيقة المتشرعة), not the original legislative usage (الحقيقة الشرعية). Thus, there is no problem at all as he imagined. If a jurist says, for example: "The chaste adulterer must be stoned, but there is no stoning for the adulterer who has practiced mut'ah due to his lack of chastity," that is only because he uses "chastity" as a technical term for the permanence of marriage with such-and-such effects. This does not contradict the fact that chastity in the parlance of the Quran exists in both permanent and temporary marriage, and each has its own specific effects.

As for his narration from some that the Shi'a do not hold the waiting period (العدة) in mut'ah, it is a manifest fabrication. Here are the collections of the Shi'a, and here are their books of jurisprudence, filled with the fact that the waiting period for the woman in mut'ah is two menstrual cycles. Some narrations regarding this have already been mentioned through Shi'a chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

Then he said: "As for the hadiths and traditions narrated regarding this, their sum total indicates that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to permit it for his companions in some expeditions, then he forbade them from it, then he permitted it once or twice, then he forbade them from it with an eternal prohibition. The concession was due to the knowledge of the difficulty of avoiding adultery while far from their wives, so it was of the nature of committing the lesser of two evils.
