For if a man contracts a temporary marriage with an unmarried woman and stays with her for that specified time, that is easier than his committing adultery with any woman he can entice."

I say: His claim that the sum total of the narrations indicates permission in some expeditions, then prohibition, then permission once or twice, then an eternal prohibition, does not align with the aforementioned narrations, given the contradictions and conflicts within them. You must refer back to them (most of them have been presented) to see that their sum total belies what he mentioned as a way of reconciliation, letter by letter.

Then he said: "The Sunnis hold that the concession in mut'ah once or twice is close to the gradualism (التدريج) in prohibiting adultery once and for all, just as gradualism occurred in the prohibition of wine. Both lewd acts were widespread in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية), but the prevalence of adultery was among slave-girls rather than free women."

I say: As for his saying that the concession in mut'ah is a type of gradualism in prohibiting adultery, its gist is that mut'ah was, in their view, a type of adultery. It was, like all adultery, widespread in the pre-Islamic period, so the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was gradual in prohibiting adultery with gentleness so that it would be accepted by the people. Thus, he prohibited other types of it and kept the "adultery of mut'ah," permitting it, then prohibiting it, then permitting it, until he was able to prohibit it once and for all, so he prohibited it eternally.

By my life, this is a scandalous playing with the pure religious legislations, by which Allah intended nothing but to purify this community and complete His favor upon them.

Firstly: The attribution of prohibition, then permission, then prohibition, then permission in mut'ah to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—given the insistence of this speaker that the verses of Surah al-Ma'arij and al-Mu'minun: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ (which are Meccan) indicate the prohibition of mut'ah—is nothing other than attributing the abrogation of the verses to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by permission, then the abrogation of this abrogation and the strengthening of the verses, then the abrogation of the verses, then their strengthening, and so on. Is this not attributing a playing with the Book of Allah to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?

Secondly: The verses prohibiting adultery in the Book of Allah the Exalted are His saying in Surah al-Isra: وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنى إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً وَساءَ سَبِيلاً — "Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency and an evil way" (Surah al-Isra 17:32). What language is more explicit than this? The verse is Meccan, situated among the verses of prohibitions. Likewise, His saying: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ —until He said— وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ — "Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: ... and do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward’" (Surah al-An'am 6:151). The word "indecencies" (الفواحش) is a plural with the definite article occurring in the context of prohibition, signifying the encompassing of the prohibition for every indecency and adultery. The verse is Meccan. Likewise, His saying: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward’" (Surah al-A'raf 7:33), and the verse is also Meccan. Likewise, His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ (Surah al-Mu'minun 23:7, Surah al-Ma'arij 70:31). Both Surahs are Meccan, and the verses prohibit mut'ah according to the statement of this speaker, just as they prohibit all other types of adultery.

These are most of the verses prohibiting adultery and forbidding indecency, and all of them are Meccan and explicit in prohibition. Where then is the gradualism in prohibition and banning that he mentioned? Or does he say—as is the explicit necessity of his statement regarding the indication of the verses of al-Mu'minun for prohibition—that Allah the Glorified prohibited it once and for all, then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was gradual in the ban by acting upon concession after concession, compromising for the sake of making it acceptable? Yet Allah the Exalted was severe with His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding this very trait, saying: وَإِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِيَ عَلَيْنا غَيْرَهُ وَإِذاً لَاتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلاً وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً — "Indeed they were about to beguile you from what We have revealed to you, so that you may forge against Us something else, and then they would have taken you for a friend. (73) Had We not fortified you, you were certainly about to incline toward them a little. (74) Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us" (Surah al-Isra 17:73-75).

Thirdly: This concession attributed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) time after time, if it were a concession without a legislation of lawfulness—and the assumption is that mut'ah is adultery and indecency—then that would be an explicit opposition from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to his Lord, were it from himself, while he is protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah the Exalted. If it were from his Lord, it would be a command from Him to commit indecency, yet He refuted that with the explicit words addressed to His Prophet: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ — "Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not command indecency’" (Surah al-A'raf 7:28).

If it were a concession with a legislation of lawfulness, then it would not be adultery or indecency. For it would be a legislated tradition, bounded by firm limits that do not overlap with the prohibited degrees, like permanent marriage. It would involve the obligation of the dower, like permanent marriage, and the waiting period that prevents the mixing of waters and the confusion of lineages. It would be accompanied by the necessity of the people's need for it. What then is the meaning of it being an indecency? Indecency is nothing but a reprehensible act that society finds ugly due to its lack of limits, its disruption of the general interest, and its prevention of fulfilling the necessary needs of society in their lives.

Fourthly: The claim that mut'ah was among the types of adultery prevalent in the pre-Islamic period is a historical fabrication and an invention that has no historical basis. There is no trace of it in history books, nor any sign. Rather, it is an original Islamic tradition and a facilitation from Allah the Exalted for this community to sustain them and protect them from the spread of adultery and other indecencies among them, had they been granted the success to establish this tradition. This is especially true if the Islamic governments did not turn a blind eye to the matter of adultery and other indecencies—an eye-closing that gradually integrated them into legal traditions, filling the world with corruption and woe.

As for his saying: "Both lewd acts were widespread in the pre-Islamic period, but the prevalence of adultery was among slave-girls rather than free women," it appears his meaning by "both lewd acts" is adultery and wine-drinking. This is so, but the claim that adultery was widespread among slave-girls rather than free women has no foundation to rely upon. Various scattered historical evidences support the opposite, such as the poetry composed regarding that. It has already been mentioned in the narration of Ibn Abbas that the people of the pre-Islamic period saw no harm in adultery as long as it was not public.

This is also indicated by the issue of claiming paternity and adoption (التبني) prevalent in the pre-Islamic period. Claiming paternity was not merely a matter of naming and attribution among them; rather, it was a practice through which their powerful men sought to increase their numbers and strength by affiliation. They based this on adultery they committed with free women, even those who were married. As for slave-girls, they—especially the powerful among them—found fault with mixing with them, or having affairs and flirting with them. The role of slave-girls in that regard was only that their masters placed them in that position for the sake of gain and profit.

Evidence for what we have mentioned includes the stories of affiliation (الإلحاق) found in biographies and traditions, such as the story of Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan affiliating Ziyad ibn Abih to his father Abu Sufyan, and the testimony given by the witness of the matter at that time, among other narrated stories.

Yes, one might cite as evidence for the lack of prevalence of adultery among free women in the pre-Islamic period the saying of Hind to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) during the pledge of allegiance: "Does a free woman commit adultery?" However, referring to the Diwan of Hassan and contemplating his lampooning of Hind after the battles of Badr and Uhud removes the confusion and reveals the reality of the matter.

Then he said, after some words of his refining the meaning of the hadiths and removing the contradiction occurring between them according to his claim: "The main points for the Sunnis regarding its prohibition are several: First, what you have learned of its contradiction with the apparent meaning of the Quran regarding the rulings of marriage, divorce, and the waiting period, if we do not say its explicit texts. Second, the hadiths explicitly stating its prohibition as an eternal prohibition until the Day of Resurrection..."—until he said—"Third, Umar's prohibition of it and his indication of its prohibition on the pulpit, and the companions' tacit approval of him in that. It is known that they would not approve of a reprehensible act, and they would correct him if he erred."

Then he chose the view that his prohibition of it was not based on his own independent legal reasoning (اجتهاد), but was based on the prohibition established by the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ban. The prohibition is only attributed to him because he was the one who clarified the prohibition or enforced it, just as it is said: "al-Shafi'i prohibited date-wine and Abu Hanifah permitted it."

I say: As for the first and second points, you have just seen, and in the preceding commentary, the reality of the matter regarding them in a way that requires no addition. As for the third point, Umar's prohibition of it—whether that was through his own reasoning or based on the Prophet's prohibition as this speaker claims, and whether the companions' silence was due to awe of him and fear of his threat, or an approval of his prohibition as he mentioned, or because it did not find acceptance among the people as indicated by the narrations from Ali, Jabir, Ibn Mas'ud, and Ibn Abbas—his prohibition and his oath to stone the one who deems it lawful and practices it does not affect the verse's indication of it. This lawfulness remains unbreached by Book or Sunnah, so the indication of the verses and their rulings is beyond doubt.

Some writers have made a strange claim, stating that mut'ah was a pre-Islamic tradition that never entered Islam at all, such that it would need to be removed from it or its abrogation would require the Book or Sunnah; that the Muslims did not know it and it only occurred in the books of the Shi'a.

I say: This speech, which is based on ignoring what the Book, hadith, consensus, and history indicate, completes the strange transformation of opinions in this matter. It was an established tradition during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), then it was prohibited during the time of Umar and the prohibition was enforced among the general people. The prohibition was justified by the abrogation of the verse of enjoyment by other verses or by the Prophet's prohibition of it. A number of companions (1) and a vast multitude of those who followed them among the jurists of the Hijaz, Yemen, and others disagreed with this, even the likes of Ibn Jurayj among the Imams of hadith—"and he used to go to extremes in practicing mut'ah until he had contracted it with seventy women (2)"—and the likes of Malik, one of the four Imams of jurisprudence (3). This was the case, then the later exegetes turned away from the indication of the verse of enjoyment for mut'ah and sought to interpret it as permanent marriage. They mentioned that mut'ah was a tradition from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) then it was abrogated by hadith. Then, in these recent times, they sought to claim that it was a type of adultery in the pre-Islamic period which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) permitted as concession after concession, then prohibited eternally until the Day of Resurrection. Then this last speaker mentioned that it was pure pre-Islamic adultery of which there is no report in Islam at all except what occurred in the books of the Shi'a. Allah knows best what the state of the matter will become in the future.

(1) Among the strange statements is what al-Zajjaj mentioned regarding this verse: "This is a verse regarding which a group has erred greatly due to their ignorance of the language. That is because they mentioned that His saying: 'For the enjoyment you have had from them' refers to mut'ah, which the people of knowledge have agreed is forbidden." Then he mentioned that the meaning of enjoyment (الاستمتاع) is marriage (النكاح). I wish I knew which part of his speech could be salvaged. Should I accuse the likes of Ibn Abbas, Ubayy, and others of ignorance of the language? Or his claim of the consensus of the people of knowledge on its prohibition? Or his claim of expertise in the language when he made "enjoyment" synonymous with "marriage"?! (2) Refer to the biography of Ibn Jurayj in Tahdhib al-Tahdhib and Mizan al-I'tidal. (3) To obtain these opinions, refer to the books of jurisprudence. For the details of its jurisprudential and theological discussions, refer to what has been authored by the masters of the art among the ancients and the moderns, especially the luminaries of the present age among the investigative researchers of proofs.

(Scholarly Discussion)

The bond of lineage (رابطة النسب)—which is the bond that links one human individual to another from the perspective of birth and the commonality of the womb—is originally a natural, ontological (تكوينية) bond that forms peoples and tribes. It carries the traits arising from the blood, transmitting them according to the transmission of the blood. It is the principle for national manners, customs, and traditions, as it mixes and blends with other influential causes and factors.

Human societies, both advanced and unadvanced, have a kind of regard for it in social traditions and laws in general: in marriage, inheritance, and so forth. Despite this, they continue to manipulate this lineage bond, expanding or narrowing it according to the interests arising from the specificities of their society. As you heard in the previous discussions, most of the past nations did not formally recognize the kinship of women, while they recognized the kinship and sonship of the adopted child (الدعي). Similarly, Islam negates kinship between the warring disbeliever and the Muslim, and attributes the child to the [marital] bed (الولد للفراش), and so on.

When Islam recognized kinship for women by giving them full partnership in wealth and full freedom in will and action—as you heard in the previous discussions—and thereby the son and the daughter became equal in the degree of formal kinship and womb-relationship, and likewise the father and the mother, the brother and the sister, the grandfather and the grandmother, the paternal uncle and aunt, and the maternal uncle and aunt, the formal pillar of lineage began to descend from the side of the daughters just as it used to descend from the side of the sons. Thus, the daughter's son became a son to the person, just like the sonship of the son's son, and so on downwards. Likewise, the son's daughter and the daughter's daughter became two daughters to the person equally. Upon this, the rulings in marriages and inheritances were established. You have already learned that the verse of prohibition: حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهاتُكُمْ وَبَناتُكُمْ indicates this.

The predecessors among our researchers fell short in this matter and its likes (which is a social and legal matter), for they considered it a linguistic matter in which one could rest upon the judgment of the language. Thus, the dispute intensified among them regarding what the word "son" (الابن), for example, was coined for—some generalizing and some specifying. All of that is an error.

Some of them mentioned: "What the language knows of sonship is what proceeds from the side of the son. As for the daughter's son and everything that proceeds from her side, because these are affiliated with their fathers and not their maternal grandfather, the Arabs do not count them as sons of the person. As for the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the two Hasans: 'These two sons of mine are Imams, whether they stand or sit,' and other such statements, this usage is an honorific usage (إطلاق تشريفي)." He recited in that regard the saying of the poet:

Our sons are the sons of our sons, but our daughters— Their sons are the sons of distant men.

Similar to it is the saying of another:

The mothers of people are but vessels, Entrusted [with seed], while lineages belong to fathers.

I say: The path of inquiry became confused for him, as he thought it was a linguistic inquiry, imagining that if the Arabs had coined the word "son" to include the daughter's son, the result of the inquiry would have changed. This is an oversight of the fact that the effects and rulings established in different human societies regarding fatherhood, sonship, and the like do not follow languages; rather, they follow the type of social structure and the traditions prevalent therein. These rulings and effects may change by changing the social tradition in the society while the language remains as it is. This reveals that the inquiry is social (اجتماعي) or related to it, not verbal or linguistic.

As for the poetry he recited, poetry is worth nothing in the market of truths—it is nothing but an imaginative decoration and an illusory embellishment—such that one would argue with everything said by a frivolous poet, especially in matters where the Quran intervenes, which is a decisive word and not a jest.

As for the issue of sons being affiliated with their fathers rather than their grandfathers from the mothers' side, besides not being a verbal or linguistic issue, it is not among the branches of lineage such that the affiliation of the son and daughter to the father would necessitate the severance of their lineage from the mother's side. Rather, it is among the branches of the man's guardianship (قيمومة) over the household in terms of maintenance, the raising of children, and the like.

In short, the mother transmits the bond of lineage to her children, whether male or female, just as the father transmits it. Among its prominent effects in Islam are inheritance and the prohibition of marriage. Yes, there are other rulings and issues that have specific criteria, such as the affiliation of the child, maintenance, and the issue of the share of the "near of kin" (أولي القربى) among the descendants of the Prophet (السادات), and each follows its own specific criterion.

(Another Scholarly Discussion)

Marriage and union are among the social traditions that have never ceased to be prevalent in human societies—whatever society it may be—according to the history of this species available to us to this day. This in itself is evidence of it being a primordial tradition (سنة فطرية).

Furthermore, among the strongest evidences for this is that the male and the female are equipped, according to their physical structure, with the means of reproduction and procreation, as we have mentioned repeatedly. Both groups (male and female) are equal in seeking that, although the female is additionally provided with the apparatus of nursing and the primordial emotions (العواطف الفطرية) suitable for raising children.

Then, there are human instincts that incline toward the love of children, accept the judgment of nature that the human being continues through the continuation of his offspring, acknowledge that the woman is a repose (سكن) for the man and vice versa, and respect the principle of inheritance after respecting the principle of ownership and exclusivity, and respect the necessity of establishing a household.

Societies that respect these primordial principles and rulings in general have no choice but to acknowledge the tradition of marriage in a way that involves exclusivity in some sense—meaning that men and women do not mix in a way that invalidates lineages, even if one assumes protection against the corruption of general health and the strength of procreation caused by the prevalence of adultery and indecency.

These are principles recognized by all nations that follow the tradition of marriage in general, whether they restrict one to one, or permit many women for one man or vice versa, or many of them for many of them, despite the variation of these traditions among nations. For they nevertheless consider marriage by its specific characteristic, which is a type of companionship and association between the spouses.

Thus, indecency (الفحشاء) and profligacy (السفاح) which sever the lineage and corrupt the ancestry are the first things loathed by the human primordial nature (الفطرة) which judges in favor of marriage. You continue to see traces of this loathing among different nations and diverse societies, even those nations that live in total freedom for men and women in sexual relations and mixing; for they are repulsed by these unrestrained abandonments, and you see them living by laws that preserve for them the rulings of lineages in some way.

The human being, while acknowledging the tradition of marriage, is not restricted in it according to his nature; he does not forbid himself a relative or a stranger, nor does the male human being avoid a mother, a sister, a daughter, or others, nor does the female avoid a father, a brother, or a son, according to the sexual drive. History and reports establish the marriage of mothers, sisters, daughters, and others in great, advanced nations as well as lowly ones. Reports confirm the adultery prevalent in civilized nations today between brothers and sisters, fathers and daughters, and others. For the tyranny of lust is not stood up to by anything. What existed among these nations of avoiding the marriage of mothers, sisters, daughters, and those who follow them was only an inherited tradition that perhaps ended in some national manners and customs.

If you compare the laws legislated in Islam to organize the matter of union with other laws and traditions prevalent in the world, and you contemplate them fairly, you will find them more precise and more guaranteed for all aspects of precaution in preserving lineages and other primordial human interests. All the rulings it has legislated regarding the matter of marriage and what follows it return to the preservation of lineages and the blocking of the path to adultery.

That which has taken into account the interest of preserving lineages without an intermediary is the prohibition of marrying married women (المحصنات). By this, the union of a woman with more than one husband at the same time is abolished, for in it is the corruption of lineages, just as it is the criterion for establishing the waiting period of divorce (عدة الطلاق) by the woman waiting for three menstrual cycles to guard against the mixing of waters.

As for the other categories of women whose marriage is prohibited—which are the fourteen categories enumerated in the verses of prohibition—the criterion in prohibiting their marriage is the blocking of the door to adultery. For the human being—within the domestic society—most frequently associates, mixes, is unrestrained, and continues in companionship with these fourteen categories. The continuity of companionship and the touch of unrestraint necessitate the complete turning of the soul and the focus of thought toward them, in a way that leads to the awakening of animal inclinations and emotions and the agitation of the drives of lust, and its driving the human being toward what his nature finds pleasurable and his soul yearns for. "He who hovers around the sanctuary is likely to fall into it."

Thus, it was necessary not to limit the matter to the mere prohibition of adultery in these cases. For the continuity of companionship, the repeated attack of the soul's whisperings, and the arrival of one concern after another would not leave the human being room to maintain a single prohibition against adultery.

Rather, it was necessary that these be prohibited eternally, and that religious upbringing be established upon this until a total despair of reaching them or attaining them settles in the hearts. This kills the attachment of lust to them, cuts off its source, and uproots it from its origin. This is what we see from many Muslims, even among those deeply involved in indecency and unrestrained in reprehensible acts, that the thought of indecency with the prohibited degrees (المحارم) and the violation of the sanctity of mothers and daughters does not cross their minds. Were it not for that, hardly a house would be free from the indecency of adultery and the like.

This is just as Islam blocked the door to adultery with those other than the prohibited degrees by mandating the veil (الحجاب) and prohibiting the mixing of men with women and women with men. Were it not for that, the prohibition of adultery would not have succeeded in partitioning the human being from this heinous act. Thus, there is one of two matters: either the prohibition of mixing, as in one group, or the settling of despair from attaining them altogether through an eternal prohibition upon which the human being is raised until he is firm in this belief—he does not see its likeness in what he sees, nor hears it in what he hears, so it never crosses his mind at all.

The confirmation of this is found in the current state of Western nations. These Christian societies used to maintain the prohibition of adultery (الزنا) and considered polygamy to be akin to it. However, they permitted the free mixing of women and men, and it was not long before indecency (الفحشاء) spread among them to such an extent that one could hardly find one in a thousand who is safe from this malady. Nor is there one in a thousand among their men who can be certain that the children attributed to him are truly from his own loins. This disease did not stop there; it spread to men with their unmarriageable kin (المحارم) — sisters, daughters, and mothers — then to relations between men and boys, then among the youths themselves, and so on and so forth. The matter has reached a point where this group [women], whom Allah, the Glorified, created only as a source of tranquility (سكن) for humanity and a blessing by which the backbone of the human race is sustained and the life of the species is made pleasant, has become a lure used for trapping in every political, economic, and social affair, and a means to attain every objective that corrupts the life of the individual and society. Human life has turned into a mere imaginary wish, and into play and diversion (لعبا ولهوا) in the full sense of the word, until the rent has become too wide for the patcher to mend.

This is the principle upon which Islam based the prohibition of the forbidden categories (المحرمات) — whether those whose prohibition is absolute (المبهمات) or others — in the chapter of marriage, with the exception of married women (المحصنات), as you have come to know.

The effect of this ruling in preventing the spread of adultery and its infiltration into the domestic society (المجتمع المنزلي) is like the effect of the ruling of the veil (الحجاب) in preventing the appearance of adultery and the spread of corruption in civil society (المجتمع المدني), as you have understood.

It has been mentioned previously that His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبَائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُم — "and your stepdaughters who are under your care" [Qarai 4:23] — is not devoid of an allusion to this wisdom. It is also possible that the allusion to this is found in His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verses of prohibition:

يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ ۚ وَخُلِقَ الْإِنسَانُ ضَعِيفًا — "Allah desires to lighten your burden, for man was created weak" [Qarai 4:28]. For the definitive prohibition of these fourteen categories by Allah, the Glorified, lifts from the shoulders of man the heavy burden of having to endure patience against desiring them, inclining toward them, or attaining them when such a thing would otherwise be possible. Man was created weak (ضعيفا) in the face of psychological inclinations and sensual motives, and He, the Exalted, has said: إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ — "Your stratagems are indeed great" [Qarai 12:28]. Indeed, it is a matter requiring immense patience for a man to live with one or more non-mahram women, accompanying them in private and in public, interacting with them night and day, while his ears and eyes are constantly filled with their subtle gestures and sweet movements time after time, and then to remain patient against what his soul whispers to him regarding them, not responding to what it yearns for. For the primary needs are two: food and marriage; everything else is secondary and returns to these two. It is as if this is what he صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم alluded to in his saying: من تزوج أحرز نصف دينه ـ فليتق الله في النصف الآخر — "Whoever marries has secured half of his religion, so let him be God-conscious (فليتق الله) regarding the other half."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا (٢٩)
O you who have faith! Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves unrightfully, but it should be trade by mutual consent. And do not kill yourselves. Indeed Allah is most merciful to you. (29)
وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ عُدْوَٰنًا وَظُلْمًا فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (٣٠)
And whoever does that in aggression and injustice, We will soon make him enter the Fire, and that is easy for Allah. (30)

(Commentary)

In this verse, there is a semblance of connection with what preceded it, as it contains a prohibition against consuming wealth wrongfully (بالباطل), while the previous verses contained a prohibition against consuming the dowries of women through constraint and transgression. Thus, in this verse, there is a transition from the specific to the general.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ (O you who have faith! Do not eat your wealth) until His saying: مِنْكُمْ (from you).

(1) Narrated in Nikah al-Wasa'il.

Eating (الأكل) is well-known; it is the exhaustion of that which can be used as nourishment by seizing and swallowing it, for example. Because it contains the meaning of domination (التسلط) and exhaustion (الإنفاد), it is said: "The fire ate the wood," likening the fire's destruction of the wood by burning it to the way eating exhausts food through consumption and swallowing. It is also said: "So-and-so ate the wealth," meaning he disposed of it by exercising authority over it. This is based on the consideration that the primary way a human disposes of things is by nourishing himself with them, as it is his most intense need for survival and his most pressing requirement. Therefore, disposal is called "eating," but not every disposal—rather, it is the disposal arising from a domination that severs the domination of others over the wealth through ownership (التملك) and the like, as if he exhausts it by extending his authority over it and disposing of it, just as eating exhausts food.

The "wrongful" (الباطل) among actions is that which does not comprise a sound, rational purpose (غرض صحيح عقلائي). "Trade" (التجارة) is the disposal of capital in pursuit of profit, as mentioned by al-Raghib in his Mufradat, where he said: "There is no word in their [the Arabs'] speech containing a ta followed by a jim other than this term." Thus, it applies to transactions of buying and selling.

In restricting His saying: لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ (Do not eat your wealth) with His saying: بَيْنَكُمْ (among yourselves), which indicates a type of collective gathering over wealth and its occurrence in their midst, there is an intimation or indication that the prohibited "eating" is that which occurs by way of circulating it among them and transferring it from one to another through exchange and rotation. Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ (Do not eat your wealth among yourselves)—after being restricted by His saying: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully), signifies the prohibition of transferential transactions that do not lead society toward its felicity and success, but rather harm it and drag it toward corruption and destruction. These are the transactions that are void (باطلة) in the view of the religion, such as usury (الربا), gambling (القمار), and uncertain sales (البيوع الغررية) like "sale by the pebble" (البيع بالحصاة), "sale by the date-stone" (النواة), and the like.

Based on this, the exception occurring in His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ تِجارَةً عَنْ تَراضٍ مِنْكُمْ (unless it be trade by mutual consent from you) is a "disjunctive exception" (استثناء منقطع) — an exception where the excepted item does not belong to the same category as the general group mentioned before it — brought to repel a potential objection. For when He prohibited consuming wealth wrongfully—and the types of transactions circulating in a corrupt society through which financial transfer and transition occur, such as usurious, uncertain, and gambling transactions and their likes, are void in the view of the Law—it was possible to imagine that this would lead to the collapse of the pillars of society and the disintegration of its parts, resulting in the destruction of the people. Thus, this was answered by mentioning a type of transaction capable of organizing the scattered elements of society, strengthening its backbone, and preserving its uprightness: namely, trade based on mutual consent and sound transaction that fulfills the needs of society. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (The day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart) (Ash-Shu'ara: 88-89). When benefit was negated for wealth and children on the Day of Resurrection, it could be imagined that there would be no success or prosperity on that day, since most of what a human benefits from is wealth and children. If these two lose their effect, nothing remains but despair and disappointment. Thus, the answer was given that there is another matter that is entirely beneficial, even if it is not of the same genus as wealth and children: the sound heart (القلب السليم).

What we have mentioned regarding the disjunctive nature of the exception is most in accord with the context of the verse and the fact that His saying: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully) is a primary restriction in the discourse, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ وَتُدْلُوا بِها إِلَى الْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا فَرِيقاً مِنْ أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ (Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves wrongfully, nor proffer it to the judges in order to eat up a part of the people’s wealth) (Al-Baqarah: 188). Accordingly, the verse is not restricted to other sound transactions and legislated matters besides trade that necessitate ownership and permit disposal of wealth, such as gifts (الهبة), settlements (الصلح), rewards (الجعالة), dowries (الأمهار), inheritance (الإرث), and the like.

It is sometimes said that the exception is "connected" (متصل) — an exception where the excepted item belongs to the same category as the general group — and that His saying: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully) is an "explanatory restriction" (قيد توضيحي) brought to clarify the state of the "excepted from" (المستثنى منه) after the exception has been excluded and the prohibition applied. The estimation would be: "Do not eat your wealth among yourselves except that it be trade by mutual consent, for if you eat it through other than the path of trade, it would be a wrongful eating that is prohibited," similar to your saying: "Do not strike the orphan unjustly except for discipline." While this type of usage is permissible and well-known among the people of the language, you have already seen that the disjunctive exception is more in accord with the context of the verse.

It has also been said that the prohibition refers to preventing the spending of wealth on what Allah does not approve of, and trade refers to spending it on what He approves of. It has also been said that the verse used to prohibit the absolute consumption of another's wealth without compensation, and that a man among them would feel constrained about eating at anyone's house after this verse was revealed, until that was abrogated by His saying in Surah al-Nur: وَلا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ until His saying أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا جَمِيعاً أَوْ أَشْتاتاً (nor [is there any blame] on yourselves if you eat from your own houses... whether you eat together or separately) (Al-Nur: 61). You have already seen that the verse is far removed from indicating such meanings.

Among the strange interpretations is what some have attempted to justify the "connected" nature of the exception while taking His saying: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully) as a "precautionary restriction" (قيد احترازي). He said, in essence: What is meant by "wrongful" is consuming wealth without an equivalent compensation. Thus, the "excepted from" clause indicates the prohibition of taking wealth from another wrongfully and without compensation; then trade was excepted from this, despite most of its instances not being free from "wrongfulness," for estimating the compensation with a "just balance" (القسطاس المستقيم) such that it truly equals the value of the item exchanged is very difficult, if not impossible.

Thus, the intent of the exception is to tolerate (التسامح) cases where one of the two compensations is larger than the other, and where the cause of the exchange is the merchant's skill in beautifying his goods and promoting them with flowery speech without cheating, deception, or fraud, as often occurs, among other reasons.

All of this is part of the "wrongfulness" of trade which the Law has permitted out of tolerance and ease for its people. Had this not been permissible in the religion through the exception, no one from its people would have desired trade, and the order of the religious society would have been disrupted. (End of summary).

The falsity of this is apparent from what we have previously established. For "wrongful" (الباطل), as known to the linguists, is that which does not result in its desired effect. The effect of selling and trade is the exchange of two properties and the change of the locus of two ownerships to fulfill the need of each of the two parties for the other's property, such that each obtains what he desires and attains his goal through equivalence (المعادلة). Just as this is achieved through equivalence in the two values, it is also achieved by countering the small with the large if there is joined to the small some desire or fear of the seeker, or some other interest by which, through its addition, it equals the large. The indicator of all this is the occurrence of mutual consent (الرضا) from both sides. When mutual consent occurs, the exchange is not considered wrongful at all.

Furthermore, one who is familiar with the style of the Noble Quran in its explanations would not doubt that it is impossible for the Quran to count a matter as "wrongful" and then command it and guide toward it, while He, the Exalted, said in its description: يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ (It guides to the truth and to a straight path) (Al-Ahqaf: 30). How can that which guides to the truth guide to the wrongful?

Moreover, the implication of this justification is that a human is guided by a true, primordial guidance (اهتداء حق فطري) to his need for the exchange of wealth, then guided by a true, primordial guidance to exchange through balancing, yet what he is guided to is not sufficient to truly fulfill his need until some "wrongfulness" is joined to it. How can the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — be guided to a matter that is insufficient to fulfill its need and only satisfies part of its concern? And how can the primordial nature be guided to the wrongful? Is the distinction between truth and falsehood in actions anything other than the guidance of the primordial nature or its lack thereof? Therefore, there is no escape for one who makes the exception "connected" but to make His saying: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully) an explanatory restriction.

Even more wondrous than this justification is what has been narrated from some: that the subtle point (النكتة) in this disjunctive exception is to indicate that everything in this world of trade and its like is of the nature of the "wrongful" because it has no stability or permanence; thus, the rational person should not be occupied by it away from preparing for the Abode of the Hereafter, which is better and more lasting. (End).

This is an error. For even assuming its correctness, it would be a subtle point for a "connected" exception, not a "disjunctive" one. Furthermore, these spiritual realities are only appropriate to be mentioned for verses like His saying, the Exalted: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ (The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life) (Al-Ankabut: 64), and His saying, the Exalted: ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ (What is with you will be exhausted but what is with Allah will remain) (Al-Nahl: 96), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ (Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and trade’) (Al-Jumu'ah: 11). As for our current context, the application of this subtle point would necessitate the legislation of the "wrongful," and the Quran is far above granting license for the wrongful in any way whatsoever.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ (and do not kill yourselves). The apparent meaning of the sentence is a prohibition against a person killing himself. However, its pairing with His saying: لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ (Do not eat your wealth among yourselves)—where the apparent meaning is taking the collective of the believers as a single soul (نفس واحدة) possessing wealth that it must not eat through wrongful means—perhaps intimates or indicates that what is meant by "souls" (الأنفس) is all the souls of the religious society, taken as a single soul where the soul of each part is the soul of the other. In such a society, a person's soul is his own soul, and the soul of another is also his own soul. Thus, if he kills himself or another, he has killed "himself." By this consideration, the sentence—meaning His saying: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ (and do not kill yourselves)—is absolute, encompassing suicide—which is a person killing himself—and a person killing another among the believers.

It may also be possible to benefit from the end of the verse—meaning His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you)—that the prohibited killing of the soul includes a person throwing himself into the danger of being killed or causing his own destruction leading to his death. This is because justifying the prohibition of killing the soul by "mercy" is more in accord and appropriate for this meaning, as is not hidden. This increases the meaning of the verse in generality and breadth. This very harmony supports that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) is a justification for His saying: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ (and do not kill yourselves) only.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً (And whoever does that in transgression and injustice) the verse. Transgression (العدوان) is absolute exceeding of bounds, whether it be permissible and praised or prohibited and blamed. He, the Exalted, said: فَلا عُدْوانَ إِلَّا عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ (so let there be no hostility except against the wrongdoers) (Al-Baqarah: 193), and He, the Exalted, said: وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ (Cooperate in piety and God-wariness, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression) (Al-Ma'idah: 2). Thus, it is broader in its application than injustice (الظلم). Its meaning in the verse is exceeding the limits (الحدود) set by Allah the Exalted. الإصلاء (to make one enter/burn) by fire is to burn with it.

In the verse, by virtue of its inclusion of His saying: ذلِكَ (that), there is a "shift" (التفات) — a rhetorical transition in person or address — from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hinting that whoever among them does that—while they are a single soul, and a single soul should not desire its own destruction—is not among the believers. Thus, the believers are not addressed regarding his punishment; rather, the Messenger is addressed, as he is the one addressed concerning the affairs of the believers and others. Therefore, the speech was built upon generality, as it was said: "And whoever does that in transgression and injustice, We shall soon make him enter [the Fire]," and it did not say: "And whoever among you does that."

The end of the verse—meaning His saying: وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً (and that is easy for Allah)—supports that the referent of His saying "that" (ذلك) is the prohibition of killing the souls, based on His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) being viewed as a justification for the prohibition of killing only, due to the perfect harmony between the two endings. For the apparent meaning is that Allah only prohibits you from killing yourselves out of mercy and compassion for you; otherwise, His punishment of the one who kills the soul by making him enter the Fire is easy for Him, not difficult. Nevertheless, the return of the justification, as well as the threat, to the sum of the two clauses in the first verse—namely the prohibition of eating wealth wrongfully and the prohibition of killing the soul—is not problematic.

As for the saying of some: that the justification and the threat, or the threat only, refers to all the prohibitions mentioned from the beginning of the Surah until this verse; and likewise the saying of others: that "that" refers to all the prohibitions mentioned from His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً (O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women against their will) (An-Nisa: 19) until here, due to the lack of a mentioned punishment for the prohibitions occurring in these verses—this is something for which there is no evidence to consider.

The change of context in His saying: فسوف نصليه نارا (We shall soon make him enter a Fire) specifically, from the context of the third person (الغيبة) occurring in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) to the context of the first person (التكلم), follows the shift (الالتفات) occurring in His saying: ذلِكَ (that) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger. Then, the return to the third person in His saying: وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً (and that is easy for Allah) is an intimation of the justification—that is, that it is easy for Him because He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: بِالْباطِلِ (wrongfully)—there are two views: one is that it is usury, gambling, short-changing, and injustice. He said: This is what is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام.

In Nahj al-Bayan, from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: that it is gambling, ill-gotten gains (السحت), usury, and [false] oaths.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Asbat ibn Salim, who said: I was with Abu Abdillah [al-Sadiq] عليه‌السلام when a man came to him and said: "Inform me about the saying of Allah: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ (O you who have faith! Do not eat your wealth among yourselves wrongfully)." He said: "He meant by that gambling." As for His saying: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ (and do not kill yourselves)—"He meant by that a man from the Muslims who attacks the polytheists alone, entering their dwellings and being killed; so Allah prohibited them from that."

I say: The verse is general regarding eating wrongfully, and the mention of gambling and its like is by way of enumerating instances (المصاديق). Likewise, the interpretation of killing the soul as what was mentioned in the narration is an expansion of the verse, not a restriction to what was mentioned.

In the same source, from Ishaq ibn Abdillah ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn, who said: Al-Hasan ibn Zayd narrated to me from his father from Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who said: سألت رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله عن الجبائر تكون على الكسير ـ كيف يتوضأ صاحبها؟ وكيف يغتسل إذا أجنب؟ قال : يجزيه المسح بالماء عليها في الجنابة والوضوء ، قلت : فإن كان في برد يخاف على نفسه ـ إذا أفرغ الماء على جسده؟ فقرأ رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً — "I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the splints (الجبائر) that are upon a person with a fracture—how should he perform ablution (الوضوء)? And how should he perform the ritual bath (الغسل) if he is in a state of major ritual impurity (الجنابة)? He said: 'It suffices him to wipe with water over them in major ritual impurity and ablution.' I said: 'What if it is in cold weather such that he fears for his soul if he pours water over his body?' Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً (and do not kill yourselves; indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you)."

In al-Faqih, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: من قتل نفسه متعمدا فهو في نار جهنم خالدا فيها ، قال الله تعالى : وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً ـ وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً ـ فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ ناراً وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً — "Whoever kills himself intentionally shall be in the fire of Hell, abiding therein forever. Allah the Exalted said: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً ـ وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً ـ فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ ناراً وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً (and do not kill yourselves; indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you. And whoever does that in transgression and injustice, We shall soon make him enter a Fire, and that is easy for Allah)."

I say: The narrations, as you see, generalize the meaning of His saying: وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ (and do not kill yourselves) the verse, as we inferred previously. There are other narrations with the same meaning as what preceded.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Majah and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Sa'id, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: إنما البيع عن تراض — "Selling is only by mutual consent."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Abbas: That the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sold to a man, then said to him: "Choose." He said: "I have chosen." He said: "Thus is the sale."

In the same source, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa'i extracted from Ibn Umar, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: البيعان بالخيار ما لم يفترقا أو يقول أحدهما للآخر : اختر — "The two parties to a sale have the right of withdrawal (الخيار) as long as they have not separated, or one of them says to the other: 'Choose'."

I say: His saying: "The two parties to a sale have the right of withdrawal as long as they have not separated" is also narrated through Shi'i chains. His saying: "or one of them says to the other: 'Choose'" is to establish the meaning of mutual consent (التراضي).

Ayah

إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَبَآئِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُم مُّدْخَلًا كَرِيمًا (٣١)
If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode. (31)

(Commentary)

The verse is not lacking in connection to what preceded it, for the preceding verses contained mention of some major sins (المعاصي الكبيرة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden) until His saying: سَيِّئاتِكُمْ (your misdeeds). Avoidance (الاجتناب) is originally derived from the side (الجنب), which is a physical limb; the verb was constructed from it as a metaphor (استعارة). This is because when a person desires something, he faces it with his countenance and the front of his body, but when he turns away from it and leaves it, he turns his side (جنبه) toward it and thus avoids it (اجتنبه). Therefore, avoidance (الاجتناب) is abandonment (الترك). Al-Raghib said: "It is more emphatic than abandonment," end quote. This is only because it is built upon a metaphor; from this same root come the terms side (الجانب), the led-animal (الجنيبة), and the stranger (الأجنبي).

Atonement (التكفير) is from concealment (الكفر), which means to cover (الستر). Its usage has become common in the Quran to refer to the pardoning of misdeeds. Major sins (الكبائر) is the plural of "major" (كبيرة), an adjective placed in the position of the noun it describes, such as "disobedient acts" (المعاصي) and the like. Greatness (الكبر) is a relative concept (معنى إضافي) that is only realized in comparison to smallness (صغر). From here, what is understood from His saying: كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ (the major sins that you are forbidden) is that among the forbidden acts of disobedience, there are those that are minor (صغيرة). Thus, two things become clear from the verse: First, that acts of disobedience are of two types: minor and major. Second, that "misdeeds" (السيئات) in the verse refers to the minor sins (الصغائر) due to the indication of contrast (المقابلة) therein.

Indeed, disobedience (العصيان) and rebellion (التمرد), however they may be, are major and a grave matter when considering the weakness of the created subject (المربوب) in the presence of Allah, whose authority is magnificent. However, the comparison in this regard is between the human and his Lord, not between one act of disobedience and another. Thus, there is no contradiction between every act of disobedience being major in one respect, and some acts of disobedience being minor in another respect.

The greatness of a sin is only realized by the importance of the prohibition (النهي) against it when compared to the prohibition related to something else. His saying, the Exalted: ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ (that which you are forbidden) is not devoid of an intimation or indication of this. The evidence for the importance of a prohibition is the intensification of the discourse through persistence therein, or the threat of punishment from the Fire, and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُدْخِلْكُمْ مُدْخَلاً كَرِيماً (and admit you to a noble entrance). Entrance (المدخل), with a damma on the mim and a fatha on the kha, is a noun of place (اسم مكان). What is meant by it is Paradise (الجنة) or the station of proximity (مقام القرب) to Allah, the Glorified, even if their ultimate reality is one.

(A Discourse on Major and Minor Sins and the Atonement of Misdeeds)

There is no doubt that His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds) indicates the division of sins into major (كبائر) and minor (صغائر), the latter being called "misdeeds" (السيئات) in the verse. Similar to it in indication is His saying, the Exalted: وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها — The Book will be set up. Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it. They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing, small or great, but has counted it!’ (18:49), as their apprehension of what is in the Book indicates that what is meant by "small" and "great" are minor and major sins.

As for the "misdeed" (السيئة), according to what its linguistic root and form provide, it is the event or action that carries unpleasantness (المساءة). Therefore, the term is sometimes applied to matters and calamities whose occurrence distresses a person, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ — and whatever misfortune befalls you is from yourself (4:79), and His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ — They ask you to hasten the ill (13:6). It is also sometimes applied to the results of sins and their external effects in this world and the Hereafter, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَأَصابَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا — So the evils of what they had done visited them (16:34), and His saying, the Exalted: سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا — the evils of what they have earned will visit them (39:51). In reality, this returns to the previous meaning. It is also sometimes applied to the sin itself, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها — The requisition of an ill is an ill like it (42:40). The "misdeed" (السيئة) in the sense of sin is sometimes applied to sins in general, encompassing both minor and major ones, as in His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ — Do those who commit vices suppose that We shall hold them as equal to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being the same? Evil is the judgment that they make! (45:21), among other verses.

And it is sometimes applied to minor sins specifically, as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds), for given the assumption of avoiding major sins, nothing remains for "misdeeds" except the minor ones.

In short, the indication of the verse regarding the division of sins into minor and major based on the comparison between the sins themselves is something that should not be doubted.

Likewise, there is no doubt that the verse is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان), and it strikes the ears of the believers with a subtle Divine care: that if they avoid some sins, He will atone for the others. This is not an incitement (إغراء) to commit minor sins, for that would be meaningless since the verse undoubtedly calls for the abandonment of major sins. Committing a minor sin from the perspective that it is minor—disregarding it and being negligent (التهاون) in its regard—becomes an instance of rebellion (الطغيان) and belittling the command of Allah, the Glorified, and this is among the greatest of major sins. Rather, the verse promises the atonement of misdeeds from the perspective that they are misdeeds that the human being—created in weakness and built upon ignorance—cannot avoid committing due to the prevalence of ignorance and passion over him. Thus, the course of this verse is like that of the verse calling to repentance (التوبة) which promises the forgiveness of sins, such as His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ — Say, ‘[Allah declares,] “O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful.” Turn to your Lord... (39:53-54). Just as it is not correct to say there that the verse incites to sin by opening the door of repentance and comforting souls thereby, so it is here. Rather, such addresses are a revival of despairing hearts with hope.

From this, it is known that the verse does not prevent the knowledge of major sins in the sense that the intent would be to guard against all sins for fear of falling into major ones; for that meaning is far from the course of the verse. Rather, what is understood from the verse is that the addressees know the major sins and distinguish these destructive sins (الموبقات) through the prohibitions related to them. At the very least, it can be said that the verse calls for the knowledge of major sins so that the legally responsible individuals (المكلفون) may be most concerned with guarding against them, without negligence regarding others, for such negligence, as you have known, is one of the destructive major sins.

This is because when a person knows the major sins, distinguishes them, and identifies them, he knows that they are sanctities (حرمات) whose violation cannot be overlooked through atonement except by definitive remorse and sincere repentance. This very knowledge is what causes a person to be mindful and to refrain from committing them.

As for intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — although it is a truth, you have already known from the previous discussions regarding it that it does not benefit one who belittles the command of Allah, the Glorified, and mocks repentance and remorse. Committing a sin while relying on intercession is a form of laxity and negligence regarding the command of Allah, the Glorified, and it is among the destructive major sins that definitively cut off the path to intercession.

From here, the meaning of what was previously mentioned becomes clear: that the greatness of a sin is known from the severity of the prohibition issued against it through persistence or the threat of punishment, as mentioned.

From the preceding discourse, the status of other views regarding the meaning of major sins becomes apparent, and they are many: Among them is what has been said: that a major sin is everything for which Allah has promised punishment in the Hereafter and for which a legal penalty (حد) has been set in this world. The flaw in this is that persistence (الإصرار) in a minor sin is a major sin, according to the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): لا كبيرة مع الاستغفار ، ولا صغيرة مع الإصرار — "There is no major sin with seeking forgiveness, and no minor sin with persistence." This has been narrated by both groups (Shi'a and Sunni), despite no legal penalty being set for it in the Sharia. Likewise, the guardianship (ولاية) of disbelievers and consuming usury (الربا), even though they are among the major things forbidden in the Quran.

Among them is the saying of some: that a major sin is everything for which Allah has promised the Fire in the Quran; some added the Sunnah to this. The flaw in this is that there is no evidence for its converse being universally true.

Among them is the saying of some: that it is everything that intimates a belittling of the religion and a lack of concern for it; this was stated by Imam al-Haramayn and deemed good by al-Razi. The flaw in this is that this is the definition of rebellion (الطغيان) and transgression (الاعتداء), which is one of the major sins, yet there are destructive major sins even if not committed under this title, such as consuming the property of the orphan, incestuous adultery, and killing a faithful soul without right.

Among them is the saying of some: that a major sin is what is forbidden for its own sake, not due to an external factor; this is like the opposite of the previous view. The flaw in this is that rebellion, belittling, and the like are among the greatest of major sins, yet they are accidental titles (عناوين طارية), and by their occurrence in a sin and their attachment to it, it becomes one of the destructive major sins.

Among them is the saying of some: that the major sins are those contained in the verses of Surah al-Nisa from the beginning of the Surah until the completion of thirty verses, as if the intent was that His saying: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden) is an allusion to the sins explained in the verses preceding it, such as severing kinship ties, consuming the orphan's property, adultery, and the like. The flaw in this is that it contradicts the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse.

Among them is the saying of some (attributed to Ibn Abbas): everything that Allah has forbidden is a major sin, perhaps because disobeying Him, the Exalted, is a grave matter. The flaw in this is that you have known that the division of sin into major and minor is only by comparing some to others. What he mentioned is based on comparing the state of the human in his disobedience—he being a servant—to Allah, the Glorified—He being the Lord of all things. It is possible that some might lean toward this view by mistakenly assuming that the genitive construction (الإضافة) in His saying: كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ is explanatory (بيانية), but this is false because the meaning of the verse would then return to our saying: "If you avoid all sins, We will atone for your misdeeds," and there is no misdeed with the avoidance of all sins. If the intent was the atonement of the misdeeds of the believers prior to the revelation of the verse, the verse would be restricted to those present at the time of revelation, which is contrary to the apparent generality (العموم) of the verse. Even if the verse were general, the meaning would return to: "If you resolve to avoid all sins and you do avoid them, We will atone for your misdeeds prior to that," and this is a rare, exceptional case or one that does not exist, which the generality of the verse cannot be carried upon, because the human species is not free from misdeeds and minor lapses (اللمم) except for those whom Allah has protected with His protection (عصمة); so understand that.

Among them: that a minor sin is that whose punishment is less than the reward of its doer, and a major sin is that whose punishment is greater than his reward; this is attributed to the Mu'tazilites. The flaw in this is that it is a matter not indicated by this verse nor any other verses of the Quran. Yes, it is established by the Quran that nullification (الحبط) exists in some sins in general, not in all of them, whether it is according to what they mentioned or not. The discussion on the meaning of nullification (الحبط) has already passed in detail in the second volume of this book.

They also said: it is mandatory to atone for misdeeds and minor sins when major sins are avoided, and it is not good to take one to task for them. This also is a matter that the verse does not indicate at all.

Among them: that greatness and smallness are two considerations that occur to every sin. Thus, a sin that a person commits out of belittling the command of Lordship, mockery, or lack of concern for it is major; yet that same sin, if committed due to a flare-up of anger, the prevalence of cowardice, or an eruption of lust, would be minor and forgiven on the condition of avoiding major sins.

Since these mentioned accidental titles are gathered by the concepts of obstinacy (العناد) and transgression (الاعتداء) against Allah, it is possible to summarize the discourse by saying that every one of the forbidden sins in the religion, if committed out of obstinacy and transgression, is major; otherwise, it is minor and forgiven on the condition of avoiding obstinacy and transgression.

Some said: In every misdeed and in every prohibition with which Allah addressed us, there is a major sin or major sins, and a minor sin or minor sins. The greatest of major sins in every sin is the lack of concern for the prohibition and the command and the respect for the legal obligation (التكليف). From this is persistence, for the one who persists in sin is neither respectful of nor concerned with the command and prohibition. Thus, Allah says: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ meaning the major sins contained within everything you are forbidden, نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ meaning We will atone for its minor part so We will not take you to task for it.

The flaw in this: that the necessity of every committed sin being accompanied by what makes it rebellion and self-exaltation over Allah, the Glorified—thereby becoming a major sin—does not necessitate that greatness revolves around this consideration alone, such that some sins would not be major in themselves without the occurrence of any of these titles. For incestuous adultery compared to looking at a stranger, and killing a forbidden soul unjustly compared to striking, are two major sins whether an accidental title occurred or not. Yes, whenever any of these destructive titles occur, the prohibition intensifies accordingly, the sin becomes greater, and the guilt becomes more magnificent; for adultery out of passion, the prevalence of lust, and ignorance is not like adultery out of deeming it permissible.

Furthermore, this meaning (that if you avoid the major aspects in every sin, We will atone for your minor ones) is a poor meaning that His saying: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ cannot bear according to its context, as is not hidden from anyone who has even a slight familiarity with the styles of speech.

Among them: what appears from the outward words of al-Ghazali, according to what is narrated from him (1), in combining the views. It is that among sins, by comparing some to others, there are major and minor ones, such as the adultery of a married woman from among one's kin compared to looking at a stranger; although some sins become major by the application of some destructive, ruinous titles to them, such as persistence in minor sins, whereby the sin becomes major after it was not.

Thus, it becomes clear that sins are divided into minor and major according to the comparison of some to others with respect to the act itself and the crime of the deed. Then, they are additionally divided into the two categories with respect to the effect of the sin and its bane in its nullification of reward by prevailing over it, or its reduction of it if it does not prevail, such that the sin vanishes with the vanishing of an amount of reward equivalent to it. For every act of obedience has a good effect on the soul that necessitates the raising of its station and its liberation from the filth of distance and the darkness of ignorance, just as every sin has an evil effect on it that necessitates the opposite: the degradation of its place and its fall into the abyss of distance and the darkness of ignorance.

So, if a person commits some sins while having prepared for his soul some light and purity through obedience, the darkness of the sin and the light of obedience must collide. If the darkness of the sin and the bane of the guilt prevail over the light of obedience and overcome it, they nullify it; and this is the major sin. If the obedience prevails with its light and purity, it removes the darkness of ignorance and the filth of the guilt by the cancellation of an amount of the light of obedience equivalent to the darkness of the guilt, and the remainder of its light and purity remains, by which the soul is enlightened and purified. This is the meaning of mutual nullification (التحابط), and it is the very same meaning as the forgiveness of minor sins and the atonement of misdeeds. This type of sin is the minor sin.

As for the equivalence of the misdeed and the good deed with respect to their punishment and reward, although it is something the intellect might entertain at first glance—the consequence of which would be the validity of assuming a "neutral" person who has neither obedience nor sin, and neither light for his soul nor darkness—it is invalidated by His saying, the Exalted: فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ — A group will be in paradise and a group in the Blaze (42:7). End of summary.

Al-Razi rejected this, stating that it is built upon the principles of the Mu'tazilites, which are false according to us. The author of al-Manar intensified the condemnation of al-Razi, saying:

"If this (meaning the division of sin into minor and major in itself) is explicit in the Quran, is it conceivable that its denial could be correctly attributed to Ibn Abbas? No, rather Abd al-Razzaq narrated from him that he was asked: 'Are the major sins seven?' He replied: 'They are closer to seventy.' And Ibn Jubayr narrated that he said: 'They are closer to seven hundred.' The view of denying the division of sins into minor and major was only attributed to the Ash'arites.

It seems that those among them who said this wanted to oppose the Mu'tazilites thereby, even if through interpretation (تأويل), as is known from the words of Ibn Furak, for he validated the words of the Ash'arites and said: 'The sins against Allah are all major; some are only called minor or major relatively (1).' The Mu'tazilites said: 'Sins are of two types: minor and major,' and this is not correct, end quote. And he interpreted the verse with a far-fetched interpretation.

Does one interpret the verses and hadiths just to oppose the Mu'tazilites, even in what they were correct about? That is not unlikely, for sectarian bias (التعصب للمذاهب) is what diverted many intelligent scholars from benefiting themselves and their nation with their intuition, and made their books a trial for the Muslims, occupying them with polemics therein away from the reality of the religion. You will see what al-Razi narrates from al-Ghazali and rejects for that reason; and where is al-Razi compared to al-Ghazali, and where is Mu'awiyah compared to Ali!" End quote. He refers at the end of his words to what we narrated from al-Ghazali and al-Razi.

(1) Meaning the relation according to the intentions of different sins, not the relation of some sins to others in themselves.

In any case, what al-Ghazali mentioned, although plausible in general, is not free from flaws in several respects.

First: That what he mentioned regarding the division of sins into minor and major according to the mutual nullification of reward and punishment does not always correspond to the division he mentioned according to the sins themselves and the core of the guilt at the beginning of his discourse. For most major sins that are accepted as such in themselves could coincide in their doer with a great reward that prevails over them; likewise, a minor sin could be assumed to coincide with the remaining reward in the soul that is smaller and less than it. Thereby, the minor and major would differ according to the two divisions: some sins would be minor according to the first division but major according to the second, and some would be the opposite. Thus, there is no total correspondence between the two divisions.

Second: That the collision between the effects of sins and acts of obedience, although established in general, is something that has never been established universally through the religious outward meanings (الظواهر الدينية) from the Book and the Sunnah. What evidence from the Book and the Sunnah indicates the realization of removal and nullification in a universal manner between the punishment of sins and the reward of acts of obedience?

That which has been detailed in the investigation regarding the noble, luminous states of the soul and the other base, dark states is also like this. For although they collide for the most part and remove and annihilate each other, that is not in a universal, permanent way. Rather, both the virtue and the vice may remain established in their place and reconcile upon staying, dividing the soul as if something of it belongs to virtue specifically and something to vice specifically. Thus, you see a Muslim man, for example, who consumes usury and does not hesitate to swallow people's wealth, nor does he listen to the plea of the destitute, oppressed debtor, yet he strives in the prescribed prayers and goes to extremes in his humility and submissiveness. Or he does not care about shedding blood, violating honors, and spreading corruption on earth, yet he is sincere to Allah with the utmost sincerity in matters of obedience and acts of proximity. This is what psychologists today call "split personality" after its multiplicity and conflict. It is when different psychological inclinations conflict and some revolt against others through crowding and contradiction, and the human remains in internal toil from that until the two traits (الملكتان) settle, pair up, and reconcile, each disappearing upon the appearance, rising, and seizing of its prey by the other, as you knew from the aforementioned example.

Third: That the consequence of what he mentioned is that the consideration of avoidance (الاجتناب) in the atonement of misdeeds would become redundant. For one who does not commit major sins—not because he restrains himself from them despite ability and psychological inclination toward them, but due to his lack of power over them and his inability to perform them—his misdeeds would be nullified by acts of obedience due to the prevalence of his reward, on the assumption, over his punishment, which is the atonement of misdeeds. Thus, no satisfactory reason remains for the consideration of avoiding major sins.

Al-Ghazali said in al-Ihya: "Avoiding a major sin only atones for a minor one if he avoids it despite ability and will, like one who is able to reach a woman and have intercourse with her but restrains himself from the act and limits himself to looking or touching. For the struggle of his soul (مجاهدة نفسه) in restraining from the act is more influential in enlightening his heart than his embarking on looking is in darkening it; this is the meaning of its atonement. If he were impotent, or his refrainment was only out of necessity due to inability, or he was able but refrained out of fear of a worldly matter, then this is not suitable for atonement at all.
