And everyone who does not desire wine by his nature, such that if it were permitted he would not drink it, his avoidance of it does not atone for the minor sins that are its preliminaries, such as listening to idle talk and stringed instruments. Yes, one who desires wine and listening to stringed instruments but restrains himself through struggle from the wine and lets himself go in the listening, his struggle of the soul in restraining might erase from his heart the darkness that rose to it from the sin of listening. All these are otherworldly rulings." End quote.

He also said in another place: "Every darkness that rises to the heart is only erased by a light that rises to it through a good deed that opposes it. Opposites are those that are proportionate; therefore, every misdeed should be erased by a good deed of its own kind so that it may oppose it. For whiteness is removed by blackness, not by heat and cold. This gradualness and precision is from the subtlety in the method of erasure; hope in it is more truthful and trust in it is greater than persisting in one type of worship, even if that is also influential in erasure." End of his words.

His words, as you see, indicate that what nullifies misdeeds is avoidance, which is restraint (الكف), even though it is not necessary according to this view.

The comprehensive discourse that can be said in this regard, supported by the noble verses, is that good deeds and misdeeds are mutually nullifying in general, but the effect of every misdeed on every good deed and vice versa in the manner of reducing it or annihilating it is something for which there is no evidence. This is indicated by considering the state of ethics and psychological states, which are the best aid in understanding these Quranic truths in the chapter of reward and punishment.

As for major and minor sins, the apparent meaning of the verse, as you have known, is that sins—by comparing some to others, such as killing a protected soul unjustly compared to looking at a stranger, and drinking wine by deeming it permissible compared to drinking it out of passion—some are major and some are minor, without this being clearly linked to the issue of nullification and atonement in its entirety.

Furthermore, the verse is apparent in that Allah, the Glorified, promises one who avoids major sins that He will atone for all his misdeeds, those that preceded and those that follow, according to the apparent absolute nature of the verse. It is well known that the apparent meaning of this avoidance is that every believer performs what he can of avoiding major sins and what is truly considered avoidance in his case, not that he avoids every single major sin by restraining from it. For one who pays the slightest attention to the series of major sins will not doubt that there does not exist anyone who inclines toward all of them and is capable of all of them, or it is so rare as to be non-existent. Interpreting the verse in this manner is not accepted by a sound disposition.

So the meaning is that whoever avoids those major sins he is capable of and which his soul yearns for—which are the major sins he is able to avoid—Allah will atone for his misdeeds, whether they are of the same kind or not.

As for whether this atonement is for the avoidance itself—such that avoidance is in itself an act of obedience that atones for misdeeds, just as repentance is—or that if a person does not commit major sins, he is left between his minor sins and his good acts of obedience, so the good deeds atone for his misdeeds—and Allah has said: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئاتِ — Indeed good deeds drive away misdeeds (11:114)—the apparent meaning of the verse (إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ) is that avoidance has a role in the atonement. Otherwise, it would have been more appropriate to state that acts of obedience atone for misdeeds, as in His saying: إِنَّ الْحَسَناتِ (Indeed good deeds...), or that Allah, the Glorified, forgives minor sins regardless, without the need to frame the speech as a conditional sentence.

The evidence for the greatness of a sin is the severity of the prohibition issued against it, or the promise of the Fire for it, or what is close to that, whether that is in the Book or the Sunnah, without evidence for restriction.

(Narrative Discourse)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The major sins are those for which Allah has made the Fire mandatory."

In al-Faqih and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): regarding major sins, he said: "Everything for which Allah has promised the Fire."

In Thawab al-A'mal, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Whoever avoids what Allah has promised the Fire for—if he is a believer—Allah will atone for his misdeeds and admit him to a noble entrance. The seven mandatory major sins are: killing a forbidden soul, being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين), consuming usury, returning to nomadism after migration (التعرب بعد الهجرة), slandering a chaste woman (قذف المحصنة), consuming the property of the orphan, and fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف)."

I say: The narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis regarding the enumeration of major sins are many; some will pass by you. Associating partners with Allah (الشرك بالله) has been counted among the seven major sins in what we will mention, except in this narration; perhaps he (peace be upon him) excluded it from among them because it is the greatest of major sins, as indicated by his saying: "if he is a believer."

In al-Majma': Abd al-Azim ibn Abdillah al-Hasani narrated from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali, from his father Ali ibn Musa al-Rida, from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Amr ibn Ubayd al-Basri entered upon Abu Abdillah Ja'far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). When he offered salutations and sat down, he recited this verse: الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَواحِشَ — Those who avoid major sins and indecencies (42:37), then he stopped. Abu Abdillah said: 'What made you silent?' He said: 'I would like to know the major sins from the Book of Allah.' He said: 'Yes, O Amr. The greatest of major sins is associating partners with Allah (الشرك بالله), because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ — Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him (4:48), and He said: مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْواهُ النَّارُ — Whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entrance to] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire (5:72). After it is despairing of the spirit of Allah (اليأس من روح الله), because Allah says: لا يَيْأَسُ مِنْ رَوْحِ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْكافِرُونَ — no one despairs of Allah’s mercy except the faithless lot (12:87). Then feeling secure from the scheme of Allah (الأمن من مكر الله), because Allah says: فَلا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخاسرُونَ — No one feels safe from Allah’s stratagem except the people who are losers (7:99). Among them is being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين), because Allah, the Exalted, made the undutiful one a tyrannical wretch in His saying: وَبَرًّا بِوالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّاراً شَقِيًّا — and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a self-willed wretch (19:32). Among them is killing the soul that Allah has forbidden except by right, because He says: وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ خالِداً فِيها — Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to abide in it [forever] (4:93). And slandering chaste women (قذف المحصنات), because Allah says: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ الْمُحْصَناتِ الْغافِلاتِ الْمُؤْمِناتِ لُعِنُوا فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخيرةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ — Indeed those who accuse chaste, neglectful, faithful women shall be cursed in this world and the Hereafter, and there shall be a great punishment for them (24:23). And consuming the property of the orphan, because of His saying: الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ الْيَتامى ظُلْماً — Those who consume the property of orphans unjustly (4:10). And fleeing from the battlefield (الفرار من الزحف), because Allah says: وَمَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَمَأْواهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — Whoever turns his back to them on such a day—unless maneuvering for battle or joining [another] troop—has certainly earned Allah’s wrath and his abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination (8:16). And consuming usury (أكل الربا), because Allah says: الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ الرِّبا لا يَقُومُونَ إِلَّا كَما يَقُومُ الَّذِي يَتَخَبَّطُهُ الشَّيْطانُ مِنَ الْمَسِّ — Those who exact usury will not stand except like one who has been confounded by Satan’s touch (2:275), and He says: فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا فَأْذَنُوا بِحَرْبٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ — And if you do not, then be informed of a declaration of war from Allah and His Apostle (2:279). And sorcery (السحر), because Allah says: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا لَمَنِ اشْتَراهُ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخيرةِ مِنْ خَلاقٍ — and they certainly knew that anyone who buys it has no share in the Hereafter (2:102). And adultery (الزنا), because Allah says: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثاماً يُضاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهاناً — and whoever does that shall encounter the reward of sin. The punishment shall be doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection, and he shall abide in it in humiliation (25:68-69). And the false oath that immerses one in sin (اليمين الغموس), because Allah says: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعهدِ اللهِ وَأَيْمانِهِمْ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً أُولئِكَ لا خَلاقَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخيرةِ — Indeed those who sell Allah’s covenant and their oaths for a paltry price shall have no share in the Hereafter (3:77). And misappropriating spoils (الغلول); Allah said: وَمَنْ يَغْلُلْ يَأْتِ بِما غَلَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ — and whoever defrauds will bring his fraud on the Day of Resurrection (3:161). And withholding the mandatory zakat (منع الزكاة المفروضة), because Allah says: يَوْمَ يُحْمى عَلَيْها فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوى بِها جِباهُهُمْ وَجُنُوبُهُمْ وَظُهُورُهُمْ — on the day when these shall be heated in hell-fire and therewith their foreheads, their sides and their backs shall be branded (9:35). And false testimony (شهادة الزور) and concealing testimony (كتمان الشهادة), because Allah says: وَمَنْ يَكْتُمْها فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ — and whoever conceals it, his heart is indeed sinful (2:283). And drinking wine (شرب الخمر), because Allah equated it with the worship of idols. And intentionally abandoning the prayer (ترك الصلاة متعمدا) or anything that Allah, the Exalted, has made mandatory—because the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) says: 'Whoever abandons the prayer intentionally has been released from the protection of Allah and the protection of His Messenger.' And breaking a covenant (نقض العهد) and severing kinship ties (قطيعة الرحم), because Allah says: أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ — for such there shall be the curse, and they shall have the ill abode (23:25)."

He said: "So Amr ibn Ubayd went out crying loudly and saying: 'Perished is he who spoke by his own opinion and contended with you in excellence and knowledge.'"

I say: Something close to it has been narrated from the paths of the Sunnis from Ibn Abbas, and two things become clear from the narration:

First: That a major sin among sins is that for which the prohibition is intensified, either by persistence and reaching the limit in prohibition, or by the promise of the Fire, from the Book or the Sunnah, as appears from the instances of his (peace be upon him) argumentation. From this, the meaning of what preceded in the (hadith of al-Kafi: "the major sin is what Allah has made the Fire mandatory for") and what preceded in the (hadith of al-Faqih and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi: "the major sin is what Allah has promised the Fire for") becomes clear. The intent by making it mandatory and promising it is broader than explicit statement and allusion in the speech of Allah or the hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

I think that what was narrated regarding this from Ibn Abbas is also like this; his intent by the promise of the Fire is broader than explicit statement and allusion in the Quran or hadith. This is supported by what is in (Tafsir al-Tabari) from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The major sins are every sin that Allah concluded with Fire, wrath, curse, or punishment." Thereby it becomes clear that what was also narrated from him in (Tafsir al-Tabari) and elsewhere: "Everything that Allah forbade is a major sin," is not a disagreement regarding the meaning of major sin, but rather a magnifying of all sins by comparing the insignificance of the human to the greatness of his Lord, as mentioned.

Second: That the restriction of major sins to some of what preceded and what follows in the narrations—or to eight, or to nine as in some prophetic narrations narrated from the paths of the Sunnis, or to twenty as in this narration, or to seventy as in other narrations—all of that is in consideration of the different levels of greatness in sin, as indicated by what is in the narration of his saying when enumerating the major sins: "And the greatest of major sins is associating partners with Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: اجتنبوا السبع الموبقات — 'Avoid the seven destructive sins.' They said: 'And what are they, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'Associating partners with Allah, killing the soul that Allah has forbidden except by right, sorcery, consuming usury, consuming the property of the orphan, turning away on the day of the battlefield, and slandering chaste, neglectful, faithful women.'"

In it: Ibn Hayyan and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Bakr ibn Muhammad ibn Amr ibn Hazm, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wrote a letter to the people of Yemen—containing the mandatory duties, the sunnahs, and the blood-monies—and he sent it with Amr ibn Hazm.

He said: And in the letter was—that the greatest of major sins before Allah on the Day of Resurrection are: associating partners with Allah, killing a faithful soul without right, fleeing on the day of the battlefield, being undutiful to parents, slandering a chaste woman, learning sorcery, consuming usury, and consuming the property of the orphan."

In it: Abdullah ibn Ahmad narrated in Zawa'id al-Zuhd, from Anas: "I heard the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'Behold, my intercession is for the people of major sins from my nation,' then he recited this verse: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ (If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will atone for your misdeeds)."

Ayahs

وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا۟ مَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ ۖ وَلِلنِّسَآءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا ٱكْتَسَبْنَ ۚ وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٣٢)
Do not covet the advantage, which Allah has given some of you over others. To men belongs a share of what they have earned, and to women a share of what they have earned. And ask Allah for His bounty. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (32)
وَلِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مَوَٰلِىَ مِمَّا تَرَكَ ٱلْوَٰلِدَانِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبُونَ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَـَٔاتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا (٣٣)
For everyone We have appointed heirs to what the parents and near relatives leave, as well as those with whom you have made a compact; so give them their share [of the heritage]. Indeed Allah is witness to all things. (33)
ٱلرِّجَالُ قَوَّٰمُونَ عَلَى ٱلنِّسَآءِ بِمَا فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ وَبِمَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٌ حَـٰفِظَـٰتٌ لِّلْغَيْبِ بِمَا حَفِظَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱلَّـٰتِى تَخَافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَّ فَعِظُوهُنَّ وَٱهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِى ٱلْمَضَاجِعِ وَٱضْرِبُوهُنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلَا تَبْغُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلًا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيرًا (٣٤)
Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others, and by virtue of their spending out of their wealth. Righteous women are obedient and watchful in the absence [of their husbands] in guarding what Allah has enjoined [them] to guard. As for those [wives] whose misconduct you fear, [first] advise them, and [if ineffective] keep away from them in the bed, and [as the last resort] beat them. Then if they obey you, do not seek any course [of action] against them. Indeed Allah is all-exalted, all-great. (34)
وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ شِقَاقَ بَيْنِهِمَا فَٱبْعَثُوا۟ حَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهِۦ وَحَكَمًا مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن يُرِيدَآ إِصْلَـٰحًا يُوَفِّقِ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَهُمَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا خَبِيرًا (٣٥)
If you fear a split between the two of them, then appoint an arbiter from his relatives and an arbiter from her relatives. If they desire reconcilement, Allah shall reconcile them. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-aware. (35)

(Commentary)

These verses are connected to the preceding rulings on inheritance and marriage. Through them, Allah reinforces the previous commands and derives universal principles (أحكام كلية) capable of rectifying certain incidental flaws (الخلال العارضة) that may arise in the social interactions between men and women.

His saying, the Exalted: «And do not covet the advantage which Allah has given some of you over others» (وَلا تَتَمَنَّوْا ما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بِهِ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ). Coveting (التمني) is for a person to say: "If only such a thing were such a way." It appears that naming the speech itself as "coveting" is a case of describing the verbal expression by the attribute of its underlying meaning. In reality, coveting is the internal creation of a psychological attachment, similar to the attachment of love toward something one perceives as impossible or nearly impossible, whether this is expressed in words or not.

The apparent sense of the verse is that it is intended to prohibit coveting an existing and established excellence (فضل) or increase (زيادة) found among people. This coveting arises because one of the two groups—men or women—possesses this excellence. It is proper, therefore, to turn away from attachment to the one who possesses the excellence and instead attach oneself to Allah by asking for the bounty (الفضل) that is with Him, the Exalted. Accordingly, it becomes certain that "excellence" (الفضل) here refers to the distinction (المزية) that Allah has granted to each of the two groups, men and women, through the legislation of rulings specific to each. Examples include the distinction of men over women in the number of wives and the larger share of inheritance, and the distinction of women over men in the obligation of the dower (المهر) being set for them and the obligation of their maintenance (النفقة) being upon the men.

The prohibition of coveting this distinction, which is specific to its possessor, is intended to uproot the tree of evil and corruption at its source. These distinctions are things to which the human soul becomes attached due to the innate love for them and the striving for them that Allah has placed in souls for the sake of inhabiting this world. Thus, the matter first appears in the form of coveting (تمني); if repeated, it transforms into internal envy (حسد); and if persisted in until it settles in the heart, it spreads to the station of external action and deed. Then, if these souls join together, it becomes a trial that corrupts the earth and destroys tilth and progeny.

From this, it becomes clear that the prohibition of coveting is an advisory prohibition (نهي إرشادي) whose benefit returns to the preservation of the aforementioned legislated rulings, rather than being a primary legislative prohibition (نهي مولوي).

In attributing the excellence to the act of Allah, the Glorified, and using the expression "some of you over others" (بَعْضَكُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ), there is an awakening of the attribute of submission to Allah’s command through faith in Him. It also stirs the instinct of love through this reminder, so that the one over whom another is preferred realizes that the preferred one is a part of himself, not something separate or alien.

His saying, the Exalted: «To men belongs a share of what they have earned, and to women a share of what they have earned» (لِلرِّجالِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبُوا وَلِلنِّساءِ نَصِيبٌ مِمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ). Al-Raghib mentioned that acquisition (الاكتساب) is only used for what a person gains for himself, while earning (الكسب) is more general, whether for oneself or for another. The preceding explanation leads to this sentence being an elucidation of the prior prohibition of coveting, serving as its justification (تعليل). That is: do not covet that, for this distinction exists with the one who possesses it because he acquired it through his specific nature (النفسية) or the labor of his body. Men were granted the permission to take four wives, for instance, while this was forbidden to women, because their position in human society necessitates this, unlike the position of women. They were also granted a share of inheritance equal to that of two females for the same reason. Similarly, women were granted half the share of men, their maintenance was made obligatory upon men, and they were granted the dower because their position necessitates that. Likewise, whatever wealth either group acquires through trade or other means is the cause of their exclusive right to it, and Allah does not desire injustice for His servants.

From this, it appears that what is meant by "acquisition" (الاكتساب) is a type of possession and exclusivity (حيازة واختصاص) that is broader than just voluntary action like acquisition through a craft or profession. It includes what results from the possessor of the excellence being characterized by a quality that necessitates it, such as being characterized by masculinity or femininity, which necessitates a specific share and portion.

Although the masters of linguistics mention that "earning" (الكسب) and "acquisition" (الاكتساب) are specific to what a person gains through voluntary action like seeking and the like, they also mention that the root meaning of earning (الكسب) is "gathering" (الجمع). It is sometimes permissible to say: "So-and-so acquired (اكتسب) fame through his beauty," and the like. Some exegetes have interpreted "acquisition" in the verse in this way. It is not far-fetched that "acquisition" in the verse is used for the aforementioned meaning by way of metaphor and figurative speech (التشبيه والاستعارة).

As for the view that "acquisition" in the verse refers only to what a person seeks through his labor—meaning: men have a share of the wealth they gained for themselves through their work, and likewise women, and that the prohibition of coveting is a prohibition of coveting the wealth in people's hands gained through craft or trade—while this is a correct meaning in itself, it unnecessarily narrows the scope of the verse and severs its connection with the preceding verses on inheritance and marriage.

In any case, the meaning of the verse based on the previous explanation is: Do not covet the financial and non-financial excellence and distinction that Allah has specifically granted to one of the two groups, men or women, whereby He preferred some of you over others. For that excellence is something granted to the one who has it because he secured it through his specific nature in human society or by the labor of his hand through trade and the like; he has a share in it, and everyone only attains their share from what they have acquired.

His saying, the Exalted: «and ask Allah for His bounty» (وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ). Bestowing something from what the benefactor possesses upon another is often called "bounty" (فضل) because it is usually something extra that the benefactor does not need. Since Allah turned the people's attention away from focusing on the excellence given to the possessors of excellence and desiring it—and since the love for life's distinctions, or rather the desire to be unique in them, to excel, and to be superior, is part of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of man and is never stripped from him, He turned them toward Himself. He directed their faces toward His bounty and commanded them to turn away from what is in the hands of people and approach His presence, asking from His bounty. For bounty is in the hand of Allah; He is the one who gave every possessor of excellence his excellence, so He has the power to give you that which will increase you and make you excel over others whose possessions you desire and whose gifts you covet.

This "bounty" which must be asked for has been left indefinite by the use of the word "from" (مِنْ). This contains several benefits: First, it teaches the etiquette of supplication (الدعاء) and asking from His presence, the Exalted. It is most fitting for a human being—who is built upon ignorance of what truly benefits or harms him in reality—when asking his Lord, who knows the reality of what benefits His creation and what harms them and is powerful over all things, to ask Him for the good in what his soul desires, without being excessive in specifying what he asks for or determining the path to its arrival. We have often seen those whose souls yearned for a specific need, such as wealth, children, status, or health, and who were persistent in supplication and asking for it, wanting nothing else; then, when their prayer was answered and their request granted, it became the cause of their destruction and the failure of their efforts in life.

Second: It is an indication that what is asked for should not be something that would nullify the Divine wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) in this excellence which Allah has established through legislation or creation. It is necessary that they ask for something of Allah's bounty that is not specific to others. If men were to ask for the excellence specific to women, or vice versa, and Allah were to grant them that, the wisdom would be nullified and the legislated rulings and laws would be corrupted. So understand this.

Therefore, when a person supplicates to Allah, the Glorified, when his soul is constrained by a need, he should not ask for what is in the hands of people to fulfill his need, but rather ask from what is with Him. And when he asks from what is with Him, he should not dictate to his Lord, who is aware of his state, the path to reaching his need; rather, he should ask Him to fulfill his need through what He knows to be good from His presence.

As for His saying, the Exalted: «Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things» (إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيماً), it is a justification for the prohibition at the beginning of the verse. That is: do not covet what Allah has given of His bounty to those He has given; indeed, Allah has knowledge of all things, He is not ignorant of the path of benefit, nor does He err in His judgment.

(A Discourse on a Quranic Reality)

The variation in temperaments and aptitudes for acquiring the distinctions of life among human individuals stems from natural, creational origins (أصول طبيعية تكوينية) whose influence on the actualization of different levels of life is unavoidable. This has been the state of human societies from their earliest eras until our present day, as far as we know.

Stronger individuals used to enslave the weak and employ them for their own desires and whims without any restriction or condition. Those poor, weak souls had no choice but to comply with their commands, finding no way but to respond to what was desired of them. Yet, hearts were filled with rage and resentment, and souls were lying in wait. People continued upon this custom, which began as a tribal system and ended as monarchical and imperial methods.

Eventually, the human species succeeded, through successive uprisings, in tearing down this oppressive structure and compelling the guardians of government and kingship to follow constitutions and laws established for the welfare and happiness of society. Thus, the rule of arbitrary whims and the dominance of autocratic customs ostensibly departed, and the disparity between classes—divided into the absolute ruling owner and the reined-in, ruled subject—was lifted. However, the tree of corruption began to grow in a different soil and with a different appearance than before, yet the fruit remained the same. This was the differentiation of status based on the disparity of wealth, with capital accumulating for some and hands remaining empty for others. The distance between the two groups became so great that the wealthy could not restrain themselves from using their wealth to penetrate all affairs of social life, nor could the destitute do anything but rise for combat and resist oppression.

This resulted in the system of Communism, which calls for sharing the materials of life, abolishing ownership, and nullifying capital, asserting that every individual in society should enjoy what his own hands have produced and what his acquired psychological perfection has prepared. Thus, the root of disparity through wealth and possession was severed. However, it inherited forms of corruption that the previous system could hardly match: the nullification of the individual's freedom of will and the stripping of his choice. Nature (الطبيعة) repels this, and creation (الخلقة) does not agree with it; far be it that anything which forces nature and oppresses creation should endure.

Moreover, the root of corruption remained settled in its place, for human nature is only energized by work in which there is the possibility of distinction and precedence, and the hope for progress and pride. With the abolition of distinctions, work ceases, and in that lies the destruction of humanity. They attempted to remedy this by diverting these distinctions toward non-material, honorary, and ceremonial goals and purposes. Thus, the problem returned to its original state; for if man does not believe in their reality, he will not submit to them, and if he does believe in them, the state of distinction through them is the same as the state of material distinction.

Democracy attempted to repel the corruption that seeped into it by clarifying the flaws of the opposing system through extensive propaganda and by imposing heavy taxes that consume a large portion of the profits from trades and businesses. But this did not benefit them; the appearance of corruption creeping into the system of their opponents does not block the path of evil attacking their own system. Nor does the transfer of most profits to the public treasury prevent the affluent from their luxury and injustices. They simply shift their efforts from owning wealth to controlling and circulating it in their hands; for from control, seizure, and management, one gains the same benefits as from ownership.

Neither these nor those have treated the disease, and there is no cure after cauterization. This is only because that which mankind has made the goal and desire of his society—namely, the enjoyment of material life—is a compass that leads to the pole of corruption; it will not change its nature no matter where it is turned or how it is set.

What Islam sees as the way to cut off the sources of this corruption is to free people in everything to which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) leads them. Then, it narrows the gap between the two classes by raising the standard of living for the poor through the established financial taxes and the like, and lowering the standard of living for the rich by prohibiting extravagance (الإسراف), wastefulness (التبذير), and the display of that which distances them from the core of the middle path. It balances this through monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and ethics, turning faces away from material distinctions toward the dignity of godwariness (التقوى) and seeking the bounty that is with Allah.

This is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: «and ask Allah for His bounty» (وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ), and His saying: «Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you» (إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ) [49:13], and His saying: «So flee toward Allah» (فَفِرُّوا إِلَى اللهِ) [51:50]. We have previously explained that turning people's faces toward Allah, the Glorified, results in their attending to the matter of real, actual causes in seeking their vital objectives, without this resulting in idleness in earning a living or laziness in seeking happiness. Thus, the claim of the one who says that Islam is a religion of idleness and stagnation in seeking human vital objectives is nothing but a shot without a marksman, born of ignorance. This is the summary of the discourse on this objective, and its various aspects have been discussed in detail in different parts of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: «For everyone We have appointed heirs to what parents and near relatives leave...» (وَلِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مَوالِيَ مِمَّا تَرَكَ الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ). Heirs (الموالي) is the plural of mawla (مولى), which means "guardian" or "one with authority" (الولي), even if it is frequently used for specific instances of authority (الولاية), such as the mawla for the master of a slave due to his authority over him, the mawla for a helper due to his authority over the affairs of the helped, and the mawla for a cousin due to his authority over the marriage of his female cousin. It is not far-fetched that it was originally a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) or a noun of place (اسم مكان) intended to refer to the person characterized by it in some way, just as we today use the terms "government" (الحكومة) or "court" (المحكمة) to refer to the governor or the judge.

"Contracting" (العقد) is the opposite of "dissolving" (الحل), and "right hand" (اليمين) is the opposite of "left" (اليسار). The yamin is the right hand, and it also means an "oath" (الحلف), among other meanings.

The occurrence of this verse alongside the previous saying: «And do not covet the advantage which Allah has given some of you over others» in a single context, and its inclusion of the recommendation to give every possessor of a share his share, and that Allah has appointed for everyone heirs (موالي) to what parents and near relatives leave—all this supports the view that this verse, i.e., His saying: «For everyone We have appointed...», combined with the previous verse, is a summary of the rulings and commands found in the verses of inheritance. It is a general recommendation for the detailed laws contained therein, just as His saying before the verses of inheritance: «To men belongs a share of what parents and near relatives leave...» was a general legislation serving as a foundation for the chapter of inheritance to which the details of inheritance rulings return.

The implication of this is that those mentioned generally as heirs and those whose property is inherited should correspond to those mentioned in detail in the verses of inheritance. Thus, "heirs" (الموالي) refers to all those mentioned as inheritors therein, including children, parents, brothers, sisters, and others.

The three categories mentioned in the verse by His saying: «parents, near relatives, and those with whom you have made a pledge» (الْوالِدانِ وَالْأَقْرَبُونَ وَالَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ) refer to the categories mentioned in the verses of inheritance, which are three: parents, near relatives, and spouses. Thus, His saying: «those with whom you have made a pledge» (الَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ) applies to the husband and the wife.

So His saying: «For everyone» (وَلِكُلٍّ) means for every one of you, male or female, We have appointed heirs (موالي), i.e., guardians in inheritance who inherit the wealth you leave behind. His saying: «to what [they] leave» (مِمَّا تَرَكَ), the "from" (من) is for beginning (للابتداء) and is connected to "heirs" (الموالي), as if the authority (الولاية) arises from the wealth; or it is connected to an omitted verb, i.e., "they inherit" or "they are given" from what is left. "What is left" is the wealth left by the deceased whose property is inherited, who are the parents, the near relatives by lineage, and the husband and wife.

The application of «those with whom you have made a pledge» (الَّذِينَ عَقَدَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ) to the husband and wife is a metonymical (كنائي) usage. It was their custom in contracts and treaties to shake hands, so it is as if their right hands (أيمانهم) with which they shook were what "contracted" the contracts and "firmed" the pledges. Thus, the meaning is: those with whom you have established the relationship of marriage through a contract.

His saying: «so give them their share» (فَآتُوهُمْ نَصِيبَهُمْ), the pronoun refers to the heirs (الموالي), and "share" (النصيب) refers to what was explained in the verses of inheritance. The "so" (الفاء) is for derivation (للتفريع), and the sentence is derived from His saying: «For everyone We have appointed heirs». Then He reinforced His command to give their share by saying: «Indeed Allah is witness over all things» (إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيداً).

This meaning we have mentioned for the verse is the closest of the meanings mentioned in its exegesis. It has been said that "heirs" (الموالي) refers to the agnates (العصبة) rather than the inheritors who are more entitled to the inheritance, but there is no evidence for this from the wording, unlike the case for the inheritors.

It has also been said that "from" (من) in His saying: «to what parents and near relatives leave» is explanatory (بيانية), and "what" refers to the heirs who are the guardians. The meaning would be: For each of you We have appointed guardians who inherit from him, and they are those whom the parents and near relatives left behind.

It has also been said that «those with whom you have made a pledge» refers to allies (الحلفاء). In the pre-Islamic era (الجاهلية), a man would contract with another saying: "My blood is your blood, my war is your war, my peace is your peace; you inherit from me and I inherit from you, you pay blood money for me and I pay for you." Thus, the ally would have a sixth of the wealth of his ally. According to this, the sentence is disconnected from what precedes it, and the meaning is: "And the allies, give them their sixth." Then this was supposedly abrogated (نسخ) by His saying: «and the blood relatives are more entitled to each other». It was also said the meaning is: "Give them their share of help, blood money, and support, but no inheritance"; according to this, there is no abrogation in the verse.

It has also been said that they are those between whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) established brotherhood in Medina, and they used to inherit from each other based on that, then it was abrogated by the verse of inheritance.

It has also been said that it refers to adopted sons (الأدعياء) whom they used to adopt in the pre-Islamic era; they were commanded in Islam to make a bequest (وصية) for them, and that is His saying: «so give them their share».

These are meanings that the context of the verse and its wording do not support, as is not hidden from the reflecting researcher. Therefore, we have refrained from lengthening the discussion on the objections against them.

His saying, the Exalted: «Men are the managers of women, because of the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others, and by virtue of what they spend of their wealth» (الرِّجالُ قَوَّامُونَ عَلَى النِّساءِ بِما فَضَّلَ اللهُ بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ وَبِما أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ). The manager (القيم) is the one who manages the affairs of another, and qawwam (قوام) and qiyam (قيام) are intensive forms of it.

What is meant by "the advantage Allah has granted some of them over others" is that in which men naturally excel and exceed women, which is the increase in the power of intellection (التعقل) and what stems from it, such as intensity of courage, strength, and the capacity for arduous tasks and the like. For the life of women is a life of feeling and emotion (إحساسية عاطفية) built upon tenderness and delicacy. What is meant by "what they spend of their wealth" is what they spend on their dowers and maintenance.

The generality of this cause implies that the ruling based upon it—namely, His saying: «Men are the managers of women»—is not restricted to husbands such that the management is specific to a man over his wife. Rather, the ruling is established for the group of men over the group of women in general matters with which the lives of both groups are linked. Thus, general social matters linked to the excellence of men, such as the areas of government and judiciary upon which the life of society depends, are managed by intellection, which is naturally greater in men than in women. Likewise, military defense, which is linked to intensity and the power of intellection—all of these are matters in which men are managers over women.

Accordingly, His saying: «Men are the managers of women» has complete generality (إطلاق تام). As for His subsequent saying: «So righteous women are obedient...» (فَالصَّالِحاتُ قانِتاتٌ) etc., which appears specific to the relationship between a man and his wife as will come, it is a branch of this absolute ruling and a particular instance derived from it, without its generality being restricted by it.

His saying, the Exalted: «So righteous women are obedient, guardians of the unseen by what Allah has guarded» (فَالصَّالِحاتُ قانِتاتٌ حافِظاتٌ لِلْغَيْبِ بِما حَفِظَ اللهُ). "Righteousness" (الصلاح) is intended in its linguistic sense, which is expressed as the fitness of the soul. Obedience (القنوت) is the persistence in obedience and submission.

Its contrast with His saying: «As for those whose recalcitrance you fear...» (وَاللَّاتِي تَخافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَ) etc., indicates that "righteous women" refers to righteous wives, and that this ruling is imposed upon women in the state of marriage, not absolutely. His saying: «obedient, guardians» (قانِتاتٌ حافِظاتٌ)—which is a command given in the form of a description, i.e., "let them be obedient and let them guard"—is a ruling linked to the affairs of marriage and domestic life. This ruling, moreover, follows its cause in its breadth and narrowness, which is the management of the man over the woman in a marital capacity. Thus, she must be obedient to him and guard him in what pertains to the marital affairs between them.

In other words, just as the management of the group of men over the group of women in society only pertains to the general matters shared between them that are linked to the man's greater intellection and intensity—namely, government, judiciary, and war—without this nullifying the woman's independence in her individual will and her own actions, such that she may desire what she likes and do what she wishes without the man having the right to oppose her in any of that except in what is wrong (المنكر)—for there is no blame upon them in what they do with themselves in an honorable manner—likewise, the man's management of his wife does not mean that the woman's will and disposal are not effective in what she owns, nor that the woman is not independent in preserving her individual and social rights, defending them, and employing the means leading to them. Rather, its meaning is that since the man spends what he spends of his wealth in exchange for enjoyment (الاستمتاع), she must comply with him and obey him in everything linked to enjoyment and intimacy when he is present, and she must guard him in the unseen (الغيب). Thus, she must not betray him in his absence by allowing another into his bed, nor allow another to enjoy from her what only the husband may enjoy, nor betray him in the wealth he has placed under her hand and given her authority over within the context of marriage and partnership in domestic life.

So His saying: «So righteous women are obedient» means they should adopt the description of righteousness for themselves, and if they are righteous, they are inevitably obedient (قانِتاتٌ), meaning they must be obedient and follow their husbands persistently in what they desire from them regarding enjoyment. They must also guard their side in all their rights when they are absent.

As for His saying: «by what Allah has guarded» (بِما حَفِظَ اللهُ), it appears that "what" (ما) is an infinitive-forming particle (مصدرية) and the "by" (الباء) is for the instrument (للآلة). The meaning is: they are obedient to their husbands and guardians of the unseen by means of the rights Allah has guarded for them, as He legislated management for them and made obedience and guarding the unseen obligatory upon the women for their sake.

It is also possible that the "by" (الباء) is for compensation (للمقابلة), and the meaning then would be: obedience and guarding the unseen are obligatory upon them in exchange for the rights of theirs that Allah has guarded, as He revived their status in human society and made the dower and maintenance obligatory upon men for them. The first meaning is more apparent.

There are other meanings mentioned in the exegesis of the verse which we have refrained from mentioning because the context does not support any of them.

His saying, the Exalted: «As for those whose recalcitrance you fear, preach to them...» (وَاللَّاتِي تَخافُونَ نُشُوزَهُنَّ فَعِظُوهُنَّ). Recalcitrance (النشوز) is disobedience and arrogance against obedience. What is meant by "fearing recalcitrance" is the appearance of its signs and indications. Perhaps the derivation from the "fear" of recalcitrance rather than recalcitrance itself is to account for the state of preaching (العظة) among the three mentioned remedies. For preaching, just as it has a place when disobedience is actualized, also has a place when the signs and indications of disobedience begin to appear.

The three matters—namely, what is indicated by His saying: «preach to them, and forsake them in the sleeping places, and strike them»—even though they are mentioned together and joined by the conjunction "and" (الواو), are sequential and gradual matters. First is preaching; if it does not succeed, then forsaking; if it does not benefit, then striking. The fact that they are intended to be gradual is indicated by the fact that they are, by nature, different means of deterrence ranging from weakness to intensity according to the order taken in the speech. Thus, the sequence is understood from the context rather than the conjunction "and".

The apparent sense of His saying: «and forsake them in the sleeping places» (وَاهْجُرُوهُنَّ فِي الْمَضاجِعِ) is that the forsaking should be while maintaining the act of sharing the bed, such as turning one's back and ceasing playfulness and the like. Although it is possible to mean ceasing to share the bed altogether, this is far-fetched. The first meaning is perhaps supported by the use of the plural "sleeping places" (المضاجع), for the second meaning would not apparently require expressing a multiplicity of beds.

His saying, the Exalted: «then if they obey you, do not seek any course against them...» (فَإِنْ أَطَعْنَكُمْ فَلا تَبْغُوا عَلَيْهِنَّ سَبِيلاً) etc. That is: do not take against them any excuse by which you justify harming them while they are obedient to you. Then He justified this prohibition by saying: «Indeed Allah is All-exalted, All-great» (إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلِيًّا كَبِيراً). This is a reminder to them that the station of their Lord is All-exalted and All-great, so let them not be deceived by the strength and intensity they find in themselves and thus wrong them by being superior and arrogant toward them.

His saying, the Exalted: «If you fear a split between the two of them, then appoint...» (وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ شِقاقَ بَيْنِهِما فَابْعَثُوا). Split (الشقاق) is separation and enmity. Allah, the Glorified, has decreed the appointment of two arbiters so that it may be further from injustice and arbitrariness. His saying: «If they desire reconciliation, Allah will bring about agreement between them» (إِنْ يُريدا إِصْلاحاً يُوَفِّقِ اللهُ بَيْنَهُما) means: if the spouses desire a type of reconciliation without stubbornness and persistence in disagreement, then the surrender of their own choice and the handing over of the matter to the two pleasing arbiters will necessitate agreement between them.

He attributed the bringing about of agreement (التوفيق) to Allah despite the presence of the ordinary cause, which is their desire for reconciliation and compliance with what the two arbiters judge. This is because He, the Exalted, is the real cause who links causes to effects and is the one who gives every possessor of a right his right. Then He completed the speech by saying: «Indeed Allah is All-knowing, All-aware» (إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَلِيماً خَبِيراً), and its suitability is apparent.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Men's Management over Women)

The Quran's strengthening of the side of sound human intellection (العقل), its preference of it over whim and the following of desires, and its rejection of submission to the judgment of intense emotions and feelings—as well as its urging and encouraging to follow it and its recommendation to preserve this Divine trust from being lost—is something that cannot be hidden. There is no need to provide scriptural evidence for it, as the Quran contains numerous verses indicating this explicitly and implicitly, in every tongue and explanation.

Yet, the Quran did not neglect the matter of good, pure emotions and the importance of their beautiful effects through which the individual is raised and the backbone of society is established. Examples include His saying: «hard against the faithless and merciful among themselves» [48:29], and His saying: «that you may find rest in them, and He established between you love and mercy» [30:21], and His saying: «Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’» [7:32]. However, He balanced them by making them conform to the judgment of intellection, so following the judgment of these emotions and inclinations became a following of the judgment of intellection.

It has been mentioned in some previous discussions that part of Islam's preservation of the side of intellection and its building of its legislated rulings upon it is that all actions, states, and ethics that nullify the uprightness of intellection in its judgment and cause it to stumble in its decree and its evaluation of social affairs—such as drinking wine, gambling, types of hazardous transactions, lying, slander, fabrication, and backbiting—are all forbidden in the religion.

The reflecting researcher can guess from this much that it is necessary to entrust the reins of universal affairs and general social aspects—which should be managed by the power of intellection and in which the rule of emotions and psychological inclinations should be avoided, such as the areas of government, judiciary, and war—to those who are distinguished by greater intellection and in whom the rule of emotions is weaker, namely the group of men rather than women.

And so it is. Allah the Exalted said: «Men are the managers of women». The Prophetic Sunnah, which is the interpreter of the Quranic explanations, has explained it as such. His (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practice during his life followed this; he never appointed a woman over a people, nor gave a woman the office of judiciary, nor called them to a military expedition in the sense of calling them to fight.

As for other areas, such as teaching and learning, trades, nursing, medical treatment, and others in which the interference of emotions does not hinder the success of the work, the Sunnah did not prevent women from them. The Prophetic practice affirmed many of them, and the Book is also not devoid of evidence for the permissibility of that for them. For that is a necessary consequence of the freedom of will and action they were given in many affairs of life. There is no meaning in removing them from the authority of men and making ownership their own right, then forbidding them from managing what their hands possess in any manner of management. Likewise, there is no meaning in granting them the right of litigation or testimony, then preventing them from appearing before the governor or the judge, and so on.

Except, of course, in what conflicts with the husband's right; for he has over her the management of obedience in presence and guarding in absence, and none of her permissible affairs are valid if they conflict with that.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «And do not covet the advantage which Allah has given...» (وَلا تَتَمَنَّوْا ما فَضَّلَ اللهُ): That is, let none of you say: "If only the blessing and the beautiful woman given to so-and-so were mine"—for that would be envy (حسد). However, it is permissible to say: "O Allah, give me the like of it." He said: This is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the like of it in his Tafsir from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahrashub from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: «That is Allah’s grace which He grants to whomever He wishes» and regarding His saying: «And do not covet the advantage which Allah has given some of you over others»—that both were revealed concerning ‘Ali (peace be upon him).

I say: The narration is by way of application (الجري والتطبيق).

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Qummi, from Ibrahim ibn Abi al-Bilad from his father from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "There is no soul but that Allah has ordained for it its provision (الرزق)—lawfully it comes to it in well-being. He also presented to it the unlawful from another side; if it partakes of anything from the unlawful, He deducts it from the lawful that He had ordained for it. And with Allah, besides both, is much bounty, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «and ask Allah for His bounty»."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from Isma‘il ibn Kathir, attributing it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This meaning was also narrated from Abu al-Hudhayl from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and al-Qummi narrated something close to it in his Tafsir from al-Husayn ibn Muslim from al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

A discourse on the reality of provision (الرزق), its ordainment, and its division into lawful and unlawful provision has preceded under His saying: «and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning» [2:212] in the second volume, so refer to it.

In the Sahih of al-Tirmidhi, from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Ask Allah for His bounty, for Allah loves to be asked."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir recorded by way of Hakim ibn Jubayr from an unnamed man who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Ask Allah for His bounty, for Allah loves to be asked; and indeed, among the best of worship is waiting for relief (الفرج)."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Zurarah, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: «For everyone We have appointed heirs to what parents and near relatives leave»—he said: "He meant by that the blood relatives in inheritance, and he did not mean the patrons of favor (أولياء النعمة)—so the most entitled to the deceased is the closest to him from the womb that draws him to it."

In it also, with its chain to Ibrahim ibn Muhriz, who said: A man asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) while I was with him. He said: A man said to his wife: "Your matter is in your hand." He (peace be upon him) said: "How can this be when Allah says: «Men are the managers of women»? This is nothing."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded by way of Ash‘ath ibn ‘Abd al-Malik from al-Hasan, who said: A woman came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) seeking redress against her husband because he slapped her. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Retaliation (القصاص)." Then Allah revealed: «Men are the managers of women»—so she returned without retaliation.

I say: It was narrated through other chains from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In some of them: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "I intended one thing and Allah intended another." Perhaps the case was one of recalcitrance (نشوز), otherwise the end of the verse: «then if they obey you, do not seek any course against them» would negate that.

There is another problem in the apparent sense of the narrations, in that they suggest that his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying "Retaliation" was an explanation of the ruling in response to a question from the petitioner, not a judgment in a case where both parties were present. This would imply that the revelation of the verse was a correction of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his ruling and legislation, which contradicts his infallibility (عصمة). Nor is it abrogation (نسخ), for it would be the lifting of a ruling before it was acted upon. Although Allah, the Glorified, may modify some of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rulings by establishing or lifting them, that only occurs in his judgment and opinion in matters of his authority, not in his ruling in what he legislated for his community, for that would be a false correction.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: «obedient» (قانِتاتٌ), he says: "Submissive."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «preach to them, and forsake them in the sleeping places, and strike them»: From Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: "He turns his back to her." And regarding the meaning of striking, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "It is striking with a tooth-stick (السواك)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: «then appoint an arbiter from his family and an arbiter from her family»—he said: "The two arbiters may stipulate that if they wish they may separate, and if they wish they may unite. If they separate, it is permissible, and if they unite, it is permissible."

I say: This meaning and what is close to it were narrated through several other chains in it and in Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) judged in the case of a woman whom a man married and stipulated for her and her family that if he married another woman over her, or deserted her, or took a concubine over her, she would be divorced. He (peace be upon him) said: 'The stipulation of Allah comes before your stipulation; if he wishes he may fulfill his stipulation, and if he wishes he may keep his wife and marry over her, take a concubine over her, and desert her if she follows the path to that. Allah said in His Book: «then marry of women such as seem good to you, two, three, or four» and He said: «permitted to you is what your right hands possess» and He said: «As for those whose recalcitrance you fear, preach to them, and forsake them in the sleeping places, and strike them; then if they obey you, do not seek any course against them».'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bayhaqi recorded from Asma’ bint Yazid al-Ansariyyah that she came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was among his companions and said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you! I am the envoy of the women to you, and I know—may my soul be your ransom—that there is no woman in the East or the West who has heard of this journey of mine except that she is of the same opinion as me.

Indeed, Allah sent you with the truth to men and women, so we believed in you and in your God who sent you. We, the community of women, are confined and restricted, the foundations of your houses, the fulfillers of your desires, and the bearers of your children. You, the community of men, have been preferred over us by the Friday prayer, the congregational prayers, visiting the sick, attending funerals, Hajj after Hajj, and better than all that, Jihad in the way of Allah. When one of you goes out for Hajj, ‘Umrah, or to guard the frontiers, we preserve your wealth for you, spin your garments for you, and raise your children for you. Do we not share with you in the reward, O Messenger of Allah?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned his whole face toward his companions and said: "Have you ever heard the speech of a woman better in her questioning regarding her religion than this one?" They said: "O Messenger of Allah, we did not think a woman would be guided to the like of this." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned to her and said: "Go back, O woman, and inform the women behind you that a woman's good companionship (حسن تبعل) with her husband, her seeking his pleasure, and her following his agreement equals all of that." The woman turned away, saying "There is no god but Allah" and "Allah is Great" out of joy.

I say: The narrations with this meaning are many, recorded in the collections of Hadith from both Shi‘i and Sunni paths. Among the most beautiful of what was narrated in this regard is what was narrated in al-Kafi from Abu Ibrahim Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "The Jihad of the woman is good companionship." Among the most comprehensive words for this meaning, while containing the foundation upon which the legislation is built, is what is in Nahj al-Balaghah, also narrated in al-Kafi with its chain to ‘Abdullah ibn Kathir from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) from ‘Ali (upon whom be the best of peace), and with its chain also to al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from him (peace be upon him) in his letter to his son: "Indeed, the woman is a fragrant flower (ريحانة), and she is not a steward (قهرمانة)."

And what was narrated in this regard from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The woman is but a plaything; whoever takes her, let him not waste her." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to wonder: "How can a woman be embraced by a hand that has struck her?" In al-Kafi also, with its chain to Abu Maryam from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Does one of you strike the woman and then remain embracing her?!" Such explanations are many in the traditions, and from reflecting upon them, the view of Islam regarding them becomes clear.

Let us return to what we were discussing regarding the tradition of Asma’ bint Yazid al-Ansariyyah. We say: It appears from reflecting upon it and its likes—which recount women entering upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and speaking to him regarding the laws of religion and the various things Islam established for them—that despite their seclusion and their being mostly restricted to domestic matters of life, they were not prevented from approaching the guardian of the affair (ولي الأمر) and striving to solve what might be problematic for them. This is the freedom of belief (حرية الاعتقاد) which we discussed within the discourse on Islamic social bonding at the end of Surah Al ‘Imran.

From it and its likes, it is also benefited: First, that the pleasing way for a woman's life in Islam is to occupy herself with managing the internal affairs of the house and raising children. Although this is an established custom (سنة مسنونة) rather than an obligatory one, the recommended encouragement and urging—within the context of religion, the atmosphere of godwariness and seeking Allah's pleasure, and the preference for the reward of the Hereafter over the transient goods of this world, and the upbringing upon righteous ethics for women such as chastity, modesty, love for children, and attachment to domestic life—used to preserve this custom.

Occupying themselves with these affairs and devoting themselves to reviving the pure emotions placed in their existence used to keep them from entering the gatherings of men and mixing with them within the limits of what Allah permitted for them. This is evidenced by the persistence of this custom among Muslims for many centuries afterward, until the Western permissiveness called "women's freedom in society" penetrated them. This brought upon them and upon the men the destruction of ethics and the corruption of life, though they do not realize it; and they shall soon know. «Had the people of the towns believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened for them blessings from the sky and the earth; but they denied, so We seized them.»

Second: That among the obligatory customs in Islam is the prevention of women from undertaking the matter of Jihad, such as judiciary and governance.

Third: That Islam did not neglect the matter of these deprivations, such as depriving the woman of the virtue of Jihad in the way of Allah, without compensating her and mending her loss with what equals it in its sight of distinctions and virtues containing real pride. Just as it made good companionship, for example, the Jihad of the woman. These deeds and noble traits may not have much value to us—in our current corrupt vital context—but the Islamic context, which evaluates matters by their real values and in which people compete for human virtues pleasing to Allah, the Glorified, and which estimates them with their true worth, estimates for every person's conduct the path to which he was invited and his adherence to the road drawn for him. Thus, there is no superiority in Islam for martyrdom in the battlefield and the sacrifice of one's lifeblood—despite the virtue it contains—over the woman's adherence to her duty in marriage. Likewise, there is no pride for a governor who manages the wheel of vital society, nor for a judge who leans upon the seat of judgment—two positions for which those who hold them in this world, if they act with truth and follow the truth, gain nothing but the bearing of the burdens of governance and judiciary, and exposure to perils and risks that threaten them time after time regarding the rights of those who have no protector but the Lord of the worlds, and «Indeed your Lord is lying in wait»—so what pride do these have over the one whom the religion prevented from entering their place, and for whom it drew a line and pointed to its adherence and conduct?

These prides are only revived and their backbone established by people's preference for them in the type of society that raises its parts upon what it invites them to without contradiction. The variation of social affairs and human actions according to the variation of societies in their atmospheres is something no one can deny.

Behold the soldier who throws himself into the most dangerous of perils, which is death in the explosion of destructive bombs, seeking what he sees as dignity and increase—which is his belief that his name will be mentioned in the index of those who sacrificed themselves for their homeland. He prides himself on this over every possessor of pride, even while believing that death is loss and nullity; it is nothing but an illusory desire and a superstitious dignity. Likewise is the preference for these stars appearing in the sky of cinemas; people magnify their worth with a magnification that heads of high governments can hardly attain. Yet, the work they were engaged in and what they gave of themselves to the public for a long time in human societies was the greatest cause for the fall of women's status and the most hideous thing for which they were shamed. All of this is only because the context of life determines what it determines to be accepted by the masses, magnifying the insignificant and belittling the momentous. It is not far-fetched that Islam magnifies matters we belittle while we are in these turbulent contexts, or belittles matters we magnify and compete for. For the context in the early days of Islam was nothing but the context of godwariness and the preference for the Hereafter over the first life.

Ayahs

۞ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا وَبِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْجَارِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْجَارِ ٱلْجُنُبِ وَٱلصَّاحِبِ بِٱلْجَنۢبِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ مُخْتَالًا فَخُورًا (٣٦)
Worship Allah and do not ascribe any partners to Him, and be good to parents, the relatives, the orphans, the needy, the near neighbour and the distant neighbour, the companion at your side, the traveller, and your slaves. Indeed Allah does not like those who are arrogant and boastful. (36)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ وَيَكْتُمُونَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (٣٧)
—Those who are [themselves] stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy, and conceal whatever Allah has given them out of His bounty; and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (37)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ وَمَن يَكُنِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لَهُۥ قَرِينًا فَسَآءَ قَرِينًا (٣٨)
And those who spend their wealth to be seen by people, and believe neither in Allah nor in the Last Day. As for him who has Satan for his companion—an evil companion is he! (38)
وَمَاذَا عَلَيْهِمْ لَوْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمْ عَلِيمًا (٣٩)
What harm would it have done them had they believed in Allah and the Last Day, and spent out of what Allah has provided them? Allah knows them well. (39)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ ۖ وَإِن تَكُ حَسَنَةً يُضَـٰعِفْهَا وَيُؤْتِ مِن لَّدُنْهُ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٤٠)
Indeed Allah does not wrong [anyone] [even to the extent of] an atom’s weight, and if it be a good deed He doubles it[s reward], and gives from Himself a great reward. (40)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍۭ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شَهِيدًا (٤١)
So how shall it be, when We bring a witness from every nation and We bring you as a witness to them? (41)
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَعَصَوُا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَوْ تُسَوَّىٰ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَا يَكْتُمُونَ ٱللَّهَ حَدِيثًا (٤٢)
On that day those who were faithless and [who] disobeyed the Apostle will wish the earth were levelled with them, and they will not conceal any matter from Allah. (42)

(Commentary)

These are seven verses containing an exhortation toward excellence (الإحسان) and spending in the way of Allah, along with a beautiful promise for doing so. They also contain a censure for abandoning it, whether due to stinginess (البخل) or due to spending out of ostentation (مراءاة) to be seen by people.

His saying, the Exalted: "Worship Allah and do not ascribe any partner to Him"—this is monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts—except that what is intended here is practical monotheism (التوحيد العملي). This refers to performing righteous deeds (الأعمال الحسنة)—among which is the excellence (الإحسان) that is the subject of this discourse—seeking the pleasure of Allah and desiring the reward of the Hereafter, rather than following whims (الهوى) or associating partners with Him.

The evidence for this is that the Exalted followed this statement—meaning His saying: "Worship Allah and do not ascribe any partner to Him"—and justified it with His saying: "Indeed Allah does not love someone who is a self-conceited boaster." He then mentioned that such a person is the one who is stingy with his wealth and the one who spends to be seen by people; these are the ones who associate partners with Allah and do not worship Him alone. Then He said: "What harm would it have done them had they believed in Allah and the Last Day and spent..." Thus, it became clear that their polytheism (شرك) was their lack of belief in the Last Day. The Exalted also said: "and do not follow desire (الهوى) lest it should lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning" (38:26). He explained that straying through following whims—and every form of polytheism is straying—is only due to forgetting the Day of Reckoning. Then He said: "Have you seen him who has taken his desire (هواه) to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly" (45:23). He thus explained that following whims is a form of worshiping them and associating them as partners with Him.

From all this, it becomes clear that practical monotheism (التوحيد العملي) is for a person to act seeking the reward of Allah while being mindful of the Day of Reckoning, wherein rewards and punishments are manifested. Conversely, polytheism in action (الشرك في العمل) is to forget the Last Day—for had he truly believed in it, he would not have forgotten it—and to perform his deed not to seek reward, but for what his whims have beautified for him, such as attachment to wealth or the praise of people and the like. Such a person has set up his whim as an object before his Lord and has associated it as a partner with Him.

Therefore, what is intended by the worship of Allah and sincerity (الإخلاص) toward Him therein is that it be done seeking His pleasure and desiring His reward, not for the following of whims.

His saying, the Exalted: "and [show] kindness to parents" until His saying: "your right hands possess." It appears that the word "kindness" (إحساناً) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an elided verb, the estimation of which is: "and act with excellence toward parents with a [great] excellence." The term excellence (الإحسان) can be transitive with both the particles bi (بـ) and ila (إلى); one says, "I acted with excellence with him" and "I acted with excellence toward him." His saying: "and to the near of kin" and what follows it are coordinated (معطوف) with "parents." The "near of kin" (ذو القربى) refers to relatives. Regarding His saying: "and the neighbor who is a kinsman, and the neighbor who is a stranger," the context of contrast in the descriptions suggests that the "neighbor who is a kinsman" (الجار ذي القربى) refers to the neighbor whose house is nearby, while the "neighbor who is a stranger" (الجار الجنب)—meaning the outsider—refers to the neighbor whose house is distant. It has been narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he defined the neighborhood as forty cubits, and in another narration, forty houses. Perhaps the two narrations refer respectively to the neighbor who is a kinsman and the neighbor who is a stranger.

His saying: "and the companion by your side" (الصاحب بالجنب) refers to the one who accompanies you, remaining by your side. In its general sense, it encompasses the travel companion among the fellow travelers on the road, as well as the companion in residence, at home, and others. His saying: "and the traveler" (ابن السبيل) — literally "the son of the road" — refers to the one about whose condition nothing is known except that he has asked you for a way; it is as if he has no one to be attributed to except the road, so he is its "son." As for his being poor, destitute, or lacking provisions or a mount, this seems to fall outside the literal linguistic meaning of the term. His saying: "and what your right hands possess" refers to male and female slaves, based on the context of their being counted among those to whom excellence should be shown. It is common to express them using the relative pronoun ma (ما) — used for inanimate or non-rational beings — referring to "what" the right hands possess, rather than "who" (من).

His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah does not love someone who is a self-conceited boaster." The self-conceited (المختال) is the one who is lost in pride, strutting about, and enslaved by his own imagination (خياله). From this comes the word khayl (خيل) for a horse, because it struts in its gait. The boaster (الفخور) is one who frequently boasts. These two attributes—self-conceit (الاختيال) and frequent boasting—are among the necessary concomitants of attachment to wealth and status, and excess in loving them. Therefore, Allah would not love the self-conceited boaster because his heart is attached to other than Him, the Exalted. What He mentioned in its explanation by His saying: "those who are stingy..." etc., and His saying: "and those who spend their wealth to be seen by people..." etc., clarifies that both groups are prone to self-conceit and boasting. The first group's heart is attached to wealth, and the second to status, even if there is a general correlation between status and wealth.

The natural flow of the discourse would have been to begin by mentioning their deeds, such as stinginess and concealment, but He began with the two descriptions to indicate the reason for the lack of [divine] love, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: "those who are stingy and bid the people to be stingy"—the verse. Their bidding people to be stingy is through their corrupt conduct and their acting upon it, whether they bid it verbally or remain silent. Since this group possesses fortune and wealth, people seek proximity to them and humble themselves before them due to the greed inherent in human nature; thus, their actions command and forbid just as their words do. As for their concealing what Allah has given them of His grace, it is their pretending to be destitute and lacking wealth because they are annoyed by people asking for what is in their hands, and out of fear for themselves if they were to refuse, and their dread of people's eyes turning toward their wealth. What is intended by "the faithless" (الكافرين) here are those who cover up (الساترون) the blessing of Allah which He has bestowed; from this comes the well-known term "disbeliever" (الكافر) because of his covering up the truth by denying it.

His saying, the Exalted: "and those who spend their wealth to be seen by people," etc., meaning for their ostentation (رئاء). In the verse, there is an indication that ostentation in spending—or ostentation in general—is a form of polytheism (شرك) toward Allah that reveals a lack of faith in Him, because the ostentatious person relies on the souls of people and their approval of his deed. It is also polytheism from the perspective of the act itself, because the ostentatious person does not intend the reward of the Hereafter by his deed; he only intends the worldly results he hopes for from his spending. Furthermore, it indicates that the ostentatious person is a companion of Satan, and what an evil companion he is.

His saying, the Exalted: "What harm would it have done them had they believed..."—the verse. This is an interrogative expressing regret or wonder. In the verse, there is an indication that the refusal to spend in the way of Allah stems from a lack of true investment in faith in Allah and the Last Day, even if one is clothed in it outwardly.
