Directing the discourse to the believers reveals that what is meant by contention is their contention among themselves, not an assumed contention between them and the Vestiges of Authority, nor an assumed contention among the Vestiges of Authority. For the first—namely, contention between them and the Vestiges of Authority—does not suit the mandate of the Vestiges of Authority's obedience upon them. Likewise, the second—namely, contention among the Vestiges of Authority—for the mandate of obedience does not suit a contention in which one of the two sides is upon falsehood. Furthermore, it does not suit the discourse being directed to the believers in His saying: فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it).

The word "thing" (شيء), although it encompasses every ruling and command from Allah, the Messenger, and the Vestiges of Authority, whatever it may be, His saying after that: فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ (refer it to Allah and the Apostle) indicates that what is assumed is the dispute regarding something in which the Vestiges of Authority do not have independence and absolute discretion (الاستبداد) from their commands within the circle of their guardianship—such as their command to mobilize, or for war, or peace, or otherwise. For there is no meaning to the obligation of referral to Allah and the Messenger in these cases while their obedience in them is mandated.

Thus, the verse indicates the obligation of referral in the religious rulings themselves, in which no one has the right to rule by execution or abrogation except Allah and His Messenger. The verse is like an explicit statement that no one has the right to dispose of a religious ruling legislated by Allah and His Messenger; the Vestiges of Authority and those below them are equal in that.

His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ (if you believe in Allah) is an intensification (تشديد) of the ruling and an indication that opposing it only arises from corruption in the stage of faith. Thus, the ruling is linked to it with a connection; opposition reveals a pretense of the attribute of faith in Allah and His Messenger while harboring disbelief, which is hypocrisy (النفاق), as indicated by the subsequent verses.

His saying: ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلاً (That is better and more favorable in outcome [4:59]), meaning the referral during contention, or the obedience to Allah, the Messenger, and the Vestiges of Authority. The outcome (التأويل) — the ultimate reality or consequence — is the real interest (المصلحة الواقعية) from which the ruling arises and which then results from the action. The discussion of its meaning preceded under His saying, the Exalted: وَابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ (and seeking its interpretation; and no one knows its interpretation except Allah [3:7]) in the third volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يَزْعُمُونَ أَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ (Have you not regarded those who claim that they believe in what has been sent down to you [4:60]) to the end of the verse. Claim (الزعم) is the belief in something whether it matches reality or not, unlike knowledge (العلم), which is the belief that matches reality. Because "claim" is used for belief in cases that do not match reality, it is sometimes thought that non-matching with reality is part of its concept, but it is not so. Taghut (الطاغوت) is a verbal noun meaning transgression (الطغيان), like rahabut, jabarut, and malakut, except that it is sometimes uttered to mean the active participle (اسم الفاعل) for emphasis. It is said: "the water transgressed" (طغى الماء) when it exceeded its container due to its abundance. Its use for a human was initially by way of metaphor (استعارة), then it became common and joined the literal meaning; it is the human's departure from the state defined for him by reason or law. Thus, the Taghut is the oppressor, the tyrant, and the one who rebels against the duties of servitude to Allah out of arrogance toward Him, and so on. To this returns what was said: that the Taghut is every deity worshipped besides Allah.

His saying: بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ (in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you) is in the position of saying: "in what Allah sent down to His messengers." He did not say: "they believed in you and in those before you," because the discussion is about the obligation of referral to the Book of Allah and His ruling. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by His saying: وَقَدْ أُمِرُوا أَنْ يَكْفُرُوا بِهِ (while they have been commanded to defy it) is the command in the heavenly Books and the revelation (الوحي) sent down to the prophets: Muhammad and those before him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ (Have you not regarded) etc. The speech is in the position of repelling an objection (دفع الدخل), as if it were said: "What is the reason for mentioning His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ etc.?" So it was said: "Have you not regarded their failure in obedience when they desire to seek judgment from the Taghut?" The interrogation is for regret (التأسف), and the meaning is: "It is a matter of regret what you have seen—that some people, while believing that they are believers in what was sent down to you of the Book and to the rest of the prophets, and the heavenly Books were only sent down to judge between people in what they differed about—as Allah clarified to them by His saying: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ (Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed [2:213])—they seek judgment during contention from the Taghut, who are the people of transgression and those who rebel against the religion of Allah, exceeding the truth. They have been commanded in these Books to disbelieve in the Taghut, and it is sufficient in prohibiting seeking judgment from them that it is an annulment of the Books of Allah and an invalidation of His laws."

In His saying: وَيُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُمْ ضَلالاً بَعِيداً (and Satan desires to lead them astray into far-reaching error [4:60]), there is an indication that their seeking judgment is only by the casting of Satan and his seduction, and the direction in it is far-reaching error.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا "And when it is said to them, ‘Come...’" (4:61) until the end of the verse. The word تعالوا (Come) is originally a command derived from al-ta‘ali (التعالي), which means rising or ascending. The phrase يصد (he turns away) comes from sadda (صد), meaning he averted or turned aside. His saying: إِلى ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَإِلَى الرَّسُولِ "to what Allah has sent down and to the Apostle" is in the position of saying: "to the judgment of Allah and the one who judges by it." In His saying: يَصُدُّونَ عَنْكَ "turning away from you," the Apostle was specifically mentioned as the object of their aversion—even though they were called to both the Book and the Apostle together—because the sorrow arises from the actions of those who claim to have believed in what Allah sent down. Thus, they are not disbelievers (كافرين) who would openly declare their aversion to the Book of Allah; rather, they are hypocrites (منافقون) in reality, who outwardly display faith in what Allah has sent down but turn away from His Apostle.

From here it becomes clear that making a distinction between Allah and His Apostle—by accepting the judgment of Allah while hesitating regarding the judgment of the Apostle—is absolute hypocrisy (نفاق).

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَيْفَ إِذا أَصابَتْهُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ "But how will it be when a misfortune strikes them..." (4:62) etc., is an announcement that this aversion and turning away from the judgment of Allah and His Apostle, and the turning toward another—namely the judgment of the Taghut (الطاغوت) — the transgressive power that exceeds the bounds of Allah's law—will result in a misfortune that strikes them, for which there is no cause except this turning away from the judgment of Allah and His Apostle and seeking judgment from the Taghut. His saying: ثُمَّ جاؤُكَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللهِ "then they come to you, swearing by Allah..." is a narration of their excuse, claiming they did not intend any evil by their reliance on the judgment of the Taghut. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: if this is their state, how will they act when the evil consequence of their deeds strikes them, and they then come to you swearing by Allah, saying: "We intended nothing by seeking judgment from other than the Book and the Apostle except goodness, reconciliation, and the settlement of disputes between the litigants."

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُ اللهُ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ "They are the ones whom Allah knows what is in their hearts..." (4:63) etc., is a refutation of what they offered as an excuse. He did not explicitly mention the state of what is in their hearts—namely, a corrupt inner state—because His saying: فأعرض عنهم وعظهم "so turn away from them and advise them" indicates this. For if what was in their hearts were not corrupt, their words would have been truthful and right, and one is not commanded to turn away from one who speaks the truth and is sincere in his speech.

His saying: وَقُلْ لَهُمْ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قَوْلاً بَلِيغاً "and speak to them reaching their souls with a far-reaching word" means a word that reaches into their souls, conveying what You want them to realize and understand regarding the corruption of this conduct, and that it is hypocrisy which, if exposed, would bring upon them woe from the wrath of Allah the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64) is an absolute refutation of everything previously narrated regarding these hypocrites—their seeking judgment from the Taghut, their turning away from the Apostle, and their swearing and excusing themselves by claiming [intentions of] goodness and reconciliation. All of this constitutes opposition to the Apostle in some way, whether accompanied by an excuse or not. Allah has made obedience to him obligatory without any restriction or condition, for He did not send him except to be obeyed by Allah's leave. No one should imagine that the obedience to be followed is only the obedience to Allah, and that the Apostle is merely a human being from among His creation who is only obeyed to achieve some benefit—such that if benefit is secured without obeying him, there is no harm in acting independently to secure it while leaving the Apostle aside. [To think otherwise] would be considered associating partners with Allah (إشراكا بالله) and worshipping His Apostle alongside Him. This [misconception] would sometimes manifest in matters they discussed with the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; when he would resolve upon an important matter, one of them would say to him: "Is this an order from Allah or from you?"

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned that the obligation of obeying the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is an absolute obligation, and it is nothing other than the obedience to Allah, for it is by His leave. This is similar to what is conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ "Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah" (4:80).

Then He mentioned that if they had returned to Allah and the Apostle in repentance when they opposed the Apostle by turning away, it would have been better for them than swearing by Allah and fabricating unjustifiable excuses that neither benefit nor please the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, because Allah, the Glorified, informs him of the reality of the matter. This is His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جاؤُكَ "Had they, when they wronged themselves, come to you..." (4:64) until the end of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge..." (4:65) etc. The word الشجر (with a sukun on the jim) and الشجور mean mixing or entanglement. It is said: shajara shajran wa shujuran (شجر شجرا وشجورا), meaning it became entangled. From this comes al-tashajur (التشاجر) and al-mushajarah (المشاجرة) — dispute or contention — as if the claims or words have become entangled with one another. From this, trees are called shajar (شجر) due to the entanglement of their branches with one another. The word الحرج (haraj) means constriction or tightness.

The apparent context at first glance suggests that this is a refutation of the hypocrites' claim that they believed in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while seeking judgment from the Taghut. Thus, the meaning is: it is not as they claim—that they believe while seeking judgment from the Taghut—rather, they do not believe until they make you a judge, etc.

However, the fact that the ruling of the ultimate goal—namely His saying: حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ "until they make you a judge"—includes those other than the hypocrites, as does His subsequent saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ "And had We ordained for them..." until His saying: ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ "they would not have done it except a few of them," supports the view that the refutation is not exclusive to the hypocrites. Rather, it encompasses them and others, based on the fact that their apparent state is that they imagine that mere assent to what was sent down from Allah—including its knowledge and laws—is true faith (إيمان) in Allah, His Apostle, and what he brought from his Lord. But it is not so; rather, faith is complete submission (تسليم), both inwardly and outwardly. How can it be possible for a true believer not to submit to the Apostle's judgment outwardly by turning away from him and opposing him, or in his inner self by feeling constriction regarding the Apostle's judgment if it opposes his own desires? Allah the Exalted has said to His Apostle: لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ "so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you" (4:105).

Therefore, if anyone feels constriction regarding what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله has decreed, he has felt constriction regarding the judgment of Allah, for He is the One who honored him by making obedience to him mandatory and his judgment binding.

If they submit to the judgment of the Apostle and their hearts feel no constriction from it, they have certainly submitted to the judgment of Allah, whether in His legislative (التشريعي) or creative (التكويني) decree. This is one of the stations of faith (الإيمان) in which the believer is clothed with several virtuous attributes, the most prominent of which is submission (التسليم) to the command of Allah. In this station, constriction, objection, and rejection fall away from the believer's tongue and heart. In the verse, submission (التسليم) is mentioned in an absolute sense.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ "But no, by your Lord!" until the end of the verse, even if it is restricted to submission to the judgment of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله according to the literal wording—since the occasion of the verses is their seeking judgment from other than the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله despite the obligation to return to him—the meaning is general to the judgment of both Allah and His Apostle, and to both legislative and creative decrees, as you have seen.

In fact, the meaning encompasses judgment in the sense of the decree of the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and every path he took or action he performed, because the effect is shared. Everything that is attributed in any way to Allah and His Apostle, in whatever manner, cannot be rejected, objected to, tired of, or disliked in any way by a believer who truly believes in Allah. All of that constitutes polytheism (شرك) in its various degrees. Allah the Exalted has said: وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him" (12:106).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ "And had We ordained for them..." until His saying: ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ "they would not have done it except a few of them" (4:66). It has been previously mentioned regarding His saying: وَلكِنْ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً "but Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness, so they do not believe except a few" (4:46), that this construction indicates that the judgment applies to the collective body of individuals—that is, the society—and that the exception is to prevent the illusion that the judgment encompasses and exhausts every single individual. Therefore, this exception is more akin to the "disjunctive exception" (المنفصل) than the "continuous exception" (المتصل), or it is an intermediate realm (برزخ) between the two types of exception because it possesses two aspects.

Accordingly, His saying: ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ "they would not have done it except a few of them" is presented as a report about the state of the collective whole: that they do not fulfill the restrictive and difficult laws and obligations that touch upon what their hearts are deeply attached to, such as their lives and their homes. The exception of the "few" is to dispel any misconception.

The meaning is: "And had We ordained (كتبنا)—that is, made mandatory—upon them to kill themselves or to leave their homes and familiar homelands, they would not have done it"—meaning they would not have fulfilled Our command. Then, since His saying "they would not have done it" might give the impression that there were none among them who were true believers truly submitting to Allah's judgment, He dispelled that by excepting a few of them. The judgment did not truly encompass them because the report was about the state of the society insofar as it is a society, and individuals were only included as part of the whole.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant is the killing of the collective by the collective, and the departure and exile of the collective from their collective homes, such as a town or village, rather than every single person killing himself and leaving his own house, as in His saying, the Exalted: فَتُوبُوا إِلى بارِئِكُمْ فَاقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ "so turn to your Maker in repentance, and slay yourselves" (2:54), for the intended recipient of the address is the group, not the individuals.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ فَعَلُوا ما يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتاً "And had they done what they were advised, it would have been better for them and more strengthening [for their faith]" (4:66). The replacement of "ordaining" (الكتابة) in His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ "And had We ordained for them" with "advice" (الوعظ) in His saying: ما يُوعَظُونَ بِهِ "what they were advised" is an indication that these laws, which appear in the form of commands and obligations, are nothing but pointers to what contains their welfare and happiness. Thus, they are in reality exhortations and counsels intended for their own good and well-being.

His saying: لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ "it would have been better for them" means in everything concerning them, in both their worldly life and the Hereafter. This is because the good of the Hereafter is inseparable from the good of this world; rather, it follows from it. His saying: وَأَشَدَّ تَثْبِيتاً "and more strengthening" means for their souls and hearts in faith, since the discourse is about it. Allah the Exalted said: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word..." (14:27).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذاً لَآتَيْناهُمْ مِنْ لَدُنَّا أَجْراً عَظِيماً "Then We would have surely given them from Us a great reward" (4:67), meaning when they become firm in steadfast faith. The discussion regarding the ambiguity of His saying: أَجْراً عَظِيماً "a great reward" is the same as the discussion regarding the absolute nature of His saying: لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ "it would have been better for them."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهَدَيْناهُمْ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً "and We would have surely guided them to a straight path" (4:68). The discussion regarding the meaning of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — has already passed under the commentary of His saying: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ "Guide us on the straight path" (1:6) in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle..." until His saying: حَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً "and what excellent companions they are!" (4:69). He combined Allah and the Apostle in this beautiful promise, even though the preceding verses dealt with obeying the Apostle and submitting to his judgment and decree, because the mention of Allah the Exalted was interspersed among them in His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ "And had We ordained for them," etc. Thus, the mandated obedience is the obedience to Him, the Exalted, and the obedience to His Apostle. The discourse began in this manner in His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ "Obey Allah and obey the Apostle..." (4:59).

His saying: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ "they are with those whom Allah has blessed" indicates joining (اللحوق) rather than becoming (الصيرورة). Thus, these people are joined to the group of those who have been blessed, who are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), which in His speech, the Exalted, is not attributed to anyone other than this group in His saying: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:6-7). In short, they are joined to them without becoming [identical to] them, as His saying: وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً "and what excellent companions they are!" is not without an allusion to this. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by this blessing (النعمة) is the guardianship (الولاية) — the divine authority and proximity to Allah.

As for these four groups—namely the prophets (النبيين), the truthful (الصديقين), the witnesses (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين)—the prophets are the recipients of revelation (الوحي) who possess news of the Unseen (الغيب). We have no knowledge of their state beyond that except through its effects. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by the witnesses (الشهداء) are the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال) as the term "witness" is used in the Qur'an, rather than those martyred on the battlefield. The righteous (الصالحين) are those who are worthy of Allah's blessings.

As for the truthful (الصديقون), what its wording indicates is that it is an intensive form of truthfulness (الصدق). Truthfulness can be in speech and it can be in action. Truthfulness in action is its conformity to speech, because action narrates the belief; if it is truthful in its narration, it narrates what is in the inner self without discrepancy. Truthfulness in speech is its conformity to reality. Since speech itself is a type of action, the one who is truthful in his actions only reports what he knows to be true and right. Thus, in his words, there is both objective truthfulness (الصدق الخبري) and subjective sincerity (المخبري).

So the truthful one (الصديق) who never lies at all is he who only does what he sees as the truth without following personal desire, and only says what he sees as the truth, and sees nothing but the truth. Thus, he witnesses the realities of things, speaks the truth, and acts upon the truth.

Accordingly, the ranks are ordered: the prophets (النبيون) are the masters; then the truthful (الصديقون), who are the witnesses of realities and deeds; then the witnesses (الشهداء), who are the witnesses of deeds; and then the righteous (الصالحين), who are those prepared for divine honor.

His saying, the Exalted: وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً "and what excellent companions they are!"—meaning in terms of companionship. It is said that this is why it was not pluralized [in Arabic]. It is also said the meaning is: "each one of them is excellent as a companion," and it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) similar to His saying: ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلاً "then We bring you out as infants" (22:5).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ الْفَضْلُ مِنَ اللهِ وَكَفى بِاللهِ عَلِيماً "That is the grace from Allah, and Allah suffices as Knower" (4:70). The advancement of ذلِكَ (That) and its use in the demonstrative form indicating distance, along with the inclusion of the definite article in the predicate, indicates the magnification of this grace, as if it is the entirety of grace. The verse concludes with knowledge (العلم) because the discourse is about the degrees of faith (الإيمان), which cannot be distinguished except by divine knowledge.

Know that in these noble verses, there are numerous instances of rhetorical transition (الالتفات) interwoven with one another. The believers are addressed in the second person at the beginning of the verses, then in His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ "And had We ordained for them," they are referred to in the third person, as previously mentioned. Similarly, He, the Exalted, refers to Himself in the third person at the beginning of the verses in His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ "Obey Allah," then in the first person plural in His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ "We did not send any apostle," then in the third person in His saying: بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "by Allah's leave," then in the first person plural in His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا "And had We ordained," then in the third person in His saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle."

Likewise, the Apostle is referred to in the third person at the beginning of the verses in His saying: وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ "and obey the Apostle," then addressed in the second person in His saying: ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ "that is better," then referred to in the third person in His saying: وَاسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ الرَّسُولُ "and the Apostle had pleaded for forgiveness for them," then addressed in the second person in His saying: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ "But no, by your Lord," then referred to in the third person in His saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle," then addressed in the second person in His saying: وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ "and what excellent [companions they are]." These are ten instances of rhetorical transition, and the subtle points specific to each instance are apparent to the contemplative reader.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh, with his chain of transmission from Jabir ibn Abdillah al-Ansari: "When Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down to His Prophet Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ (O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you), I said: 'O Apostle of Allah, we know Allah and His Apostle—but who are those vested with authority (أولو الأمر) whose obedience Allah has coupled with your obedience?' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'They are my successors, O Jabir, and the leaders (الأئمة) of the Muslims after me. The first of them is Ali ibn Abi Talib, then al-Hasan, then al-Husayn, then Ali ibn al-Husayn, then Muhammad ibn Ali, known in the Torah as al-Baqir—you shall live to see him, O Jabir, so when you meet him, convey my greetings to him—then al-Sadiq Ja‘far ibn Muhammad, then Musa ibn Ja‘far, then Ali ibn Musa, then Muhammad ibn Ali, then Ali ibn Muhammad, then al-Hasan ibn Ali, then my namesake Muhammad and the one who bears my kunya, the Proof of Allah (حجة الله) on His earth and His remnant (بقيته) among His servants, the son of al-Hasan ibn Ali. He is the one through whose hands Allah, exalted be His mention, will conquer the easts of the earth and its wests. He is the one who will be hidden from his followers (شيعته) and his friends in an occultation (غيبة) such that no one will remain firm upon the belief in his leadership (إمامته) except those whose hearts Allah has tested for faith.'"

Jabir said: "I said to him: 'O Apostle of Allah, will his followers benefit from him during his occultation?' He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'Yes, by the One who sent me with the Prophethood! They will seek light from his light and benefit from his guardianship (ولايته) during his occultation just as people benefit from the sun even when it is covered by clouds. O Jabir, this is from the hidden secret of Allah and the stored knowledge of Allah, so keep it concealed except from those worthy of it.'"

I say: From al-Nu‘mani, with his chain of transmission from Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali, from Ali عليه‌السلام, there is a narration with the same meaning as the previous one. Ali ibn Ibrahim also narrated it with his chain from Sulaym from him عليه‌السلام. There are other narrations from both Shi‘i and Sunni paths in which their leadership (إمامتهم) is mentioned by their names. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the book Yanabi‘ al-Mawaddah, the book Ghayat al-Maram by al-Bahrani, and others.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Jabir al-Ju‘fi, who said: "I asked Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام about this verse: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ. He said: 'The executors (الأوصياء) — the divinely appointed successors of the Prophet.'"

I say: In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Umar ibn Sa‘id from Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام, there is a similar narration, which adds: "Ali ibn Abi Talib and the executors after him."

From Ibn Shahrashub: "Al-Hasan ibn Salih asked al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام about that, and he said: 'The Imams (الأئمة) from the People of the House (أهل بيت) of the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.'"

I say: Al-Saduq narrated something similar from Abu Basir from al-Baqir عليه‌السلام, which adds: "He said: 'The Imams from the progeny of Ali and Fatimah until the Hour is established.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu Masruq from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I said to him: 'We debate the theologians (أهل الكلام) and we argue against them using the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ, but they say: "It was revealed concerning the believers." And we argue against them using the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives), but they say: "It was revealed concerning the relatives of the Muslims."'" He said: "I did not leave anything that occurred to me from this and its like but that I mentioned it." He said to me: "If that is the case, then invite them to mutual imprecation (المباهلة) — the act of calling for Allah's curse upon the liar among the disputing parties." I said: "How should I do it?" He said: "Refine your soul for three days and purify it." He said: "Fast, perform the ritual bath (اغتسل), and go out, you and he, to the mountains. Then intertwine the fingers of your right hand with his fingers, then be fair to him, start with yourself, and say: 'O Allah, Lord of the seven heavens and Lord of the seven earths, Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful, if Abu Masruq has denied a truth or claimed a falsehood, then send down upon him a bolt from the sky and a painful punishment.' Then turn the invocation upon him and say: 'And if he has denied a truth or claimed a falsehood, then send down upon him a bolt from the sky and a painful punishment.'"

Then he said to me: "For you will not wait long before you see that in him." [Abu Masruq said:] "By Allah, I never found anyone who would agree to do it with me."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abdillah ibn Ajlan from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ, he said: "It is concerning Ali and the Imams; Allah has placed them in the positions of the prophets, except that they do not make anything lawful (يحلون) nor do they make anything forbidden (يحرمونه)."

I say: The exception in the narration is what we previously mentioned in the commentary on the verse: that it indicates there is no legislative judgment except for Allah and His Apostle.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Burayd ibn Mu‘awiyah, who said: "Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام recited: 'Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you—and if you fear a dispute in the matter—then refer it to Allah, to the Apostle, and to those vested with authority among you.'"

He said: "How could He command obedience to them and then permit disputing with them? He only said that to the deviants (المارقين) who were told: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ."

I say: The narration indicates nothing more than that what he عليه‌السلام recited was an interpretation (تفسير) of the verse and an explanation of its intended meaning. The previous commentary clarified its indication of this. The intention was not that this was the [literal] reading (القراءة), as might be sensed from the phrase "Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام recited."

This is supported by the variation in wording found in the narrations, as in Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain from Hariz from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "It was revealed: 'If you dispute in anything—then refer it to Allah, to the Apostle, and to those vested with authority among you.'"

And what is in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Burayd ibn Mu‘awiyah from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام (which is the previous narration of al-Kafi): "In the hadith: Then he said to the people: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, thus gathering the believers until the Day of Resurrection, أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ—He meant us specifically—'and if you fear a dispute in the matter—then return to Allah, to the Apostle, and to those vested with authority among you.' Thus it was revealed. And how could He command them to obey those vested with authority and then permit them to dispute with them? That was only said to those commanded, who were told: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: In the narration of Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, he said: "It was revealed (meaning the verse أَطِيعُوا اللهَ) concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام." I said to him: "People say to us: 'What prevented Him from naming Ali and his household in His Book?'" Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام replied: "Tell them: 'Allah sent down the prayer (الصلاة) to His Apostle, but He did not name three or four [units] until it was the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله who explained that to them. And He sent down the pilgrimage (الحج) but did not send down "circumambulate seven times" until the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله explained that to them. And Allah sent down: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ; it was revealed concerning Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn عليه‌السلام. And he said concerning Ali: "Whomever I am his master (مولاه), then Ali is his master." And the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I enjoin you to the Book of Allah and my household (أهل بيتي); I asked Allah not to separate them until they return to me at the Pond (الحوض), and He granted me that." And he said: "Do not teach them, for they are more knowledgeable than you. They will never take you out of a door of guidance, nor will they ever enter you into a door of misguidance." If the Apostle of Allah had remained silent and not clarified its people, the family of Abbas, the family of Aqil, and the family of so-and-so would have claimed it. But Allah sent down in His Book: إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيراً (Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification). Thus, Ali, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and Fatimah عليه‌السلام were the realization (تأويل) of this verse. So the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله took the hand of Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and brought them under the cloak (الكساء) in the house of Umm Salamah and said: "O Allah, every prophet has a weighty trust (ثقلا) and a household; these are my weighty trust and my household." Umm Salamah said: "Am I not from your household?" He said: "You are toward good, but these are my weighty trust and my household."'" (The hadith).

I say: It is narrated in al-Kafi with its chain from Abu Basir from him عليه‌السلام similarly, with a slight variation in wording.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahrashub from the Tafsir of Mujahid: "It was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful when the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله left him behind in Medina. He said: 'O Apostle of Allah, are you leaving me behind with the women and children?' He replied: 'O Commander of the Faithful, are you not pleased to be to me as Aaron was to Moses? When he said to him: اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي وَأَصْلِحْ (Take my place among my people, and put things right). So Allah said: وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ.'"

He said: "Ali ibn Abi Talib was the one whom Allah vested with the authority over the nation after Muhammad, when the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله left him behind in Medina; so Allah commanded the servants to obey him and abandon opposing him."

In the same source, from him from Abanah al-Falaki: "It was revealed when Abu Buraydah complained about Ali عليه‌السلام." (The report).

In al-Abaqat, from the book Yanabi‘ al-Mawaddah by Shaykh Sulayman ibn Ibrahim al-Balkhi, from al-Manaqib, from Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali from Ali in a hadith, he said: "The minimum by which a servant becomes astray is that he does not recognize the Proof of Allah (حجة الله), blessed and exalted is He, and His witness over His servants, whom Allah commanded His servants to obey and whose guardianship (ولاية) He made mandatory."

Sulaym said: "I said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, describe them to me.' He said: 'Those whom Allah coupled with Himself and His Prophet, saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ.' I said to him: 'May Allah make me your ransom, clarify for me.' He said: 'Those about whom the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said in several places—and in his last sermon on the day Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, took him to Himself: "I have left among you two things after which you will never go astray if you hold fast to them: the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and my progeny (عترتي), the People of my House (أهل بيتي). For the All-Attentive, the All-Aware has covenanted to me that they shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond like these two"—and he joined his two index fingers—"and I do not say like these two"—and he joined his index and middle fingers—"so hold fast to them and do not precede them, lest you go astray."'"

I say: The narrations from the Imams of the People of the House عليه‌السلام regarding the previous meanings are very numerous. We have limited ourselves in what we have quoted to providing a sample of each category. Whoever seeks them should refer to the compendiums of hadith.

As for what is narrated from the early commentators, there are three opinions: the Rightly Guided Caliphs, the commanders of the expeditions, and the scholars. What is narrated from al-Dahhak—that they are the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—returns to the third opinion, for the wording narrated from him is: "They are the companions of the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله who are the callers and narrators," and its apparent meaning is that it is a justification based on knowledge, thus returning to the interpretation of "the scholars."

Know also that many things and various different stories have been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) for these verses. However, reflection upon them leaves no doubt that they are all of the nature of theoretical application (التطبيق النظري) by their narrators. Therefore, we have omitted mentioning them due to the lack of benefit in doing so. If you wish to verify this, you should refer to al-Durr al-Manthur, Tafsir al-Tabari, and their likes.

In Mahasin al-Barqi, with its chain from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah the Exalted: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (the verse), he said: "Submission (التسليم), contentment (الرضا), and being satisfied (القنوع) with His decree."

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Abdillah al-Kahili, who said: "Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'If a people worshipped Allah alone without any partner, established the prayer, gave the zakat, performed the pilgrimage to the House, and fasted the month of Ramadan, but then said regarding something Allah did or the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did: "Why did he do such and such? If only he had done the opposite of what he did," or if they found that [objection] in their hearts, they would be polytheists (مشركين) by that.' Then he recited this verse: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً. Then Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'You must submit (عليكم بالتسليم).'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abdillah ibn Yahya al-Kahili from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I heard him saying: 'By Allah, if a people worshipped Allah alone without any partner, established the prayer, gave the zakat, performed the pilgrimage to the House, and fasted the month of Ramadan, but then said regarding something the Apostle of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did: "Why did he do such and such?" and they found that in themselves, they would be polytheists by that.' Then he read: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً—regarding what Muhammad and the family of Muhammad decreed—وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً."

I say: There are other narrations with the meaning of these two. What he عليه‌السلام mentioned is a generalization of the verse based on the underlying rationale (الملاك) from two aspects: first, that in judgment, there is no difference between it being a legislative or creative decree; and second, that in the one who judges, there is no difference between it being Allah or His Apostle.

Know that there are narrations that apply the verses—namely His saying: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ until the end of the verses—to the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali عليه‌السلام or the guardianship of the Imams of the People of the House عليه‌السلام. This is an instance of applying [the general verse] to specific instances (التطبيق على المصاديق), for Allah, the Glorified, His Apostle صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the Imams from the People of the House عليه‌السلام are the instances of the verses, and they are applicable to them.

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain to Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who said: "A man from the Ansar came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'O Apostle of Allah, I cannot bear to be apart from you. I enter my home and remember you, so I leave my property and come until I look at you out of love for you. But I remembered that when the Day of Resurrection comes and you are entered into Paradise, you will be raised to the highest of the high (Illiyyin); so how will I be with you, O Prophet of Allah?' Then there was revealed: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً. So the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله called the man, read it to him, and gave him the glad tidings of that."

I say: This meaning is also narrated through Sunni paths. It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah, and al-Diya’ al-Maqdisi in Sifat al-Jannah (who graded it as fair) from Aisha; and from al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh through al-Sha‘bi from Ibn Abbas; and from Sa‘id ibn Mansur and Ibn al-Mundhir from al-Sha‘bi; and from Ibn Jarir from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahrashub from Anas ibn Malik from someone he named from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ—meaning Muhammad—وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ—meaning Ali, and he was the first who believed (صدق)—وَالشُّهَداءِ—meaning Ali, Ja‘far, Hamzah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn عليه‌السلام.

I say: There are other reports with this meaning.

In al-Kafi, from al-Baqir عليه‌السلام, who said: "Help us with godwariness (الورع) — the cautious avoidance of sins and doubtful matters — for whoever meets Allah with godwariness will have joy with Allah. For Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ." He recited the verse and then said: "From us is the Prophet, from us is the Truthful (الصديق), and from us are the Witnesses (الشهداء) and the Rightly-Acting (الصالحون)."

In the same source, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "The believer is of two types: a believer who fulfilled for Allah His conditions which He stipulated for him—that one is with the prophets, the truthful, the witnesses, and the righteous, and what excellent companions they are! That is one who intercedes and for whom intercession is not [needed]; that is one who is not struck by the terrors of this world nor the terrors of the Hereafter. And a believer whose foot slipped—that one is like a tender stalk of grain (خامة الزرع); however the wind tilts it, it tilts. That is one who is struck by the terrors of this world and the terrors of the Hereafter, and he is interceded for, and he is upon good."

I say: In al-Sihah: al-khamah (الخامة) is the tender, moist part of a plant. (End quote). It is said: "I tilted (كفأت) so-and-so and he tilted (انكفأ)," meaning I turned him and he turned and went back. He عليه‌السلام is pointing in the hadith to what was previously mentioned in the commentary of His saying: صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ "the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:7)—that what is meant by the blessing (النعمة) is the guardianship (الولاية). Thus, it applies to His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve—those who have faith and are God-conscious" (10:62-63). The terrors of events have no way to reach the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) who have none but Allah, the Glorified.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ خُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ فَٱنفِرُوا۟ ثُبَاتٍ أَوِ ٱنفِرُوا۟ جَمِيعًا (٧١)
O you who have faith! Take your precautions, then go forth in companies, or go forth en masse. (71)
وَإِنَّ مِنكُمْ لَمَن لَّيُبَطِّئَنَّ فَإِنْ أَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىَّ إِذْ لَمْ أَكُن مَّعَهُمْ شَهِيدًا (٧٢)
Among you is indeed he who drags his feet, and should an affliction visit you, he says, ‘It was certainly Allah’s blessing that I did not accompany them!’ (72)
وَلَئِنْ أَصَـٰبَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ لَيَقُولَنَّ كَأَن لَّمْ تَكُنۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُۥ مَوَدَّةٌ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ مَعَهُمْ فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٧٣)
But should a bounty from Allah come to you, he will say—as if there were no [tie of friendship and] affection between you and him—‘I wish I were with them so that I had achieved a great success!’ (73)
۞ فَلْيُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَن يُقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيُقْتَلْ أَوْ يَغْلِبْ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٧٤)
Let those who sell the life of this world for the Hereafter fight in the way of Allah; and whoever fights in the way of Allah, and then is slain, or he subdues [the enemy], We shall soon give him a great reward. (74)
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلظَّالِمِ أَهْلُهَا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا وَٱجْعَل لَّنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ نَصِيرًا (٧٥)
Why should you not fight in the way of Allah and the oppressed men, women, and children, who say, ‘Our Lord, bring us out of this town whose people are oppressors, and appoint for us a guardian from Yourself, and appoint for us a helper from Yourself’? (75)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ كَانَ ضَعِيفًا (٧٦)
Those who have faith fight in the way of Allah, and those who are faithless fight in the way of fake gods. So fight the friends of Satan; indeed the stratagems of Satan are always flimsy. (76)

(Commentary)

The verses, in relation to what preceded them—as you can see—stand in the position of the objective relative to the preamble. They urge and mobilize the believers for jihad (الجهاد) — the striving and struggling in the way of Allah — in the path of Allah. The trial was severe upon the believers during the days these verses were revealed, which was approximately the second quarter of the period of the Messenger of Allah’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) residence in Medina. The Arabs had agitated against them from every side to extinguish the light of Allah and to demolish the elevated structure of the religion (الدين). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was raiding the polytheists of Mecca and the tyrants (طواغيت) of Quraysh, sending expeditions (السرايا) to the regions of the Peninsula, and raising the foundations of the religion among the believers. Meanwhile, within their own ranks was the gathering of the hypocrites (المنافقين), who possessed power and influence. It became clear on the day of Uhud that they possessed a number not much less than half the number of the believers (1).

They used to subvert matters for the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), awaiting misfortunes to befall him, and discouraging the believers. Among the believers were those with sickness in their hearts who would listen to them. Around them were the Jews, who would tempt the believers and provoke them.

(1) It has been mentioned previously in the narrations of Uhud that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) set out for Uhud with one thousand men, then three hundred of the hypocrites returned with Abd Allah ibn Ubayy, and seven hundred remained with the Prophet.

The Arabs of Medina used to respect the Jews and hold them in high esteem since ancient times. Thus, the Jews would cast false speech and misleading reports toward the believers to nullify their sincere resolve and break their firm determination. On the other hand, they would encourage the polytheists against them, comforting their souls in their resistance and in remaining steadfast upon their disbelief (الكفر) and denial, while causing discord among the believers in their midst.

Thus, the preceding verses were as if driven to nullify the plotting of the Jews against the Muslims and to erase the effects of their suggestions upon the believers. What is contained in these verses regarding the discourse on the hypocrites serves as a completion of the guidance for the believers and a perfection of their acquaintance with the current situation, so they may have insight into their affairs and be wary of the hidden malady that has crept into their interior and permeated their gathering. This is so that the plotting of their external enemies surrounding them may be nullified, their breaths may be driven back into their chests, and the light of the religion may be perfected in its radiance—and Allah is the perfecter of His light, even if the polytheists and disbelievers (الكافرين) detest it.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ أَوِ انْفِرُوا جَمِيعاً — "O you who have faith! Take your precautions, then go forth in detachments, or go forth en masse" (4:71). The word ḥidhr (الحذر) refers to that which one protects oneself with; it is the instrument of precaution, such as weaponry. It is also said to be a verbal noun (مصدر). Al-Nafar (النفر) is the act of journeying toward a specific destination; its root meaning is "fright" or "startling," so "fleeing from" a place of departure or "rushing toward" a destination. Thubāt (الثبات) is the plural of thubah (ثبة), which refers to a group in a state of separation. Thus, thubāt means group after group, such that the second is distinct from the first, and the third from the second. This is supported by the contrast between His saying: فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ (go forth in detachments) and His saying: أَوِ انْفِرُوا جَمِيعاً (or go forth en masse).

The derivation in His saying: فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ from His saying: خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ outwardly supports the view that "precaution" (الحذر) refers to the means of precaution, such that it is an allusion (كناية) to complete readiness for setting out for jihad. The meaning would be: Take your weapons—meaning, prepare for departure—and go forth against your enemy in detachments (سرايا) or go forth against them all together as an army (عسكراً).

It is well known that preparation and readiness vary according to the number and strength of the enemy. Therefore, the alternative in His saying: "or go forth" is not a choice in the manner of going forth, but rather the alternative is based on the variation of the enemy in terms of number and strength. That is, if their number is small, then go in a detachment (ثبة), and if it is large, then all together (جميعاً).

The meaning ultimately—especially considering the following verse: وَإِنَّ مِنْكُمْ لَمَنْ لَيُبَطِّئَنَّ (And among you there is he who will surely lag behind)—amounts to a prohibition against them laying down their arms or stripping themselves of seriousness and the exertion of effort in the matter of jihad, lest their resolve die and their vigor in establishing the banners of truth be lost, and they become lazy, slow, or discouraged in fighting the enemies of Allah and cleansing the earth of their filth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ مِنْكُمْ لَمَنْ لَيُبَطِّئَنَّ — "And among you there is he who will surely lag behind" (4:72). It is said that the first lām is the lām of inception (لام الابتداء) because it enters upon the subject of inna, and the second lām is the lām of the oath (لام القسم) because it enters upon the predicate, which is a verbal sentence emphasized with the heavy nūn of emphasis. Al-tabṭi’ah (التبطئة) and al-ibṭā’ (الإبطاء) carry the same meaning: delaying in action.

His saying: وَإِنَّ مِنْكُمْ (And among you) indicates that these individuals are from among the believers addressed at the beginning of the verse by His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who have faith!), according to the apparent meaning of the word "among you" (مِنْكُمْ). This is also indicated by what follows in His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ (Have you not regarded those to whom it was said, ‘Keep your hands off...’), for it is apparent that these were also from the believers, along with His saying thereafter: فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتالُ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ النَّاسَ (But when fighting was prescribed for them, behold, a part of them were afraid of people...), and His saying: وَإِنْ تُصِبْهمْ حَسَنَةٌ (If a good fortune comes their way...), and so on. Likewise, His saying: فَلْيُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ الَّذِينَ (Let those fight in the way of Allah...), His saying: وَما لَكُمْ لا تُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ (Why should you not fight in the way of Allah...), and His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ (Those who have faith fight in the way of Allah). All of this is an incitement and mobilization of the believers, among whom were these laggers, as suggested by the continuity of the verses.

Furthermore, there is nothing in the verses that outwardly indicates that these laggers were from the hypocrites who did not believe except with an outward profession of speech. In fact, some of what Allah narrates about them indicates their faith to some extent, such as His saying: فَإِنْ أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ قالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيَّ (then if a misfortune befalls you, he says, ‘Allah has indeed blessed me...’) and His saying: رَبَّنا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا الْقِتالَ (Our Lord! Why did You prescribe fighting for us?...).

Yes, the commentators have mentioned that the intended meaning of His saying: وَإِنَّ مِنْكُمْ لَمَنْ (And among you there is he who...) refers to the hypocrites, and that the meaning of their being "among you" is their inclusion in their number, or their shared lineage—so they are from them by lineage—or their sharing with the believers in the outward rulings of the Sharia (الشريعة) regarding the protection of blood, inheritance, and the like, due to their outward profession of the two testimonies. You have already seen that this is an unjustified departure from the apparent meaning of the Quran.

What prompted them toward this interpretation was a favorable opinion of the Muslims in the early days of Islam (everyone who met the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and believed in him). However, an analytical study of what history has recorded of their conduct and lives with the Prophet and after him weakens this assumption, and the sharp Quranic addresses specifically regarding them undermine this estimation.

The world has not permitted, even to this day, a pure nation or group composed of pure individuals without exception—believers standing on a footing of sincerity without ever stumbling (except for what has been narrated in the account of al-Taff). Rather, the believers of the early days of Islam were like all other human groups: among them were the hypocrite, the one with a diseased heart, the follower of whims, and the one with a pure inner self.

What distinguished the first generation of Muslims was that their society was a virtuous society (مجتمعاً فاضلاً) led by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), enveloped by the light of faith (الإيمان), and governed by the authority of the religion. This was the state of their society as a society, even if there existed among the individuals both the righteous and the wicked, and in their spiritual traits both virtue and vice, and every shade of character and disposition (الملكات).

This is what the Quran mentions of their state and explains of their attributes. The Exalted said: مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً سِيماهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ مِنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure. Their mark is on their faces from the effect of prostration..." until He said: وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً — "...Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward" (48:29). He began by mentioning their social attributes and virtues in an absolute sense, and concluded by mentioning forgiveness and reward for their individuals in a conditional sense.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ أَصابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةٌ — "then if a misfortune befalls you" (4:72), meaning being killed or wounded, قالَ قَدْ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيَّ إِذْ لَمْ أَكُنْ مَعَهُمْ شَهِيداً — "he says, ‘Allah has indeed blessed me that I was not present with them’" (4:72), so that I was not afflicted with the like of what the believers were afflicted with.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ أَصابَكُمْ فَضْلٌ مِنَ اللهِ — "But if a bounty from Allah comes to you" (4:73), such as war booty and the like. Bounty (الفضل) refers to wealth and its equivalents. His saying: لَيَقُولَنَّ كَأَنْ لَمْ تَكُنْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُ مَوَدَّةٌ يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ مَعَهُمْ — "he will surely say, as if there were no friendship between you and him, ‘I wish I had been with them...’" (4:73) is a comparison and representation of their state. For they are believers, and the Muslims are a single hand, bound to one another by the strongest of ties, which is faith in Allah and His signs—a tie that governs all other ties of lineage, guardianship, allegiance, or affection. However, due to the weakness of their faith, they do not see for themselves the slightest connection binding them to the believers. Thus, they wish to have been with them and present in their jihad just as a stranger wishes for a bounty attained by another stranger. So one of them says: يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ مَعَهُمْ فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً — "‘...I wish I had been with them, so that I had achieved a great victory!’" (4:73). Among the signs of the weakness of their faith is their aggrandizement of these spoils and their counting the acquisition of bounty and wealth as a "great victory," while considering every misfortune that befalls the believers in the way of Allah—whether killing, wounding, or exhaustion—as a calamity.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلْيُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ الَّذِينَ يَشْرُونَ — "Let those fight in the way of Allah who sell..." (4:74). It is said in al-Majma‘: It is said sharaitu meaning "I sold," and ishtaraitu meaning "I bought." So the meaning of His saying "who sell (يشرون) the life of this world for the Hereafter" is that they sell their worldly life and exchange it for the Hereafter.

The verse is a derivation from what preceded of the urging toward jihad and the censure of those who lag behind in going forth to it. In it is a renewal of the incitement to fight in the way of Allah by reminding that all these are believers who have, by their submission (إسلام) to Allah the Exalted, sold the life of this world for the Hereafter, as He said: إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ — "Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise being theirs..." (9:111). Then He explicitly stated the good benefit of fighting and that it is a great reward in any case, by His saying: وَمَنْ يُقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ — "And whoever fights in the way of Allah..." (4:74).

He explained that the matter of the fighter in the way of Allah ends in one of two praised outcomes: either he is killed in the way of Allah, or he overcomes the enemy of Allah; and in either case, he has a great reward. He did not mention the third possibility—which is defeat—as an allusion that the fighter in the way of Allah does not flee.

He mentioned being killed before overcoming because its reward is more abundant and more certain. For the fighter who overcomes the enemy of Allah, although a great reward is written for him, is at risk of nullification (الحبط) by committing some acts that necessitate the nullification of righteous deeds, and the following of a bad deed after a good one. This is in contrast to being killed, for there is no life after it except the life of the Hereafter. Thus, the one killed in the way of Allah will certainly receive his great reward in full, whereas the one who overcomes in the way of Allah, his matter is deferred regarding the full receipt of his reward.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما لَكُمْ لا تُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ — "Why should you not fight in the way of Allah and the oppressed..." (4:75). This is conjoined to the position of the Divine Name. The verse contains another urging and incitement to fight in the form of an interrogation, by reminding that your fighting is a fighting in the way of Allah, the Glorified—He for Whose pleasure alone you seek in your happy life, and there is no happiness greater than proximity to Him—and in the way of the oppressed (المستضعفين) among your men, women, and children.

Thus, in the verse is a mobilization and provocation for all the believers and an enticement for them: as for the believers of pure faith and clean hearts, the mention of Allah, majestic is His mention, is sufficient for them to stand for the truth, respond to the call of their Lord, and answer His caller. As for those believers below them, if that does not suffice them, then let it suffice them that their fighting, in addition to being in the way of Allah, is a fighting in the way of those whom the disbelievers have oppressed among their men, women, and offspring; so let them feel protective jealousy for them and show zeal.

Although Islam has nullified every lineage and connection other than faith, it has nonetheless endorsed, after the donning of faith, national lineages and connections. Thus, it is upon the Muslim to sacrifice for his Muslim brother who is connected to him by the bond of faith, and for his relatives among his men, women, and offspring if they are upon Islam, for that ultimately returns to the way of Allah and none other.

These oppressed ones, who are parts of them and their very heartstrings, are believers in Allah the Glorified, as evidenced by His saying: الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا — "those who say, ‘Our Lord!...’" (4:75). Despite this, they are humiliated and tormented, crying out for help and seeking rescue by saying: "Our Lord! Bring us out of this town whose people are wrongdoers." He mentioned wrongdoing (الظلم) in an absolute sense and did not say "whose people are wrongdoers to themselves," which suggests that they were wronging them with various types of torture and harm, and such was indeed the case.

He expressed their seeking of rescue and help in the most beautiful terms and finest expression. He did not narrate that they say: "O men!", "O leaders!", "O my people!", or "O my kinsfolk!" Rather, He narrated that they call upon their Lord and seek rescue from their True Master, saying: رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ الظَّالِمِ أَهْلُها — "‘Our Lord! Bring us out of this town whose people are wrongdoers...’" (4:75). Then they point toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those with him of the mujahideen (المجاهدين) — those who strive in the way of Allah — among the believers, by their saying: وَاجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا وَاجْعَلْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ نَصِيراً — "‘...and appoint for us from Yourself a guardian, and appoint for us from Yourself a helper’" (4:75). They long for a guardian and they long for a helper, but they are not satisfied without asking their Lord for the guardian and the helper.

(A Discourse on Protective Jealousy and Zeal)

Look at this brilliant divine etiquette brought by the Mighty Book and compare it to what we have of that according to the judgment of nature; you will see something wondrous.

There is no doubt that in the human constitution there is that which impels him to defend what he respects and aggrandizes, such as offspring, women, status, the dignity of ancestry, and the like. This is a judgment necessitated by the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and inspired by it. However, this defense may be praised if it is for the truth and for the sake of the truth, and it may be censured, leading to misery and the corruption of the affairs of life, if it is for falsehood and against the truth.

Islam preserves the root of this judgment, which belongs to the primordial nature, and nullifies its details first, then directs it toward the side of Allah the Glorified by diverting it from everything else. Then it returns it to its many instances, casting the whole into the mold of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — through faith in Allah. Thus, it invites man to be zealous for his men, women, and offspring, and for every right, by referring all of it to the side of Allah. So Islam supports the judgment of the primordial nature and refines it from the mixture of whims and corrupt desires. It purifies its matter in all instances and makes them all a human path (شريعة) which man traverses upon the primordial nature, delivering it from the darkness of contradiction to the light of harmony and peace. Thus, what Islam calls to and legislates has no contradiction or opposition between its parts and sides; all of them share in being among the affairs of monotheism, and all of them gather in being the following of the truth. All rulings then return to being universal, perpetual, and stable, without deviation or variation.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ — "Those who have faith fight in the way of Allah..." until His saying الطَّاغُوتِ — "...the Rebel (الطاغوت) — the one who exceeds all bounds in rebellion against Allah" (4:76). This is a comparison between those who believe and those who disbelieve from the perspective of the description of their fighting—or in other words, from the perspective of the intention of each of the two groups in their fighting—so that the nobility of the believers over the disbelievers in their way may be known, and that the path of the believers ends with Allah the Glorified and relies upon Him, unlike the path of the disbelievers. This serves as another incitement for the believers to fight them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقاتِلُوا أَوْلِياءَ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّ كَيْدَ الشَّيْطانِ كانَ ضَعِيفاً — "So fight the friends of Satan; indeed the stratagems of Satan are always frail" (4:76). Those who disbelieve, by falling into the way of the Rebel, are outside the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah. They have no master except the master of polytheism and the worship of other than Allah the Exalted, which is Satan. So he is their guardian, and they are his friends.

The stratagem of Satan was deemed weak because it is the way of the Rebel which opposes the way of Allah, and power belongs to Allah entirely. Thus, nothing remains for the way of the Rebel, which is the trickery of Satan, except weakness. Therefore, He incited the believers against them by explaining the weakness of their way and encouraged them to fight them. The weakness of Satan's stratagem in relation to the way of Allah does not contradict its strength in relation to those who follow their whims, and this is obvious.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ (4:71), he said: Weaponry is called ḥidhr because it is the instrument by which precaution is maintained. He said: And it is narrated from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him). He said: And it is narrated from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) that the meaning of thubāt is detachments (السرايا), and by jamī‘an is the army (العسكر).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Sulayman ibn Khalid, from Abu Abd Allah (peace be upon him): "O you who have faith!"—so He called them believers, yet they are not believers nor is there any honor [for them]. He said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ أَوِ انْفِرُوا جَمِيعاً until His saying: فَأَفُوزَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً. And if the people of the heavens and the earth were to say: "Allah has indeed blessed me that I was not present with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)," they would thereby be polytheists. And if a bounty from Allah reaches them, he says: يا لَيْتَنِي كُنْتُ مَعَهُمْ (I wish I had been with them) so that I might fight in the way of Allah.

I say: Al-Tabarsi narrated this meaning in al-Majma‘, and al-Qummi in his Tafsir from him (peace be upon him). The meaning of polytheism (الشرك) in his saying (peace be upon him) is spiritual polytheism, not the disbelief (الكفر) that strips the outward rulings of Islam from one who is characterized by it, as has been previously explained.

In the same source, from Humran, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ (4:75)—he said: "We are those." I say: It was also narrated from Sama‘ah, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and its wording is: "As for His saying: وَالْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ, those are we," the hadith. The two narrations are in the context of application (التطبيق) and complaint against the transgression of the transgressors of this nation, and they are not in the context of exegesis (التفسير).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Dawud in his Nasikh, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan extracted via Ata from Ibn Abbas: regarding Surah al-Nisa: خُذُوا حِذْرَكُمْ فَانْفِرُوا ثُباتٍ أَوِ انْفِرُوا جَمِيعاً, [meaning] in groups and detachments. He said: It was abrogated by: وَما كانَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنْفِرُوا كَافَّةً (9:122).

I say: The two verses are not contradictory such that the second would be judged to abrogate the first, and this is obvious. Rather, if anything, it is a case of specification (التخصيص) or qualification (التقييد). And praise be to Allah.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ ٱلنَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْقِتَالَ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ ۗ قُلْ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلدُّنْيَا قَلِيلٌ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٧٧)
Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from warfare], and maintain the prayer and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them, behold, a part of them feared the people as if fearing Allah, or were even more afraid, and they said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You prescribe fighting for us? Why did You not respite us for a short time?!’ Say, ‘The enjoyments of this world are trifle and the Hereafter is better for the Godwary, and you will not be wronged so much as a single date-thread. (77)
أَيْنَمَا تَكُونُوا۟ يُدْرِككُّمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ وَلَوْ كُنتُمْ فِى بُرُوجٍ مُّشَيَّدَةٍ ۗ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦ مِنْ عِندِكَ ۚ قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَالِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا (٧٨)
Wherever you may be death will overtake you, even if you were in fortified towers.’ If any good befalls them, they say, ‘This is from Allah;’ and when an ill befalls them, they say, ‘This is from you.’ Say, ‘All is from Allah.’ What is the matter with these people that they would not understand any matter? (78)
مَّآ أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ۚ وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (٧٩)
Whatever good befalls you is from Allah; and whatever ill befalls you is from yourself. We sent you as an apostle to mankind, and Allah suffices as witness. (79)
مَّن يُطِعِ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا (٨٠)
Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah; and as for those who turn their backs [on you]; We have not sent you to keep watch over them. (80)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, and they all share a single context. These verses include a citation of the case of another group of believers who were weak in faith (ضعفاء الإيمان). Within them is an exhortation and a reminder of the transience of the world and the permanence of the blessings of the Hereafter, as well as an exposition of a Quranic reality regarding the specific nature of good deeds (الحسنات) and evil deeds (السيئات).

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ — until His saying — أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً — "Withholding the hands" is a metaphor (كناية) for refraining from fighting, because the killing that occurs therein is an act of the hands. This speech indicates that the believers, in the beginning of their affair, found it difficult to witness the transgression and outrage of the disbelievers against them. It was hard for them to remain patient and not retaliate by drawing their swords. Thus, Allah commanded them to withhold from that and to establish the rituals of religion (شعائر الدين) such as prayer (الصلاة) and the alms-tax (الزكاة), so that the structure of the religion might strengthen and its backbone might be established, after which Allah would grant them permission to wage jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah — against His enemies. Had it not been for this, the framework of the religion would have dissolved, its pillars would have collapsed, and its parts would have vanished.

Thus, the verses contain a reproach for them because they were the ones who sought to hasten the fighting against the disbelievers and would not be patient in withholding and enduring harm when they lacked the equipment and strength sufficient to meet their enemy. Yet, when fighting was prescribed for them, a group of them feared the enemy—who are humans like themselves—as they should fear Allah, or with an even greater fear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا رَبَّنا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا الْقِتالَ — "And they said, ‘Our Lord! Why have You prescribed fighting for us?’" (4:77). Its apparent meaning is that it is coordinated with His saying: إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ — "behold, a part of them" (4:77), especially considering the change in context from the present tense verb (يَخْشَوْنَ النَّاسَ) — "fearing the people" — to the past tense (قالُوا) — "they said." Thus, the ones who uttered this statement were those who had been anticipating the fighting and found patience difficult when they were commanded to withhold their hands.

It is possible that their saying: رَبَّنا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا الْقِتالَ لَوْ لا أَخَّرْتَنا إِلى أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ — "‘Our Lord! Why have You prescribed fighting for us? Why did You not respite us for a short time?’" (4:77) is narrated as an expression of their state (لسان حالهم), just as it is possible that they uttered it with their actual tongues; for the Quran employs every type of such literary devices (عنايات).

The description of the term (الأجل) — which is the term of death by natural causes — as "near" (قريب) does not mean they were asking to escape death and live for only a short time. Rather, it is an allusion (تلويح) from them that even if they were to live without being killed until they died naturally, it would only be a brief life and a near term. So why does Allah—Glorified is He—not accept for them to live this brief life instead of trying them with being killed and hastening death for them?

This speech issued from them because their souls were attached to this worldly life (الحياة الدنيا), which, in the teaching of the Quran, is a "paltry enjoyment" (متاع قليل) that is enjoyed and then quickly ends and its trace is erased.
