Beyond it is the life of the Hereafter (الحياة الآخرة), which is the true, everlasting life; thus, it is better. For this reason, they were answered by His saying: قُلْ — "Say..." (4:77) etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَتاعُ الدُّنْيا قَلِيلٌ — "Say, ‘The enjoyment of this world is little’" (4:77) etc. This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to answer these weak ones with that which clarifies the error of their view in preferring the brief worldly life over the honor of jihad and being killed in the way of Allah the Exalted. The gist of it is that they ought to be God-conscious (متقين) in their faith. The worldly life is an enjoyment that is little when measured against the Hereafter, and the Hereafter is better for him who is God-conscious (اتقى). Therefore, it is incumbent upon them to choose the Hereafter, which is better, over the paltry enjoyment of the world, because they are believers and are upon the path of godwariness (التقوى). There remains no reason for them to fear that Allah might act unjustly toward them or wrong them, such that they would choose the enjoyment in their hands over the good they are promised; they have no such reason, for Allah does not wrong them even the "extent of a fiber on a date-stone" (فتيلا).

It has become clear through this exposition that His saying: لِمَنِ اتَّقى — "for him who is God-conscious" — is an instance of placing the attribute in place of the described subject to indicate the reason for the ruling and the claim of its applicability to the case. The intended meaning—and Allah knows best—is: "The Hereafter is better for you because, by virtue of your faith, you ought to be people of godwariness (أهل تقوى), and godwariness is the cause for attaining the good of the Hereafter." Thus, His saying: لِمَنِ اتَّقى is like a metaphor (كناية) containing an allusion (تعريض).

His saying, the Exalted: أَيْنَما تَكُونُوا يُدْرِكْكُمُ الْمَوْتُ وَلَوْ كُنْتُمْ فِي بُرُوجٍ مُشَيَّدَةٍ — أَيْنَما تَكُونُوا يُدْرِكْكُمُ الْمَوْتُ وَلَوْ كُنْتُمْ فِي بُرُوجٍ مُشَيَّدَةٍ — "Wherever you may be, death will overtake you, even if you were in fortified towers" (4:78). البروج (Towers) is the plural of burj (برج), which is a structure built upon fortresses. Its construction is made as strong as possible to repel the enemy from it and by it. Its root meaning is "manifestation" (الظهور), from which comes tabarruj (التبرج) — the display of beauty and the like. التشييد (Fortification/Elevation) means raising high; its root is from shīd (الشيد), which is plaster (gypsum), because it strengthens the building, raises it, and beautifies it. Thus, "fortified towers" (البروج المشيدة) are the strong, elevated structures upon fortresses where a person seeks refuge from any approaching enemy.

The speech is structured as a representation (تمثيل) by mentioning some of what one uses to protect oneself from the disliked, making it a parable for every "strong pillar" (ركن شديد) by which hardships are avoided. The sum of the meaning is: Death is a matter whose overtaking you cannot escape, even if you were to seek refuge from it in any strong, solid sanctuary. Therefore, you should not imagine that if you had not witnessed the fighting and it had not been prescribed for you, you would have been safe from death and its overtaking you would have failed; for the term of Allah is surely coming.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ — "If a good fortune comes their way, they say, ‘This is from Allah’" (4:78) (until the end of the verse). These are two other sentences regarding their lapses which Allah the Exalted has narrated about them, commanding His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to answer them by clarifying the reality of the matter regarding the good and evil that befalls man.

The continuity of the context dictates that the aforementioned weak ones among the believers are the ones who said this. They said it either through the "tongue of their state" (لسان حالهم) or their actual speech (مقالهم). There is nothing strange in this, for Moses was also confronted with similar speech, as Allah the Glorified narrated by His saying: فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا بِمُوسى وَمَنْ مَعَهُ أَلا إِنَّما طائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "But when good times came their way, they would say, ‘This is our due.’ And if an ill fortune befell them, they would take it as a bad omen for Moses and those who were with him. Look! Their ill omen is only with Allah, but most of them do not know" (7:131). This is also reported from other nations regarding their prophets. This nation, in their treatment of their Prophet, is no less than other nations; and He, the Exalted, has said: تَشابَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ — "Their hearts are alike" (2:118). Moreover, they are the nation most resembling the Children of Israel. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "They [the previous nations] would not enter a lizard's hole but that you would enter it too." The narrations regarding this have been previously cited from the paths of both schools.

Most commentators have labored (تمحل) over these verses by claiming they were revealed specifically regarding the Jews, the hypocrites, or both. You can see that the context refutes this.

In any case, the verse testifies by its context that what is meant by "good fortune" (الحسنة) and "ill fortune" (السيئة) are those things that can be attributed to Allah the Glorified. They attributed one part to Allah—which is the good fortune—and one part to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—which is the ill fortune. These good and ill fortunes are the events that they encountered after the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came to them and began raising the structures of the religion and spreading its call and fame through jihad. Thus, they are victory, triumph, and booty in the wars and expeditions where they were victorious, and killing, wounding, and affliction in others. Their attribution of ill fortunes to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is in the sense of taking him as a bad omen (التطير) or attributing weakness of judgment and poor management to him.

So Allah commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to answer them by saying: قُلْ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ — "Say, ‘All is from Allah’" (4:78). For these are events and occurrences organized by the Organizer of the cosmic system (النظام الكوني), who is Allah alone, without partner; since things only submit in their existence (وجودها), their permanence (بقائها), and all the events they encounter to Him, the Exalted, and none other, according to what the teaching of the Quran provides.

Then He asked a question of wonderment at the stagnation of their understanding and the dullness of their intuition in grasping this reality, saying: فَما لِهؤُلاءِ الْقَوْمِ لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثاً — "What is the matter with these people that they do not seem to understand any talk?" (4:78).

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللهِ وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ — "Whatever good may happen to you, it is from Allah; and whatever misfortune may befall you, it is from yourself" (4:79). When He mentioned that they "do not seem to understand any talk" and then intended to clarify the reality of the matter, He turned the address away from them due to the failure of their understanding and directed the speech toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He clarified the reality of what befalls him of good or evil for that reason. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله has no personal particularity in this reality, which is among the existential laws (الأحكام الوجودية) that circulate among all existents, or at least among all human individuals—whether believer or disbeliever, righteous or wicked, prophet or otherwise.

Thus, good fortunes (الحسنات)—which are the things that man naturally finds good, such as well-being, blessing, security, and prosperity—all of that is from Allah the Glorified. And ill fortunes (السيئات)—which are the things that grieve man, such as illness, humiliation, poverty, and trial—all of that returns to man, not to Him, the Glorified. The verse is close in content to His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَأَنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ — "That is because Allah would never change a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls, and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" (8:53). This does not contradict the return of all good and ill fortunes, from another universal perspective, to Him, the Exalted, as will be explained.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَرْسَلْناكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولاً — "We have sent you as an apostle to mankind" (4:79). That is, you have no mark (سمة) from Us except that you are a messenger whose duty is conveyance (البلاغ) and whose station is messengership (الرسالة); you have no station other than that, and you have no part in the command such that you could influence good or bad fortune, or bring ill fortunes to people and withhold good fortunes from them. In this is an allusive refutation (رد تعريضي) to the statement of those who took ill omens regarding misfortunes, saying: هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ — "This is from you" — as a bad omen regarding him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Then He supported that by His saying: وَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً — "and Allah suffices as a witness" (4:79).

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ — "Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah" (4:80). This is a new sentence (استئناف) containing emphasis and confirmation of His saying in the previous verse: وَأَرْسَلْناكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولاً. It is like a justification (تعليل) for His ruling; meaning: You are nothing but a messenger from Us; whoever obeys you in your capacity as a messenger has indeed obeyed Allah. "And as for those who turn their backs [on you], We have not sent you to be their keeper" (4:80).

From here it becomes clear that His saying: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ is an instance of placing the attribute in place of the described subject to indicate the cause of the ruling, similar to what preceded in His saying: وَالْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِمَنِ اتَّقى وَلا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً. Accordingly, the context remains consistent without a shift (التفات) from the second-person address in His saying: وَأَرْسَلْناكَ to the third-person in His saying: مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ, and then back to the second-person in His saying: فَما أَرْسَلْناكَ.

(A Discourse on the Attribution of Good and Ill Fortunes to Him, the Exalted)

It seems that the first thing man became aware of regarding the meaning of "beauty" or "goodness" (الحسن) was from observing beauty in his fellow humans, which is the balance of form (اعتدال الخلقة) and the proportion of the limbs, especially in the face. Then he extended this to other sensible matters of the natural world. Ultimately, it returns to the conformity of a thing to what is naturally intended for its kind.

Thus, the beauty of a human face is that each of the eyes, eyebrows, ears, nose, mouth, and others are in a state or attribute in which they ought to be structured, as well as their proportion to one another. At that point, the soul is attracted and the nature inclines toward it. The state of a thing being contrary to this description is called "evil" (السوء), "misfortune" (المساءة), or "ugliness" (القبح), according to the various considerations observed. Thus, "evil" or "misfortune" is a privative meaning (معنى عدمي), just as "beauty" or "goodness" is an existential meaning (معنى وجودي).

Then this was generalized to actions, conventional meanings (المعاني الاعتبارية), and intended titles within the social sphere, in terms of their compatibility with the purpose of society—which is the happiness of human life or the enjoyment of life—or their lack of compatibility. Thus, justice (العدل) is good (حسن), and benevolence (الإحسان) to one who deserves it is good, and teaching, upbringing, sincere advice, and the like in their proper places are good deeds (حسنات). Conversely, injustice (الظلم), aggression (العدوان), and the like are ugly ill deeds (سيئات قبيحة), because the first category is compatible with human happiness or his complete enjoyment within his social sphere, while the second category is not. This type of goodness and its opposite follows the action it describes in terms of its compatibility with the social purpose. Some actions have a permanent, fixed goodness if their compatibility with the goal and purpose of society is likewise, such as justice; and some have a permanent ugliness, such as injustice.

There are actions whose state varies according to circumstances, times, places, or societies. Laughter and joking are good among friends but not before the great, and in gatherings of joy but not at funerals, nor in mosques and places of worship. Adultery and drinking wine are considered good among Westerners but not among Muslims.

Do not listen to the words of those who say that goodness and ugliness are absolutely different and changing, without stability, permanence, or universality. They argue this regarding things like justice and injustice by saying that what is justice for one nation through the implementation of certain social regulations is different from what is justice for another nation through the execution of other social regulations; thus, the meaning of justice does not settle on a specific thing. For example, flogging the adulterer is justice in Islam but not so among Westerners, and so on.

This is because these people have become confused; they have mistaken the concept (المفهوم) for the instance (المصداق). We have no debate with those whose level of understanding is this limited.

Man, according to the transformation of the factors influencing societies, may accept the change of all his social rulings suddenly or gradually, but he never accepts being stripped of the attribute of justice and being called unjust, nor does he find injustice in an oppressor except with an excuse for it. This discussion has a long tail that would take us away from what is more important.

Then the meaning of goodness and ugliness was generalized to all other external events that encounter man throughout his life according to the influence of various factors. These are individual or social events, some of which agree with man's hopes and suit his happiness in his individual or social life—such as well-being, health, or prosperity—and are called good fortunes (حسنات). Others contradict that—such as calamities and trials like poverty, illness, humiliation, or captivity—and are called ill fortunes (سيئات).

It has become clear from what preceded that good and ill fortune describe things or actions from the perspective of their relation to the perfection of a species, the happiness of an individual, or otherwise. Thus, goodness and ugliness are relative attributes (وصفان إضافيان), even if the relation is fixed and necessary in some cases and variable in others—like the giving of wealth, which is good in relation to one who deserves it and bad in relation to one who does not.

Furthermore, goodness is always a positive, existential matter (أمر ثبوتي), while misfortune and ugliness are privative meanings (معنى عدمي)—namely, the absence of the aforementioned attribute of compatibility and agreement. Otherwise, the essence of the thing or action, regardless of the said compatibility or lack thereof, is one and the same without any difference at all.

Thus, an earthquake or a destructive flood, if they strike the territory of a people, are two good blessings for their enemies, while they are two evil misfortunes for the people themselves. Every general calamity, in the view of religion, is a "joy" (سراء) if it descends upon the corrupt disbelievers on earth or the rebellious wicked, while it is the very same "distress" (ضراء) if it descends upon the believing, righteous nation.

Eating food is a good, permissible act if it is from the eater's own property, for example; yet it is the very same prohibited ill deed if it is from the property of another without their consent, due to its lacking compliance with the prohibition issued against eating another's property without consent, or compliance with the command issued to restrict oneself to what Allah has made lawful. Sexual intercourse between a man and a woman is a good, permissible act if it is through marriage, for example; yet it is a prohibited ill deed if it is fornication without marriage, due to its lacking conformity with the divine obligation (التكليف الإلهي). Thus, good fortunes are existential titles (عناوين وجودية) in things and actions, while ill fortunes are privative titles (عناوين عدمية) in them, while the essence of the thing described as good or evil is one.

That which the Noble Quran holds is that everything to which the name "thing" (شيء) applies—except Allah, exalted is His Name—is a creation of Allah. He, the Exalted, said: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62), and He said: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً — "He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" (25:2). These two verses establish "creation" (الخلقة) in everything. Then He, the Exalted, said: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — "who perfected everything He created" (32:7). Thus, He established goodness (الحسن) for every creature; it is a goodness inseparable from creation, revolving wherever it revolves.

So every thing has a share of goodness in proportion to its share of creation and existence (الوجود). Reflection on the meaning of goodness (as preceded) clarifies this further; for goodness (الحسن) is the conformity and compatibility of a thing with the desired purpose and the intended goal for it. The parts of existence and the portions of this cosmic system are compatible and harmonious. Far be it from the Lord of the worlds to create that whose parts contradict one another, or where some parts nullify others, thereby frustrating the desired purpose, or rendering Him, the Exalted, incapable, or nullifying what He intended for this wondrous system that baffles the intellect and bewilders thought. He, the Exalted, has said: هُوَ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount" (39:4). And He said: وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ — "He is the All-dominant over His servants" (6:18). And He said: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ كانَ عَلِيماً قَدِيراً — "Nothing in the heavens or on the earth can frustrate Allah. Indeed, He is All-knowing, All-powerful" (35:44). Thus, He, the Exalted, is not overcome by anything, nor does anything frustrate Him in what He intends for His creation and wills for His servants.

Therefore, every good blessing in existence is attributed to Him, the Exalted. Likewise, every evil misfortune is also attributed to Him, the Exalted, in itself—that is, according to the primary relation circulating among created existents—even if, according to another relation, it is an ill fortune. This is what is provided by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا هذِهِ مِنْ عِنْدِكَ قُلْ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ فَما لِهؤُلاءِ الْقَوْمِ لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثاً — "If a good fortune comes their way, they say, ‘This is from Allah;’ and if an ill fortune befalls them, they say, ‘This is from you.’ Say, ‘All is from Allah.’ What is the matter with these people that they do not seem to understand any talk?" (4:78). And His saying: فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا بِمُوسى وَمَنْ مَعَهُ أَلا إِنَّما طائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "But when good times came their way, they would say, ‘This is our due.’ And if an ill fortune befell them, they would take it as a bad omen for Moses and those who were with him. Look! Their ill omen is only with Allah, but most of them do not know" (7:131), among other verses.

As for the aspect of the "ill fortune" (السيئة), the Noble Quran attributes it in man to the soul of man itself, by His saying, the Exalted, in this Surah: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللهِ وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ — "Whatever good may happen to you, it is from Allah; and whatever misfortune may befall you, it is from yourself" (4:79). And His saying: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned; and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). And His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls" (13:11). And His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — "That is because Allah would never change a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53), and other verses.

The clarification of this is that the previous verses, as you have learned, make these evil misfortunes—like the good fortunes—good things in their creation. Thus, there remains nothing for their being "evil" except that they do not suit the natures of some things that are harmed by them. The matter ultimately returns to the fact that Allah did not find for these afflicted things, which are harmed, what they seek and long for according to their natures. This withholding of bounty (إمساك الجود) is what is considered an evil calamity in relation to these harmed things, as is fully clarified by His saying, the Exalted: ما يَفْتَحِ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلا مُمْسِكَ لَها وَما يُمْسِكْ فَلا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds no one can release it after Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise" (35:2).

Then He, the Exalted, clarified that the withholding of bounty from what it is withheld from, or the increase and decrease in the effusion of His mercy, only follows or agrees with the measure of what its vessel can contain and what it can receive of that. He, the Exalted, said in the parable He struck: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity" (13:17). And He said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). Thus, He, the Exalted, only gives according to what the thing deserves and according to what He knows of its state. He said: أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ — "Does He who created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware" (67:14).

It is well known that blessing (النعمة), vengeance (النقمة), calamity (البلاء), and prosperity (الرخاء) in relation to everything are what suit its specific state, as clarified by His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns" (2:148). For everything turns toward and seeks its own specific direction and the goal that suits its state.

From here, you can surmise that "joy" (السراء) and "distress" (الضراء), blessing and calamity, in relation to this human being who lives in the sphere of choice (الاختيار), are matters linked to his choice in the teaching of the Quran. For he is situated on a path that leads him, through good conduct or its lack, to his happiness or wretchedness; all of that is of the kind in which his choice has an entry.

The Noble Quran confirms this surmise. He, the Exalted, said: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — "That is because Allah would never change a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53). Thus, what is in their souls of pure intentions and righteous deeds has an entry into the blessing they were specifically given. If they change, Allah changes by withholding His mercy. And He said: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned; and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). Thus, their deeds have an influence on the occurrences that descend upon them and the misfortunes that strike them, and Allah excuses many of them.

And He, the Exalted, said: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللهِ وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ — "Whatever good may happen to you, it is from Allah; and whatever misfortune may befall you, it is from yourself" (4:79).

Beware of thinking that Allah, the Glorified, when He revealed this verse to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, forgot the brilliant reality He clarified by His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62) and His saying: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — "who perfected everything He created" (32:7), whereby He counted everything as a creation of His, good in itself. He has said: وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا — "and your Lord is not forgetful" (19:64) and He said: لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى — "My Lord neither errs nor forgets" (20:52). Thus, the meaning of His saying: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ (the verse) is that whatever good fortune befalls you—and everything that befalls you is a good fortune—is from Allah. And whatever misfortune befalls you, it is a misfortune in relation to you, as it does not suit what you intend and desire, even if it is good in itself; for your soul drew it to you by its evil choice, and thus summoned it from Allah. For Allah is too exalted to initiate evil or harm toward you.

The verse, as preceded, although it specifically addresses the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, its meaning is general for everyone. In other words, this verse, like the other two verses—ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً and وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ—is responsible for the social address just as it is responsible for the individual address.

For human society has a human existence (كينونة), a will, and a choice other than that of the individual.

Society possesses an existence in which the past and gone among its members are absorbed, and their successors are taken to task for the evil deeds of the predecessors, and the dead for the evil deeds of the living, and the individual who did not commit the sin for the sin of the perpetrators, and so on. This is never correct for the individual according to the ruling regarding himself. A portion of this discussion preceded in the study of the rulings of deeds in the second volume of this book.

Thus, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was wounded in the Battle of Uhud in his face and incisors, and the Muslims were struck by what struck them. He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is an infallible prophet (نبي معصوم); if what struck him is attributed to his society—and they had disobeyed the command of Allah and His Messenger—then that was an evil misfortune that struck him because of what the hands of his society had earned while he was among them. But if it is attributed to his noble person, then that was a divine trial (محنة إلهية) that struck him in the way of Allah and on the path of his pure call to Allah upon insight; thus, it is a blessing that raises ranks.

Likewise, all the ill fortunes that strike a people are attributed to their deeds according to what the Quran holds—and it holds nothing but the truth. As for the good fortunes that strike them, they are from Allah the Glorified.

Yes, there are other verses that perhaps attribute good fortunes to them in some sense, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven" (7:96). And His saying: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكانُوا بِآياتِنا يُوقِنُونَ — "And We appointed from among them leaders who guided by Our command, when they had been patient and were certain of Our signs" (32:24). And His saying: وَأَدْخَلْناهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِنا إِنَّهُمْ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "And We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed they were among the righteous" (21:86). The verses in this sense are very many.

However, Allah the Glorified mentions in His speech that nothing of His creation is capable of anything it intends of its goal, nor is it guided to any good, except by Allah's enabling and guidance. He, the Exalted, said: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). And He, the Exalted, said: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure" (24:21). Through these two verses and what preceded, another meaning becomes clear for good fortunes being for Allah, exalted is His Name: that man does not possess any good fortune except by Allah's making him possess it and His conveying it. Thus, all good fortunes are for Allah and all ill fortunes are for man. By this, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: ما أَصابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللهِ وَما أَصابَكَ مِنْ سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِنْ نَفْسِكَ (the verse) becomes manifest.

So for Allah the Glorified are the good fortunes insofar as every good is created by Him, and creation and goodness are inseparable. And for Him are the good fortunes insofar as they are "goods" (خيرات), and in His hand is all good; no one else possesses it except by His making them possess it. No ill fortune is attributed to Him, for the ill fortune, from the perspective that it is an ill fortune, is not created, and His affair is creation. Rather, the ill fortune is, for example, man's loss of a mercy from His presence, which He withheld because of what the hands of people sent forth. As for good and ill fortune in the sense of obedience (الطاعة) and disobedience (المعصية), the discussion regarding their attribution to Allah the Glorified preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً — "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable" (2:26) in the first volume of this book.

If you were to refer to the commentaries on this point, you would find such a scattering of opinions, differing views and whims, and types of objections as would baffle you. I hope that what we have mentioned is sufficient for one who reflects on His speech, the Exalted. In this study, you must separate the various aspects of the research from one another, and understand what the Quran recognizes as the meaning of good and ill fortune, blessing and vengeance, and the difference between the personality of the society and the individual, so that the core of the speech becomes clear to you.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا (the verse): al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated via Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas: That Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf and some companions of his came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "O Prophet of Allah, we were in honor when we were polytheists, but when we believed, we became humiliated." He said: "I have been commanded to forgive, so do not fight the people." When Allah moved him to Medina, He commanded him to fight, but they withheld. So Allah revealed: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ (the verse).

In the same source: Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Qatadah regarding the verse: He said: There were people among the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, while they were in Mecca before the Hijrah (الهجرة) — the migration for the sake of Allah — who were hastening to fight. They said to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Let us take pickaxes and fight the polytheists with them." It was mentioned to us that Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was among those who said that. The Prophet of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله forbade them from that, saying: "I have not been commanded with that." When the Hijrah occurred and they were commanded to kill, the people disliked that and did regarding it what you hear. Allah the Exalted said: قُلْ مَتاعُ الدُّنْيا قَلِيلٌ وَالْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِمَنِ اتَّقى وَلا تُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Safwan ibn Yahya from Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام, he said: Allah the Exalted said: "O son of Adam! By My will, you were the one who wills and says; and by My strength, you performed My obligation to Me; and by My blessing, you were strong enough to disobey Me. Whatever good fortune befalls you is from Allah, and whatever misfortune befalls you is from yourself. That is because I am more worthy of your good deeds than you, and you are more worthy of your evil deeds than Me. That is because I am not questioned about what I do, but they are questioned."

I say: The narration was previously cited with a different wording in the first volume of this book under His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً (2:26), and the discussion regarding it was presented there.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Hajjaj, he said: Calamity (البلاء) and what Allah specifically assigns to the believer were mentioned before Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام. He said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked: "Who among people are most severely tried in the world?" He replied: "The Prophets, then the next best, then the next best. The believer is then tried according to his faith and the goodness of his deeds. He whose faith is sound and whose deed is good, his trial is severe; and he whose faith is flimsy and whose deed is weak, his trial is little."

I say: Among the famous narrations is his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "The world is the prison of the believer and the paradise of the disbeliever."

In the same source, also via several paths from both of them [Imams al-Baqir and al-Sadiq] عليه‌السلام: "When Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, loves a servant, He douses him in calamity (غثه بالبلاء غثا)." (1)

In the same source, also from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "The believer is like the pan of a scale; whenever his faith is increased, his trial is increased."

In the same source, also from al-Baqir عليه‌السلام, he said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, attends to the believer with calamity just as a man attends to his family with a gift from his absence; and He protects him from the world just as the physician protects the patient."

In the same source, also from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Allah has no need for one who has no share [of trial] in his wealth or his body."

In al-Ilal, from Ali ibn al-Husayn from his father عليه‌السلام, he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Even if the believer were on a mountain, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, would appoint for him someone to harm him so that He may reward him for that."

In the book al-Tamhis, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: "Anxieties and sorrows do not cease to afflict the believer until they leave him with no sin."

From him عليه‌السلام, he said: "Forty nights do not pass over a believer except that an affair occurs to him that saddens him, reminding him of his Lord."

In Nahj al-Balaghah, he [Imam Ali] عليه‌السلام said: "If a mountain were to love me, it would crumble (لتهافت)." And he عليه‌السلام said: "Whoever loves us, the People of the House (أهل البيت), let him prepare a cloak for calamity."

I say: Ibn Abi al-Hadid said in his commentary: "It has been established that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: 'None loves you but a believer, and none hates you but a hypocrite.' And it has been established that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed, trial (البلوى) is faster to the believer than water to a slope.' From these two premises, a true conclusion follows: that if a mountain were to love him, it would crumble." (End of quote).

Know that the reports regarding these meanings are many, and they support the exposition we have provided.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn al-Mundhir and al-Khatib narrated from Ibn Umar, who said: We were with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله among a group of his companions, and he said: "O you people! Do you not know that I am the Messenger of Allah to you?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Do you not know that Allah revealed in His Book that whoever obeys me has indeed obeyed Allah?" They said: "Yes, we bear witness that whoever obeys you has indeed obeyed Allah, and that part of His obedience is obedience to you." He said: "Then indeed, part of the obedience to Allah is that you obey me, and part of the obedience to me is that you obey your leaders (أئمتكم); and if they pray sitting, then pray sitting, all of you."

I say: His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "and if they pray..." etc., is a metaphor for the obligation of complete following (الاتباع).

(1) al-Ghath (الغث) means immersion (الغمس).

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُونَ طَاعَةٌ فَإِذَا بَرَزُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ بَيَّتَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى تَقُولُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَكْتُبُ مَا يُبَيِّتُونَ ۖ فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٨١)
They profess obedience [to you], but when they go out from your presence, a group of them conspire overnight [to do] something other than what you say. But Allah records what they conspire overnight. So disregard them and put your trust in Allah, for Allah suffices as trustee. (81)
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۚ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا۟ فِيهِ ٱخْتِلَـٰفًا كَثِيرًا (٨٢)
Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it. (82)
وَإِذَا جَآءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْنِ أَوِ ٱلْخَوْفِ أَذَاعُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِلَىٰٓ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَنۢبِطُونَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَٱتَّبَعْتُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٨٣)
When a report of safety or alarm comes to them, they immediately broadcast it; but had they referred it to the Apostle or to those vested with authority among them, those of them who investigate would have ascertained it. And were it not for Allah’s grace upon you and His mercy, you would have surely followed Satan, [all] except a few. (83)
فَقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ ۚ وَحَرِّضِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْسًا وَأَشَدُّ تَنكِيلًا (٨٤)
So fight in the way of Allah: you are responsible only for yourself, but urge on the faithful [to fight]. Maybe Allah will curb the might of the faithless, for Allah is greatest in might and severest in punishment. (84)

(Commentary)

The verses do not resist being connected to what preceded them; it is as if they are a continuation of the discourse regarding the reprimand of the weak-willed among the Muslims. Their purpose is to exhort them with that which would grant them insight, had they reflected and sought to perceive the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ طاعَةٌ (And they say, ‘Obedience!’) [4:81], etc. The word "Obedience" (طاعَةٌ) is in the nominative case as a predicate (خبر), according to what has been said, and the implied meaning is: "Our affair is obedience," meaning: "We obey you with full obedience." Emergence (البروز) means appearing and going out. Plotting by night (التبييت) is derived from staying overnight (البيتوتة), and its meaning is to finalize a matter and devise a plan by night. The pronoun in "you say" (تَقُولُ) refers either to the "party" (طائِفَةٌ) or to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: These people say, in response to your call to jihad (الجهاد) — the striving in the way of Allah: "Our affair is obedience." Yet, when they leave your presence, a party of them devises by night a matter other than what they answered you with and said to you, or other than what you said to them. This is an allusion to their firm intention to disobey the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

Then Allah commanded His Messenger to turn away from them and to rely on Him in the matter and in his resolve, saying: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ وَكَفى بِاللهِ وَكِيلاً (So turn away from them and trust in Allah, and Allah suffices as a trustee) [4:81]. There is no evidence in the verse indicating that those being reported about are the hypocrites (المنافقين), as some have mentioned; rather, looking at the continuity of the context, the matter suggests otherwise.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ (Do they not contemplate the Quran?) [4:82]. The verse is an exhortation in the form of an interrogation. Contemplation (التدبير) — the act of reflecting on the end of a matter or taking one thing after another — in the context of the verse, means reflecting on one verse after another, or repeated reflection upon a verse. However, since the objective is to demonstrate that there is no discrepancy (اختلاف) in the Quran, and such a thing only occurs between more than one verse, the first meaning—namely, reflecting on one verse following another—is the primary one, though it does not negate the second meaning.

The intent is to encourage them to contemplate the Quranic verses and to refer every revealed ruling (حكم), explained wisdom (حكمة), story (قصة), or exhortation (عظة) to all the verses related to it—whether revealed in Makkah or Madinah, whether they be the clear (المحكم) or the metaphorical (المتشابه) — and to join some to others until it becomes clear to them that there is no discrepancy between them. Thus, the older verses confirm the newer ones, and they bear witness to one another without any assumed discrepancy—neither the discrepancy of contradiction (التناقض), where some negate others or conflict with them, nor the discrepancy of variation (التفاوت), where two verses differ in the clarity of expression or the firmness of meanings and objectives, such that some are more solid in expression or stronger in foundation than others. كتابا متشابها مثاني تقشعر منه الجلود (a Book, consistent in its parts, and repeated, whereat quiver the skins) [39:23].

The absence of these discrepancies from the Quran guides them to the fact that it is a Book revealed by Allah and is not from anyone else. For if it were from other than Him, it would not be free from much discrepancy. This is because anything other than the Exalted among these cosmic existents—especially man, whom the doubters suspect is the author of this speech—is inherently subject, by its existential being (الكينونة الوجودية) and the nature of the cosmos (طبيعة الكون), to movement, change, and gradual perfection (التكامل). There is not one among them whose existential duration is not characterized by differing stages and varying states.

There is no human being who does not see every day that he is more rational than he was yesterday, and that the work, craft, or the like that he produces, or the opinion or view he devises later, is firmer and more solid than what he produced initially. Even a single work that has some existential extension, such as a book written by an author, a poem composed by a poet, or a sermon delivered by an orator—upon close scrutiny, its end is found to be better than its beginning, and some parts of it superior to others.

Thus, an individual human being is not free from discrepancy within himself or in the work he produces. It is not just one or two instances of variation or contradiction, but rather much discrepancy. This is a universal law (ناموس كلي) applicable to man and all creatures beneath him that fall under the dominion of general transformation and perfection. You do not see one of these existents remaining in a single state for two consecutive moments; rather, its essence and states are constantly changing.

From here, the reason for the qualification "much" in His saying: اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً (much discrepancy) becomes clear. The descriptor is an explanatory (توضيحي) one, not restrictive (احترازي). The meaning is: "Had it been from other than Allah, they would have found discrepancy in it, and that discrepancy would have been 'much,' according to the scale of the 'much discrepancy' found in everything that is from other than Allah." It does not mean that what is negated from the Quran is only "much" discrepancy to the exclusion of "slight" discrepancy.

In short, those who contemplate will soon observe that the Quran is a Book that permeates all affairs related to humanity: from the knowledge of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and creation and origination (الإيجاد); then the general human virtues; then the social and individual laws that govern the species in a way that neither the minute nor the grand escapes; then the stories, lessons, and exhortations with an eloquence that the people of the world were challenged to match. All this was revealed in stages over a period of twenty-three years under varying circumstances: night and day, at home and during travel, in war and peace, in hardship and ease, in severity and prosperity. Yet its state never changed in its miraculous, خارقة (extraordinary) eloquence, nor in its high knowledge and sublime wisdom, nor in its social and individual laws. Rather, its end bends back toward what its beginning settled upon, and its details and branches return to where its roots and principles are established. The details of its legislations and wisdoms return, through analysis (التحليل), to the core of pure monotheism (التوحيد), and its pure monotheism transforms, through synthesis (التركيب), into the very details it provided. This is the status of the Quran.

A person who contemplates it with such contemplation will judge, by his living intuition and innate judgment (القضاء الجبلي), that the Speaker of this discourse is not one who is governed by the passage of days or the transformation and perfection that act upon the cosmos; rather, He is Allah, the One, the All-Paramount (الواحد القهار).

It has become clear from the verse: First, that the Quran is something attainable by ordinary understanding. Second, that the verses of the Quran explain one another. Third, that the Quran is a Book that accepts neither abrogation (النسخ), nor nullification (الإبطال), nor completion, nor refinement, nor any judge that could ever rule over it. This is because whatever accepts any of these must necessarily accept a type of transformation and change. Since the Quran does not accept discrepancy, it does not accept transformation or change; thus, it accepts neither abrogation, nor nullification, nor anything else. The necessary consequence of this is that the Islamic sharia (الشريعة) — the divine system of law — continues until the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ أَذاعُوا بِهِ (When there comes to them a matter of security or fear, they broadcast it) [4:83]. Broadcasting (الإذاعة) is spreading and circulating. In the verse, there is a type of blame and reproach for them regarding this broadcasting. In His saying at the end of the verse: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ (Were it not for the grace of Allah...) [4:83], there is an indication that the believers were in danger of going astray due to this broadcasting. This is none other than the danger of disobeying the Messenger, for the discourse in these verses is centered on that. This is supported by what follows in the next verse, where the Messenger is commanded to fight even if he remains alone without a helper.

It appears from this that the "matter of security or fear" that came to them was some of the rumors (الأراجيف) brought by the hands of the disbelievers and their messengers sent to create hypocrisy and discord among the believers. The weak among the believers would broadcast it without contemplation or insight, which caused a weakening in the resolve of the believers. However, Allah, the Glorified, protected them from following these devils who came with those reports to humiliate the believers.

Thus, the verse applies to the story of the "Lesser Badr" (بدر الصغرى), and the discussion regarding it has already passed in Surah Al-Imran. The verses here resemble the verses there in content, as is apparent to those who contemplate them. The Exalted said in Surah Al-Imran: الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَصابَهُمُ الْقَرْحُ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا مِنْهُمْ وَاتَّقَوْا أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ (Those who responded to Allah and the Apostle after they had been wounded—for those of them who have been virtuous and God-conscious there shall be a great reward. Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have mustered against you, so fear them,’ but it only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee.’) [3:172-173]—until His saying—إِنَّما ذلِكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِياءَهُ فَلا تَخافُوهُمْ وَخافُونِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ (That is only Satan frightening his allies. So do not fear them, but fear Me, if you are believers.) [3:175].

The verses, as you see, mention that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was calling the people—after they had been wounded, which was the ordeal of Uhud—to go out against the disbelievers, and that some people were discouraging the people and deserting them from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, frightening them with the gathering of the polytheists.

Then it mentions that all of this was the frightening of Satan, speaking through the mouths of his allies, and it commands the believers not to fear them but to fear Allah, if they are truly believers.

One who contemplates these and the verses under discussion—namely, His saying: وَإِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ أَذاعُوا بِهِ — will not doubt that Allah, the Glorified, in this verse, is mentioning the story of the Lesser Badr and counting it among the traits for which He blames these weak-willed ones, such as His saying: فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتالُ (But when fighting was prescribed for them...) [4:77], and His saying: وَقالُوا رَبَّنا لِمَ كَتَبْتَ عَلَيْنَا الْقِتالَ (they said, ‘Our Lord! Why did You prescribe fighting for us?’) [4:77], and His saying: وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ (If a good fortune comes their way...) [4:78], and His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ طاعَةٌ (And they say, ‘Obedience!’) [4:81]. Then, following this course, comes His saying: وَإِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ أَذاعُوا بِهِ.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ وَإِلى أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ لَعَلِمَهُ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَنْبِطُونَهُ مِنْهُمْ (And if they had referred it to the Apostle and to those in authority among them, those of them who investigate it would have known it) [4:83]. He did not mention referring it to Allah here, as He did in His saying: فإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ (Then if you fall out regarding anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle) [4:59], because the referral mentioned there is the referral of a disputed legal ruling (الحكم الشرعي), in which no one has a say except Allah and His Messenger.

As for the referral mentioned here, it is the referral of a rumor circulating among the people regarding security or fear. There is no meaning in referring it to Allah and His Book; rather, the authority in it belongs to the Messenger and to those in authority (أولي الأمر) among them. If it were referred to them, they would be able to investigate it and inform those who referred it of its soundness or infirmity, and its truth or falsehood.

Thus, the intent of "knowing" (العلم) is discernment—discerning truth from falsehood and honesty from lying, similar to His saying: لِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ مَنْ يَخافُهُ بِالْغَيْبِ (so that Allah may know who fears Him in the Unseen) [5:94], and His saying: وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ (Allah will surely know those who have faith, and He will surely know the hypocrites) [29:11].

Investigation (الاستنباط) — the extraction of a matter from a state of ambiguity to the stage of discernment and knowledge — its root is from al-nabat (النبط), which is the first water that comes out of a well. Based on this, "investigation" could be a description of the Messenger and those in authority, meaning they verify the matter and attain the truth and honesty. Or it could be a description of those who refer the matter, such that if they referred it, they would know the truth of the matter and its honesty through the information provided by the Messenger and those in authority to them.

The meaning of the verse then becomes: If the intent of "those who investigate it among them" is the Messenger and those in authority—as is apparent from the verse—then "those who wished to investigate it from the Messenger and those in authority would have known it," meaning if those asked approved of it and saw it as being in the interest of the community. If the intent is those who refer the matter, then: "those who inquire and go to great lengths to obtain the origin of the report from these referrers would have known it."

As for "those in authority" (أولي الأمر) in His saying: وَإِلى أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ, the intent behind them is the same as the intent in His saying: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ (Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those in authority among you) [4:59], according to what has preceded regarding the disagreement of the commentators in its interpretation. It was previously mentioned that the primary opinions on this return to five, but the meaning we have derived is more apparent in this verse.

As for the opinion that "those in authority" are the commanders of the military detachments (أمراء السرايا), these individuals had no role except command over a detachment in a specific event that did not exceed their expertise or the circle of their work. As for matters like the subject of the verse—namely, the disruption of security and the creation of general fear and terror, which the polytheists sought through sending spies and secret messengers who broadcasted reports to discourage the believers—the commanders of detachments had no role in that such that they could clarify the truth of it to the people if they were asked about such reports.

As for the opinion that "those in authority" are the scholars (العلماء), its lack of suitability for the verse is even more apparent. For the scholars—who were then the narrators of hadith, the jurists, the reciters, and the theologians—their expertise was only in jurisprudence, hadith, and the like. The subject of His saying: وَإِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ consists of reports with political roots connected to various sides. Accepting, rejecting, or neglecting them might lead to vital corruption and social harms that might not be rectifiable by any other means, or might nullify the efforts of a nation on the path to its happiness, or take away their sovereignty and strike them with humiliation, poverty, killing, and captivity. What expertise do the scholars have—insofar as they are narrators, jurists, or reciters—in these issues such that Allah would command referring and returning them to them? What hope is there for solving such problems at their hands?

As for the opinion that "those in authority" are the Rightly Guided Caliphs—namely Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali—besides the fact that there is no evidence for it from the Book or the definitive Sunnah (السنة), it is refuted by the fact that the ruling of the verse is either specific to the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or general, covering his time and what follows. On the first assumption, it would have been necessary for them to be known for this role as these four specifically among the people and among the companions in particular; yet hadith and history do not record for them specifically a role of this kind. On the second assumption, it would necessitate the cessation of the verse's ruling with the end of their lives, and it would have required the verse to undertake the clarification of that, as is the case with all rulings specific to a portion of time mentioned in the Quran, such as the rulings specific to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, yet there is no trace of that in the verse.

As for the opinion that the intent of "those in authority" is "those who loosen and bind" (أهل الحل والعقد) — and this proponent, seeing that there was no specific group in the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله who were "those who loosen and bind" in the sense found among civilized nations with organized societies like a cabinet of ministers or a parliament, since the nation was only governed by the ruling of Allah and His Messenger—was forced to interpret it as the people of consultation (أهل الشورى) among the companions and the close associates of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

Whatever the case, it is refuted by the fact that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to include in his consultation both believers and hypocrites, such as Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions; the account of his consultation on the day of Uhud is well-known. How could Allah command referring matters to the likes of him?

Furthermore, among those who were undoubtedly of this status during the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and after him was Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf. Yet these verses, laid out in reprimand of the weak believers and reproaching them for what occurred from them, actually began with him and his companions—namely His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا (Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off...’) [4:77]. It has been recorded in the Sahih that it was revealed concerning Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf and his companions; this was narrated by al-Nasa'i in his Sahih, by al-Hakim in his Mustadrak (who authenticated it), and by al-Tabari and others in their commentaries. The narration has already passed in the preceding "Discussion on Traditions." If the matter is of this nature, how could it be commanded in the verse to return and refer the matter to the likes of these?

Therefore, what is determined is what we favored in His saying, the Exalted: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَاتَّبَعْتُمُ الشَّيْطانَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً (Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, you would have surely followed Satan, [all] except a few) [4:83]. It has preceded that the most apparent view is that the verses refer to the story of the Lesser Badr, and Abu Sufyan's sending of Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud al-Ashja'i to Madinah to spread fear and terror among the people and to discourage them from going out to Badr. Thus, the intent of "following Satan" is believing the news he brought and following him in failing to go out to Badr.

By this, the soundness of the meaning of the exception becomes clear without the need for affectation or artifice. For Nu'aym was informing them that Abu Sufyan had gathered the masses and equipped the armies, so "fear them and do not throw yourselves into the pools of widespread slaughter." This affected the hearts of the people, so they made excuses to avoid going out to their appointment at Badr. None were safe from this except the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and some of his close associates, and that is the intent of His saying: إِلَّا قَلِيلاً (except a few). For the people had wavered except for a few of them, then they joined those few and marched.

This meaning that we have derived for the exception is what is supported by the aforementioned clues, along with its inherent soundness.

The commentators have various views regarding this exception, none of which is free from flaw or affectation. It has been said: The intent of "grace and mercy" is what Allah guided them to of the obligation of obeying Him, His Messenger, and those in authority among them. The intent of the excepted ones is the believers with sound primordial nature (الفطرة السليمة) and pure hearts. The meaning of the verse would be: "Were it not for this which Allah guided you to of the obligation of obedience and referring the matter to the Messenger and those in authority, you would have all followed Satan by falling into misguidance, except a few of you from the people of sound primordial nature, for they do not deviate from truth and righteousness." The flaw in this is that it restricts "grace and mercy" to a specific ruling without evidence, which is far from the Quranic style of expression, especially since the apparent meaning of the verse is a reminder of a favor in a past, concluded matter.

It was also said that the verse is according to its apparent meaning, and that the non-sincere believers need extra grace and mercy, even if the sincere ones are also not independent of divine care. The flaw here is that what the apparent meaning suggests in this case is something that the eloquence of the Quran must dispel, yet it was not dispelled in the verse. Indeed, the Exalted said: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ ما زَكى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَداً (Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, not one of you would ever have been pure) [24:21]. And He said, addressing His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who is the best of people: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلاً إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ (Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly leaned towards them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death) [17:74-75].

It was also said: The intent of "grace and mercy" is the Quran and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. It was said: The intent is victory and triumph; thus the exception becomes sound because the majority only remain firm upon the truth through that which pleases their hearts of victory, triumph, and similar outward divine favors, while none endure the bitterness of truth except the few believers who possess insight into their affair. It was said: The exception is from His saying: أَذاعُوا بِهِ (they broadcast it). It was said: The exception is from His saying: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَنْبِطُونَهُ (those who investigate it). It was said: The exception is only in the wording and is an evidence of totality and encompassment; thus the meaning of the verse is: "Were it not for Allah's grace and mercy on you, you would have all followed Satan." This is similar to His saying: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ (We shall have you recite, and you will not forget, except what Allah wishes) [87:6-7], where the exception of the Will (المشية) signifies the generality of the ruling in negating forgetfulness. All these views are not free from obvious affectation.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ وَحَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (So fight in the way of Allah: you are responsible only for yourself; and urge on the believers) [4:84]. Responsibility (التكليف) is from al-kulfah, meaning hardship, because it involves imposing hardship on the one being charged. Exemplary punishment (التنكيل) is from al-nakal, which, according to al-Majma', is that which prevents corruption out of fear of a similar punishment; it is the punishment of the offender so that he does not return to the like of it and so that others may take heed.

The "so" (الفاء) in His saying: فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ is for derivation (التفريع), and the command to fight is derived from what was gathered from the contents of the previous verses. This is the people's sluggishness in going out to the enemy and their delay in that. This is indicated by the following phrases, namely His saying: لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ (you are responsible only for yourself), for the meaning is: "Since they are sluggish in the matter of jihad and dislike fighting, then you, O Messenger of Allah, fight by yourself. Let not their sluggishness and their opposition to the command of Allah, the Glorified, be hard upon you, for the responsibility of others is not directed toward you. Rather, only the responsibility for yourself is directed toward you, not their responsibility. Your only duty regarding others is to urge them on." So fight وَحَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَكُفَّ بَأْسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (and urge on the believers. Maybe Allah will curb the might of the faithless) [4:84]. His saying: لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ means: "You are not charged with anything except your own action," so the exception is by assuming a suppressed noun (مضاف).

His saying: عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَكُفَّ (Maybe Allah will curb...), it has already been mentioned that "maybe" (عسى) indicates hope, whether that hope resides in the speaker, the addressee, or the context of the address. Thus, there is no need for what they mentioned—that "maybe" from Allah is a certainty.

In the verse, there is an indication of an increased reproach from Allah, the Glorified, for the sluggish people, as their sluggishness led to Allah commanding His Prophet to undertake the fighting by himself, and to turn away from the sluggish ones, not to persist in seeking their response, to leave them to their own devices, and not to let his breast be straitened by that. He is only responsible for himself and for urging the believers—whether they obey or disobey.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Ajlan, who said: I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "Allah reproached certain groups for broadcasting (الإذاعة) in His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذا جاءَهُمْ أَمْرٌ مِنَ الْأَمْنِ أَوِ الْخَوْفِ أَذاعُوا بِهِ. So beware of broadcasting."

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Hamid ibn Abi al-Daylam from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ, and He said: وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ وَإِلى أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ ، لَعَلِمَهُ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَنْبِطُونَهُ مِنْهُمْ. Thus, He referred the affair of the people to those in authority (أولي الأمر) among them, whom He commanded to be obeyed and to whom matters should be referred."

I say: The narration supports what we previously stated—that the intent of "those in authority" in the second verse is the same as those mentioned in the first verse.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abdullah ibn Ajlan from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: وَلَوْ رَدُّوهُ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ وَإِلى أُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْهُمْ, he said: "They are the Imams (الأئمة)."

I say: This meaning was also narrated from Abdullah ibn Jundub from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in a letter he wrote to him regarding the matter of the Waqifiyyah. This meaning was also narrated by al-Mufid in al-Ikhtisas from Ishaq ibn Ammar from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a long tradition.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ, he said: "The grace (الفضل) is the Messenger of Allah, and His mercy (رحمته) is the Commander of the Faithful (أمير المؤمنين)."

In the same source, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), and Humran from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "لَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ — he said: 'The grace of Allah is His Messenger, and His mercy is the guardianship (ولاية) of the Imams.'"

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from the Righteous Servant (peace be upon him), who said: "The mercy is the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the grace is Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him)."

I say: These narrations are of the nature of application (الجري), and the intent is Prophethood (النبوة) and Guardianship (الولاية). For they are the two connected means by which Allah saved us from the abyss of misguidance and the trap of Satan. One is a conveying means, and the other is an implementing means. The last narration is closer to consideration, as Allah called His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in His Book "mercy" where He said: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ (We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds) [21:107].

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Ali ibn Hadid from Murazim, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Allah charged the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with that which He did not charge anyone else among His creation. Then He charged him to go out against all the people alone by himself, even if he did not find a group to fight with him. He did not charge anyone among His creation with this, neither before him nor after him." Then he recited this verse: فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ.

Then he said: "And Allah granted him that he should receive what he took for himself, for the Mighty and Majestic said: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها (Whoever brings a virtue shall receive ten times its like) [6:160], and He made the blessing (الصلاة) upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله equal to ten virtues."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Sulayman ibn Khalid, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "What about the people's saying regarding Ali (peace be upon him): 'If he had a right, what prevented him from rising for it?'" He said: "Allah does not charge any single human being with this except the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He said: فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ وَحَرِّضِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ. This is for none but the Messenger. And He said to others: إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى فِئَةٍ (unless it be to maneuver for battle or to join another company) [8:16]—and on that day, there was no company to help him in his affair."

In the same source, from Zayd al-Shahham from Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was never asked for anything to which he said 'No.' If he had it, he gave it; and if he did not have it, he said: 'It will be, Allah willing.' He never repaid an evil with an evil. And he never met a military detachment since this was revealed to him: فَقاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ لا تُكَلَّفُ إِلَّا نَفْسَكَ, but that he led it himself."

I say: There are other narrations with these meanings.

Ayahs

مَّن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ نَصِيبٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ كِفْلٌ مِّنْهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا (٨٥)
Whoever intercedes for a good cause shall receive a share of it, and whoever intercedes for an evil cause shall share its burden, and Allah is prepotent over all things. (85)
وَإِذَا حُيِّيتُم بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا۟ بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْهَآ أَوْ رُدُّوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَسِيبًا (٨٦)
When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it, or return it; indeed Allah takes account of all things. (86)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۗ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حَدِيثًا (٨٧)
Allah—there is no god except Him—will surely gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt; and who is more truthful in speech than Allah? (87)
۞ فَمَا لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَٱللَّهُ أَرْكَسَهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوٓا۟ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَهْدُوا۟ مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا (٨٨)
Why should you be two groups concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them relapse [into unfaith] because of their deeds? Do you desire to guide someone Allah has led astray? Whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him. (88)
وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَمَا كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَكُونُونَ سَوَآءً ۖ فَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٨٩)
They are eager that you should disbelieve like they have disbelieved, so that you all become alike. So do not make friends [with anyone] from among them, until they migrate in the way of Allah. But if they turn their backs, seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and do not take from among them friends or helpers, (89)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ أَوْ جَآءُوكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ أَن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ أَوْ يُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَسَلَّطَهُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ فَلَقَـٰتَلُوكُمْ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱعْتَزَلُوكُمْ فَلَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ فَمَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سَبِيلًا (٩٠)
excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you, or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have imposed them upon you, and then they would have surely fought you. So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any course [of action] against them. (90)
سَتَجِدُونَ ءَاخَرِينَ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَأْمَنُوكُمْ وَيَأْمَنُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ كُلَّ مَا رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ أُرْكِسُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ يَعْتَزِلُوكُمْ وَيُلْقُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَمَ وَيَكُفُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ جَعَلْنَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (٩١)
You will find others desiring to be secure from you, and secure from their own people; yet whenever they are called back to polytheism, they relapse into it. So if they do not keep out of your way, nor offer you peace, nor keep their hands off [from fighting], then seize them and kill them wherever you confront them, and it is such against whom We have given you a clear sanction. (91)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, as they all (85–91) address matters related to fighting a group of polytheists (المشركين), specifically the hypocrites (المنافقون) among them. It appears from contemplating them that they were revealed concerning a group of polytheists who displayed faith (الإيمان) to the believers, then returned to their home and joined the polytheists in their polytheism (شركهم). Consequently, doubt arose regarding fighting them, and the views of the Muslims differed concerning their affair. Some argued for fighting them, while others forbade it and interceded for them because of their outward display of faith. Allah, the Glorified, then prescribed for them either emigration (المهاجرة) or combat, and warned the believers against interceding on their behalf.

They are followed by another group, and then another, for whom it is prescribed to either offer peace (إلقاء السلم) or face combat. To introduce the objectives contained in the verses, the discourse begins by explaining the state of intercession (الشفاعة) in one verse, and the state of greeting (التحية) in another, due to its relevance to the offering of peace.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يَشْفَعْ شَفاعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُنْ لَهُ نَصِيبٌ مِنْها — Whoever intercedes with a good intercession shall have a share of it (4:85). The "share" (النصيب) and the "portion" (الكفل) carry the same meaning. Since intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah or among people — is a type of mediation to repair a deficiency or to acquire a merit and the like, it possesses a kind of causality in rectifying an affair. Thus, it carries a degree of consequence (تبعة) or reward (مثوبة) related to the purpose for which the intercession was made, which is the objective of both the intercessor and the one interceded for. Therefore, the intercessor possesses a share of the good or evil resulting from the intercession, which is His saying, the Exalted: "Whoever intercedes with an intercession..." (the verse).

Mentioning this reality serves as a reminder for the believers and a notification for them to be vigilant when interceding as to what they are interceding for. They must avoid it if the purpose of the intercession involves evil or corruption, such as interceding for the hypocrites among the polytheists so that they are not fought. Indeed, leaving a small corruption as it is and allowing it to grow and magnify results in a subsequent corruption that nothing can withstand, leading to the destruction of tilth and progeny. Thus, the verse carries the meaning of a prohibition against an evil intercession, which is the intercession for the people of oppression, tyranny, hypocrisy, and polytheism who spread corruption in the land.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا حُيِّيتُمْ بِتَحِيَّةٍ فَحَيُّوا بِأَحْسَنَ مِنْها — When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it (4:86). This is a command to respond to a greeting (التحية) with that which exceeds it or equals it. It is a general ruling for every greeting one is greeted with, although the context of the verses specifically concerns the greeting of peace and reconciliation offered to the Muslims, as appears from the subsequent verses.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَيَجْمَعَنَّكُمْ — Allah—there is no god except Him. He will surely gather you (4:87). The meaning of the verse is clear, and it serves as a rationale for the content of the two preceding verses. It is as if it were said: Adhere to what Allah has charged you with regarding good and evil intercession, and do not nullify the greeting of one who greets you by turning away or rejecting it, for before you is a Day on which Allah will gather you and requite you for responding to what He called you toward or rejecting it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما لَكُمْ فِي الْمُنافِقِينَ فِئَتَيْنِ وَاللهُ أَرْكَسَهُمْ — Why should you be two parties concerning the hypocrites, while Allah has made them regress (4:88). The "party" (الفئة) is the group, and "regression" (الإركاس) is the turning back.

The verse, given its content, is as if it branches out from the preceding introduction and preparation—namely His saying: "Whoever intercedes with an intercession..." (the verse). The meaning is: If an evil intercession gives its practitioner a portion of its wretchedness, then why is it, O believers, that you have split into two parties concerning the hypocrites and divided into two factions? One party sees the necessity of fighting them, while another party intercedes for them and urges the abandonment of fighting them, overlooking the tree of corruption that grows with their growth and bears fruit with their maturity. Allah has returned them to misguidance after they had emerged from it, as a recompense for the evil deeds they earned.
