Do you desire, through your intercession, to guide those whom Allah has led astray? And whomever Allah leads astray, for him you will find no way to guidance.

In His saying: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ سَبِيلاً — and whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him (4:88), there is a transition (التفات) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This is an indication that those among the believers who intercede for them do not fully comprehend the reality of this discourse; had they understood it, they would not have interceded on their behalf. Thus, He turned away from addressing them with it and directed it to the one for whom it is clear and evident, namely the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: وَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ كَما كَفَرُوا فَتَكُونُونَ سَواءً — They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved, so that you may be all alike (4:89). This serves as an explanation for His saying: "while Allah has made them regress because of what they have earned. Do you desire to guide those whom Allah has led astray?" The meaning is: They disbelieved, and added to that their wish and desire that you should disbelieve like them so that you become equal.

Then He forbade taking them as allies (الولاية) unless they emigrate in the way of Allah. If they turn away, then you have no obligation toward them except to seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and to avoid taking them as allies or helpers. In His saying: "But if they turn away" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا), there is an indication that the believers must charge them with emigration; if they respond, they may be allied with, but if they turn away, they are to be killed.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ إِلى قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ أَوْ جاؤُكُمْ حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ — excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant (4:90). Allah, the Glorified, excepted two groups from His saying: "But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them." The first: "those who join..." (الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ) etc., meaning those between whom and some of the people of the treaty (أهل الميثاق) there is a connection that joins them, such as an alliance or the like. The second: those who feel constrained (يتحرجون) from fighting the Muslims and from fighting their own people, whether due to their killing or other factors. They withdraw from the believers and offer them peace, being neither for the believers nor against them in any way. These two groups are excepted from the aforementioned ruling. His saying: "their hearts being reluctant" (حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ) means they became constricted.

His saying, the Exalted: سَتَجِدُونَ آخَرِينَ — You will find others (4:91). This is an announcement that you will encounter another group who may resemble the second of the two excepted groups, in that they wish to be secure from you and secure from their own people. However, Allah, the Glorified, informs that they are hypocrites who are not to be trusted in their promises or their peace-making. Therefore, He replaced the two positive conditions mentioned regarding others—namely His saying: "So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace"—with the negative condition—namely His saying: "If they do not keep out of your way, nor offer you peace, nor restrain their hands" (4:91). This is in the sense of alerting the believers to be cautious of them. The meaning of the verse is clear.

A Discourse on the Meaning of Greeting (التحية)

Nations and peoples, despite their differences in civilization, primitivity, progress, or backwardness, are never without a greeting (تحية) in their societies that they recognize when meeting one another. These greetings take various forms and types, such as nodding the head, gesturing with the hand, tipping hats, and so on. They vary according to the different factors operating within their societies.

If you contemplate these greetings circulating among nations in all their diversity, you will find them indicative of a type of submission, humility, and self-abasement shown by the lower to the higher, the lowly to the noble, the obedient to the one obeyed, and the slave to the master. In short, they reveal a custom of enslavement (الاستعباد) that has remained prevalent among nations during eras of barbarism and beyond, even if its colors have changed. For this reason, we see that such greetings begin from the obedient and end with the obeyed, starting from the lowly subordinate and concluding with the high and noble. Thus, they are among the fruits of idolatry (الوثنية) which suckles from the breast of enslavement.

Islam—as you know—has as its greatest concern the erasure of idolatry and every custom that leads to it or is born from it. Therefore, it adopted for this matter a straight path and a tradition (سنة) opposite to the tradition of idolatry and the custom of enslavement. This is the offering of peace (إلقاء السلام), which is a form of security for the one greeted from being transgressed against or having his innate human freedom (الحرية الفطرية) granted to him suppressed. The first thing required for cooperative society among individuals is that they feel secure from one another regarding their lives, honor, and property, and every matter that reverts to one of these three.

This is the "Peace" (السلام) which Allah the Exalted has legislated to be offered at every meeting between two people. The Exalted said: فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمْ بُيُوتاً فَسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُبارَكَةً طَيِّبَةً — But when you enter houses, greet yourselves with a salutation from Allah, blessed and good (24:61). And the Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتاً غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّى تَسْتَأْنِسُوا وَتُسَلِّمُوا عَلى أَهْلِها ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ — O you who have faith! Do not enter houses other than your own until you have announced your presence and greeted their inmates. That is better for you. Maybe you will take admonition (24:27). Allah disciplined His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by greeting the believers, even though he is their master, saying: وَإِذا جاءَكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِنا فَقُلْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ كَتَبَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ — When those who have faith in Our signs come to you, say, ‘Peace be to you! Your Lord has made mercy incumbent upon Himself’ (6:54). He also commanded him to greet others in His saying: فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ فَسوفَ يَعْلَمُونَ — So overlook them and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know (43:89).

The greeting by offering peace (إلقاء السلام) was practiced among the Arabs of the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية), as evidenced by what has been transmitted from them in poetry and the like. In Lisan al-Arab: "The Arabs in the pre-Islamic period used to greet by one saying to his companion: 'May your morning be pleasant' (أنعم صباحا) and 'May you avoid being cursed' (أبيت اللعن). They would also say 'Peace be upon you' (سلام عليكم), as if it were a sign of peacefulness and that there was no war. Then Allah brought Islam, so they restricted it to 'Peace' (السلام) and were commanded to spread it." (End quote).

However, Allah, the Glorified, recounts it in the stories of Abraham from him (peace be upon him) frequently. This is not without evidence that it was among the remnants of the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف) of Abraham among the Arabs, like the Hajj and similar rites. The Exalted said, recounting his dialogue with his father: قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي — He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you’ (19:47). And the Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا إِبْراهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرى قالُوا سَلاماً قالَ سَلامٌ — Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news. They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said (11:69). This story occurs in more than one place in the Noble Quran.

Allah, the Glorified, has adopted it as a greeting for Himself and used it in various instances of His speech. The Exalted said: سَلامٌ عَلى نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ — ‘Peace be to Noah throughout all the worlds!’ (37:79). And He said: سَلامٌ عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ — ‘Peace be to Abraham!’ (37:109). And He said: سَلامٌ عَلى مُوسى وَهارُونَ — ‘Peace be to Moses and Aaron!’ (37:120). And He said: سَلامٌ عَلى إِلْ ياسِينَ — ‘Peace be to Ilyasin!’ (37:130). And He said: وَسَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ — And peace be to the apostles! (37:181).

The Exalted mentioned that it is the greeting of His noble angels, saying: الَّذِينَ تَتَوفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ — those whom the angels take away while they are pure, saying [to them], ‘Peace be to you!’ (16:32). And He said: وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ — and the angels will enter on them from every door: ‘Peace be to you!’ (13:23–24). He also mentioned that it is the greeting of the people of Paradise, saying: وَتَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ — and their greeting therein will be ‘Peace!’ (10:10). And the Exalted said: لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً — They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, but only the word, ‘Peace! Peace!’ (56:25–26).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "When you are greeted..." (the verse): He said: Ali ibn Ibrahim mentioned in his Tafsir, from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them): "The meaning of the greeting (التحية) in the verse is peace (السلام) and other forms of righteousness (البر)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-Sukuni, he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: السلام تطوع والرد فريضة — "The greeting is voluntary, and the response is an obligation."

In the same source, with its chain from Jarrah al-Mada'ini from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: يسلم الصغير على الكبير ، والمار على القاعد ، والقليل على الكثير — "The younger should greet the older, the passerby should greet the one sitting, and the few should greet the many."

In the same source, with its chain from 'Uyaynah (1) from Mus'ab from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: القليل يبدءون الكثير بالسلام ، والراكب يبدأ الماشي ، وأصحاب البغال يبدءون أصحاب الحمير ، وأصحاب الخيل يبدءون أصحاب البغال — "The few should initiate the greeting to the many, the rider should initiate to the walker, the owners of mules should initiate to the owners of donkeys, and the owners of horses should initiate to the owners of mules."

In the same source, with its chain from Ibn Bukayr from some of his companions from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I heard him saying: يسلم الراكب على الماشي ، والماشي على القاعد ، وإذا لقيت جماعة سلم الأقل على الأكثر ، وإذا لقي واحد جماعة سلم الواحد على الجماعة — "The rider greets the walker, and the walker greets the one sitting. When a group meets another, the smaller group greets the larger one, and when one person meets a group, the individual greets the group."

I say: Something similar was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi from Zayd ibn Aslam from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the same source, with the chain from him (peace be upon him), he said: إذا مرت الجماعة بقوم أجزأهم أن يسلم واحد منهم ، وإذا سلم على القوم وهم جماعة ، أجزأهم أن يرد واحد منهم — "If a group passes by a people, it suffices for one of them to greet. If a greeting is given to a people who are a group, it suffices for one of them to respond."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim, he said: I entered upon Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) while he was in prayer and said: "Peace be upon you." He replied: السلام عليك — "Peace be upon you." I then said: "How have you reached the morning?" He remained silent. When he finished, I asked: "Does one respond to the greeting while in prayer?" He said: نعم ، مثل ما قيل له — "Yes, exactly as it was said to him."

In the same source, with its chain from Mansur ibn Hazim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: إذا سلم عليك الرجل وأنت تصلي ، قال : ترد عليه خفيا كما قال — "If a man greets you while you are praying, he said: You respond to him quietly just as he said."

In al-Faqih, with its chain from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him), he said: لا تسلموا على اليهود ، ولا على النصارى ، ولا على المجوس ، ولا على عبدة الأوثان ، ولا على موائد شراب الخمر ، ولا على صاحب الشطرنج والنرد ، ولا على المخنث ، ولا على الشاعر الذي يقذف المحصنات ، ولا على المصلي لأن المصلي لا يستطيع أن يرد السلام ، لأن التسليم من المسلم تطوع والرد فريضة ، ولا على آكل الربا ، ولا على رجل جالس على غائط ـ ولا على الذي في الحمام ، ولا على الفاسق المعلن بفسقه — "Do not greet the Jews, nor the Christians, nor the Magians, nor the idol-worshippers, nor those at tables where wine is drunk, nor the player of chess and backgammon, nor the effeminate, nor the poet who slanders chaste women, nor the one praying—because the one praying cannot respond to the greeting, for the initiation of peace by the Muslim is voluntary while the response is an obligation—nor the consumer of usury, nor a man sitting for defecation, nor the one in the bathhouse, nor the profligate who is open about his profligacy."

I say: The narrations carrying the meaning of what has preceded are numerous. Grasping the preceding explanation clarifies the meaning of the narrations. Peace (السلام) is a greeting signaling the extension of peace and the spreading of security between those meeting on the basis of equality (المساواة) and balance (التعادل), free from superiority or suppression. What is found in the narrations regarding the younger initiating the greeting to the older, the few to the many, and the individual to the group does not contradict the principle of equality. Rather, it is based on the necessity of observing rights (الحقوق). Islam did not command its followers to abolish rights or neglect the matter of virtues and merits; rather, it commanded the one without merit to observe the merit of the one possessing it and the right of the right-holder. It only forbade the one with merit from being conceited with his merit and acting arrogantly toward others, thereby transgressing against people without right, which would destroy the balance between the parts of human society.

As for the prohibition mentioned regarding greeting certain individuals, it branches out from the prohibition of taking them as allies (توليهم) or leaning toward them (الركون إليهم), as the Exalted said: لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصارى أَوْلِياءَ — do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies (5:51). And He said: لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ — do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends (60:1). And He said: وَلا تَرْكَنُوا إِلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا — And do not incline toward the wrongdoers (11:113), among other verses.

Yes, sometimes the interest of drawing near to the wrongdoers to convey the religion or to make them hear the word of truth necessitates greeting them, so that complete familiarity is achieved and souls are blended, as the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded in His saying: "So overlook them and say, ‘Peace!’" (43:89), and as in His saying describing the believers: وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً — and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’ (25:63).

In Tafsir al-Safi, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: A man said to him: "Peace be upon you." He replied: وعليك السلام ورحمة الله — "And upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah." Another said: "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah." He replied: وعليك السلام ورحمة الله وبركاته — "And upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah and His blessings." Another said: "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah and His blessings." He replied: وعليك — "And upon you." The man said: "You have diminished me; where is what Allah said: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it'?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: إنك لم تترك فضلا ورددت عليك مثله — "You left no room for increase, so I returned to you its like." I say: Something similar was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ahmad in al-Zuhd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh with a good chain from Salman al-Farsi.

In al-Kafi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) passed by a people and greeted them. They said: "Upon you be peace and the mercy of Allah, His blessings, His forgiveness, and His pleasure." The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said to them: لا تجاوزوا بنا مثل ما قالت الملائكة لأبينا إبراهيم ، قالوا : رحمة الله وبركاته عليكم أهل البيت — "Do not exceed for us what the angels said to our father Abraham; they said: 'May Allah’s mercy and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household!'"

I say: In this is an indication that the tradition (السنة) of the complete greeting—which is the Muslim saying "Peace be upon you and the mercy of Allah and His blessings"—is taken from the primordial religion (الحنيفية) of Abraham (peace be upon him), and it supports what was mentioned earlier that the greeting of peace is from the primordial religion.

In the same source, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Part of the completion of the greeting for the resident is the handshake (المصافحة), and the completion of the greeting for the traveler is the embrace (المعانقة)."

In al-Khisal, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "When one of you sneezes, say: 'May Allah have mercy on you' (يرحمكم الله), and he should say: 'May Allah forgive you and have mercy on you' (يغفر الله لكم ويرحمكم). Allah the Exalted said: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it' (the verse)."

In al-Manaqib: A slave-girl brought a bunch of aromatic herbs to al-Hasan (peace be upon him). He said to her: "You are free for the sake of Allah." When questioned about this, he (peace be upon him) said: "Allah the Exalted disciplined us, saying: 'When you are greeted with a salute, greet with a better one than it' (the verse), and the better response was her manumission."

I say: The narrations, as you see, generalize the meaning of the greeting (التحية) in the verse.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Why should you be two parties concerning the hypocrites..." (the verse): He said: They differed as to whom this verse was revealed about. It was said: It was revealed concerning a people who came to Medina from Mecca and displayed Islam to the Muslims, then returned to Mecca—because they found the climate of Medina unhealthy—and displayed polytheism. Then they traveled with the goods of the polytheists to al-Yamamah. The Muslims wanted to raid them but differed: some said, "We shall not do so, for they are believers," while others said, "They are polytheists." So Allah revealed the verse concerning them. He said: This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved..." (the verse): It was revealed concerning Ashja' and Banu Damrah, which are two tribes. The account of them is that when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went out for the campaign of al-Hudaybiyyah, he passed near their lands. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had already made a truce with Banu Damrah and made a promise to them before that. The companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O Messenger of Allah, these are Banu Damrah near us, and we fear they might act against us in Medina or aid the Quraysh against us; if only we were to start with them." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "By no means! They are the most dutiful of the Arabs to parents, the most maintaining of kinship, and the most faithful to covenants."

The lands of Ashja' were near the lands of Banu Damrah, who were a branch of Kinanah. There was an alliance between Ashja' and Banu Damrah for grazing and security. When the lands of Ashja' suffered drought and the lands of Banu Damrah became fertile, Ashja' moved to the lands of Banu Damrah. When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله heard of their movement to Banu Damrah, he prepared to march to Ashja' to raid them because of the truce between him and Banu Damrah. Then Allah revealed: "They wish that you should disbelieve just as they have disbelieved, so that you may be all alike. So do not take allies from among them until they migrate in the way of Allah. But if they turn away, then seize them and kill them wherever you find them, and do not take from among them any ally or helper."

Then He excepted Ashja', saying: "excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty, or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have given them mastery over you, and they would have surely fought you. So if they keep out of your way and do not fight you, and offer you peace, then Allah does not allow you any way [of sanction] against them."

The dwellings of Ashja' were al-Bayda, al-Hull, and al-Mustabah. They had drawn near to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and feared, due to their proximity, that he would send someone to raid them. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had also feared they might strike at his outskirts, so he intended to march toward them. While he was in that state, Ashja' arrived with their leader, Mas'ud ibn Rujaylah, numbering seven hundred. They encamped in the valley of Sal', and that was in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal in the sixth year of the Hijrah. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله called Usayd ibn Husayn and said to him: "Go with a group of your companions until you see what has brought Ashja'."

Usayd went out with three of his companions and stood before them, asking: "What has brought you?" Mas'ud ibn Rujaylah, the leader of Ashja', stood up, greeted Usayd and his companions, and said: "We have come to make a truce with Muhammad." Usayd returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "The people feared I would raid them, so they wanted peace between me and them." Then he sent them ten loads of dates, presenting them before him, and said: "What a fine thing is a gift before a need." Then he came to them and said: "O people of Ashja', what has brought you?" They said: "Our dwellings are near you, and there is no tribe among our people fewer in number than us. We are constrained from fighting you due to our proximity to you, and we are constrained from fighting our people due to our small number among them. So we have come to make a truce with you." The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله accepted from them and made a truce with them. They stayed for their day and then returned to their lands. Concerning them, this verse was revealed: "excepting those who join a people between whom and you there is a treaty... (to His saying) then Allah does not allow you any way [of sanction] against them."

In al-Kafi, with its chain from al-Fadl Abu al-Abbas from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "or such as come to you with hearts reluctant to fight you or to fight their own people," he said: "It was revealed concerning Banu Mudlij, for they came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'Our hearts are reluctant to testify that you are the Messenger of Allah, so we are neither with you nor with our people against you.'" He said: I asked: "What did the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله do with them?" He said: "He made a truce with them until he finished with the Arabs, then he would call them; if they responded, [well and good], otherwise he would fight them."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Sayf ibn 'Amirah, he said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about: "to fight you or to fight their own people. Had Allah wished, He would have given them mastery over you, and they would have surely fought you." He said: "My father used to say: It was revealed concerning Banu Mudlij; they withdrew and did not fight the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, nor were they with their people." I asked: "What did he do with them?" He said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not fight them until he finished with his enemy, then he renounced [the treaty] to them on equal terms." He said: "And 'their hearts being reluctant' (حَصِرَتْ صُدُورُهُمْ) means constriction."

In al-Majma': It is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said: "The meaning of His saying, the Exalted: 'a people between whom and you there is a treaty' refers to Hilal ibn 'Uwaymir al-Sulami, who made a treaty on behalf of his people with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He said in his truce: 'On the condition, O Muhammad, that we do not frighten whoever comes to us, and you do not frighten whoever comes to you.' So Allah forbade interfering with anyone who had a covenant with them."

I say: These meanings and similar ones have been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various paths from Ibn Abbas and others.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu Dawud in his Nasikh, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from Ibn Abbas: Regarding His saying: "excepting those who join a people..." (the verse), he said: "It was abrogated by Bara'ah: 'Then, when the sacred months have passed, slay the polytheists wherever you find them' (9:5)."

Ayahs

وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَن يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِنًا إِلَّا خَطَـًٔا ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَـًٔا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ وَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَصَّدَّقُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَّكُمْ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ وَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ تَوْبَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٩٢)
A believer may not kill another believer, unless it is by mistake. Anyone who kills a believer by mistake should set free a believing slave, and pay blood-money to his family, unless they remit it in charity. If he belongs to a people that are hostile to you but is a believer, then a believing slave is to be set free. And if he belongs to a people with whom you have a treaty, the blood-money is to be paid to his family and a believing slave is to be set free. He who cannot afford [to pay the blood-money], must fast two successive months as a penance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (92)
وَمَن يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِنًا مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآؤُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَلَعَنَهُۥ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُۥ عَذَابًا عَظِيمًا (٩٣)
Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to remain in it [forever]; Allah shall be wrathful at him and curse him and He shall prepare for him a great punishment. (93)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا۟ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَغَانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٩٤)
O you who have faith! When you issue forth in the way of Allah, try to ascertain: do not say to someone who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer,’ seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Yet with Allah are plenteous gains. You too were such earlier, but Allah did you a favour. Therefore, do ascertain. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (94)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً "It is not for a believer to kill a believer except by mistake" (4:92). The word "mistake" (الخطأ) — an act committed without intent or correct judgment — is pronounced with two short vowels and without elongation; with elongation, it follows the pattern of fi‘āl. It is the opposite of what is correct (الصواب). What is meant here is that which stands in contrast to intentionality (التعمد), as it is contrasted with the following verse: وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً "Should anyone kill a believer intentionally" (4:93).

The negation in His saying, "It is not for a believer to kill a believer," is a negation of requirement (نفي الاقتضاء). That is to say, there does not exist within a believer, after having entered the sanctuary and protection of faith (الإيمان), any requirement or inclination to kill a believer who is like him in that regard, regardless of the nature of the killing, except for accidental killing (قتل الخطإ). The exception here is connected (متصل), meaning the sense of the discourse returns to the fact that a believer does not desire to kill another believer qua believer—in that he would intend his death while knowing he is a believer. Similar to this sentence in its deployment for the negation of requirement are His sayings, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ "It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him" (42:51); مَا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَنْ تُنْبِتُوا شَجَرَهَا "It was not for you to cause its trees to grow" (27:60); and فَمَا كَانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِمَا كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ "But they would not believe what they had denied before" (10:74), among others.

Furthermore, the verse is phrased as a metonymy (كناية) for a legal prohibition (النهي التشريعي). This means that Allah the Exalted has never permitted, nor will He ever permit, a believer to kill a believer; He has forbidden it except in cases of accidental killing. Since there was no intent to kill a believer in such a case—either because the killing was not intended at all, or it was intended but under the assumption that the slain person was a disbeliever whose killing is permissible, for example—no prohibition is established there.

A group of exegetes mentioned that the exception in His saying, "except by mistake," is disconnected (منقطع). They argued that the phrase "except by mistake" was not treated as a literal exception because that would lead to the command or permission of accidental killing. However, you have already seen that this only leads to the removal of prohibition from accidental killing, or the non-placement of prohibition therein, and there is absolutely no problem in that. Thus, the truth is that the exception is connected (متصل).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ قَتَلَ مُؤْمِناً خَطَأً — until His saying — يَصَّدَّقُوا "...and whoever kills a believer by mistake, [there shall be] the freeing of a believing slave and blood-money submitted to his family, unless they remit it as charity" (4:92). Freeing (التحرير) means making a slave free. The "neck" (الرقبة) refers to the physical neck, but it is commonly used as a metaphor (مجاز) for the enslaved person (النفس المملوكة). Blood-money (الدية) is the wealth given as compensation for a life, a limb, or otherwise. The meaning is: whoever kills a believer by accidental killing (قتل الخطإ), it is obligatory upon him to free an enslaved believing soul and to give blood-money, delivering it to the family of the deceased, unless the guardians of the slain person give the blood-money as charity (يتصدق) to the one who owes it and pardon him, in which case the blood-money is no longer obligatory.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَكُمْ "If he belongs to a people who are your enemies" (4:92). The pronoun refers back to the slain believer. The "enemy people" (القوم العدو) are the combatant disbelievers (الكفار المحاربون). The meaning is: if the one killed by mistake was a believer but his family are combatant disbelievers who do not inherit, then freeing a slave is obligatory, but there is no blood-money, as a combatant disbeliever inherits nothing from a believer.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ "And if he belongs to a people with whom you have a treaty" (4:92). The pronoun in "he was" (كان) also refers to the slain believer, as indicated by the context. A treaty (الميثاق) — a formal covenant or binding agreement — is a general term for any pledge, encompassing the protection-covenant (الذمة) and every other pact. The meaning is: if the slain believer is from a people with whom you have a covenant, then blood-money and the freeing of a slave are obligatory. The blood-money is mentioned first here as an emphasis on observing the side of the treaty.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ "But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast" (4:92). That is, whoever is unable to free a slave—as this is the closest referent in the text—must fast for two consecutive months.

His saying, the Exalted: تَوْبَةً مِنَ اللهِ "as a penance from Allah" (4:92). That is, this ruling—the obligation of fasting—is a turning (توبة) and an act of mercy from Allah toward the one who lacks a slave to free. This applies as a mitigation (التخفيف), so the ruling is a concession from Allah for the one who is unable. It is also possible that the word "penance" (توبة) is a qualifier referring to all the mentioned forms of expiation (الكفارة) in the verse, namely His saying: "the freeing of a slave," etc. The meaning would be that making expiation obligatory for the accidental killer is a turning and a grace from Allah for the killer regarding the stain that undoubtedly attached to him due to this act. It also serves to make him cautious so that he does not act recklessly in rushing to kill, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقِصاصِ حَيَاةٌ "There is life for you in retribution" (2:179).

Likewise, it is a turning from Allah for the community and a grace for them, as it adds one person to the ranks of the free after they have lost one, and it repairs the financial harm that befell the family of the deceased through the delivered blood-money.

From here it becomes apparent that Islam views freedom (الحرية) as life and enslavement (الاسترقاق) as a type of death; it also views the average benefit of an individual's existence as being represented by the full blood-money (الدية الكاملة). We shall clarify this meaning in the upcoming discussions.

As for determining the specific meanings of mistake (الخطأ), intent (العمد), freeing (التحرير), blood-money (الدية), the family of the deceased, the treaty (الميثاق), and other matters mentioned in the verse, this is referred to the Sunnah (السنة). Whoever wishes to investigate them should consult jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ "Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his ostensible reward shall be hell" (4:93). Intentionality (التعمد) is the aiming for an act under the specific description (العنوان) that belongs to it. Since a voluntary act is never devoid of an intended description, and it is possible for one act to have more than one description, a single act can be intentional from one perspective and accidental from another. For instance, one who shoots at a silhouette believing it to be game, when in reality it is a human being, has intended to hunt but has acted accidentally in killing the human. Similarly, if one strikes a person with a staff intending to discipline him, but the blow kills him, the killing is accidental. Based on this, the one who kills a believer intentionally is he who intends by his act the killing of the believer, while knowing that it is a killing and that the slain person is a believer.

Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, has spoken severely regarding the threat to the intentional killer of a believer with eternal Fire. However, you have already learned in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him" (4:48), that this verse, as well as His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" (39:53), are capable of qualifying this verse. Thus, while this verse threatens with eternal Fire, it is not explicit in its inevitability (الحتم); therefore, pardon is possible through repentance (التوبة) or intercession (الشفاعة).

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا "O you who have faith! When you go forth in the way of Allah, distinguish" (4:94). "Going forth" (الضرب) refers to traveling through the land. Its qualification by "in the way of Allah" indicates that what is meant is going out for Jihad (الجهاد). "Distinguishing" (التبين) means differentiation, and what is meant is differentiating between the believer and the disbeliever, as evidenced by His saying: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقى إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِناً "and do not say to him who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer’" (4:94). Offering peace (إلقاء السلام) refers to offering the greeting—the greeting of the people of faith. It has also been recited as: "to him who offers you submission (السلم)" with a short vowel on the lām, meaning surrender.

What is meant by "seeking the goods of the life of this world" (ابتغاء عرض الحياة الدنيا) is the pursuit of wealth and spoils (الغنيمة). His saying: فَعِنْدَ اللهِ مَغانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ "with Allah are plenteous spoils" (4:94). The word "spoils" (مغانم) is the plural of magnam, meaning booty. That is, the spoils with Allah are better than the spoils of this world which they desire, due to their abundance and permanence; therefore, these are what you must prefer.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ كُنْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوا "You too were such before, but Allah beckoned to you; so distinguish" (4:94). That is, you were upon this description—seeking the goods of the life of this world—before you believed. Then Allah graced you with faith, which turns you away from seeking the goods of the life of this world toward the plenteous spoils that are with Allah. Since this is the case, it is obligatory upon you to distinguish. The repetition of the command to distinguish serves as an emphasis of the ruling.

The verse, while containing an exhortation and a type of reproach, does not explicitly state that this killing—which it appears occurred—was the intentional killing of a believer. It seems that it was an accidental killing by some believers of some polytheists who offered peace, because the killer did not trust that he was truly a believer, assuming he only displayed faith out of fear for his life. The verse reproaches him by stating that Islam only considers the outward (الظاهر), and leaves the matter of the hearts to the Subtle, the All-Aware.

Accordingly, His saying: "seeking the goods of the life of this world" is placed in the discourse based on the requirement of the situation (اقتضاء الحال). That is, your situation in killing one who displays faith to you, without paying heed to his case or distinguishing his state, is the situation of one who desires wealth and spoils; such a person kills the believer who outwardly professes faith with the slightest excuse, without it being a valid justification. This was the state the believers were in before their faith—seeking nothing but the world. Thus, when Allah blessed them with faith and graced them with Islam, it became obligatory for them to distinguish in what they do and not to be led by the character of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) or the remnants of its impulses within them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً (the verse): Ibn Jarir narrated from ‘Ikrimah who said: "Al-Harith ibn Yazid ibn Nabishah, from the tribe of ‘Amir ibn Lu’ayy, used to torture ‘Ayyash ibn Abi Rabi‘ah along with Abu Jahl. Later, he [Al-Harith] went out as a migrant to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. ‘Ayyash met him at al-Harrah and struck him down with a sword, thinking he was still a disbeliever. He then came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him, so the verse was revealed: وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً. The Prophet read it to him and then said: 'Stand up and free [a slave].'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated through other chains as well. In some of them, it is mentioned that he killed him in Mecca on the day of the Conquest, when ‘Ayyash came out—having been in the bonds of the polytheists until that day while they tortured him—and met Harith, who had accepted Islam, but ‘Ayyash did not know of his Islam, so he killed him then. What we have established from the narration of ‘Ikrimah is more consistent with reason and more appropriate to the chronology of the revelation of Surah al-Nisa’.

Al-Tabari narrated in his Tafsir from Ibn Zayd: "The one concerning whom the verse was revealed was Abu al-Darda’. He was in a raiding party and turned into a mountain pass for a personal need. He found a man from the [enemy] people with some sheep. He attacked him with a sword, and the man said, 'There is no god but Allah.' He struck him anyway, then brought his sheep to the party. Later, he felt something in his soul, so he came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him, and the verse was revealed."

It is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthūr from al-Ruyani, Ibn Mandah, and Abu Nu‘aym, from Bakr ibn Harithah al-Juhani: "He was the one concerning whom the verse was revealed, due to a story similar to that of Abu al-Darda’." In any case, the narrations do no more than provide applications (التطبيق) of the verse.

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from his men, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Every manumission is permissible for a newborn except in the expiation for killing, for Allah the Exalted says: فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُؤْمِنَةٍ (the freeing of a believing slave)—meaning by that one who professes [faith] and has reached the age of puberty (بلغت الحنث).'" (The hadith).

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Musa ibn Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: He was asked, "How is a believing woman recognized?" He said: "By the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being."

In al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: Regarding a Muslim man in the land of polytheism whom the Muslims killed, and the Imam later learned of it, he عليه‌السلام said: "He shall free a believing slave in his place, and that is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: فَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَكُمْ (If he belongs to a people who are your enemies)."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the same.

In his saying, "He shall free in his place," there is an indication that the reality of manumission is the addition of one person to the free Muslims, since one was subtracted from their number, as previously indicated.

Perhaps it can be inferred from this that the benefit of manumission in general for expiations is the addition of a free, non-disobedient person to their number, since one of them was subtracted by the sin. Understand this.

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "If a man is obligated to fast two consecutive months and he breaks the fast or falls ill in the first month, he must restart the fast. But if he fasts the first month and some of the second month, and then something occurs for which he has an excuse, he only has to complete what remains."

I say: That is, he completes what remains of it, as has been said, and this is inferred from the requirement of "consecutive" (التتابع).

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from him عليه‌السلام: He was asked about a believer who kills a believer intentionally—does he have any repentance? He said: "If he killed him for his faith, he has no repentance. But if he killed him out of anger or for some worldly reason, then his repentance is that he be subjected to retaliation (يقاد منه). If he was not caught, he should go to the guardians of the slain, confess to them the killing of their companion; if they pardon him and do not kill him, he gives them the blood-money, frees a soul, fasts two consecutive months, and feeds sixty poor people as a repentance to Allah, Mighty and Majestic."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Abu al-Safatij from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ, he said: "His reward is Hell if He rewards him."

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthūr from al-Tabarani and others, from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The narrations, as you see, contain the subtle points of the verses we previously mentioned. In the chapter of killing and retaliation (القود), there are many narrations; whoever desires them should consult the compendiums of hadith.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ (the verse), it is said: "It was revealed concerning Miqyas ibn Dubabah al-Kinani. He found his brother Hisham slain among the Banu al-Najjar, so he mentioned that to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The Prophet sent Qays ibn Hilal al-Fihri with him and said to him: 'Tell the Banu al-Najjar: If you know the killer of Hisham, hand him over to his brother to take retaliation; if you do not know, then hand over his blood-money to him.' Al-Fihri delivered the message, and they gave him the blood-money. When he departed with al-Fihri, Satan whispered to him, saying: 'You have done nothing; you took the blood-money for your brother and it will be a disgrace upon you. Kill the one with you so it will be a life for a life, and the blood-money will be an extra gain.' So he struck him with a rock, killed him, rode a camel, and returned to Mecca as a disbeliever, reciting:

I killed Fihri in his stead and placed his blood-money Upon the chiefs of Banu al-Najjar, the masters of Fari‘ Thus I attained my vengeance and lay down pillowed And I was the first to return to the idols.

The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله then said: 'I do not grant him safety in the profane land nor the sanctuary.'" This was narrated by al-Dahhak and a group of exegetes. (End).

I say: Something similar was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, and others.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ (the verse), it is said that it was revealed when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله returned from the expedition of Khaybar and sent Usamah ibn Zayd with cavalry to some Jewish villages in the direction of Fadak to invite them to Islam. There was a man called Mirdas ibn Nahik al-Fadaki in one of the villages. When he sensed the cavalry of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he gathered his family and wealth in a part of the mountain and began saying, "I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah." Usamah ibn Zayd passed by him and stabbed him, killing him. When he returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him of that, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: "Did you kill a man who bore witness that there is no god but Allah and that I am the Messenger of Allah?" He replied: "O Messenger of Allah, he only said it to seek refuge from being killed." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "You did not uncover the veil from his heart, nor did you accept what he said with his tongue, nor did you know what was in his soul." After that, Usamah swore that he would never kill anyone who bore witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; thus, he held back from [supporting] the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام in his wars. Regarding this, the verse was revealed: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقى إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِناً تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا (the verse).

I say: Al-Tabari narrated this meaning in his Tafsir from al-Suddi. Many narrations are found in al-Durr al-Manthūr regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse. In some, the story is attributed to Miqdad ibn al-Aswad; in others, to Abu al-Darda’; in others, to Muhallim ibn Jaththamah; and in some, the names of the killer and the slain are not mentioned, leaving the story ambiguous. However, the oath of Usamah ibn Zayd and his excuse to ‘Ali عليه‌السلام for holding back from his wars is well-known and mentioned in history books. And Allah knows best.

Ayahs

لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْقَـٰعِدُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ غَيْرُ أُو۟لِى ٱلضَّرَرِ وَٱلْمُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ فَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَفَضَّلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَى ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٩٥)
Not equal are those of the faithful who sit back—excepting those who suffer from some disability—and those who wage jihad in the way of Allah with their possession and their persons. Allah has graced those who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons by a degree over those who sit back; yet to each Allah has promised the best reward, and Allah has graced those who wage jihad over those who sit back with a great reward: (95)
دَرَجَـٰتٍ مِّنْهُ وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٩٦)
ranks from Him, forgiveness, and mercy, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (96)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ فِيمَ كُنتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا۟ فِيهَا ۚ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (٩٧)
Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves, they ask, ‘What state were you in?’ They reply, ‘We were oppressed in the land.’ They say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast enough so that you might migrate in it?’ The refuge of such shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (97)
إِلَّا ٱلْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ ٱلرِّجَالِ وَٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْوِلْدَٰنِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا (٩٨)
Except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither access to any means nor are guided to any way. (98)
فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا (٩٩)
Maybe Allah will excuse them, for Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (99)
۞ وَمَن يُهَاجِرْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يَجِدْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُرَٰغَمًا كَثِيرًا وَسَعَةً ۚ وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِنۢ بَيْتِهِۦ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ ٱلْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٠٠)
Whoever migrates in the way of Allah will find many havens and plenitude in the earth. And whoever leaves his home migrating toward Allah and His Apostle, and is then overtaken by death, his reward shall certainly fall on Allah, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (100)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَسْتَوِي الْقاعِدُونَ (Not equal are those who sit [at home])—until His saying—وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ (and their souls). Harm (الضرر) is a deficiency in existence that prevents one from undertaking the matter of struggle (الجهاد) and combat (القتال), such as blindness, lameness, or illness. What is meant by struggling with wealth (الأموال) is spending it in the way of Allah to achieve victory over the enemies of the religion (الدين), and with their souls (الأنفس) refers to combat.

His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى (To each Allah has promised the best [reward]) indicates that those meant by "those who sit" (القاعدين) are those who refrain from going out to fight when there is no necessity for their departure because others have gone out in sufficient numbers (حد الكفاية). Thus, the speech is directed toward encouraging people and inciting them to undertake the matter of struggle, to compete in it, and to hasten toward it.

Evidence for this is that Allah, the Glorified, exempted those with harm (أولي الضرر) and then ruled on the lack of equality, even though those with harm are like those who sit in their lack of equality with the mujahideen (المجاهدين) — those who struggle — in the way of Allah. Even if we say that Allah, the Glorified, compensates for their harm through their righteous intentions, there is no doubt that struggle (الجهاد), martyrdom (الشهادة), or overcoming the enemy of Allah are among the virtues (الفضائل) by which the mujahideen in the way of Allah are preferred over others. In short, the speech contains an incitement (تحضيض) for the believers and a stirring (تهييج) of them, awakening the spirit of their faith to race toward goodness and virtue.

His saying, the Exalted: فَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً (Allah has graced those who strive with their possessions and their persons over those who sit [at home] by a rank). This sentence serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: لا يَسْتَوِي (Not equal), which is why it was not connected by a conjunction or similar device. Rank (الدرجة) is a station (المنزلة), and ranks (الدرجات) are station after station. His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى (To each Allah has promised the best [reward]) means Allah promised each of the sitters and the mujahideen, or each of the sitters without harm, the sitters with harm, and the mujahideen, the "best reward" (الحسنى). The "best reward" (الحسنى) is an adjective whose noun is omitted, meaning the best outcome (العاقبة الحسنى), the best recompense (المثوبة الحسنى), or something similar. The sentence is intended to repel a potential misconception (دفع الدخل), for a believer who sits might imagine from His saying: لا يَسْتَوِي (Not equal)—until His saying—دَرَجَةً (by a rank) that he is empty-handed and that no benefit returns to him from his faith and other deeds. Thus, He repelled that with His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً دَرَجاتٍ مِنْهُ وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً (and Allah has graced those who strive over those who sit [at home] with a great reward: ranks from Him, and forgiveness, and mercy). This preference (التفضيل) serves as an explanation and elaboration (البيان والشرح) for the general preference mentioned first. It also provides another benefit: an indication that it is not fitting for the believers to be content with the "good promise" (الوعد الحسن) contained in His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى, thereby becoming sluggish in struggle in the way of Allah and the obligatory striving to exalt the word of truth and abolish falsehood. For the preference of the mujahideen over the sitters consists of ranks of forgiveness and mercy that are not to be underestimated.

The matter of the verse in its context is remarkable. Firstly, because it qualified the mujahideen (firstly) with His saying: فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ (in the way of Allah with their wealth and their souls), and (secondly) with His saying: بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ (with their wealth and their souls), and (thirdly) mentioned them without qualification. Secondly, because it mentioned in the preference (firstly) that it is a "rank" (درجة), and (secondly) that it is "ranks from Him" (درجات منه).

As for the first point, it is because the speech in the verse is directed toward explaining the virtue of struggle over sitting. Virtue (الفضل) only belongs to struggle if it is in the way of Allah, not in the way of personal desire (هوى النفس), and through generosity and sacrifice of the most precious things to a human being, which is wealth, and what is even more precious, which is the soul. Therefore, it was said first: وَالْمُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ (and those who strive in the way of Allah with their wealth and their souls) so that the matter would be fully clarified and ambiguity removed. Then, when it was said: فَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً (Allah has graced those who strive with their possessions and their persons over those who sit [at home] by a rank), there was no need to mention the qualifications from this aspect because the ambiguity had been removed by the preceding explanation. However, since the sentence was paired with His saying: وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ اللهُ الْحُسْنى, the speech required an explanation of the cause of the virtue, which is the spending of wealth and the sacrifice of the soul despite the love for both. Thus, it sufficed to mention them as a qualification for the mujahideen, so it was said: الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ. As for His saying thirdly: وَفَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً (and Allah has graced those who strive over those who sit [at home] with a great reward), there remained no need to mention the qualifications at all, neither all of them nor some of them, and thus they were all omitted.

As for the second point, His saying: فَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَأَنْفُسِهِمْ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ دَرَجَةً, the word rank (درجة) is in the accusative case as a specification (التمييز). It indicates that the preference is in terms of rank and station, without addressing whether this rank that necessitates virtue is one or more. His saying: وَفَضَّلَ اللهُ الْمُجاهِدِينَ عَلَى الْقاعِدِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً دَرَجاتٍ مِنْهُ, the word graced (فَضَّلَ) here implies the meaning of "giving" or something similar. His saying: دَرَجاتٍ مِنْهُ (ranks from Him) is an appositive (بدل) or an explanatory substitute (عطف بيان) for His saying: أَجْراً عَظِيماً (a great reward). The meaning is: Allah gave the mujahideen a great reward, preferring them over the sitters, giving or recompensing them with a great reward, which is the ranks from Allah. Thus, the speech explains at its beginning that the virtue of the mujahideen over the sitters is in station (المنزلة) with Allah, while remaining silent on whether this station is one or many; and it explains at its end that this station is not a single station, but rather many stations and ranks, which is the great reward with which the mujahideen are recompensed.

Perhaps what we have mentioned resolves the difficulty raised regarding the apparent contradiction between His saying first "a rank" (درجة) and secondly "ranks from Him" (درجات منه). The commentators have mentioned several ways to resolve this difficulty, most or all of which are not free from affectation (تكلف).

Among them: that the preference intended in the beginning of the verse is the preference of the mujahideen over the sitters with harm by one rank, and in the end of the verse, the preference of the mujahideen over the sitters without harm by many ranks.

Among them: that the "rank" (الدرجة) in the beginning of the verse refers to worldly station, such as spoils, good reputation, and the like, and the "ranks" (الدرجات) at the end of the verse refer to the stations of the Hereafter, which are greater relative to the world. The Exalted said: وَلَلْآخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجاتٍ (and the Hereafter is surely greater in ranks) (17:21).

Among them: that the "rank" in the beginning of the verse refers to the station with Allah, which is a spiritual matter, and the "ranks" at the end of the verse refer to the stations of Paradise and its high degrees, which are sensory. You are aware that these views have no evidence from the perspective of the wording.

The pronoun in His saying: مِنْهُ (from Him) likely refers to Allah, the Glorified, and this is supported by His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً (and forgiveness and mercy), assuming it is an explanation for the ranks, as forgiveness and mercy are from Allah. It is also possible that the pronoun refers to the "reward" (الأجر) mentioned previously.

His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً (and forgiveness and mercy) apparently serves as an explanation for the ranks, for the ranks—which are the stations from Allah, the Glorified, whatever they may be—are instances (مصداق) of forgiveness and mercy. You have learned in some previous discussions that mercy (الرحمة)—which is the divine bestowal of blessing—depends on the removal of the veil and the lifting of the barrier to attaining it, which is forgiveness (المغفرة). This necessitates that every level of the levels of blessings, and every high rank and station, is forgiveness relative to the level that follows it and the rank above it. Thus, it is correct that the ranks of the Hereafter, whatever they may be, are forgiveness and mercy from Allah, the Glorified. Most often when mercy and its like are mentioned in the Quran, forgiveness is mentioned with them, such as His saying: مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (forgiveness and a great reward) (5:9), His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (and forgiveness and a noble provision) (8:4), His saying: مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (forgiveness and a big reward) (11:11), His saying: وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْوانٌ (and forgiveness from Allah and [His] pleasure) (57:20), and His saying: وَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا (and forgive us and have mercy on us) (2:286), among other verses.

Then He concluded the verse with His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً (and Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful). The suitability of the two Names with the content of the verse is apparent, especially after His saying at its end: وَمَغْفِرَةً وَرَحْمَةً.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ (Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves). The word تَوَفَّاهُمُ (take them away) is either in the past tense or the future tense—originally tatatowaffahum (تتوفاهم), where one of the two ta's was omitted for ease of pronunciation—similar to His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ ما كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِنْ سُوءٍ (those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves. Then they will proffer submission [saying], ‘We were not doing any evil!’) (16:28).

What is meant by wrongdoing (الظلم), as supported by the parallel verse, is their wrongdoing to themselves by turning away from the religion of Allah and failing to establish its rites due to residing in the lands of polytheism (الشرك) and being amidst the disbelievers, where there is no means to learn the knowledge of the religion (معارف الدين) or to perform the duties of servitude (وظائف العبودية) that it calls for. This is what the context indicates in His saying: قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ (They will say, ‘What state were you in?’ They will say, ‘We were oppressed in the land.’) until the end of the three verses.

Allah, the Glorified, has explained "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) (when used generally) in His saying: لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً (The curse of Allah is on the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked) (7:44-45). The sum of the two verses is the explanation of wrongdoing as turning away from the religion of Allah and seeking it to be crooked and distorted, which corresponds to what appears from the verse we are currently discussing.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ (They will say, ‘What state were you in?’), meaning: in what state were you regarding the religion? The letter "m" (م) is the interrogative ma (ما) from which the alif was omitted for ease.

In the verse, there is an indication in general of what the narrations (الأخبار) call the "questioning of the grave" (سؤال القبر), which is the angels' questioning of the deceased about his religion after death occurs, as also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ ما كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِنْ سُوءٍ بَلى إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ فَادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا خَيْراً (those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves. Then they will proffer submission [saying], ‘We were not doing any evil!’ ‘Yes, indeed Allah knows best what you used to do. So enter the gates of hell, to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the abode of the arrogant.’ And those who were God-conscious will be asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They will say, ‘Good.’) (16:28-30).

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ قالُوا أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها (They will say, ‘We were oppressed in the land.’ They will say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast, so that you might have migrated in it?’). The angels' question (فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ) was a question about the state they were living in regarding the religion. These questioned individuals were not in a state of any account regarding the religion, so they answered by putting the cause in place of the effect, which is that they were living in a land where they were unable to adhere to the religion because the inhabitants of the land were powerful polytheists who oppressed them (استضعفوهم) and stood between them and adopting the laws of the religion and acting upon them.

Since this oppression (الاستضعاف) they mentioned—had they been truthful in it—only befell them because of their clinging to the land of polytheism, and their oppression was due to the polytheists' dominance over the specific land they mentioned, and they had no authority over other parts of the earth, they were not oppressed in every case, but rather in a state they could have changed by leaving and migrating. Thus, the angels belied them in their claim of oppression, stating that Allah's earth was vaster than the place they fell into and remained in, and they could have left the domain of oppression through migration (المهاجرة). They were not truly oppressed because they possessed the power to escape the bond of oppression, but they chose this state through their own poor choice.

Thus, His saying: أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها, the interrogation in it is for rebuke (التوبيخ), as in His saying: فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ. It is possible that the first of the two interrogations is for affirmation (التقرير), as is apparent from the previously cited verses of Surah al-Nahl, since the question there is about both the wrongdoers and the God-conscious, while the second interrogation is for rebuke in any case.

The angels attributed the earth to Allah, which is not without an allusion to the fact that Allah, the Glorified, first prepared vastness in His earth and then called them to faith and action, as also suggested two verses later by His saying: وَمَنْ يُهاجِرْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ يَجِدْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُراغَماً كَثِيراً وَسَعَةً (And whoever migrates in the way of Allah will find many havens in the earth and vastness).

Describing the earth as vast (السعة) is what necessitated expressing migration with the phrase: فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها (so that you might have migrated in it), meaning migrating from one part of it to another. Had vastness not been assumed, it would have been said: "so that you might have migrated from it."

Then Allah ruled regarding them after presenting the questioning by saying: فَأُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً (the abode of such shall be hell, and it is an evil destination).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَالنِّساءِ وَالْوِلْدانِ (except the oppressed among men, women and children). The exception is disconnected (منقطع). In applying the term "oppressed" (المستضعفين) to these individuals with the explanation provided, there is an indication that the aforementioned wrongdoers were not truly oppressed because they were able to lift the bond of oppression from themselves. Rather, oppression is the description of those mentioned in this verse. The detailed mention of them as men, women, and children clarifies the divine ruling and removes ambiguity. His saying: لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلاً (who can neither devise any stratagem nor find a way). Stratagem (الحيلة) is as if it were a form of "interposing" (حيلولة), then it was used to mean a tool; it is that which is used as a means to interpose between one thing and another, or one state to obtain another thing or state. Its usage became dominant in what is done secretly and in blameworthy matters. In its root, in any case, is the meaning of change, as mentioned by al-Raghib in his Mufradat.

The meaning is: they are not able and do not have the power to devise a way to divert the oppression of the polytheists directed at them, nor do they find a way (سبيلاً) by which they can escape them. What is meant by "way," as the context suggests, is broader than a physical path, such as the road to Medina for the Muslims of Mecca who wished to migrate to it, and a spiritual way, which is anything that saves them from the hands of the polytheists and their oppression of them through torment and trial (الفتنة).

(A Discussion on the Oppressed)

It becomes clear from the verse that ignorance of the knowledge of the religion, if it arises from incapacity and weakness in which the ignorant person has no hand, is an excuse before Allah, the Glorified.

To clarify: Allah, the Glorified, considers ignorance of the religion and every prevention from establishing the rites of the religion as wrongdoing (ظلم) that does not receive divine pardon. Then He exempts from that the oppressed (المستضعفين) and accepts from them their excuse of oppression. Then He defines them by a description that encompasses them and others, which is their lack of power over that which would repel the harm from them. This meaning, just as it is realized in one who is surrounded in a land where there is no way to receive the knowledge of the religion due to the absence of a scholar expert in its details, or no way to act according to that knowledge due to unbearable torment and the inability to leave and migrate to the Abode of Islam (دار الإسلام) and join the Muslims due to weakness in intellect, illness, physical deficiency, financial poverty, or the like—it is likewise realized in one whose mind has not turned toward a fixed truth in religious knowledge and whose thought has not been guided to it, despite being one who does not oppose the truth nor is arrogant toward it at all; rather, if the truth appeared to him, he would follow it, but the truth was hidden from him due to various factors necessitating that.

Such a person is "oppressed," who can neither devise a stratagem nor find a way, not because the paths were closed to him by being surrounded by the enemies of truth and religion with sword and whip, but rather because other factors oppressed him by imposing heedlessness (الغفلة) upon him. There is no power with heedlessness, and no way with this ignorance.

This is what is necessitated by the generality of the explanation in the verse, which carries the meaning of a universal cause (عموم العلة). This is also indicated by other verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها لَها ما كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْها مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ (Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. To its benefit is what it earns, and to its detriment is what it incurs) (2:286). A matter that is overlooked (المغفول عنه) is not within a person's capacity, just as a matter from which one is prevented by an obstacle is not within a person's capacity.

This verse—meaning the verse of al-Baqarah—just as it removes the obligation (التكليف) with the removal of capacity, also provides a universal criterion (ضابطا كليا) for identifying the case of excuse and distinguishing it from others. This criterion is that the act must not be attributed to the person's "earning" (اكتساب), and he must have no hand in the prevention of the matter that was prevented for him. Thus, the one ignorant of the religion entirely, or of some of its true knowledge, if his ignorance is attributed to what he neglected and his poor choice, then the omission is attributed to him and it is a sin. But if his ignorance is not attributed to his negligence in it or in any of its preliminaries, but rather to factors outside his choice that necessitated ignorance, heedlessness, or the omission of action, then the omission is not attributed to his choice, and he is not considered a doer of sin, one who intentionally opposes, or one who is arrogant toward the truth and denies it. For him is what he earned, and against him is what he incurred; and if he did not earn, then there is nothing for him nor against him.

From here it appears that the oppressed person is empty-handed; there is nothing for him and nothing against him because he did not earn anything. Rather, his affair is with his Lord, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted, after the verse of the oppressed: فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ وَكانَ اللهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً (the abode of such may be Allah’s pardon, and Allah is All-excusing, All-forgiving) and His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall turn to them clemently, and Allah is All-knowing, All-wise) (9:106). And His mercy precedes His wrath.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ (the abode of such may be Allah’s pardon). These individuals, even if they did not earn an evil deed due to being excused in their ignorance, we have previously explained that the human condition revolves between felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة), and it suffices for one's wretchedness that he does not possess felicity for himself. Thus, a person is never independent of the divine pardon (العفو الإلهي) by which the effect of wretchedness is pardoned, whether he was righteous, wicked, or neither. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the hope for their pardon.

The mention of the hope for their pardon was chosen, followed by His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً, from which the inclusion of pardon for them is hinted, because they were mentioned in the form of an exception from the wrongdoers who were threatened that their abode is hell and an evil destination.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُهاجِرْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ يَجِدْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُراغَماً كَثِيراً وَسَعَةً (And whoever migrates in the way of Allah will find many havens in the earth and vastness). Al-Raghib said: al-ragham (الرغام) (with a fatha on the ra) is fine dust. "So-and-so's nose was rubbed in the dust" (رغم أنف فلان) means it fell into the dust, and another "rubbed his nose in the dust" (أرغمه). This is used to express displeasure (السخط), as the poet said:

When those noses are rubbed in the dust, I do not please them, Nor do I seek their favor, but rather I increase it.

Its being contrasted with "pleasing" (الإرضاء) alerts one to its indication of "displeasing" (الإسخاط). Based on this, it is said: "Allah rubbed his nose in the dust" (أرغم الله أنفه), and "he displeased him" (أرغمه). A "displeased woman" (راغمة) is one who is angry. Then "mutual rubbing of noses" (المراغمة) is used metaphorically for contention (المنازعة). Allah, the Exalted, said: يَجِدْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُراغَماً كَثِيراً, meaning a place to go to when he sees an evil that he must be angry at, as you say: "I was angry at so-and-so because of such-and-such and I rubbed my nose against him" (رغمت إليه) (End of quote).

The meaning is: (وَمَنْ يُهاجِرْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ), meaning seeking His pleasure in adhering to the religion in knowledge and action, will find in the earth many places; whenever a preventer in some of them prevents him from establishing the religion of Allah, he finds relief in another by migrating to it to "rub the nose" of the preventer and displease him, or to contend with the preventer and make him angry, and he finds vastness in the earth.

He, the Exalted, said in the preceding verses: أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً (Was not Allah’s earth vast?), and the necessary consequence of this is to say: "and whoever migrates will find vastness in the earth," except that when His saying: مُراغَماً كَثِيراً was added—which is among the necessities of the earth's vastness for one who wishes to follow the way of Allah—the migration was also qualified as being "in the way of Allah" to correspond with the purpose of the speech, which is to exhort the believers residing in the land of polytheism, to stir them, to encourage them to migrate, and to comfort their souls.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَخْرُجْ مِنْ بَيْتِهِ مُهاجِراً إِلَى اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (And whoever leaves his home migrating toward Allah and His Apostle) etc. Migrating toward Allah and His Apostle is a metonymy (كناية) for migrating to the land of Islam where one is able to learn the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Apostle and act upon them.

The "overtaking of death" (إدراك الموت) is a metaphor (استعارة) by metonymy for its occurrence or its suddenness, for "overtaking" (الإدراك) is the striving of the one following to reach the one ahead and then arriving at him. Likewise, the "falling of the reward upon Allah" (وقوع الأجر على الله) is a metaphor by metonymy for the necessity of the reward and recompense for Him, the Exalted, and His taking that into His charge. Thus, there is a beautiful reward and an abundant recompense that will inevitably reach the servant, and Allah, the Glorified, will fulfill it through His Divinity, which nothing can overcome, nothing can frustrate, and nothing can prevent from what He wills, and He does not break the promise. The speech concluded with His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً as a confirmation of the beautiful promise of the necessity of fulfilling the reward and recompense.

Allah, the Glorified, has divided the believers in these verses—meaning those who claim faith—in terms of residence in the Abode of Faith (دار الإيمان) and the Abode of Polytheism (دار الشرك) into categories, and explained the recompense of each group in a way that suits its condition, to be an exhortation and a reminder, then an encouragement to migrate to the Abode of Faith, to gather there, to strengthen the Islamic society, to unite and cooperate in righteousness and godwariness (التقوى), to exalt the word of truth, and to raise the banner of monotheism (التوحيد) and the emblems of the religion.

One group resided in the Abode of Islam, consisting of mujahideen in the way of Allah with their wealth and their souls, sitters without harm, and sitters with harm; to each Allah promised the best reward, and Allah preferred the mujahideen over the sitters by a rank.

Another group resided in the Abode of Polytheism, and they are wrongdoers who do not migrate in the way of Allah; their abode is hell and an evil destination. A group among them is oppressed and not wrongdoers, who can neither devise a stratagem nor find a way; those, it may be that Allah will pardon them. And a group among them is not oppressed, who left their homes migrating toward Allah and His Apostle, then death overtook them; their reward has indeed fallen upon Allah.
