The verses, in their contents, apply to Muslims at all times and ages, even if the occasion of their revelation (شأن النزول) was the condition of the Muslims in the Arabian Peninsula during the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) between his migration to Medina and the conquest of Mecca. The earth was divided then into the Abode of Islam—which was Medina and its surroundings, where the group of Muslims were free in their religion and a group of polytheists and others were not interfered with in their affairs due to a treaty or the like—and the Abode of Polytheism—which was Mecca and its surroundings, under the authority of the polytheists who remained in their idolatry and interfered with the Muslims in the matter of their religion, subjecting them to evil torment and trying them to turn them back from their religion.

However, the verses judge the Muslims by their underlying criterion (ملاك) always. Thus, a Muslim must reside where he is able to learn the features of the religion and can establish its rites and act upon its rulings, and he must migrate from the land where there is no knowledge of the religion and no way to act upon its rulings, without distinction as to whether it is called today the Abode of Islam or the Abode of Polytheism. For names have changed today and have abandoned their named realities; religion has become a nationality, and Islam a mere naming without regard in its naming for belief in its knowledge or acting upon its rulings.

The Noble Quran only accords effect to the reality of Islam, not its name, and tasks people with the action that contains something of its spirit, not what is its mere form. The Exalted said: لَيْسَ بِأَمانِيِّكُمْ وَلا أَمانِيِّ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً يُجْزَ بِهِ وَلا يَجِدْ لَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيراً (It is not by your fancies, nor by the fancies of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he shall not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah. And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful, such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone) (4:123-124). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالنَّصارى وَالصَّابِئِينَ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—their reward is with their Lord, and there shall be no fear nor shall they grieve) (2:62).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "There was a group of the people of Mecca who had embraced Islam but were hiding their Islam. The polytheists took them out with them on the day of Badr, and some of them were struck and some were killed. The Muslims said: 'These companions of ours were Muslims and were coerced,' so they sought forgiveness for them. Then this verse was revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ—until the end of the verse."

He said: "So a letter was written to those Muslims remaining in Mecca with this verse, stating that there was no excuse for them. So they went out, but the polytheists caught up with them and subjected them to trial (الفتنة). Then this verse was revealed concerning them: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ ، فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ (Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah,’ but if they are made to suffer in Allah’s cause, they take the people’s persecution to be like Allah’s punishment) (29:10). The Muslims wrote to them about that, and they became sad and despaired of all good. Then there was revealed concerning them: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ، ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَصَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (Then indeed your Lord, to those who migrated after they were persecuted, then strove and were patient—indeed after that your Lord is All-forgiving, All-merciful) (16:110). So they wrote to them about that, saying that Allah has made a way out for you, so go out. They went out, and the polytheists caught up with them—so they fought them until those who were saved were saved and those who were killed were killed."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Dahhak regarding the verse: "He said they were people from the hypocrites (المنافقين) who stayed behind from the Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in Mecca and did not go out with him to Medina. They went out with the polytheists of Quraysh to Badr and were struck on the day of Badr among those who were struck. So Allah revealed this verse concerning them."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Zayd regarding the verse: "He said: When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent and became manifest, and faith sprouted, hypocrisy (النفاق) sprouted with it. Men came to the Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Apostle of Allah, were it not that we fear these people would torment us and do such-and-such, we would have embraced Islam, but we bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Apostle of Allah.' They used to say that to him. When it was the day of Badr, the polytheists stood up and said: 'No one shall stay behind from us except that we demolish his house and seize his wealth.' So those who used to say that saying to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out with them; a group of them was killed and a group was taken prisoner."

He said: "As for those who were killed, they are the ones about whom Allah said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ (the whole verse) أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها—and leave these who oppress you—فَأُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً."

"Then Allah excused the people of truthfulness and said: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَالنِّساءِ وَالْوِلْدانِ ، لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلاً—to which they could turn; if they went out, they would perish—فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ for their residence among the polytheists."

"And those who were taken prisoner said: 'O Apostle of Allah, you know that we used to come to you and bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are the Apostle of Allah, and that we went out with these people out of fear.' So Allah said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِمَنْ فِي أَيْدِيكُمْ مِنَ الْأَسْرى ، إِنْ يَعْلَمِ اللهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْراً يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْراً ، مِمَّا أُخِذَ مِنْكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ (O Prophet! Say to the captives who are in your hands, ‘If Allah finds any good in your hearts, He will give you [something] better than what has been taken from you, and He will forgive you) (8:70)—your deed that you did, your going out with the polytheists against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—وَإِنْ يُرِيدُوا خِيانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خانُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ (But if they seek to betray you, they have already betrayed Allah before) (8:71)—by going out with the polytheists—so He gave power over them."

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Jarir narrated from Ikrimah regarding His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ، قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ—until His saying—وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً. He said: "It was revealed concerning Qays ibn al-Fakih ibn al-Mughirah, al-Harith ibn Zam'ah ibn al-Aswad, Qays ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Abu al-As ibn Munabbih ibn al-Hajjaj, and Ali ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf."

He said: "When the polytheists of Quraysh and their followers went out to protect Abu Sufyan ibn Harb and the caravan of Quraysh from the Apostle of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his companions, and to seek what had been taken from them on the day of Nakhlah, they went out with young men who were reluctant and had embraced Islam. They gathered at Badr without a prior appointment, and they were killed at Badr as disbelievers, having turned back from Islam; they are those whom we have named."

I say: The narrations with meanings close to these from the paths of the [Sunni] group are many. Although their outward appearance is more like an application (التطبيق), it is a good application.

Among the most important things to be benefited from them, as well as from the verses after reflection, is the existence of hypocrites in Mecca before the migration and after it. For that has an impact on the discussion about the state of the hypocrites, as will come in Surah al-Bara'ah, if Allah the Mighty wills.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "There was a man in Mecca called Damrah from the Banu Bakr, and he was ill. He said to his family: 'Take me out of Mecca, for I feel the heat.' They said: 'Where shall we take you out?' He pointed with his hand toward the road to Medina. So they went out with him, and he died two miles from Mecca. Then this verse was revealed: وَمَنْ يَخْرُجْ مِنْ بَيْتِهِ مُهاجِراً إِلَى اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ الْمَوْتُ."

I say: The narrations with this meaning are many, but there is a strong disagreement in them regarding the naming of this person whom death overtook. In some of them, it is Damrah ibn Jundub; in some, Aktham ibn Sayfi; in some, Abu Damrah ibn al-Ays al-Zurqi; in some, Damrah ibn al-Ays from the Banu Layth; in some, Junda' ibn Damrah al-Junda'i; and in some, it was revealed concerning Khalid ibn Hizam, who went out migrating to Abyssinia and was bitten by a snake on the way and died.

In some narrations from Ibn Abbas: that it was Aktham ibn Sayfi. The narrator said: "I said: 'Then where is the Laythi?' He said: 'This was a long time before the Laythi; it is specific yet general.'"

I say: He means that it was revealed concerning Aktham specifically and then applied to others generally. What is gathered from the narrations is that three of the Muslims were overtaken by death in the way of migration: Aktham ibn Sayfi, a Laythi, and Khalid ibn Hizam. As for the revelation of the verse concerning any of them, it seems to be an application by the narrator.

In al-Kafi, from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the oppressed (المستضعف). He said: 'He is the one who cannot devise a stratagem toward disbelief so as to disbelieve, nor is he guided to a way toward faith; he cannot believe and he cannot disbelieve. Among them are children, and among men and women, those with intellects like the intellects of children; the pen is lifted from them.'" I say: The hadith is widely transmitted (مستفيض) from Zurarah, narrated by al-Kulayni, al-Saduq, and al-Ayyashi through several paths from him.

In the same source, with his chain of transmission from Isma'il al-Ju'fi, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the religion which the servants are not excused for being ignorant of. He said: 'The religion is vast, but the Kharijites (الخوارج) narrowed it upon themselves out of their ignorance.' I said: 'May I be your ransom, shall I tell you my religion that I follow?' He said: 'Yes.' I said: 'I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that Muhammad is His servant and His Apostle, and I affirm what he brought from Allah the Exalted, and I take you as masters (أتولاكم), and I disassociate (أبرأ) from your enemies and those who rode upon your necks, and took command over you, and wronged you of your right.' He said: 'By Allah, you have not been ignorant of anything; it is, by Allah, that which we follow.' I said: 'Then does anyone who does not know this matter attain salvation (يسلم)?' He said: 'Except the oppressed.' I said: 'Who are they?' He said: 'Your women and your children.'"

Then he said: "Have you seen Umm Ayman? For I bear witness that she is from the people of Paradise, and she did not know what you follow."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Sulayman ibn Khalid from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the oppressed. He said: 'The simple-minded women (البلهاء) in their seclusion, and the servant girl to whom you say: "Pray," and she prays, not knowing except what you told her; and the imported slave (الجليب) who knows nothing except what you told him; and the decrepit old man, and the child, and the small one—these are the oppressed. As for a man with a stiff neck, a contentious debater who handles buying and selling, whom you cannot help in anything—do you say: "This is the oppressed?" No, and no honor [to him].'"

In al-Ma'ani, from Sulayman, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse. He said: "O Sulayman, among these oppressed is one whose neck is thicker than yours. The oppressed are a people who fast and pray, whose bellies and private parts are chaste, and they do not see that the truth is in other than us, holding onto the branches of the tree. Those, it may be that Allah will pardon them, if they were holding onto the branches and were to know those [the Imams]. If Allah pardons them, it is by His mercy, and if He punishes them, it is for their straying."

I say: His saying "they do not see that the truth is in other than us" refers to the absence of enmity (النصب) or negligence leading to it, as indicated by the following narrations.

In the same source, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He mentioned that the oppressed are of various types, differing from one another. Whoever is from the people of the Qibla and is not an enemy (ناصبا) is oppressed.

In the same source and in the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse. He said: "They cannot devise a stratagem toward enmity (النصب) so as to become enemies, nor are they guided to a way toward the truth so as to enter it. These will enter Paradise through righteous deeds and by avoiding the prohibitions that Allah forbade, but they will not attain the stations of the righteous (الأبرار)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from Durays al-Kunasi from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'May I be your ransom, what is the state of the monotheists (الموحدين) who affirm the prophethood of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) among the sinners, who die and have no Imam, and do not know your guardianship (ولايتكم)?' He said: 'As for these, they are in their pits [graves] and do not come out of them. Whoever had a righteous deed and no enmity was manifest from him, a furrow is dug for him to the Paradise that Allah created in the West, and the spirit enters upon him in his pit until the Day of Resurrection, until he meets Allah and He settles his account with his good deeds and his evil deeds; then either to Paradise or to Fire. These are the ones waiting for Allah's decree.' He said: 'And likewise is done with the oppressed, the simple-minded, the children, and the children of the Muslims who have not reached puberty.'"

"As for the enemies (النصاب) from the people of the Qibla, a furrow is dug for them to the Fire that Allah created in the East, and the flame, sparks, smoke, and the boiling of the scalding water enter upon them until the Day of Resurrection, then their destination is to the Hellfire."

In al-Khisal, from al-Sadiq from his father from his grandfather from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Paradise has eight gates: a gate through which the Prophets and the Truthful (الصديقون) enter; a gate through which the Martyrs and the Righteous enter; and five gates through which our Shi'a and our lovers enter..." until he said: "and a gate through which the rest of the Muslims enter—from those who bear witness that there is no god but Allah and in whose heart there is not a mustard seed's weight of hatred for us, the People of the House (أهل البيت) (peace be upon him)."

In al-Ma'ani and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Humran, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ. He said: 'They are the people of guardianship (الولاية).' I said: 'Which guardianship?' He said: 'Indeed, it is not guardianship in religion, but it is guardianship in marriage, inheritance, and social interaction. They are neither believers nor disbelievers, and they are the ones waiting for the decree of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.'"

I say: This is an allusion to His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ (And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall turn to them clemently) (9:106), and the discussion related to it will come, if Allah the Mighty wills.

In al-Nahj, he (peace be upon him) said: "The name of oppression (الاستضعاف) does not apply to one whom the proof (الحجة) has reached, whose ear has heard it, and whose heart has grasped it."

In al-Kafi, from al-Kazim (peace be upon him): He was asked about the weak (الضعفاء), so he (peace be upon him) wrote: "The weak is the one for whom the proof has not been raised and who does not know the disagreement. If he knows the disagreement, then he is not weak."

In the same source, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He was asked: "What do you say about the oppressed?" He said, as if startled: "Have you left anyone to be oppressed? Where are the oppressed? By Allah, this matter of yours has reached the young maidens in their seclusion, and the water-carrying women have spoken of it on the road to Medina."

In al-Ma'ani, from Umar ibn Ishaq, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked: 'What is the limit of the oppressed whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mentioned?' He said: 'One who does not know a single surah of the Quran, yet Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created him with a constitution such that it is not fitting for anyone not to know [at least that much].'"

I say: There are other narrations here besides what we have cited, but what has passed contains the core of the objectives within them. The narrations, although they may appear different at first glance, agree—regardless of the specifics of their explanations according to the specific levels of oppression—on a single meaning, which is the requirement of the verse's generality as we previously stated: that oppression is the lack of being guided to the truth without negligence.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَقْصُرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَن يَفْتِنَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كَانُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا (١٠١)
When you journey in the land, there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayers, if you fear that the faithless may trouble you; indeed the faithless are your manifest enemies. (101)
وَإِذَا كُنتَ فِيهِمْ فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَلْتَقُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُم مَّعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَهُمْ فَإِذَا سَجَدُوا۟ فَلْيَكُونُوا۟ مِن وَرَآئِكُمْ وَلْتَأْتِ طَآئِفَةٌ أُخْرَىٰ لَمْ يُصَلُّوا۟ فَلْيُصَلُّوا۟ مَعَكَ وَلْيَأْخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ ۗ وَدَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ عَنْ أَسْلِحَتِكُمْ وَأَمْتِعَتِكُمْ فَيَمِيلُونَ عَلَيْكُم مَّيْلَةً وَٰحِدَةً ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن كَانَ بِكُمْ أَذًى مِّن مَّطَرٍ أَوْ كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَن تَضَعُوٓا۟ أَسْلِحَتَكُمْ ۖ وَخُذُوا۟ حِذْرَكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (١٠٢)
When you are among them, leading them in prayers, let a group of them stand with you, carrying their weapons. And when they have done the prostrations, let them withdraw to the rear, then let the other group which has not prayed come and pray with you, taking their precautions and [bearing] their weapons. The faithless are eager that you should be oblivious of your weapons and your baggage, so that they could assault you all at once. But there is no sin upon you, if you are troubled by rain or are sick, to set aside your weapons; but take your precautions. Indeed Allah has prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (102)
فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَا ٱطْمَأْنَنتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَـٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا (١٠٣)
When you have finished the prayers, remember Allah, standing, sitting, and lying down, and when you feel secure, perform the [complete] prayers, for the prayer is indeed a timed prescription for the faithful. (103)
وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ فِى ٱبْتِغَآءِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ۖ إِن تَكُونُوا۟ تَأْلَمُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ يَأْلَمُونَ كَمَا تَأْلَمُونَ ۖ وَتَرْجُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَرْجُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٠٤)
Do not slacken in the pursuit of these people. If you are suffering, they are also suffering like you, but you expect from Allah what they do not expect, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (104)

(Commentary)

The verses legislate the prayer of fear (صلاة الخوف) and the shortening of prayer (القصر) during travel, and conclude by encouraging the believers to pursue and seek out the polytheists. They are connected to the preceding verses concerning struggle (الجهاد) and its various related matters.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلاةِ "When you journey in the land, there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayer" (4:101). The term "sin" (الجناح) refers to transgression, hardship, or deviation. "Shortening" (القصر) refers to the reduction of the prayer. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: Regarding the shortening of prayer, there are three linguistic forms: qasartu al-salata aqsuruha, which is the language of the Quran; qassartuha taqsiran; and aqsartuha iqsaran.

The meaning is: If you travel, there is no obstacle of hardship or sin in reducing something from the prayer. The mere negation of "sin" (الجناح), which ostensibly indicates permission (الجواز), does not preclude its occurrence in a context denoting obligation (الوجوب), as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الصَّفا وَالْمَرْوَةَ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللهِ فَمَنْ حَجَّ الْبَيْتَ أَوِ اعْتَمَرَ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِ أَنْ يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِما "Indeed Safa and Marwah are among Allah’s sacraments. So whoever makes hajj to the House, or performs the ‘umrah, there is no sin upon him to circuit between them" (2:158), despite the circumambulation (الطواف) being obligatory. This is because the context is one of legislation (التشريع), and it suffices therein to merely disclose the establishment of the ruling without the need to exhaust all aspects and specifics of the ruling. A similar instance to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ "and that you fast is better for you" (2:184).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "if you fear that the faithless may trouble you" (4:101). Although "trouble/trial" (الفتنة) has many different meanings, its customary usage in the Quran specifically regarding the disbelievers and polytheists refers to torture, such as killing, beating, and the like. The contextual clues also support this; thus, the meaning is: If you fear that they will torture you by attacking and killing.

This clause is a condition for His saying: فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ "there is no sin upon you." It indicates that the beginning of the legislation for shortening the prayer was during the fear of trouble (الفتنة). This does not contradict the legislation later expanding to cover all forms of legal travel (السفر الشرعي), even if not accompanied by fear. The Book explained a portion of it, and the Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition and practice — explained its inclusion of all cases, as will be seen in the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا كُنْتَ فِيهِمْ "When you are among them" until His saying: وَلْيَأْخُذُوا حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ "and let them take their precautions and their weapons" (4:102).

The verse mentions the method of the prayer of fear (صلاة الخوف) and addresses the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by assuming him as the leader (إمام) in the prayer of fear. This is a form of explanation by providing an example to be clearer, while simultaneously being concise and beautiful.

What is meant by His saying: فَأَقَمْتَ لَهُمُ الصَّلاةَ "and you maintain the prayer for them" is the congregational prayer (الصلاة جماعة). What is meant by His saying: فَلْتَقُمْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ مَعَكَ "let a group of them stand with you" is their standing in prayer with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by way of following (الايتمام), and they are the ones commanded to take their weapons. What is meant by His saying: فَإِذا سَجَدُوا "then when they have prostrated" etc., is when they have prostrated and completed the prayer, so that after completing their prostration, they may be behind the people. Likewise, what is meant by His saying: وَلْيَأْخُذُوا حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ "and let them take their precautions and their weapons" is that the second group praying with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله should take their precautions and their weapons.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: When you, O Messenger of Allah, are among them while in a state of fear, and you maintain the prayer for them—meaning you lead them in congregational prayer—they should not all enter the prayer together. Rather, let a group of them stand with you by following you, and let them take their weapons with them. It is understood that the other group will be guarding them and their belongings. Then, when those praying with you have prostrated and finished the prayer, let them be behind you guarding you and the belongings, and let another group who has not yet prayed come and pray with you. Let these worshippers also, like the first group that prayed, take their precautions and their weapons.

The description of the group as "another" (الأخرى) and the return of the masculine plural pronoun to it is sometimes out of regard for the literal wording (اللفظ) and other times for the meaning (المعنى), as has been said. In His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَأْخُذُوا حِذْرَهُمْ وَأَسْلِحَتَهُمْ "and let them take their precautions and their weapons," there is a subtle type of metaphor (استعارة), which is making "precaution" (الحذر) an instrument for defense similar to "weapons" (السلاح), as the act of "taking" (الأخذ) is attributed to it just as it is attributed to weapons.

His saying, the Exalted: وَدَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ تَغْفُلُونَ "The faithless be eager that you should be oblivious" until His saying: واحِدَةً "at one stroke" (4:102) is in the position of providing the rationale for the legislated ruling, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ "There is no sin upon you" to the end of the verse. This is another concession (تخفيف), which is that if they are troubled by rain falling upon them or if some of them are ill, there is no obstacle to them laying down their weapons, but they must nevertheless take their precautions and not be oblivious to the disbelievers, for the disbelievers are intent upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا قَضَيْتُمُ الصَّلاةَ فَاذْكُرُوا اللهَ قِياماً وَقُعُوداً وَعَلى جُنُوبِكُمْ "When you have finished the prayer, remember Allah, standing, sitting, and lying on your sides" (4:103). "Standing" (قياماً) and "sitting" (قعوداً) are either plurals or verbal nouns, and they are circumstantial qualifiers (حالان), as is His saying: عَلى جُنُوبِكُمْ "on your sides," which is an allusion (كناية) to continuous remembrance (الذكر) that encompasses all states.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا اطْمَأْنَنْتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ "Then when you are secure, maintain the prayer" (4:103). What is meant by "security/tranquility" (الاطمينان) is stability. Since it is contrasted with His saying: وَإِذا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "When you journey in the land," as supported by the context, it appears that what is meant by it is returning to one's homeland. Accordingly, what is meant by "maintaining the prayer" (إقامة الصلاة) is completing it, for the expression of the prayer of fear as "shortening the prayer" (القصر من الصلاة) alludes to this.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ كانَتْ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتاباً مَوْقُوتاً "Indeed the prayer is a timed prescription for the believers" (4:103). "Prescription" (الكتابة) is an allusion to obligation and necessity, like His saying, the Exalted: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيامُ كَما كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ "Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those before you" (2:183). "Timed" (الموقوت) is from "timing such-and-such" (وقت كذا), meaning a time was set for it. Thus, the apparent meaning of the wording is that the prayer is a timed obligation, divided into parts to be performed at their specific times and intervals.

However, it appears that "time" (الوقت) in the prayer is an allusion to firmness and lack of change, by mentioning the concomitant (الملزوم) to refer to the consequence (اللازم). Thus, the meaning of it being a "timed prescription" is that it is an established obligation that is not subject to change at all; the prayer is never waived under any circumstance. This is because keeping the word "timed" (الموقوت) to its initial apparent meaning does not suit the preceding content, as there is no pressing need to mention that the prayer is an act of worship with specific times. Furthermore, His saying: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ "Indeed the prayer" is in the position of providing the rationale for His saying: فَإِذَا اطْمَأْنَنْتُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ "Then when you are secure, maintain the prayer." Thus, it appears that what is meant by it being "timed" is its being fixed such that it is never waived, nor does it change or transform into something else, like fasting changing into a ransom (الفدية), for example.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَهِنُوا فِي ابْتِغاءِ الْقَوْمِ "Do not be faint-hearted in pursuing the enemy" (4:104). "Faint-heartedness" (الوهن) is weakness, and "pursuing" (الابتغاء) is seeking. "Pain" (الألم) is the opposite of pleasure. His saying: وَتَرْجُونَ مِنَ اللهِ ما لا يَرْجُونَ "while you hope from Allah what they do not hope" (4:104) is a circumstantial qualifier for the third-person plural pronoun. The meaning is: The state of both groups in that each of them suffers pain is the same; thus, you are not in a worse state than your enemies. Rather, you are more comfortable and fortunate than them, as you have the hope of victory, triumph, and forgiveness from your Lord, Who is your Guardian (ولي). As for your enemies, they have no guardian, nor do they have hope from any side to comfort their souls, invigorate them in their work, or drive them toward their objective. And Allah is All-Knowing (عليماً) of the benefits, All-Wise (حكيماً), precise in His command and prohibition.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: It was revealed—meaning the verse of the prayer of fear—when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went out to al-Hudaybiyyah intending Mecca. When the news reached Quraysh, they sent Khalid ibn al-Walid with two hundred horsemen to intercept the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He used to confront the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on the mountains. When they were at a certain point on the road and the time for the noon prayer (الظهر) arrived, Bilal gave the call to prayer and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prayed with the people. Khalid ibn al-Walid said: "If we had attacked them while they were in prayer, we would have struck them, for they do not interrupt their prayer. But another prayer is coming to them now which is dearer to them than the light of their eyes; when they enter the prayer, we shall raid them." Then Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with the prayer of fear in His saying: وَإِذا كُنْتَ فِيهِمْ "When you are among them."

In al-Majma‘: Regarding His saying: وَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنْ كانَ بِكُمْ أَذىً مِنْ مَطَرٍ "There is no sin upon you if you are troubled by rain" (the verse), it was revealed while the Prophet was at ‘Usfan and the polytheists were at Dajnan. They faced each other, and the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his companions prayed the noon prayer with full bowing and prostration. The polytheists intended to raid them, and some of them said: "They have another prayer dearer to them than this one," meaning the afternoon prayer (العصر). So Allah revealed this verse to him, and he prayed the afternoon prayer with them as the prayer of fear. That was the reason for Khalid ibn al-Walid's conversion to Islam (the story).

In the same source: Abu Hamzah—meaning al-Thumali—mentioned in his Tafsir: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went on a raid against the Muharib of Banu Anmar. Allah defeated them, and the Muslims captured the families and wealth. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Muslims dismounted—not seeing a single enemy—and they laid down their weapons. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went out to relieve himself, having laid down his weapon, and a valley came between him and his companions. By the time he finished his business, the valley had flooded and the sky was drizzling—so the valley separated the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from his companions. He sat in the shade of a tree, and al-Ghawrath ibn al-Harith al-Muharibi spotted him. His companions said to him: "O Ghawrath, this is Muhammad, separated from his companions." He said: "May Allah kill me if I do not kill him." He descended from the mountain with a sword, and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not notice him until he was standing over his head with the sword drawn from its scabbard. He said: "O Muhammad, who will protect you from me now?" The Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Allah." Then the enemy of Allah fell on his face. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stood up, took his sword, and said: "O Ghawrath, who will prevent you from me now?" He said: "No one." He said: "Do you bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that I am the servant of Allah and His Messenger?" He said: "No, but I pledge that I will never fight you, nor will I ever help an enemy against you." So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله gave him his sword. Ghawrath said to him: "By Allah, you are better than me." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I am more entitled to that."

Ghawrath went out to his companions, and they said: "O Ghawrath, we saw you standing over his head with the sword—what prevented you from him?" He said: "Allah. I lunged at him with the sword to strike him, but I do not know who pushed me between my shoulders? I fell on my face, my sword fell, and Muhammad reached it before me and took it." It was not long before the valley subsided, and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله crossed to his companions and informed them of the news, and he recited to them: إِنْ كانَ بِكُمْ أَذىً مِنْ مَطَرٍ "if you are troubled by rain" the entire verse.

In al-Faqih, with its chain of narration from ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi ‘Abdillah from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام that he said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prayed with his companions in the raid of Dhat al-Riqa‘. He divided his companions into two groups; he stationed one group facing the enemy and one group behind him. He said "Allahu Akbar" and they said it; he recited and they listened; he bowed and they bowed; he prostrated and they prostrated. Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله remained standing, and they prayed one unit (ركعة) for themselves, then they greeted one another, then they went out to their companions and stood facing the enemy.

Then their companions came and stood behind the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He said "Allahu Akbar" and they said it; he recited and they listened; he bowed and they bowed; he prostrated and they prostrated. Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sat and recited the testimony (تشهد), then he greeted them. They then stood up and performed one unit for themselves, then they greeted one another. Allah the Exalted had said to His Prophet: وإذا كنت فيهم فأقمت لهم الصلاة "When you are among them and you maintain the prayer for them" until His saying: كِتاباً مَوْقُوتاً "a timed prescription." This is the prayer of fear that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with.

And he said: Whoever prays the sunset prayer (المغرب) in fear with the people—he prays one unit with the first group, and two units with the second group (the hadith).

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام about the prayer of fear and the prayer of travel—are they both shortened? He said: "Yes, and the prayer of fear is more entitled to be shortened than the prayer of travel in which there is no fear." In al-Faqih, with its chain of narration from Zurarah and Muhammad ibn Muslim, they said: We said to Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: "What do you say regarding the prayer of travel? How is it and how many units is it?" He said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: وَإِذا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلاةِ 'When you journey in the land, there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayer.' Thus, shortening during travel became obligatory just as the full prayer is obligatory while at home (الحضر)." They said: We said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, only said: فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ 'there is no sin upon you,' and He did not say: 'Do it.' How did He make it obligatory just as He made the full prayer obligatory while at home?" He عليه‌السلام said: "Has Allah not said: إِنَّ الصَّفا وَالْمَرْوَةَ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللهِ فَمَنْ حَجَّ الْبَيْتَ أَوِ اعْتَمَرَ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِ أَنْ يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِما 'Indeed Safa and Marwah are among Allah’s sacraments. So whoever makes hajj to the House, or performs the ‘umrah, there is no sin upon him to circuit between them'? Do you not see that the circuit between them is an obligatory duty? Because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mentioned it in His Book and His Prophet performed it. Likewise, shortening during travel is something the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did and Allah the Exalted mentioned it in His Book."

They said: We said to him: "So whoever prays four units while traveling, should he repeat it or not?" He said: "If the verse of shortening had been recited to him and explained to him, yet he prayed four, he must repeat it. But if it had not been recited to him and he did not know it, then there is no repetition upon him."

All the obligatory prayers during travel are two units—every prayer except the sunset prayer (المغرب), for it is three and there is no shortening in it; the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله left it as three units during travel and while at home (the hadith).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ahmad, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Majah, Ibn al-Jarud, Ibn Khuzaymah, al-Tahawi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas in his Nasikh, and Ibn Hibban from Ya‘la ibn Umayyah, who said: I asked ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, saying: فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ أَنْ تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلاةِ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayer if you fear that the faithless may trouble you," yet the people are now secure? ‘Umar said to me: "I wondered about what you wonder about, so I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about that. He said: 'It is a charity that Allah has bestowed upon you, so accept His charity.'"

In the same source, narrated by ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Hibban, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan from Umayyah ibn Khalid ibn Asad: that he asked Ibn ‘Umar: "What do you think of shortening the prayer during travel? We do not find it in the Book of Allah; we only find mention of the prayer of fear." Ibn ‘Umar said: "O son of my brother, Allah sent Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and we knew nothing; we only do as we saw the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله doing. Shortening the prayer during travel is a Sunnah established by the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

In the same source: narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), and al-Nasa’i from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: We prayed with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله between Mecca and Medina—while we were secure and feared nothing—two units.

In the same source: narrated by Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa’i from Harithah ibn Wahb al-Khuza‘i, who said: I prayed with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله the noon and afternoon prayers at Mina—when the people were most numerous and most secure—two units.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Dawud ibn Farqad, who said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ كانَتْ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتاباً مَوْقُوتاً? He said: "A fixed prescription. It is not such that if you hasten a little or delay a little it would harm you, as long as you do not commit that neglect—for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: أَضاعُوا الصَّلاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَواتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا 'who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites; so they will soon encounter leeway' (19:59)."

I say: This is an indication that the obligations are flexible (موسعة) in terms of time, as indicated by other narrations.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام: He said regarding the sunset prayer during travel: "It is not to be abandoned if it is delayed for an hour, then you pray it if you wish to pray the late evening prayer (العشاء الآخرة). And if you wish, you may walk for an hour until the twilight disappears. Indeed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prayed the midday (الهاجرة) and afternoon prayers together, and the sunset and late evening prayers together; he used to delay and advance. Indeed, Allah the Exalted said: إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ كانَتْ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتاباً مَوْقُوتاً; He only meant its obligation upon the believers, He did not mean anything else. If it were as they say, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would not have prayed like this, and he was the most knowledgeable and informed. If it were better, Muhammad the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would have commanded it."

The people missed with the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام on the day of Siffin the noon, afternoon, sunset, and late evening prayers. ‘Ali, the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام, commanded them, and they said "Allahu Akbar," "La ilaha illa Allah," and "Subhan Allah" while walking and riding, due to the saying of Allah: فإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجالاً أَوْ رُكْباناً "And if you are in fear, then [pray] on foot or riding" (2:239). So ‘Ali عليه‌السلام commanded it and they did that.

I say: The narrations, as you see, agree with what we presented in the preceding commentary. The narrations regarding the previous meanings, especially from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, are very numerous; we have only cited a sample of what has been narrated.

Know that there are other narrations from the paths of the Sunnis that contradict what has preceded. Moreover, they conflict with one another. Examining them and the rest of the narrations recounting the method of the prayer of fear specifically, and the shortening of prayer during travel generally, belongs to the field of jurisprudence (الفقه).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: وَلا تَهِنُوا فِي ابْتِغاءِ الْقَوْمِ (the verse), it is coordinated with His saying in Surah Al ‘Imran: إِنْ يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ الْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِثْلُهُ "If you have suffered a wound, they too have suffered a similar wound" (3:140). We have already mentioned the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) for that verse there.

Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِمَآ أَرَىٰكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَا تَكُن لِّلْخَآئِنِينَ خَصِيمًا (١٠٥)
Indeed We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you; do not be an advocate for the traitors, (105)
وَٱسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٠٦)
and plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (106)
وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلْ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ خَوَّانًا أَثِيمًا (١٠٧)
And do not plead for those who betray themselves; indeed Allah does not like someone who is treacherous and sinful. (107)
يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ مَا لَا يَرْضَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطًا (١٠٨)
They try to hide [their real character] from people, but they do not try to hide from Allah, though He is with them when they conspire overnight with a discourse that He does not approve of. And Allah comprehends whatever they do. (108)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ جَـٰدَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَمَن يُجَـٰدِلُ ٱللَّهَ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَم مَّن يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا (١٠٩)
Aha! There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world! But who will plead for them with Allah on the Day of Resurrection, or will be their defender? (109)
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَسْتَغْفِرِ ٱللَّهَ يَجِدِ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١١٠)
Whoever commits evil or wrongs himself and then pleads to Allah for forgiveness, will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful. (110)
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُۥ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١١١)
Whoever commits a sin, commits it only against himself; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (111)
وَمَن يَكْسِبْ خَطِيٓـَٔةً أَوْ إِثْمًا ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِۦ بَرِيٓـًٔا فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلَ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (١١٢)
But someone who commits an iniquity or sin and then accuses an innocent person of it, is indeed guilty of calumny and a flagrant sin. (112)
وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَهَمَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُضِلُّوكَ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۖ وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُن تَعْلَمُ ۚ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا (١١٣)
Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and great is Allah’s grace upon you. (113)
۞ لَّا خَيْرَ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّن نَّجْوَىٰهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلَـٰحٍۭ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ فَسَوْفَ نُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١١٤)
There is no good in much of their secret talks, excepting him who enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people, and whoever does that, seeking Allah’s pleasure, soon We shall give him a great reward. (114)
وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَتَّبِعْ غَيْرَ سَبِيلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ نُوَلِّهِۦ مَا تَوَلَّىٰ وَنُصْلِهِۦ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا (١١٥)
Whoever defies the Apostle, after the guidance has become manifest to him, and follows a way other than that of the faithful, We shall abandon him to his devices and We shall make him enter hell, and it is an evil destination. (115)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١١٦)
Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. And whoever ascribes partners to Allah has certainly strayed into far error. (116)
إِن يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ إِنَـٰثًا وَإِن يَدْعُونَ إِلَّا شَيْطَـٰنًا مَّرِيدًا (١١٧)
They invoke none but females besides Him, and invoke none but a froward Satan, (117)
لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۘ وَقَالَ لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبَادِكَ نَصِيبًا مَّفْرُوضًا (١١٨)
whom Allah has cursed, and who said, ‘I will surely take of Your servants a settled share, (118)
وَلَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ وَلَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ ءَاذَانَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَلَـَٔامُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّخِذِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ وَلِيًّا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ خَسِرَ خُسْرَانًا مُّبِينًا (١١٩)
and I will lead them astray and give them [false] hopes, and prompt them to slit the ears of cattle, and I will prompt them to alter Allah’s creation.’ Whoever takes Satan as a guardian instead of Allah has certainly incurred a manifest loss. (119)
يَعِدُهُمْ وَيُمَنِّيهِمْ ۖ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (١٢٠)
He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, yet Satan does not promise them anything but delusion. (120)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ عَنْهَا مَحِيصًا (١٢١)
The refuge of such shall be hell, and they will not find any escape from it. (121)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ قِيلًا (١٢٢)
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever—a true promise of Allah, and who is truer in speech than Allah? (122)
لَّيْسَ بِأَمَانِيِّكُمْ وَلَآ أَمَانِىِّ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ مَن يَعْمَلْ سُوٓءًا يُجْزَ بِهِۦ وَلَا يَجِدْ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٢٣)
It will be neither after your hopes nor the hopes of the People of the Book: whoever commits evil shall be requited for it, and he will not find for himself any guardian or helper besides Allah. (123)
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيرًا (١٢٤)
And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone. (124)
وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِينًا مِّمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ وَٱتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۗ وَٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ خَلِيلًا (١٢٥)
Who has a better religion than him who submits his will to Allah, being virtuous, and follows the creed of Abraham, a Hanif? And Allah took Abraham for a dedicated friend. (125)
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطًا (١٢٦)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah comprehends all things. (126)

(Commentary)

What is gained from contemplating these verses is that they possess a single context, concerned with the exhortation to justice in adjudication (القضاء) and the prohibition against a judge inclining in his judgment, or a ruler in his ruling, toward those in the wrong (المبطلين), or acting unjustly against those in the right (المحقين), whoever they may be.

This is done by referring to certain incidents that occurred at the time of the revelation of the verses, followed by an inquiry into the religious truths related to that and the command to adhere to and observe them. It serves as a reminder to the believers that religion is a reality (حقيقة), not merely a name (اسم), and that what benefits is being truly characterized by it (التلبس به), not merely being titled by it (التسمي).

It appears that this story is what is referred to by His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً "But whoever commits a sin or a crime, and then blames it on an innocent person, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin." (4:112). This indicates that there was some form of disobedience (المعاصي) that could be attributed to another, such as theft, murder, destruction of property, or causing harm, and that it was expected that they would attempt to lead the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray in his judgment, while Allah is his protector.

It also appears that this story is what the initial verses refer to, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً "and do not be an advocate for the treacherous" (4:105), and His saying: يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ "They hide from people" (4:108), and His saying: ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ "Aha! There you are, pleading for them" (4:109). For although treachery (الخيانة) literally appears to refer to what occurs regarding deposits and trusts, the context of His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ خَوَّاناً أَثِيماً يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ "Indeed Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner. They hide from people" (4:107-108)—as will be explained—suggests that what is meant is that which is realized in theft and the like. This is based on the consideration that the believers are as a single soul; the wealth belonging to some is the responsibility of others in terms of respecting its sanctity and being concerned with its preservation and protection. Thus, the transgression of some against the wealth of others is a betrayal (خيانة) of themselves.

Contemplation suggests that the story was likely a theft committed by one of them, after which the matter was brought to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The thief then cast the blame upon someone else who was innocent. The thief's people then pressured him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to judge in their favor and went to great lengths to turn him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against the innocent accused. Consequently, the verses were revealed, and Allah cleared him of what they said.

Thus, the verses apply most strongly to what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) concerning the story of the theft by Abu Tu‘mah ibn al-Abayriq, even if the occasions of revelation—as you have heard repeatedly—are mostly of the nature of applying traditional stories to the Quranic verses that suit them.

From these verses, one can derive the authority (حجية) of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله judgment, his impeccability (العصمة) — the divine protection from sin and error — and other truths that will be explained, Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ "We have sent down the Book to you with the truth, so that you may judge between the people by what Allah has shown you." (4:105). The apparent meaning of "judging between the people" is to adjudicate between them in their disputes and litigations regarding judicial matters and to resolve differences through a ruling. Allah the Exalted has made judging between people the purpose of sending down the Book. Thus, the content of the verse aligns with what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed." (2:213). A detailed discussion on this has already passed.

This verse, إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ (etc.), is similar in its specific context to that verse, كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً, in its generality. However, it adds to it by indicating the establishment of the right of judgment for the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the authority (الحجية) of his opinion and view. For judgment (الحكم), which is the decisive resolution in adjudication and the settling of disputes, is inseparable from the exercise of the judge's view and the expression of his conviction, in addition to the knowledge he possesses of general rulings and universal laws (القوانين الكلية) in cases of litigation. Knowledge of universal rulings and the rights of people is one thing, while the decisive resolution and judgment that the specific case of dispute corresponds to one ruling rather than another is something else.

Therefore, what is meant by "showing" (الإراءة) in His saying: لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ is the creation of the view and the making known of the judgment, not the teaching of rulings and laws (الشرائع), as some have conjectured.

The content of the verse, according to what the context provides, is that Allah sent down the Book to you and taught you its rulings, laws, and wisdom so that you may add to them the view He has created for you and the judgment He has made known to you, thereby judging between the people and resolving their differences.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً "and do not be an advocate for the treacherous" (4:105). This is conjoined to the preceding declarative sentence because it carries the meaning of a command (الإنشاء), as if it were said: "So judge between them and do not be an advocate for the treacherous."

The advocate (الخصيم) is the one who defends a claim or what is in its position. In this is a prohibition for him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from being an advocate for the treacherous against those who demand their rights from them, thereby defending the treacherous and nullifying the rights of those who are in the right among the claimants.

It might be possible to derive from the conjunction of His saying: وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ to what preceded it—which is His absolute command to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to judge—that what is meant by treachery (الخيانة) is absolute transgression against the rights of others by those from whom such behavior is unbecoming, not specifically the betrayal of trusts. Although a specific term is sometimes conjoined to a general one for a particular concern regarding its status, the context here seems devoid of such specific concern. A completion of this discussion will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً "And plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (4:106). It appears that seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) here is to ask Allah, the Glorified, to veil (الستر) what is in human nature of the possibility of encroaching upon rights and inclining toward desire (الهوى), and to forgive that. It has been mentioned repeatedly that pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) are used in His speech in various contexts, all unified by the concept of sin (الذنب) — which is a distancing from the truth in some way. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Do not be an advocate for the treacherous nor incline toward them, and ask Allah, the Glorified, to grant you success in that and to veil your soul from inclining toward defending their treachery and from the caprice of the soul (هوى النفس) gaining mastery over you.

The evidence for this intended meaning is what is found at the end of these noble verses: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm." (4:113). For the verse explicitly states that they do not harm the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله even if they exert their utmost effort in stirring his emotions to favor falsehood and make it prevail over the truth. Thus, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is in a state of divine security from harm, and Allah protects him (يعصمه); so he does not act unjustly in his judgment, nor does he incline toward injustice, nor does he follow desire. Among injustice and the blameworthy inclination toward desire is to differentiate in one's judgment between the strong and the weak, or a friend and an enemy, or a believer and a protected disbeliever (كافر ذمي), or a relative and a stranger. Thus, the command for him to seek forgiveness is not due to the commission of a sin carrying a burden or consequence, nor because he was on the verge of something unpraiseworthy; rather, it is to ask Allah to make him prevail over the caprice of the soul. There is no doubt of his need for his Lord in this and his lack of independence from Him, even if he possesses impeccability (عصمة), for Allah, the Glorified, does as He wills.

This impeccability (العصمة) operates within the sphere of what is considered obedience (طاعة) and disobedience (معصية), and actions for which one is praised or blamed, not within the sphere of external reality (الواقع الخارجي). In other words, the verses indicate that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is secure from following desire and inclining toward falsehood. As for whether the judgment he passes based on the prescribed rules and the apparent laws of adjudication—such as his saying: البينة على المدعي واليمين على من أنكر "The burden of proof is on the claimant and the oath is upon the one who denies"—and the like, always coincides with what is the truth in reality (في الواقع), thereby always resulting in the victory of the one in the right and the defeat of the one in the wrong in their claim; the verses do not indicate this at all. Nor are the apparent laws capable of leading to that with certainty, for they are signs (أمارات) that distinguish between truth and falsehood mostly (غالباً), not always. It is obvious that the "mostly" does not necessitate the "always."

From what has preceded, the flaw in the words of some commentators becomes apparent where they mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَغْفِرِ اللهَ, that it is a command to seek forgiveness for what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله intended of defending and protecting this aforementioned traitor in the verse, after his people had asked him to defend him and be an advocate for him against a Jew.

This is because even this much would be a blameworthy effect from them, and Allah, the Glorified, has negated all harm from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُجادِلْ عَنِ الَّذِينَ يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ "And do not plead for those who deceive themselves" (4:107). It is said that the attribution of treachery (الخيانة) to the soul is because its consequence returns to it, or that every disobedience is considered a betrayal of the soul, just as it is considered an injustice (ظلم) against it, as He, the Exalted, said: عَلِمَ اللهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ "Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves" (2:187).

It is possible to derive from the verse, with the aid of what the Quran indicates—that the believers are as a single soul, and that the wealth of one of them is the wealth of all of them, which all must preserve and protect from loss and destruction—that the transgression of some against others through theft and the like is a betrayal (اختياناً) of themselves.

In His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ مَنْ كانَ خَوَّاناً أَثِيماً "Indeed Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner" (4:107), there is an indication of the persistence of these traitors in their treachery. This is reinforced by His saying: أَثِيماً (sinner), for athīm is more emphatic in meaning than ithm (sin), being an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) indicating stability. Furthermore, His saying: يَخْتانُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ (they deceive themselves) is not devoid of an indication of persistence, as is His saying: لِلْخائِنِينَ (for the treacherous), where He expressed it with a descriptive noun (الوصف) rather than saying something like "for those who betrayed," as He did in His saying: فَقَدْ خانُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ "they have already been treacherous to Allah before, and He gave [you] mastery over them" (8:71).

From these clues and their like, it appears that the meaning of the verse—with respect to the occasion of revelation—is: Do not be an advocate for these people, nor plead for them, for they are persistent in treachery, excessive in it, and established in sin, and Allah does not love one who is a treacherous sinner. This supports what has been narrated in the occasions of revelation regarding the verses being revealed about Abu Tu‘mah ibn al-Abayriq, as will follow.

The meaning of the verse—disregarding the specific incident—is: Do not defend in your judgment those who are persistent in treachery and continue in it, for Allah does not love the treacherous sinner. Just as He, the Exalted, does not love much treachery, He does not love a little of it. If it were possible for Him to love a little of it, it would be possible for Him to love much of it. Since this is the case, Allah prohibits defending a little treachery just as He prohibits defending much of it. As for one who was treacherous in one matter but then disputes in another matter while being in the right in his dispute, defending him is a defense that is neither prohibited nor forbidden, nor is it prohibited by His saying: وَلا تَكُنْ لِلْخائِنِينَ خَصِيماً (etc.).

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَلا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللهِ "They hide from people, but they do not hide from Allah" (4:108). This is also among the evidences for what we previously stated: that the verses (105–126) all possess a single context, revealed concerning a single story, which is the one referred to by His saying: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً (etc.). This is because hiding (الاستخفاء) is only suitable for actions that can be blamed on others, such as theft and the like. Thus, it is reinforced that what this verse and the preceding verses refer to is the same as what is referred to by His saying: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ (etc.).

Hiding from Allah is an impossible matter, as nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the heaven. Therefore, its opposite—namely, not hiding—is also a compelled matter beyond one's power. If it were beyond power, it would not be subject to blame or reproach, as is the apparent sense of the verse. However, it appears that "hiding" is a metaphor (كناية) for "feeling shame" (الاستحياء). For this reason, He qualified His saying: وَلا يَسْتَخْفُونَ مِنَ اللهِ (firstly) with His saying: وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ إِذْ يُبَيِّتُونَ ما لا يَرْضى مِنَ الْقَوْلِ "while He is with them when they plot at night with words that do not please Him" (4:108). This indicates that they were devising a ruse by night to clear themselves of this blameworthy treachery, plotting in that a speech with which Allah, the Glorified, is not pleased. Then He qualified it (secondly) with His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطاً "and Allah encompasses what they do" (4:108), indicating His encompassing them in all states, including the state of the crime they committed. The qualification with these two constraints—namely, His saying: وَهُوَ مَعَهُمْ and His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ—is a qualification by the general after the specific. It is, in reality, a justification for their lack of shame before Allah by a specific cause and then by a general one.

His saying, the Exalted: ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "Aha! There you are, pleading for them in the life of this world" (4:109). This is an explanation of the futility of pleading for them, and that they do not benefit from it, presented in the form of an interrogative. The meaning is that if pleading for them were to benefit them, it would only benefit them in the life of this world, which has no value before Allah. As for the afterlife, which has great value before Allah, or the occasion for defense on the Day of Resurrection, there will be no defender there for the treacherous, nor any to plead for them; rather, they will have no trustee (وكيل) on that day to undertake the management of their affairs or the rectification of their conditions.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ سُوءاً أَوْ يَظْلِمْ نَفْسَهُ "Whoever commits an evil or wrongs himself" (4:110). In this is an encouragement and urging for those traitors to return to their Lord by seeking forgiveness. It appears that the alternative between "evil" (السوء) and "wronging oneself" (ظلم النفس), and the progression from evil to wronging, is because what is meant by evil is transgression against others, and by wronging is transgression against oneself. Or, it may be that evil is lesser than wronging, like a small disobedience compared to a large one; and Allah knows best.

This verse and the two verses after it are all speech directed toward a single purpose: the explanation of the matter of sin (الإثم) that a person earns by his action. Each of the three verses undertakes the explanation of one of its aspects. The first verse explains that the disobedience a person commits, whose consequence affects his soul and is recorded in the book of his deeds, is something for which the servant may repent to Allah and seek His forgiveness; if he does so, he will find Allah all-forgiving, all-merciful.

The second verse reminds man that the sin he earns, he earns only against himself, and it is not something that can bypass him and attach to another through blaming or fabrication and the like.

The third verse clarifies that the fault (الخطيئة) or sin (الإثم) that a person earns, if he blames it on an innocent person, that blaming is another sin beyond the original fault or sin.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ إِثْماً فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ وَكانَ اللهُ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً "Whoever earns a sin, earns it only against himself; and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." (4:111). It has already been mentioned that the verse is linked in content to the following verse dealing with blaming others for a fault or sin; thus, this one is like a premise for that one. Accordingly, His saying: فَإِنَّما يَكْسِبُهُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ is directed toward a restrictive designation (قصر التعيين). In the verse is an admonition for whoever earns a sin and then blames it on an innocent person. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: It is incumbent upon whoever earns a sin to remember that the sin he earns, he earns only against himself, not against another; and that he is the one who did it, not anyone else, even if he blames it on another or if another undertakes to carry his sin for him. Allah is all-knowing (عليماً), knowing that this earner did it, and that he is the one who did it, not the innocent person blamed for it; and He is all-wise (حكيماً), not punishing for a sin except its sinner, nor for a burden except its bearer, as He, the Exalted, said: لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ "It shall have what it has earned, and it shall bear [the responsibility of] what it has perpetrated" (2:286), and He said: وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى "no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (6:164), and He said: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ وَما هُمْ بِحامِلِينَ مِنْ خَطاياهُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ "The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and we will bear your sins.’ But they will not bear any of their sins. They are indeed liars." (29:12).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً ثُمَّ يَرْمِ بِهِ بَرِيئاً فَقَدِ احْتَمَلَ بُهْتاناً وَإِثْماً مُبِيناً "But whoever commits a sin or a crime, and then blames it on an innocent person, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin." (4:112). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "If someone intends one thing but another happens from him, it is said: he erred (أخطأ). If it happens as he intended, it is said: he was correct (أصاب). It may also be said of one who does an act that is not good or intends an intention that is not beautiful: he erred. For this reason, it is said: he hit the error, and he missed the right, and he hit the right, and he missed the error. This word is homonymous, as you see, fluctuating between meanings that one who seeks truths must contemplate."

He said: "The fault (الخطيئة) and the evil deed (السيئة) are close, but 'fault' is most often said of that which is not intended in itself, but rather the intention is a cause for the generation of that act from him, such as one who shoots at game but hits a person, or drinks an intoxicant and commits a crime in his intoxication. The cause is of two types: a prohibited cause, such as drinking an intoxicant, where the error generated from it is not excused; and a non-prohibited cause, such as shooting at game. He, the Exalted, said: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُناحٌ فِيما أَخْطَأْتُمْ بِهِ وَلكِنْ ما تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ 'There is no sin upon you in what you may mistake therein, but [only] in what your hearts intend' (33:5). And He, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً, so the 'fault' here is that which is not from an intention to do it." (End of quote).

I think that "fault" (الخطيئة) is among the descriptions that have become independent of their nouns due to frequent use, like "calamity" (المصيبة), "misfortune" (الرزية), and "disposition" (السليقة). The weight fa‘īlah indicates the storage and settlement of the event. Thus, the fault is the action in which error (الخطأ) has been stored and settled. Error is the occurring act that a person does not intend, such as accidental killing; this is its original meaning. Then it was expanded to include what a person ought not to intend if his soul were in its primordial soundness (سلامتها الفطرة). Thus, every disobedience and effect of disobedience is among the instances of error according to this expansion. The fault is the action or the effect of the action that a person did not intend (and is not then considered a disobedience) or ought not to have intended (and is then considered a disobedience or the consequence of a disobedience).

However, since Allah, the Glorified, attributed it to earning (الكسب) in His saying: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً, what is meant by it is the fault that is a disobedience. Thus, the fault in the verse is that which arises from an intention to do it, even if it is of a nature that it ought not to be intended.

It has already passed regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ "Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them’" (2:219) that sin (الإثم) is the action that, by its consequence, causes the deprivation of many good things for man, such as drinking wine, gambling, and theft, which prevent man from acquiring the vital goods of life and cause a social decline that reduces his social weight and strips him of general trust and confidence.

Accordingly, the combination of "fault" and "sin" in the form of an alternative and the attribution of both to earning in His saying: وَمَنْ يَكْسِبْ خَطِيئَةً أَوْ إِثْماً (etc.) necessitates the specialization of each with its own meaning. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Whoever earns a disobedience whose consequence does not exceed its specific instance, such as neglecting some obligations like fasting or committing some prohibitions like eating blood; or earns a disobedience whose consequence persists, such as killing a soul without right or theft, and then blames it on an innocent person by attributing it to him, has certainly shouldered a calumny and a flagrant sin.

In naming the attribution of an evil deed to another "blaming" (الرمي) — literally "throwing," used for arrows — and likewise in the use of "shouldering" (الاحتمال) for accepting the burden of the sin of calumny, there is a subtle metaphor (استعارة). It is as if the fabricator strikes the innocent accused by shooting him with an arrow, and his strike causes him to carry a burden that occupies him from every good for the rest of his life without ever leaving him.

From what has preceded, the reason for the variation in the expression for disobedience in the noble verses becomes clear—sometimes as sin (إثم), and other times as fault (خطيئة), evil (سوء), wronging (ظلم), treachery (خيانة), and misguidance (ضلال). Each of these words is suitable in its meaning for the place it occupies.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ (to the end of the verse). The context indicates that what is meant by their being bent on leading the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray is their being bent on making him agree to defend those whom Allah, the Exalted, named at the beginning of the verses as the treacherous, and to plead for them. Accordingly, what is meant by this "group" (طائفة) are also those whom Allah, the Glorified, turned to address with His saying: ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ جادَلْتُمْ عَنْهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا (etc.), and it applies to the people of Abu Tu‘mah according to what will follow.

As for His saying: وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ "but they do not mislead anyone except themselves," what is meant by it, by the clue of His saying after it: وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "and they cannot do you any harm," is that the misguidance of these people does not exceed themselves nor does it pass from them to you. They are the misguided ones by what they intended, because it is a disobedience, and every disobedience is misguidance (ضلال).

This speech has another meaning that was previously alluded to in the discussion on His saying: وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ "but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they are not aware" (3:69) in the third volume of this book, but it is not suitable for this context.

As for His saying: وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ "and they cannot do you any harm, and Allah has sent down to you," it contains an absolute negation of their harming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. However, the apparent context is that it is qualified by His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ, considering it a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) referring to the pronoun in His saying: يَضُرُّونَكَ, even if most often a verbal sentence starting with a past tense verb is accompanied by qad, as mentioned by the grammarians. Accordingly, the speech is directed toward negating people's harming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله absolutely, whether in knowledge or action.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ ما لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ "and Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know" (4:113). The apparent meaning of the speech, as we alluded, is that it is in the position of providing the reason for His saying: وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ, or for the whole of His saying: وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ. In any case, this sending down and teaching is what prevents their effect in leading him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله astray; thus, it is the criterion for his impeccability (عصمته).

(A Discussion on the Meaning of Impeccability)

The apparent sense of the verse is that the matter by which impeccability (العصمة) is realized is a type of knowledge (علم) that prevents its possessor from being characterized by disobedience and error.
