The woman is stingy with her marital rights, such as clothing, maintenance, the marriage bed, and sexual intercourse; and the man is stingy with harmony and inclination if he desires separation and dislikes companionship. There is no blame on them then if they reconcile between themselves by one or both of them overlooking some of their rights.

Then He, the Exalted, said: وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً — "But if you be virtuous and godwary, Allah is indeed all-aware of what you do" (4:128). This is an exhortation to men not to transgress the path of virtue (الإحسان) and godwariness (التقوى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and to remember that Allah is all-aware of what they do. They should not be unjust in companionship, nor should they coerce women into waiving their rightful rights, even if the women have the right to do so.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129) is an explanation of the ruling of justice (العدل) between wives that was legislated for them against men in His saying, the Exalted, at the beginning of the Surah: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً — "...but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3). Similarly, His saying in the previous verse: وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا — "But if you be virtuous and godwary..." (4:128) points to this, as it is not free from a hint of threat. This necessitates confusion in identifying the reality of justice between them. Justice (العدل) is the middle point between excess (الإفراط) and negligence (التفريط), and it is extremely difficult to identify, especially regarding the heart's attachment in terms of love, for heart-felt love is not always subject to choice.

Thus, He, the Exalted, explained that justice between wives in its true sense—which is maintaining the exact middle point—is something a human being is unable to do, even if he is eager for it. What is mandatory for a man is only that he does not incline completely toward one side, especially the side of negligence, thereby leaving the woman like one suspended (كالمعلقة)—neither having a husband from whom she benefits, nor being a widow so she may marry or go about her business.

Therefore, the mandatory justice for a man between wives is to treat them equally in practice by giving them their rights without deviation. What is recommended (المندوب) for him is to be virtuous toward them, not to show dislike for their companionship, and not to treat them with bad character. Such was the practice (سيرة) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

This concluding part—that is, His saying: فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ — "...so do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended" (4:129)—is the proof that His saying: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129) does not mean the negation of absolute justice such that, when joined with His saying: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً — "...but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one..." (4:3), it would result in the abolition of polygyny in Islam, as has been claimed.

This is because the concluding part indicates that what is negated is the true, ontological justice (العدل الحقيقي الواقعي) without any deviation whatsoever by adhering to the exact middle point, and that what is legislated is approximate justice (العدل التقريبي) in practice without undue hardship.

Furthermore, the Prophetic Sunnah (السنة النبوية), the prevalence of the matter in the sight and hearing of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the continuous practice (السيرة المتصلة) among Muslims refute this delusion.

Moreover, to interpret His saying in the first verse of polygyny: فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four..." (4:3) as a mere intellectual hypothesis devoid of any actual application is nothing but an obfuscation from which His speech, the Glorified, is far removed.

Then His saying: وَإِنْ تُصْلِحُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً — "And if you come to an agreement and be godwary, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (4:129) is a confirmation and encouragement for men to reconcile when signs of dislike and discord appear, by explaining that it is part of godwariness (التقوى), and godwariness entails forgiveness and mercy. This, coming after His saying: وَالصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ — "...and reconciliation is better" (4:128) and His saying: وَإِنْ تُحْسِنُوا وَتَتَّقُوا — "But if you be virtuous and godwary..." (4:128), is a confirmation upon confirmation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ يَتَفَرَّقا يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ — "But if they separate, Allah will provide for each out of His bounty..." (4:130). That is, if the man and woman separate through divorce, Allah will enrich each of them from His vastness. Enrichment (الإغناء), by the context of the situation, refers to enrichment in everything related to marriage—harmony, intimacy, physical contact, and the wife's clothing and maintenance. For Allah did not create one of these two spouses solely for the other such that if they separated, neither would find another spouse for the rest of their lives; rather, this custom is a primordial custom (سنة فطرية) widespread among the individuals of this species, toward which every individual inclines according to their primordial nature (فطرة).

And His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ واسِعاً حَكِيماً وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — "...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise. To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (4:130-131) is a justification for the ruling mentioned in His saying: يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ — "...Allah will provide for each out of His bounty."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ وَصَّيْنَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ أَنِ اتَّقُوا اللهَ — "We have certainly enjoined those who were given the Book before you, and you, that you should be godwary of Allah..." (4:131) is a confirmation in calling them to observe the quality of godwariness (التقوى) in all stages of marital companionship and in every state. Neglecting it constitutes ingratitude (كفر) for Allah's blessing, based on the fact that godwariness achieved through obedience to Allah is nothing but gratitude for His blessings; or that the neglect of godwariness toward Allah, the Exalted, has no source other than disbelief (الكفر)—either manifest disbelief as in the disbelievers and polytheists, or latent, internal disbelief as in the transgressors among the believers.

By what we have explained, the meaning of His saying: وَإِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — "...But if you are ungrateful, then to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth..." (4:131) becomes clear. That is: if you do not preserve what We have enjoined upon you and those before you, and you waste this testament and are not godwary—which is disbelief in Allah or stems from disbelief in Allah—then that does not harm Allah, the Glorified, as He has no need of you or your godwariness; to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-sufficient (غني), all-laudable (حميد).

If you ask: What is the reason for the repetition of His saying: لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth"? For it has been mentioned three times.

I say: As for the first, it is a justification for His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ واسِعاً حَكِيماً — "...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-wise." As for the second, it stands in the place of the response to the condition (جواب الشرط) in His saying: وَإِنْ تَكْفُرُوا — "...But if you are ungrateful," and the implied meaning is: "If you are ungrateful, then He is self-sufficient (غني) without you," and it is a justification for the response, which has appeared in His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ غَنِيًّا حَمِيداً — "...and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable."

As for the third, it is a commencement (استيناف) and a justification in a way for His saying: إِنْ يَشَأْ — "If He wishes..."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَكَفى بِاللهِ وَكِيلاً — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah suffices as a guardian" (4:132). The meaning of His ownership (ملك) has been explained repeatedly. He, the Exalted, is a Guardian (وكيل) Who manages the affairs and concerns of His servants, and He suffices as a Guardian, needing no support or assistance. Thus, if He were not pleased with the deeds of a people and their conduct angered Him, He could remove them and bring others, or delay them and bring others forward. With this meaning, which is supported and indeed indicated by the context, His saying in the following verse is linked: إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ — "If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind..." (4:133).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ — "If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind, and bring others..." (4:133). The context—which is the call to adhere to godwariness (التقوى) that Allah enjoined upon this nation and those before them of the People of the Book—indicates that the manifestation of self-sufficiency and lack of need signified by His saying: إِنْ يَشَأْ — "If He wishes" pertains to the matter of godwariness.

The meaning is that Allah enjoined you all to adhere to godwariness, so be godwary of Him. If you disbelieve, He is self-sufficient without you; He is the Owner of everything, disposing of it however He wishes and for whatever He wishes. If He wishes to be worshipped and feared, and you do not fulfill that as it should be fulfilled, He is capable of delaying you and bringing forward others who will fulfill what He loves and is pleased with; and Allah is all-powerful over that.

Accordingly, the verse looks toward replacing the people—if they are not godwary—with other people who will be godwary of Allah. It has been narrated (1) that when the verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) struck his hand on Salman's back and said: "They are the people of this one." This supports this meaning, and you should reflect upon it.

As for what some commentators have suggested—that the meaning is: "If He wishes, He will annihilate you and create another people in your place or another creation in place of humanity"—this meaning is far from the context. Yes, it is acceptable in verses such as His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بِالْحَقِّ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ وَما ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ بِعَزِيزٍ — "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth? If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring a new creation, and that is not a great matter for Allah" (14:19-20).

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا فَعِنْدَ اللهِ ثَوابُ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَكانَ اللهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً — "Whoever desires the reward of this world, with Allah is the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing" (4:134). This is another explanation clarifying the error of one who abandons godwariness toward Allah and wastes His testament. If he does so seeking the reward and gain of this world, the matter has become confused for him, for the reward of both this world and the Hereafter together is with Allah and in His hand. Why then does he restrict his vision to the baser of the two and not seek the more noble of them, or both of them together? So it has been said.

What is more apparent—and Allah knows best—is that the meaning is that the reward of this world and the Hereafter, and the felicity of both together, is only with Allah, the Glorified. So let one seek nearness to Him, even if he desires the reward and felicity of this world. For felicity is not found for a human being except in the godwariness of Allah achieved through His religion which He legislated for him. Religion is nothing but the path to true felicity. How then can anyone attain a reward without His granting and bestowing it from Himself? And Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, who said: "None used to inherit except the man who had reached maturity—to manage the wealth and work with it; neither the child nor the woman would inherit anything. When the [verses of] inheritance were revealed in Surah al-Nisa’, that became difficult for the people, and they said: 'Shall the child inherit who does not manage wealth, and the woman who is likewise, inheriting just as the man inherits?' They hoped that some new decree (حدث) would come from heaven regarding that, so they waited. When they saw that no new decree came, they said: 'If this continues, it is an obligation from which there is no escape.' Then they said: 'Ask,' so they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and Allah revealed: وَيَسْتفتُونَكَ فِي النِّساءِ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِيهِنَّ — 'They seek your ruling concerning women. Say, "Allah gives you a ruling concerning them..."' — وَما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ — '...and what is recited to you in the Book...' — at the beginning of the Surah — فِي يَتامَى النِّساءِ اللَّاتِي لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ وَتَرْغَبُونَ أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ — '...concerning orphan women whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them, and whom you desire to marry...'" (The hadith).

In the same source: 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibrahim regarding the verse, who said: "If a girl was an orphan and ugly, they would not give her her inheritance and would prevent her from marriage until she died, so they could inherit from her. So Allah revealed this."

I say: These meanings are narrated through many chains from both Shi'i and Sunni sources, and some of them were mentioned at the beginning of the Surah.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا تُؤْتُونَهُنَّ ما كُتِبَ لَهُنَّ — "...whom you do not give what has been prescribed for them" (4:127), [meaning] what was prescribed for them of inheritance, he said: "It is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً — "If a woman fears recalcitrance from her husband..." (4:128): "It was revealed concerning the daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah—she was the wife of Rafi' ibn Khadij. She was a woman who had advanced in age, and he married a young woman in addition to her, and she was more pleasing to him than the daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah. The daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah said to him: 'Do I not see you turning away from me and preferring her over me?' Rafi' said: 'She is a young woman and she is more pleasing to me. If you wish, I will keep you on the condition that she has two or three days from me, and you have one day.' The daughter of Muhammad ibn Maslamah refused to be satisfied, so he divorced her once, then divorced her again. She said: 'No, by Allah, I will not be satisfied unless you treat me and her equally.' Allah says: وَأُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ — '...and souls are prone to greed.' The daughter of Muhammad did not seek her own portion and was greedy for him. Rafi' offered her either to be satisfied or he would divorce her the third time. She was greedy for her husband and became satisfied, so she reconciled with him on what was mentioned. Allah said: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما أَنْ يُصْلِحا بَيْنَهُما صُلْحاً وَالصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ — '...there is no blame on them if they reconcile between themselves, and reconciliation is better.' When she was satisfied and settled, he was unable to be just between them, so it was revealed: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ — 'You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended'—that he goes to one and leaves the other, neither unmarried nor having a husband. This is the Sunnah in such cases: if the woman agrees and is satisfied with what her husband reconciled with her upon, there is no blame on the husband or the woman. If she refuses, he divorces her or treats them equally; he has no other choice."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Malik, 'Abd al-Razzaq, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—in a summarized form.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Tayalisi, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Rahwayh, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib: that he was asked about this verse and said: "It is a man who has two wives, and one of them has become old or is ugly and he wants to separate from her. She reconciles with him on the condition that he stays with her for one night and with the other for several nights and does not separate from her. Whatever she is pleased with, there is no harm in it. If she changes her mind, he must treat them equally."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِنِ امْرَأَةٌ خافَتْ مِنْ بَعْلِها نُشُوزاً أَوْ إِعْراضاً — 'If a woman fears recalcitrance or disregard from her husband...' (4:128). He said: 'It is the woman who is with a man and he dislikes her, so he says to her: "I want to divorce you." She says to him: "Do not do so, for I hate that people should gloat over me. But look at my night and do with it as you wish, and whatever else there is, it is for you, and leave me as I am." That is the saying of Him, the Blessed and Exalted: فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِما أَنْ يُصْلِحا بَيْنَهُما صُلْحاً — "...there is no blame on them if they reconcile between themselves." And this is the reconciliation.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning in al-Kafi and in Tafsir al-'Ayyashi.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأُحْضِرَتِ الْأَنْفُسُ الشُّحَّ — "...and souls are prone to greed" (4:128), he said: "He said: 'Souls are prone to greed; from it is what she chose, and from it is what she did not choose.'"

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager..." (4:129), he said: "In affection (المودة)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Nuh ibn Shu'ayb and Muhammad ibn al-Hasan, who said: "Ibn Abi al-'Awja’ asked Hisham ibn al-Hakam, saying to him: 'Is Allah not Wise?' He said: 'Yes, He is the Wisest of judges.' He said: 'Then inform me about His saying: فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four; but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one." Is this not an obligation?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then inform me about His saying: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended." What kind of Wise One speaks like this?' Hisham had no answer.

So he traveled to Madinah to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: 'At a time other than Hajj or 'Umrah?' He said: 'Yes, may I be your ransom, for a matter that concerned me. Ibn Abi al-'Awja’ asked me a question for which I had nothing.' He said: 'And what is it?' So he informed him of the story.

Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to him: 'As for the saying of Him, the Mighty and Majestic: فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا فَواحِدَةً — "...then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four; but if you fear that you may not be just, then [marry only] one," it means in maintenance (النفقة). And as for His saying: وَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعُوا أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا بَيْنَ النِّساءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ فَلا تَمِيلُوا كُلَّ الْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ — "You will not be able to be just between wives, even if you be eager. So do not incline with a total inclination, leaving her like one suspended," it means in affection (المودة).'

He said: When Hisham returned to him with this answer and informed him, he said: 'By Allah, this is not from you!'"

I say: A similar hadith was also narrated from al-Qummi that some heretics (الزنادقة) asked Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal about the exact same issue. He traveled to Madinah and asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about it, and he answered him with the same answer. Abu Ja'far returned to the man and informed him, and he said: "This you have carried from the Hijaz."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَتَذَرُوها كَالْمُعَلَّقَةِ — "...leaving her like one suspended" (4:129)—meaning you leave the one you do not incline toward like one who is neither having a husband nor unmarried. He said: "It is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him)." In the same source, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): that he used to divide [his time] among his wives and say: اللهم هذه قسمتي فيما أملك فلا تلمني فيما تملك ولا أملك — "O Allah, this is my division in what I possess, so do not blame me for what You possess and I do not possess."

I say: The non-Shi'i scholars (الجمهور) narrated it through several chains. What is meant by his saying "what You possess and I do not possess" is heart-felt love. However, the narration is not free from some doubt, for Allah is too Majestic to blame anyone for what they do not possess at all, and He, the Exalted, has said: لا يُكَلِّفُ اللهُ نَفْساً إِلَّا ما آتاها — "Allah does not task any soul except [according to] what He has given it" (65:7). The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is more knowledgeable of the station of his Lord than to ask Him to bring about what already exists.

In al-Kafi, narrated from Ibn Abi Layla, who said: "'Asim ibn Humayd narrated to me, saying: 'I was with Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) when a man came to him and complained of need. He commanded him to marry. He said: Then his need intensified. He came to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) who asked him about his state. He said: "My need has intensified." He said: "Separate." So he separated. He said: Then he came to him and he asked him about his state. He said: "I have become wealthy and my state has improved." Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "I commanded you with two things that Allah commanded. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَأَنْكِحُوا الْأَيامى مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبادِكُمْ وَإِمائِكُمْ — 'Marry off those of you who are single, and those of your male and female slaves who are righteous...' until His saying: وَاللهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ — '...and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing' (24:32). And He said: وَإِنْ يَتَفَرَّقا يُغْنِ اللهُ كُلًّا مِنْ سَعَتِهِ — 'But if they separate, Allah will provide for each out of His bounty' (4:130).'"'

Ayah

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ شُهَدَآءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوِ ٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ ۚ إِن يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيرًا فَٱللَّهُ أَوْلَىٰ بِهِمَا ۖ فَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن تَلْوُۥٓا۟ أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (١٣٥)
O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah, even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives, and whether it be [someone] rich or poor, for Allah has a greater right over them. So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair, and if you distort [the testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (135)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ "O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah" (4:135). Justice (القسط) is equity (العدل), and maintaining justice (القيام بالقسط) means acting according to it and being vigilant in its preservation. Thus, "those who maintain justice" (القوامين بالقسط) refers to those who uphold it in the most complete and perfect manner, without swerving or deviating from it toward its opposite due to any factor such as whim (الهوى), emotion, fear, greed, or anything else.

This quality is the closest factor and the most complete means for following the truth (الحق) and preserving it from being lost. Among its branches is the necessity of truthfulness in bearing and delivering testimony (الشهادة).

From this, it becomes clear that beginning with this quality in this verse—which is directed toward explaining the ruling of testimony—and then mentioning the quality of testimony itself, is a form of progression from a general description to one of its derivatives. It is as if it were said: "Be witnesses for the sake of Allah; yet this will not be possible for you until you are maintainers of justice; so be maintainers of justice so that you may be witnesses for the sake of Allah."

His saying, شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ "witnesses for the sake of Allah," the preposition "for" (اللام) denotes purpose; that is, be witnesses such that your testimony is for Allah, as He, the Exalted, said: وَأَقِيمُوا الشَّهادَةَ لِلَّهِ "and maintain the testimony for the sake of Allah" (65:2). The meaning of the testimony being "for Allah" is that it be in adherence to the truth and for the sake of manifesting and reviving it, as clarified by His saying: فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve" (4:135).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوِ الْوالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ "even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives" (4:135). That is, even if the testimony is contrary to the interests of yourselves, your parents, or your relatives. Let not the love of your own interests or the love of parents and relatives incite you to distort or abandon it. What is meant by the testimony being "against oneself" or "against parents and relatives" is that the testimony one bears would, if delivered, be harmful to his own state or the state of his parents and relatives. This applies whether the person harmed is the one testified against directly—such as if his father and another person are in litigation and he testifies against his father—or if the harm is indirect, such as if two people are in litigation and the witness bears testimony for one of them which, if delivered, would also harm the witness himself, just as it would the other litigant.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيراً فَاللهُ أَوْلى بِهِما "whether he be rich or poor, Allah is closer to both" (4:135). The dual pronoun (بهما) refers back to "the rich" and "the poor" despite the presence of the disjunctive "or" (أو) because what is meant by the rich and the poor is the hypothetical, unknown individual who recurs according to the occurrence of events. Thus, he may be rich in one instance and poor in another; the disjunction is according to the hypothetical explanation, while in external reality, there is multiplicity. This is how some have explained it. The meaning, then, is that Allah is closer to the rich man in his wealth and to the poor man in his poverty.

What is meant—and Allah knows best—is: let not the wealth of the rich man cause you to incline toward him away from the truth, nor the poverty of the poor man cause you to favor his condition by deviating from the truth. Rather, uphold the testimony for Allah, the Glorified, and then leave the matter between Him and the rich and the poor. He is closer to them both and more merciful toward their conditions. It is from His mercy that He made the truth that which must be followed and made justice (القسط) that which is enjoined to be upheld. In the upholding of justice and the manifestation of truth lies the felicity of the human species, by which the backbone of the rich is strengthened and the condition of the poor is rectified.

Even if one of them might benefit from a distorted or abandoned testimony in a specific case or cases, this will inevitably weaken the truth and kill justice. In that lies the strength of falsehood and the life of tyranny and injustice, which is the incurable disease and the destruction of humanity.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve" (4:135). That is, for fear that you might deviate from the truth and justice by following whims and abandoning testimony for Allah. Thus, His saying أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا "lest you should swerve" is an object of purpose (مفعول لأجله). It is also possible that it is governed by an elided preposition, relating to "following," meaning: "so that you swerve."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً "and if you twist [your tongues] or disregard [the truth], then Allah is indeed aware of what you do" (4:135). "Twisting" (اللي) regarding testimony is a metaphor for distorting it, derived from "twisting the tongue" (لي اللسان). "Disregarding" (الإعراض) is to abandon the testimony altogether.

It has also been recited as وَإِنْ تَلُوا (with a damma on the lam and a silent waw), derived from "guardianship" or "authority" (الولاية). The meaning would then be: "If you take charge of the matter of testimony and deliver it, or if you disregard it, then Allah is aware of your deeds and will recompense you for them."

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, it is narrated that Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, a believer has seven rights over another believer. The most obligatory of them is that a man speaks the truth—even if it be against himself or against his parents—and does not incline away from the truth for their sake." Then he said: فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve; and if you twist [your tongues] or disregard [the truth]"—meaning, from the truth.

I say: In this, there is a generalization of the meaning of "testimony" to include speaking the truth absolutely, based on the generality of His saying: كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ "Be maintainers of justice."

In al-Majma', it is said: It is said that the meaning of "if you twist" (تلووا) is: if you alter the testimony; and "or disregard" (أو تعرضوا) is: if you conceal it. He said: This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١٣٦)
O you who have faith! Have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the Book that He has sent down to His Apostle and the Book He had sent down earlier. Whoever disbelieves in Allah and His angels, His Books and His apostles and the Last Day, has certainly strayed into far error. (136)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا لَّمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلًۢا (١٣٧)
As for those who believe and then disbelieve, then believe [again] and then disbelieve and then increase in disbelief, Allah shall never forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way. (137)
بَشِّرِ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٣٨)
Inform the hypocrites that there is a painful punishment for them (138)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ ٱلْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا (١٣٩)
—those who take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful. Do they seek honour with them? [If so,] indeed all honour belongs to Allah. (139)
وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَنْ إِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ يُكْفَرُ بِهَا وَيُسْتَهْزَأُ بِهَا فَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا مِّثْلُهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ جَامِعُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ جَمِيعًا (١٤٠)
Certainly He has sent down to you in the Book that when you hear Allah’s signs being disbelieved and derided, do not sit with them until they engage in some other discourse, or else you [too] will be like them. Indeed Allah will gather the hypocrites and the faithless in hell all together (140)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَرَبَّصُونَ بِكُمْ فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ فَتْحٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ وَإِن كَانَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نَصِيبٌ قَالُوٓا۟ أَلَمْ نَسْتَحْوِذْ عَلَيْكُمْ وَنَمْنَعْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَلَن يَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلًا (١٤١)
—those who lie in wait for you: if there is a victory for you from Allah, they say, ‘Were we not with you?’ But if the faithless get a share [of victory], they say, ‘Did we not prevail upon you and defend you against the faithful?’ Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah will never provide the faithless any way [to prevail] over the faithful. (141)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَهُوَ خَـٰدِعُهُمْ وَإِذَا قَامُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ قَامُوا۟ كُسَالَىٰ يُرَآءُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ وَلَا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٤٢)
The hypocrites indeed seek to deceive Allah, but it is He who outwits them. When they stand up for prayer, they stand up lazily, showing off to the people and not remembering Allah except a little, (142)
مُّذَبْذَبِينَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ لَآ إِلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَلَآ إِلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ سَبِيلًا (١٤٣)
wavering in between: neither with these, nor with those. And whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him. (143)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۚ أَتُرِيدُونَ أَن تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (١٤٤)
O you who have faith! Do not take the faithless for friends instead of the faithful. Do you wish to give Allah a clear sanction against yourselves? (144)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِى ٱلدَّرْكِ ٱلْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُمْ نَصِيرًا (١٤٥)
Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire, and you will never find any helper for them, (145)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَعَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٤٦)
except for those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion [exclusively] to Allah. Those are with the faithful, and soon Allah will give the faithful a great reward. (146)
مَّا يَفْعَلُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابِكُمْ إِن شَكَرْتُمْ وَءَامَنتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ شَاكِرًا عَلِيمًا (١٤٧)
Why should Allah punish you if you give thanks and be faithful? And Allah is appreciative, all-knowing. (147)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا آمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالْكِتابِ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ عَلى رَسُولِهِ — "O you who have faith! Have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the Book that He has sent down to His Apostle" (4:136). He commands the believers to have faith a second time, as evidenced by the detailed elaboration of the objects of this second faith—namely, His saying: "in Allah and His Apostle and the Book..." etc. It is also evidenced by the warning and threat issued for abandoning faith in any of these details. This is, in fact, a command for the believers to expand their general faith (إجمال إيمانهم) into the specific details of these realities, for they are items of knowledge (معارف) that are interconnected and necessitate one another.

Allah, the Glorified—there is no god but He; to Him belong the Most Beautiful Names and the Sublime Attributes. These attributes necessitate that He create a creation and guide them toward that which provides them with direction and happiness, and then resurrect them for the Day of Recompense. This cannot be completed except by sending messengers as bearers of good news and warners, and by revealing books to judge between people in that wherein they differ, and to clarify for them the knowledge of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), as well as the foundations of the divine laws and ordinances.

Thus, faith in any one of these realities is not complete except with faith in all of them without exception. Rejecting some while accepting others is disbelief (كفر) if manifested, and hypocrisy (نفاق) if concealed and hidden. It is a form of hypocrisy for a believer to adopt a course that leads to the rejection of some of these truths—such as separating from the community of believers and seeking proximity to the community of disbelievers, taking them as allies, and affirming them in some of the slanders they cast against faith and its people, or their objections to and mockery of the Truth and its elite. For this reason, the Exalted followed this verse by addressing the state of the hypocrites and threatening them with a painful punishment.

The meaning we have mentioned is what the literal sense (ظاهر) of the verse dictates. It is more appropriate than what some exegetes have stated: that the meaning of "O you who have faith! Have faith" is: "O you who have faith outwardly by professing Allah and His Messenger, have faith inwardly so that your outward may match your inward." Similarly, it is more appropriate than the view of some who said the meaning of "Have faith" is "Remain steadfast in your faith," or the view of others that the address is to the believers among the People of the Book—meaning: "O you who have believed from the People of the Book, believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Book He revealed to His Messenger, which is the Quran."

While these meanings are correct in themselves, the contextual clues (القرائن الكلامية) of the speech stand against them, and the weakest of these views is the last one.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِاللهِ وَمَلائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالاً بَعِيداً — "and whoever disbelieves in Allah, His angels, His books, His apostles and the Last Day, has certainly strayed into remote error" (4:136). Since the first part of the verse—from "O you who have faith! Have faith..." to "...from before"—was a call to combine all that was mentioned therein based on the claim that the parts of this whole are interconnected and inseparable, this second elaboration carries the meaning of a disjunction. The meaning is: "Whoever disbelieves in Allah, or His angels, or His books, or His apostles, or the Last Day"—that is, whoever disbelieves in any part of the components of faith—has certainly strayed into remote error.

The use of the conjunction "and" (الواو) is not intended to mean that the ruling applies only to the collective whole such that all these items form a single subject with a single ruling—meaning that disbelief in the whole as a whole is remote error, while disbelief in some parts to the exclusion of others is not. Furthermore, the Quranic verses are explicit regarding the disbelief of anyone who disbelieves in any single one of these mentioned items in detail.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلاً — "As for those who believe and then disbelieve, then believe [again] and then disbelieve, and then increase in disbelief, Allah shall never forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way" (4:138). If this verse were taken alone, isolated from what precedes and follows it, it would indicate the recompense Allah the Exalted gives to the people of apostasy (أهل الردة) when their apostasy is repeated—such that they believe, then disbelieve, then believe, then disbelieve, and then increase in disbelief. Allah the Glorified warns them—while they are in this state—that He will not forgive them nor guide them to a path. Such a thing is not to be hoped for or expected from His mercy because of their lack of stability in faith and their treating the command of Allah as a game to play with. Whoever is in this state will naturally not remain steadfast in a sincere faith that could be accepted from him. However, if they were to believe with a sincere faith, forgiveness and guidance would encompass them, for repentance (التوبة) through true faith in Allah is something Allah never rejects in any circumstance, according to what He has promised His servants. We have already discussed this regarding His saying: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ — "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for..." (4:17) in the fourth volume of this book.

Thus, the verse judges their deprivation based on what follows the course of nature and habit (الطبع والعادة), and it does not preclude an exception if faith and steadfastness upon it were to occur rarely from such a group, as can be inferred from a similar verse. The Exalted said: كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللهُ قَوْماً كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ وَشَهِدُوا أَنَّ الرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ وَجاءَهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "How shall Allah guide a people who have disbelieved after their belief and [after] bearing witness that the Apostle is true and [after] manifest proofs had come to them? Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (3:86), until He said: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً لَنْ تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الضَّالُّونَ — "excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. Indeed those who disbelieve after their belief and then increase in disbelief, their repentance shall never be accepted, and it is they who are the astray" (3:89-90).

The verses—as you see—make an exception for those who disbelieve after their faith and are met with the denial of forgiveness and guidance; yet, they simultaneously deny the acceptance of the repentance of those who increase in disbelief after faith. The beginning of the verses concerns those who disbelieve after faith and after witnessing the truth of the Messenger and the appearance of manifest signs; such disbelief is apostasy out of stubbornness and obstinacy. An "increase" in it only occurs with the settling of stubbornness and rebellion in their hearts and the entrenchment of transgression and arrogance in their souls. Returning and repenting does not habitually (عادة) occur from one in such a state.

This is what the context of the verse necessitates if taken alone, as mentioned. However, all these verses collectively possess an apparent meaning or indication of being part of a single, continuous context. On this premise, His saying: "Indeed, those who believe then disbelieve..." serves as a justification (تعليل) for His saying: "And whoever disbelieves in Allah... has certainly strayed into remote error." The two verses would then share the same referent (مصداق)—meaning that the one who disbelieves in Allah, His angels, His books, His messengers, and the Last Day is the one who believed, then disbelieved, then believed, then disbelieved, then increased in disbelief. He would also be among the hypocrites whom the Exalted addresses afterward in His saying: بَشِّرِ الْمُنافِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — "Inform the hypocrites that there is a painful punishment for them" (4:138), until the end of the verses.

Accordingly, the intended meaning of His saying: "Indeed, those who believe then disbelieve..." (to the end of the verses) varies according to how one interprets His saying: "O you who have faith! Have faith in Allah and His Apostle..." based on the different interpretations mentioned earlier:

If it is interpreted as "Believe in Allah and His Messenger inwardly just as you have believed in Him outwardly," then the meaning of believing, then disbelieving, then believing, then disbelieving, refers to the constant fluctuation of state that hypocrites suffer from when they meet the believers and when they meet the disbelievers.

If it is interpreted as "Remain steadfast in the faith you have donned," then the meaning of believing, then disbelieving, and so on, refers to the well-known repeated apostasy.

If it is interpreted as an invitation to the People of the Book to believe in Allah and His Messenger, then the meaning of believing, then disbelieving, and so on, refers to believing in Moses then disbelieving in him by worshipping the calf, then believing in Ezra or Jesus then disbelieving in him, then increasing in it by disbelieving in Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and what he brought from his Lord, as has been suggested.

If it is interpreted as "Expand your general faith into the specific details of the realities," as we have favored, then His saying: "Indeed, those who believe then disbelieve..." is a justification that applies to the state of the hypocrites mentioned afterward, who are defined by His saying: الَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "those who take the faithless for allies instead of the faithful" (4:139). For whoever connects with the disbelievers while separating from the community of believers cannot avoid attending their gatherings, finding intimacy with them, participating in their conversations, and affirming some of the talk they engage in which Allah the Glorified does not approve of—such as the slanders and evils they attribute to the religion and its leaders, and their mockery and ridicule of it.

Thus, whenever he meets the believers and participates with them in any of the rituals of the religion, he believes in it; and whenever he meets the disbelievers and approves of some of what they fabricate, he disbelieves. He continues to believe for a time and disbelieve for a time until this disposition (السجية) becomes entrenched in him, which constitutes an increase in disbelief. Allah knows best.

Since he is afflicted by this fluctuation of state and lack of stability, there is no repentance for him, because he is not steadfast in a state of regret even if he were to regret what he did—unless he repents and remains firm in his repentance with a stability that is not shaken by changing circumstances nor moved by the storms of desires. Therefore, Allah the Glorified conditioned the accepted repentance of such a hypocrite with conditions that leave no room for change or transformation, saying in the upcoming exception: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِاللهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ — "except those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion [purely] to Allah" (4:146).

His saying, the Exalted: بَشِّرِ الْمُنافِقِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً الَّذِينَ يَتَّخِذُونَ — "Inform the hypocrites that there is a painful punishment for them—those who take..." (4:138-139) is a threat to the hypocrites. He described them as taking the disbelievers as allies instead of the believers. This description is broader in its application (أعم مصداقاً) than just the hypocrites whose hearts never believed and who only feign faith; for a group of believers remained afflicted by taking the disbelievers as allies, cutting themselves off from the community of believers, connecting with them inwardly, and taking confidants from among them, even during the time of the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

This provides some support for the idea that these "hypocrites" refer to a group of believers who take the disbelievers as allies instead of the believers. This is further supported by the literal sense of His saying in the subsequent verse: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ — "He has already sent down to you in the Book that when you hear..." until His saying: إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ — "you will then be like them" (4:140). This is a confirmation of the threat to the hypocrites, yet the address therein is to the believers. It is also supported by how He will later describe their state in their hypocrisy by saying: وَلا يَذْكُرُونَ اللهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "and they do not remember Allah but little" (4:142); He affirms for them some degree of remembering Allah, which is unlikely to apply to hypocrites who never believed in their hearts at all.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "Do they seek honour with them? Indeed all honour belongs to Allah" (4:139). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) followed by an answer that confirms the denial. For honor (العزة) is a branch of sovereignty (الملك), and sovereignty belongs to Allah alone. The Exalted said: قُلِ اللهُمَّ مالِكَ الْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِي الْمُلْكَ مَنْ تَشاءُ وَتَنْزِعُ الْمُلْكَ مِمَّنْ تَشاءُ وَتُعِزُّ مَنْ تَشاءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَنْ تَشاءُ — "Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and withdraw sovereignty from whomever You wish; You exalt whomever You wish, and abase whomever You wish’" (3:26).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ — "He has already sent down to you in the Book that when you hear..." until His saying: مِثْلُهُمْ — "like them" (4:140). He refers to what He revealed in Surah al-An'am: وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِي آياتِنا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى يَخُوضُوا فِي حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِ وَإِمَّا يُنْسِيَنَّكَ الشَّيْطانُ فَلا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ الذِّكْرى مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "When you see those who gossip about Our signs, keep away from them until they gossip about other themes. And should Satan potentially cause you to forget, then do not sit with the wrongdoing people after remembering" (6:68). This is because Surah al-An'am is Meccan, while Surah al-Nisa is Medinan.

From the verse's reference to the verse in al-An'am, it can be inferred that some Quranic addresses were directed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) specifically, while the intended meaning encompasses the entire community (الأمة).

His saying: إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ — "you will then be like them" is a justification for the prohibition—meaning: "We have prohibited you because if you sit with them in this state, you will be like them." And His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ جامِعُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْكافِرِينَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ — "Indeed Allah will gather the hypocrites and the faithless in hell all together" (4:140).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَتَرَبَّصُونَ بِكُمْ فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ فَتْحٌ مِنَ اللهِ — "those who lie in wait for you: if there is a victory for you from Allah..." (4:141). Waiting (التربص) means expecting. Dominance (الاستحواذ) means overcoming and gaining mastery. This is another description of these hypocrites; they maintained a bond of connection with both parties: the believers and the disbelievers. They seek to draw benefit from both groups and profit from whichever of them is in a good state. If there is a victory for the believers, they say: "Were we not with you?"—so that they may have a share of the spoils and the like. And if there is a portion for the disbelievers, they say: "Did we not prevail over you and defend you from the believers?"—meaning, from believing in what they believed in and connecting with them, so we deserve a share of the portion you received, or we have a favor over you since we brought this portion to you.

It is said that what belongs to the believers was expressed as "victory" (الفتح) because it is what is promised to them, while what belongs to the disbelievers was expressed as "a portion" (نصيب) to belittle it, for it is of no account compared to what Allah promised the believers of victory and that Allah is their Guardian. Perhaps for this reason, victory was attributed to Allah, while the portion was not.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَلَنْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلاً — "Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah will never provide the faithless any way [to prevail] over the faithful" (4:141). The address is to the believers, though it extends to the hypocrites and disbelievers as well. As for His saying: "and Allah will never provide...", it means that the judgment on that day will be for the believers over the disbelievers, and the matter will never be reversed. In this is a cause for despair for the hypocrites—meaning: let these hypocrites despair, for the ultimate triumph belongs to the believers over the disbelievers in the Hereafter.

It is possible that the denial of a "way" (السبيل) is general to both realms: this world and the Hereafter. For the believers are always victorious by Allah's permission as long as they adhere to the requirements of their faith. The Exalted said: وَلا تَهِنُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Do not become hollow or grieve, for you shall have the upper hand, should you be faithful" (3:139).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ يُخادِعُونَ اللهَ وَهُوَ خادِعُهُمْ — "The hypocrites indeed seek to deceive Allah, but it is He who outwits them" (4:142). Deception (المخادعة) implies frequency or intensity in trickery, based on the principle that an increase in the structure of a word indicates an increase in its meaning.

His saying: وَهُوَ خادِعُهُمْ — "but it is He who outwits them" is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال)—meaning they seek to deceive Allah while He is outwitting them. The meaning results in this: these people intend by their acts stemming from hypocrisy—such as manifesting faith, approaching the believers, and attending their gatherings and scenes—to deceive Allah, meaning the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers, to draw benefits from them through their outward faith and deeds without any reality. They do not realize that the One who left them to these deeds and did not prevent them is Allah the Glorified, and this is a deception from Him toward them and a recompense for their evil intentions and the malice of their deeds. Thus, their deception of Him is itself His outwitting of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قامُوا إِلَى الصَّلاةِ قامُوا كُسالى يُراؤُنَ النَّاسَ وَلا يَذْكُرُونَ اللهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "When they stand up for prayer, they stand up lazily, showing off to the people and not remembering Allah except a little" (4:142). This is another of their attributes: standing for prayer—when they do stand for it—lazily, showing off to people. Prayer is the best worship in which Allah is remembered; if their hearts were attached to their Lord and believed in Him, laziness and lethargy would not overtake them in turning toward Him and remembering Him, nor would they perform their deeds to show off to people. They would have remembered Allah the Exalted much, as is the nature of the heart's attachment and the mind's preoccupation.

His saying, the Exalted: مُذَبْذَبِينَ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ لا إِلى هؤُلاءِ وَلا إِلى هؤُلاءِ — "wavering in between: neither with these nor with those" (4:143). It is said in al-Majma': It is said: "I wavered it (ذبذبته) and so it wavered (فذبذب)," meaning I moved it and it moved; it is like the movement of a suspended object. Thus, for something to be wavering (مذبذباً) is to oscillate between two sides without being attached to either. This is the description of the hypocrites; they waver between "that"—meaning what was mentioned of faith and disbelief—"neither with these," meaning not with the believers alone like the true believers, "nor with those," meaning not with the disbelievers alone like the pure disbelievers.

His saying: وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ سَبِيلاً — "and whomever Allah leads astray, you will never find any way for him" (4:143) is in the position of a justification for the preceding discussion of wavering. The reason for their oscillation between the two sides without attachment to either is that Allah has led them astray from the path, so there is no path for them to tread.

For this very reason, it was said: مُذَبْذَبِينَ (wavered) and not mutadhabdhibin (wavering by themselves)—meaning the divine compulsion (القهر الإلهي) is what drives this type of movement that does not end at a fixed, tranquil goal.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا الْكافِرِينَ أَوْلِياءَ — "O you who have faith! Do not take the faithless for allies..." (4:144) until the end of the two verses. Authority (السلطان) is the proof (الحجة). Level (الدرك)—with a fatha on the first two letters, and sometimes the ra is quiescent—al-Raghib said: al-dark is like al-daraj (step/degree), but step (الدرج) is used in consideration of ascent, while level (الدرك) is used in consideration of descent. For this reason, it is said: the degrees (درجات) of Paradise and the levels (دركات) of the Fire. Because of the conception of descent in the Fire, it was named the Abyss (هاوية).

The verse—as you see—prohibits the believers from connecting with the guardianship of the disbelievers and abandoning the guardianship of the believers. Then the second verse justifies this with the severe threat directed at the hypocrites. This is only because Allah the Glorified considers this act to be hypocrisy, warning the believers against falling into it.

The context indicates that His saying: "O you who have faith! Do not take..." is like a conclusion drawn from what preceded or a branch stemming from it. This is almost explicit in that the preceding verses address the state of those with diseased hearts and weak faith among the believers, calling them hypocrites—or at least including them. He then exhorts the believers not to approach this sanctuary nor expose themselves to Allah's wrath, and not to give Allah the Exalted a clear proof against themselves, whereby He would lead them astray, outwit them, and make them waver in the life of this world, then gather them and the disbelievers in Hell all together, then settle them in the lowest level of the Fire, and cut them off from every helper who might help them or intercessor who might intercede for them.

From the two verses, it appears, firstly: that leading astray, outwitting, and every divine wrath of this kind only occurs due to a clear proof provided by the servants' own deeds. It is an abasement by way of reciprocity and recompense. Far be it from the Divine Presence to initiate them with evil and wretchedness without something preceding from them that necessitates it. Thus, His saying: "Do you wish to give Allah a clear proof against yourselves?" follows the course of His saying: وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "and He misleads by it only the transgressors" (2:26).

Secondly: that in the Fire there are ranks for its inhabitants that differ in their depth; inevitably, their punishment intensifies accordingly. Allah the Exalted calls these "levels" (الدركات).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِاللهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ — "except those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion [purely] to Allah" (4:146). This is an exception from the threat mentioned regarding the hypocrites in His saying: "Indeed, the hypocrites are in the lowest level of the Fire..." (the verse). The necessary consequence of this is their exit from the group of hypocrites and their joining the ranks of the believers. Therefore, He concluded the exception by mentioning their being with the believers and mentioning the reward of all the believers, saying: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَسَوْفَ يُؤْتِ اللهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً — "they are with the faithful, and Allah will soon give the faithful a great reward."

Allah has described those He excepted from the hypocrites with several heavy attributes; the roots and veins of hypocrisy are not uprooted except by them. He mentioned repentance (التوبة), which is returning to Allah; but returning and repenting alone do not benefit until they reform (يصلحوا) all that was corrupted of their souls and deeds. Nor does reform benefit unless they hold fast (يعتصموا) to Allah—meaning they follow His Book and the Sunnah of His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for there is no way to Allah except what He has designated, and anything else is the way of Satan.

Nor does the aforementioned holding fast benefit unless they dedicate their religion (يخلصوا دينهم)—which is that wherein they hold fast—purely to Allah. For polytheism (الشرك) is a wrong that is neither excused nor forgiven. If they repent to Allah, reform every corrupt part of themselves, hold fast to Allah, and dedicate their religion purely to Allah, they are at that point believers whose faith is not tainted by polytheism. Thus, they are safe from hypocrisy and are guided. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there is security, and they are the guided ones" (6:82).

It appears from the context of the verse that the intended "believers" are the pure believers who are sincere in faith. Allah the Exalted has defined them as those who repent, reform, hold fast to Allah, and dedicate their religion purely to Allah. These attributes encompass the details of all that Allah the Exalted has counted in His Book of their attributes and descriptions, such as His saying: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ — "Certainly, the faithful have attained salvation—those who are humble in their prayers, and who avoid vain talk" (23:1-3), and His saying: وَعِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْناً وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً وَالَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّداً وَقِياماً — "The servants of the All-beneficent are those who walk humbly on the earth, and when the ignorant address them, say, ‘Peace!’ and those who spend the night for their Lord, prostrating and standing" (25:63-64), and His saying: فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their souls any burning regret over your verdict and submit in total submission" (4:65).

This is what the Quran intends by "the believers" (المؤمنين) when the term is used absolutely without any context indicating otherwise.

The Exalted said: فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "they are with the faithful" and did not say: "they are from the faithful," because by the realization of these attributes in them at their first instance, they join them; they will not be "from" them until the attributes continue in them with stability. Understand this.

His saying, the Exalted: ما يَفْعَلُ اللهُ بِعَذابِكُمْ إِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ وَآمَنْتُمْ — "Why should Allah punish you if you give thanks and be faithful?" (4:147). Its literal sense is that it is an address to the believers, for the speech is proceeding in addressing them. They are addressed with this speech while setting aside their faith and assuming them to be devoid of it, as is the nature of such an address.

It is an allusion to His lack of need for their punishment, and that if they did not deserve punishment by abandoning gratitude and faith, there would be nothing from Allah's side to necessitate their punishment. For He does not benefit from their punishment such that He would prefer it, nor is He harmed by their existence such that He would repel it from Himself by punishing them. So the meaning is: there is no cause for your punishment if you show gratitude for Allah's blessing by fulfilling His due right and believe in Him. And Allah is appreciative (شاكراً) of whoever thanks Him and believes in Him, All-Knowing (عليماً), not ignorant of its occasion.

In the verse is an indication that the punishment encompassing its people is only from their side, not from His; likewise, everything that necessitates punishment—such as error, polytheism, or disobedience. If any of that were from Allah the Exalted, the punishment that follows it would also be from Him, for the effect is attributed to whoever the cause is attributed to.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim: from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding the saying of Allah: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدادُوا كُفْراً — "Indeed, those who believe then disbelieve then believe then disbelieve then increase in disbelief." They said: "It was revealed concerning Abdullah ibn Abi Sarh—whom Uthman sent to Egypt—then he increased in disbelief when nothing of faith remained in him."

In the same source, from Abu Basir, who said: I heard him saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا (the verse): "One who claims that wine is forbidden then drinks it, and one who claims that adultery is forbidden then commits it, and one who claims that zakat is a right but does not pay it."

I say: In this is a generalization of the verse to disbelief in all its levels; among its levels is the abandonment of obligations and the commission of prohibitions. This supports what was mentioned in the Commentary.

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl: from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: وَقَدْ نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الْكِتابِ أَنْ إِذا سَمِعْتُمْ آياتِ اللهِ until His saying: إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً مِثْلُهُمْ. He said: "If you hear a man denying the truth and lying about it—and attacking its people—then stand up from his presence and do not sit with him."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In al-Uyun, with its chain to Abu al-Salt al-Harawi: from al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, majestic is His majesty: وَلَنْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلاً. He said: "He is saying: Allah will never provide the disbelievers any proof (حجة) over the believers. Indeed, Allah the Exalted informed of disbelievers who killed their prophet unjustly—yet despite their killing them, Allah did not provide them any way (سبيلاً) over His prophets."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ali: وَلَنْ يَجْعَلَ اللهُ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ سَبِيلاً — he said: "In the Hereafter."

I say: It has already been mentioned that the literal context is the Hereafter; if it were generalized to other than it by taking the sentence alone, it would encompass the proof (الحجة) in this world.

In al-Uyun, with its chain to al-Hasan ibn Faddal, who said: I asked Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) about His saying: يُخادِعُونَ اللهَ وَهُوَ خادِعُهُمْ. He said: "Allah, blessed and exalted is He, does not deceive, but He recompenses them with the recompense of deception."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Mas'adah ibn Ziyad from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father: that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was asked: "In what lies salvation tomorrow?" He said: "Salvation is that you do not seek to deceive Allah, lest He outwit you—for whoever seeks to deceive Allah, He outwits him, and strips faith from him, and he only deceives himself if he but perceived."

It was asked: "How does one seek to deceive Allah?" He said: "He acts according to what Allah commanded, then intends by it other than Him—so beware of showing off (الرئاء), for it is polytheism (شرك) with Allah. Indeed, the one who shows off will be called on the Day of Resurrection by four names: O disbeliever, O profligate, O traitor, O loser! Your deed is nullified, your reward is void, and you have no portion today; so seek your reward from the one for whom you used to work."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu al-Mu'azza al-Khassaf, reaching back to him, who said: Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever remembers Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in secret has remembered Allah much. Indeed, the hypocrites used to remember Allah openly and not remember Him in secret, so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: يُراؤُنَ النَّاسَ وَلا يَذْكُرُونَ اللهَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — 'showing off to the people and not remembering Allah except a little.'" I say: This is another subtle meaning for the "littleness" of remembrance.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ali, who said: "No deed is little with godwariness (تقوى); and how can that which is accepted be little?" I say: This is also a subtle meaning, and its return is in reality to what passed in the previous report.

In the same source: Muslim, Abu Dawud, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan extracted from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "That is the prayer of the hypocrite: he sits watching the sun until, when it is between the two horns of Satan, he stands and pecks four [units], not remembering Allah therein except a little."

I say: This is another meaning for the "littleness" of remembrance; for such a prayer, the remembrance is merely the turning toward Allah by standing for prayer, whereas he could have been immersed in His remembrance with presence and tranquility in his prayer.

The meaning of the sun being "between the two horns of Satan" is its proximity to the horizon of sunset, as if he makes the day and night two horns for Satan with which he gores the son of Adam or appears to the son of Adam.

In the same source: Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari in his Tarikh, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Umar, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The likeness of the hypocrite is like the wandering sheep between two flocks—it wanders to this one once and to that one once, not knowing which one to follow."

In the same source: Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Every 'authority' (سلطان) in the Quran is a proof (حجة)."

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Marwazi in Zawa'id al-Zuhd, and Abu al-Shaykh ibn Hibban extracted from Makhul, who said: It reached me that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "No servant dedicates himself purely (أخلص) to Allah for forty mornings except that the springs of wisdom appear from his heart upon his tongue."

I say: The narration is among the famous ones, and it has been narrated with its wording or its meaning through other paths.

In the same source: al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawadir al-Usul extracted from Zayd ibn Arqam, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Whoever says 'There is no god but Allah' sincerely (مخلصاً) shall enter Paradise." It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, and what is its sincerity (إخلاصها)?" He said: "That it restrains him from the prohibitions."

I say: The narration is widely transmitted (مستفيضة) in meaning and has been narrated through various paths in the collections of both the Sunnis and the Shi'a from the Prophet and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); we will mention the main versions of its transmitted wordings in a suitable place, if Allah the Exalted wills.

Regarding the conclusion of these verses, there are various and scattered narrations concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول); we have omitted mentioning them because they clearly fall under the category of applying the general rule to specific instances (الجري). Allah knows best.

Ayahs

۞ لَّا يُحِبُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْجَهْرَ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَن ظُلِمَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ سَمِيعًا عَلِيمًا (١٤٨)
Allah does not like the disclosure of [anyone’s] evil [conduct] in speech except by someone who has been wronged, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (148)
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ خَيْرًا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا۟ عَن سُوٓءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيرًا (١٤٩)
Whether you disclose a good [deed that you do] or hide it, or excuse an evil [deed], Allah is indeed all-excusing, all-powerful. (149)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public unless one has been wronged" (4:148). Al-Raghib said regarding the root "جهر" (public/manifest): "It is said of the manifestation of a thing to an excessive degree for the sense of sight or the sense of hearing. As for sight, it is like 'I saw him openly' (جهاراً); Allah the Exalted says: لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللهَ جَهْرَةً 'We will not believe you until we see Allah visibly' (2:55), and أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً 'Show us Allah visibly' (4:153)—until he said—and as for hearing, from it is His saying, the Exalted: سَواءٌ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ وَمَنْ جَهَرَ بِهِ 'It is the same [to Him] whether any of you speaks secretly or aloud' (13:10)." Evil speech (السوء من القول) refers to every word that distresses the one about whom it is said, such as invoking evil against him, or reviling him for the faults and defects he possesses or those he does not. Allah does not like the public utterance (الجهر) or disclosure of any of this. It is well known that He, the Exalted, is transcendent above love (الحب) and hatred (البغض) in the sense found within us, the human collective, and the animals that resemble us. However, since command and prohibition in our case naturally stem from love and hatred, these terms are used as metaphors for His will (الإرادة) and dislike (الكراهة), and for His command and prohibition.

Thus, His saying: لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public" is a metaphor for legislative dislike (الكراهة التشريعية), which is broader than both prohibition (التحريم) and disapproval (الكراهة).

His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ "unless one has been wronged" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع); meaning: but he who has been wronged, there is no harm in him speaking evil publicly regarding the one who wronged him, in the context of the wrong committed. This serves as the contextual clue (القرينة) that he is only permitted to speak evil publicly to clarify the wrong done to him and to expose the faults of the wrongdoer that pertain to the injustice committed. As for transgressing beyond that to things the wrongdoer does not possess, or things unrelated to the injustice, there is no evidence in the verse for the permissibility of speaking such things publicly.

Although the exegetes have differed in interpreting "evil speech" (السوء من القول)—with some saying it is invoking evil against the wrongdoer, and others saying it is mentioning his injustice and his transgression against him, and so on—all of these are included in the general application (إطلاق) of the verse. Therefore, there is no reason to restrict the discourse to only some of them.

His saying: وَكانَ اللهُ سَمِيعاً عَلِيماً "and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" (4:148) is in the position of emphasizing the prohibition derived from His saying: لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ "Allah does not like the utterance... in public." That is, it is not fitting for anyone other than the wronged party to speak evil publicly, for Allah is All-hearing (سميع), hearing what is said, and All-knowing (عليم), knowing the intent behind it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تُبْدُوا خَيْراً أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ أَوْ تَعْفُوا عَنْ سُوءٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيراً "Whether you reveal a good [deed] or hide it, or pardon an evil, Allah is indeed All-excusing, All-powerful" (4:149). This verse is not without a connection to the preceding one, for it encompasses the manifestation of good speech in gratitude for a blessing bestowed by a benefactor upon a person, and it encompasses pardoning evil and injustice so that one does not speak evil publicly against the wrongdoer.

Revealing (إبداء) good means making it manifest, whether it is an action—such as publicly spending on those who deserve it, and likewise every virtuous deed (معروف) because it involves exalting the word of the religion and encouraging people toward virtue—or whether it is a word, such as publicly thanking a benefactor and mentioning him with beautiful speech because it involves proper appreciation and encourages the people of bounty.

The hiding (إخفاء) of good refers to concealing the performance of a virtuous deed so that it may be further removed from ostentation (الرئاء) and closer to sincerity (الخلوص), as He said: إِنْ تُبْدُوا الصَّدَقاتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِيَ وَإِنْ تُخْفُوها وَتُؤْتُوهَا الْفُقَراءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنْكُمْ مِنْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ "If you disclose your charities, that is well, but if you hide them and give them to the poor, that is better for you, and it will atone for some of your misdeeds" (2:271).

Pardoning (العفو) an evil means covering it up verbally by not mentioning the wrongdoer's injustice, not tarnishing his reputation among the people, and not speaking evil against him publicly; and practically, by not confronting him with a response equivalent to his ill-treatment, and not taking revenge against him in matters where it would otherwise be permissible, as the Exalted said: فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ "So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah" (2:194).

His saying: فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ عَفُوًّا قَدِيراً "Allah is indeed All-excusing, All-powerful" is a cause placed in the position of the effect. The implied meaning is: if you pardon an evil, you have characterized yourselves with one of the perfect attributes (صفات كمالية) of Allah—which is pardoning (العفو) despite having power (القدرة)—for Allah is the Possessor of pardon despite His power. Thus, the recompense is a reward in relation to some of the conditions. As for revealing good and hiding it—meaning performing it in any state—this is also among His attributes, the Exalted, insofar as He is Allah, the Exalted, and the discourse may allude to this.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', it is said: "Allah does not like reviling in seeking redress except for one who has been wronged—so there is no harm for him to seek redress from the one who wronged him in ways permissible in the religion." He said: "And this is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu al-Jarud, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "The public utterance of evil speech is to mention a man for what is [actually] in him."
