In Tafsir al-Qummi: "And in another tradition regarding the interpretation of this, he said: 'If a man comes to you and says regarding you what is not in you of good, praise, and righteous deeds—do not accept it from him and belie him, for he has wronged you.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, with its chain of narration from al-Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah: from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: لا يُحِبُّ اللهُ الْجَهْرَ بِالسُّوءِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ إِلَّا مَنْ ظُلِمَ "Allah does not like the utterance of evil in public unless one has been wronged," he said: "Whoever hosts a people and treats them poorly in his hospitality—he is among those who have committed a wrong, so there is no sin upon them in what they say about him." I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma' from him (peace be upon him) as a disconnected report (مرسلاً), and it is narrated through Sunni channels from Mujahid.

The narrations, in any case, indicate a general application (التعميم) as we derived from the verse.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا (١٥٠)
Those who disbelieve in Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and disbelieve in some’ and seek to take a way in between (150)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ حَقًّا ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (١٥١)
—it is they who are truly faithless, and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment. (151)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَلَمْ يُفَرِّقُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَوْفَ يُؤْتِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (١٥٢)
But those who have faith in Allah and His apostles and make no distinction between any of them—them He will soon give their rewards, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (152)

(Commentary)

This is a transition to the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those to whom a scripture was previously revealed, specifically the Jews and Christians — and an exposition of the reality of their disbelieving (كفر), as well as an explanation of several of their injustices, acts of disobedience, and the corruption of their claims.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ "Those who deny Allah and His apostles" (4:150). These are the People of the Book among the Jews and the Christians. The Jews believe in Moses but disbelieve in Jesus and Muhammad, while the Christians believe in Moses and Jesus but disbelieve in Muhammad, may Allah’s peace be upon them all. According to their own claim, they do not disbelieve in Allah and all of His messengers; rather, they only disbelieve in some of the messengers. However, Allah has characterized them as disbelievers in Allah and all His messengers. Therefore, it became necessary to clarify what is intended by the general application of His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ "Those who deny Allah and His apostles." For this reason, He followed the phrase "those who deny" with His saying: وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ "and seek to cause a division between Allah and His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and deny others’" (4:150), as an explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير). Furthermore, the parts of this conjoined phrase explain one another: they are disbelievers in Allah and His messengers because, by saying "We believe in some and deny others," they seek to cause a division between Allah and His messengers. Thus, they believe in Allah and some of His messengers while disbelieving in others, despite the latter being a messenger from Allah; and the rejection of a messenger is a rejection of Allah, the Exalted.

Then He clarified this with another explanation by conjoining to it, as an explanatory conjunction, His saying: وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً "and seek to take a way in between" (4:150). That is, a middle path between believing in Allah and all His messengers and disbelieving in Allah and all His messengers—which is to believe in some and disbelieve in others. Yet, there is no path to Allah except by believing in Him and all His messengers. This is because a messenger, insofar as he is a messenger, possesses nothing of himself nor any authority in the matter; thus, belief in him is belief in Allah, and disbelief in him is pure disbelief in Allah.

Therefore, disbelieving in some and believing in others and in Allah is nothing but a separation between Allah and His messengers and an attribution of independence (الاستقلال) to the messenger. In such a case, belief in him would be disconnected from belief in Allah, and disbelief in him would be disconnected from disbelief in Him; he would then become a separate party rather than a medium. How can the premise of messengership (الرسالة) be valid for one whose belief and disbelief are not intrinsically linked to belief in Allah and disbelief in Him?

It is thus evident and beyond doubt that believing in one whose status is such, and submitting to him, constitutes polytheism (الشرك) — the act of associating partners with Allah in His divinity or attributes — against Allah, the Supreme. For this reason, you see that after describing them as seeking, through their belief in some messengers and disbelief in others, to divide between Allah and His messengers and to take a path in between, the Exalted stated that they are truly disbelievers, saying: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا "it is they who are truly faithless" (4:151). Then He threatened them, saying: وَأَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً "and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment" (4:151).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَلَمْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ "But those who believe in Allah and His apostles and do not make a distinction between any of them" (4:152). Since He characterized those who divide between Allah and His messengers as disbelievers, and mentioned that they are disbelievers in Allah and His messengers, He mentioned those who stand in contrast to them—those who believe in Allah and His messengers without making any distinction—in order to complete the classification of groups.

In these verses, there is a grammatical shift (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person plural in His saying: وَأَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً مُهِيناً "and We have prepared for the faithless a humiliating punishment," and then to a direct address in His saying: أُولئِكَ سَوْفَ يُؤْتِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ "to such He will soon give their rewards" (4:152). Perhaps the reason for this is that attributing the recompense to the Speaker (Allah) makes its occurrence seem more imminent, according to the tone of the discourse, than attributing it to the third person.

The shift occurring in the second verse also provides this benefit; for directing the address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding a beautiful promise—while he knows of its certain fulfillment by the Exalted—conveys the proximity of its occurrence.

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُكَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا۟ مُوسَىٰٓ أَكْبَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَرِنَا ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَعَفَوْنَا عَن ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ سُلْطَـٰنًا مُّبِينًا (١٥٣)
The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky. Certainly they asked Moses for [something] greater than that, for they said, ‘Show us Allah visibly,’ whereat a thunderbolt seized them for their wrongdoing. Then they took up the Calf [for worship], after all the manifest proofs that had come to them. Yet We excused that, and We gave Moses a manifest authority. (153)
وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَهُمُ ٱلطُّورَ بِمِيثَـٰقِهِمْ وَقُلْنَا لَهُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ لَا تَعْدُوا۟ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (١٥٤)
And We raised the Mount above them for the sake of their covenant, and We said to them, ‘Enter the gate prostrating’ and We said to them, ‘Do not violate the Sabbath,’ and We took from them a solemn covenant. (154)
فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَكُفْرِهِم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتْلِهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَقَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَلْ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهَا بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٥٥)
Then because of their breaking their covenant, their defiance of Allah’s signs, their killing of the prophets unjustly and for their saying, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised’… Indeed, Allah has set a seal on them for their unfaith, so they do not have faith except a few. (155)
وَبِكُفْرِهِمْ وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ بُهْتَـٰنًا عَظِيمًا (١٥٦)
And for their faithlessness, and their uttering a monstrous calumny against Mary, (156)
وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَـٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا (١٥٧)
and for their saying, ‘We killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the apostle of Allah’—though they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them. Indeed those who differ concerning him are surely in doubt about him: they do not have any knowledge of that beyond following conjectures, and certainly, they did not kill him. (157)
بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٥٨)
Indeed, Allah raised him up toward Himself, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (158)
وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا (١٥٩)
There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection, he will be a witness against them. (159)
فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَـٰتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا (١٦٠)
Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them [earlier], and for their barring many [people] from the way of Allah, (160)
وَأَخْذِهِمُ ٱلرِّبَوٰا۟ وَقَدْ نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ ۚ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٦١)
and for their taking usury—though they had been forbidden from it—and for eating up the wealth of the people wrongfully. And We have prepared for the faithless among them a painful punishment. (161)
لَّـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ وَٱلْمُقِيمِينَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَٱلْمُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (١٦٢)
But as for those who are firmly grounded in knowledge from among them, and the faithful, they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you—those who maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and believe in Allah and the Last Day—them We shall give a great reward. (162)
۞ إِنَّآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ كَمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَعِيسَىٰ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُونُسَ وَهَـٰرُونَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا (١٦٣)
We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him, and [as] We revealed to Abraham and Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the Tribes, Jesus and Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon—and We gave David the Psalms— (163)
وَرُسُلًا قَدْ قَصَصْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَرُسُلًا لَّمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ ۚ وَكَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا (١٦٤)
and apostles We have recounted to you earlier and apostles We have not recounted to you—and to Moses Allah spoke directly— (164)
رُّسُلًا مُّبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ حُجَّةٌۢ بَعْدَ ٱلرُّسُلِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (١٦٥)
apostles, as bearers of good news and warners, so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (165)
لَّـٰكِنِ ٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ أَنزَلَهُۥ بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (١٦٦)
But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge—and the angels bear witness [too], and Allah quite suffices as witness. (166)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ ضَلَـٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا (١٦٧)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah, have certainly strayed into far error. (167)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَظَلَمُوا۟ لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلَا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقًا (١٦٨)
Indeed those who are faithless and do wrong, Allah shall never forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way, (168)
إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (١٦٩)
except the way to hell, to remain in it forever, and that is easy for Allah. (169)

(Commentary)

The verses mention the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — those who were given previous scriptures, namely the Jews and Christians — asking the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) to bring down a book from heaven upon them, as they were not satisfied with the descent of the Quran through the revelation (الوحي) — the divine communication of guidance to a prophet — of the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) in stages. The verses then respond to their request.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ — "The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a book from the sky" (4:153). The People of the Book are the Jews and the Christians, according to what is customary in the parlance of the Quran in such contexts. Accordingly, the askers are both groups together, not just the Jews.

This is not contradicted by the fact that the injustices and crimes enumerated within the verses are specific to the Jews—such as asking to see Allah, taking the calf for worship, breaking the covenant (الميثاق) — the solemn binding agreement between Allah and His servants — at the raising of the Mount (الطور), the command to prostrate, the prohibition of transgression on the Sabbath, and so forth.

For both groups trace back to a single origin, which is the Children of Israel (شعب إسرائيل), to whom Moses and Jesus (عليه السلام) were sent, even though the call of Jesus spread after his ascension among non-Israelites such as the Romans, Arabs, Abyssinians, Egyptians, and others. Indeed, the people of Jesus were no less unjust toward Jesus than the Jews were toward Moses (عليه السلام).

Because both groups are considered to have a single origin, the Jews are specifically mentioned regarding the punishment that pertains to them, where He says: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ — "So due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We forbade them certain good things that had been lawful for them" (4:160). For this reason also, Jesus is counted among the messengers mentioned afterward, just as Moses (عليه السلام) is counted among them; had the discourse been directed at the Jews alone, this would not have been correct. Furthermore, it is for this reason that it is said after these verses: يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ وَلا تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ — "O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute anything to Allah except the truth. The Messiah..." (4:171).

In short, the askers are all the People of the Book, and the discourse is directed at them due to their shared national characteristic (الخصيصة القومية), which is arbitrariness (التحكم), speaking without truth, recklessness (المجازفة), and a lack of commitment to covenants and treaties. The discourse proceeds with them regarding what they share; when one group is distinguished by something, the speech is specified to them.

What they asked of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was that he bring down a book from heaven upon them. They did not ask this before the descent of the Quran and its recitation to them—how could they, when this event occurred in Medina, and they had already received what was revealed of the Quran in Mecca and a portion of what was revealed in Medina? Rather, they were not satisfied with it as a proof of prophethood (النبوة) — the divine office of being a prophet chosen by Allah.

They did not consider it a heavenly book, even though the Quran was revealed accompanied by a challenge (التحدي) and a claim of inimitability (الإعجاز) — the miraculous nature of the Quran that cannot be matched by human effort — as found in the chapters of al-Isra, Yunus, Hud, and al-Baqarah, all of which were revealed before Surah al-Nisa.

Thus, their request for the descent of a book from heaven, after witnessing the matter of the Quran, was nothing but an arbitrary request that issues only from one who does not submit to the truth (الحق) nor yield to reality (الحقيقة). Such a person merely engages in nonsense and delirium (يهذو) prompted by the hands of whims (الأهواء), without being bound by any constraint or standing upon any foundation. This is similar to the arbitrary demands made by the Quraysh despite the descent of the Quran and the manifestation of its call, when they said, as Allah the Glorified recounted of them: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (10:20), and: أَوْ تَرْقى فِي السَّماءِ وَلَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ — "...or you ascend into the sky, and we will not believe your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read" (17:93).

For this reason, Allah the Glorified answered their request, firstly, by stating that they are a people persisting in ignorance (الجهالة) and deviation (الضلالة), who do not refrain from various types of injustice (الظلم), however great, nor from disbelief (الكفر) and denial (الجحود) even when clear proof (البينة) — the manifest evidence that establishes the truth — has come, nor from breaking covenants even when they are solemn, along with other lies and slanders. One whose state is such is not fit to have his request answered or his proposal entertained.

Secondly, the Book that Allah revealed—which is the Quran—is accompanied by the testimony of Allah the Glorified and His angels (الملائكة), and it is that which articulates challenge after challenge through its noble verses.

Thus, He said in response to them first: فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ — "They had asked Moses for something greater than that" (4:153), meaning greater than what they asked of you regarding the descent of a book from heaven to them. فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً — "For they said, 'Show us Allah openly'" (4:153), meaning a visual seeing that we may behold with our eyes. This is the ultimate limit of human ignorance, nonsense, and rebellion (الطغيان). فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ — "So the thunderbolt seized them for their wrongdoing" (4:153). The story is mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah (2:55-56) and Surah al-A'raf (7:155).

Then He said: ثُمَّ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ — "Then they took up the Calf [for worship] after the manifest proofs had come to them" (4:153). This refers to idol worship after its falsehood had become manifest, or after the clarification that Allah the Glorified is transcendent (منزه) above any trace of corporeality (الجسمية) or contingency (الحدوث). This is among the most heinous of human ignorances. فَعَفَوْنا عَنْ ذلِكَ وَآتَيْنا مُوسى سُلْطاناً مُبِيناً — "Yet We excused that, and We gave Moses a manifest authority" (4:153). Moses had commanded them in that regard to repent to their Creator by killing themselves; they began to do so, and then Allah excused them before the killing was complete and before all were killed—this is what is meant by "excused" (العفو). He gave Moses (عليه السلام) a manifest authority (سلطاناً مبيناً) by giving him power over them, over the Samiri, and over his calf. The story is mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah (2:54).

Then He said: وَرَفَعْنا فَوْقَهُمُ الطُّورَ بِمِيثاقِهِمْ — "And We raised the Mount above them at their pledge" (4:154). This is the covenant that Allah took from them, then He raised the Mount above them. The story is mentioned twice in Surah al-Baqarah (2:63, 93).

Then He said: وَقُلْنا لَهُمُ ادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً وَقُلْنا لَهُمْ لا تَعْدُوا فِي السَّبْتِ وَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً — "And We said to them, 'Enter the gate prostrating,' and We said to them, 'Do not transgress on the Sabbath,' and We took from them a solemn pledge" (4:154). Both stories are mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah (2:58-65) and Surah al-A'raf (7:161-163). It is not unlikely that the mentioned covenant refers to these two stories and others, for the Quran mentions the taking of the covenant from them repeatedly, such as His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائيلَ لا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللهَ — "When We took a pledge from the Children of Israel: 'Worship no one but Allah...'" (2:83), and His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَكُمْ لا تَسْفِكُونَ دِماءَكُمْ وَلا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنْفُسَكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ — "And when We took a pledge from you: 'You shall not shed your own blood, and you shall not expel your own people from your homes,' then you pledged, and you were witnesses [to it]" (2:84).

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ — "So, for their breaking their pledge" (4:155). The letter fa (الفاء) indicates a result (التفريع), and the prepositional phrase is connected to what will come after several verses—wherein their crimes are mentioned—namely His saying: حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ — "We forbade them" (4:160). The verses are aimed at explaining the dire worldly and otherworldly punishment with which Allah repaid them. In them is the mention of some of their evil ways (سننهم السيئة) that were not mentioned initially.

His saying: فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ is a summary of the breaking of covenants mentioned of them and of the covenants taken from them that were not mentioned.

His saying: وَكُفْرِهِمْ بِآياتِ اللهِ — "and their defiance of the signs of Allah" (4:155) is a summary of the types of disbelief they committed during the time of Moses (عليه السلام) and after him, many of which the Quran has recounted. Among them are the two instances mentioned at the beginning of the verses, namely His saying: فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً and His saying: ثُمَّ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ. These were placed at the beginning, yet delayed in this verse, because the two contexts are different and their requirements vary. The beginning of the verses addresses their request for the descent of a book from heaven; thus, mentioning their request for something greater than that and their worship of the calf is more suitable and closely linked to it. This verse and those following it address their punishment in exchange for their deeds after they had answered the call of truth, and mention the reasons for that; starting with the mention of breaking the covenant is more suitable in this context.

His saying: وَقَتْلِهِمُ الْأَنْبِياءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ — "and their killing the prophets unjustly" (4:155) refers to Zechariah, John, and others whose killing the Quran mentions in general without naming them.

His saying: وَقَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنا غُلْفٌ — "and their saying, 'Our hearts are uncircumcised'" (4:155). The word غُلْفٌ is the plural of أغلف (uncircumcised/covered), meaning they are in coverings (أغشية) that prevent them from hearing the prophetic call and accepting the truth if they are called to it. This is a statement they made intending to reject the call, attributing their lack of response to Allah the Glorified, as if they were claiming they were created with covered hearts, or that they were made so regarding any call other than that of Moses, without this being attributed to their own choice or action.

Therefore, Allah the Glorified refuted them with His saying: بَلْ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — "Rather, Allah has set a seal on them for their unfaith, so they do not believe except a few" (4:155). He clarified that their hearts' refusal to hear the true call is attributed to Allah's action, but not as they claim—that they have no part in it. Rather, He did that to them in response to their disbelief and denial of the truth. The result of this was that these people do not believe except for a few of them.

The discussion regarding this exception has preceded, noting that this divine retribution (النقمة الإلهية) befell them due to their national identity and society. Thus, the collective as a collective is decreed with retribution, and their hearts are sealed; it is impossible for them to believe in their entirety. This does not contradict the faith of a small portion of them.

His saying, the Exalted: بِكُفْرِهِمْ وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلى مَرْيَمَ بُهْتاناً عَظِيماً — "and for their faithlessness, and their uttering against Mary a monstrous calumny" (4:156). This refers to their accusing her (عليه السلام) of adultery regarding the birth of Jesus. This is both disbelief and slander (بهتان) together. Jesus had spoken to them at his birth and said: إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا — "Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet" (19:30).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا الْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ اللهِ وَما قَتَلُوهُ وَما صَلَبُوهُ وَلكِنْ شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ — "and for their saying, 'We killed the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, the apostle of Allah'—though they did not kill him nor did they crucify him, but so it was made to appear to them" (4:157). It has preceded in the stories of Jesus (عليه السلام) in Surah Al 'Imran that they differed regarding the manner of his killing, whether by crucifixion or otherwise. Perhaps His recounting their claim of killing him first, then mentioning both killing and crucifixion in the context of refutation and negation, is to express a complete negation that leaves no room for doubt. For crucifixion, being a specific type of punishment for criminals, does not always necessitate death, nor is it the first thing that comes to mind when "killing" is mentioned. Since they differed on the manner of his killing, the mere negation of killing might have allowed for the interpretation that they did not kill him in an ordinary way, which would not contradict their having killed him by crucifixion. Therefore, the Exalted mentioned after His saying وَما قَتَلُوهُ (they did not kill him) the phrase وَما صَلَبُوهُ (nor did they crucify him) so that the speech would fulfill its right of clarity, explicitly stating that he (عليه السلام) did not die at their hands, neither by crucifixion nor otherwise. Rather, the matter was made to appear so to them (شبه لهم), so they took someone other than the Messiah (عليه السلام) in his place and killed or crucified him. This is not unlikely according to custom, for killing in such barbaric gatherings, raids, and riots often results in the real culprit being mistaken for another. He was killed by Roman soldiers who did not have perfect knowledge of his condition, so it is possible they took someone else in his place. Furthermore, narrations have been transmitted stating that Allah placed his likeness upon someone else, who was then taken and killed in his stead.

Some historical researchers have mentioned that the recorded historical accounts regarding him (عليه السلام), the events linked to his call, and the stories of his contemporary rulers and callers apply to two different men both named the Messiah—with more than five hundred years between them. The earlier one was truthful and not killed, while the later one was a fabricator and was crucified. (1) Based on this, what the Quran mentions of the "appearing so" (التشبيه) is the mistaking of the Messiah Jesus son of Mary, the Messenger of Allah, for the crucified Messiah. Allah knows best.

His saying: وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "Indeed those who differ concerning him" (4:157), meaning they differed regarding Jesus or regarding his killing, لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ — "are surely in doubt about it" (4:157), meaning in ignorance regarding his matter. ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا اتِّباعَ الظَّنِّ — "They do not have any knowledge of it beyond following conjectures" (4:157), which is guesswork or the preponderance of something based on what some of them heard from others.

His saying: وَما قَتَلُوهُ يَقِيناً — "and they did not kill him for certain" (4:157), meaning they did not kill him with a certain killing, or "I inform you of this with certain news." It has been said that the pronoun in وَما قَتَلُوهُ refers to "knowledge" (العلم), meaning they did not kill knowledge with certainty. "Killing knowledge" in linguistic usage means to purify it and free it from doubt and suspicion. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to "conjecture" (الظن), meaning they did not verify their conjecture nor were they firm in it. This meaning, even if established, is a strange one that the wording of the Quran does not support.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللهُ إِلَيْهِ وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً — "Rather, Allah raised him up toward Himself, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise" (4:158). Allah the Glorified recounted this story in Surah Al 'Imran, saying: إِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسى إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرافِعُكَ إِلَيَّ — "When Allah said, 'O Jesus, I shall take you fully and raise you up toward Myself...'" (3:55), mentioning the taking fully (التوفي) then the raising (الرفع).

This verse, according to the context, negates the occurrence of the claimed killing and crucifixion; thus, he was saved from their killing and crucifixion. The apparent meaning of the verse is also that the one whom they claimed was struck by killing and crucifixion—who is Jesus (عليه السلام) in his physical person—is the one whom Allah raised to Himself and protected from their plot. Thus, Jesus was raised with his body and soul; it is not that he died and then his soul was raised to the Exalted. This is something the apparent meaning of the verse does not tolerate according to the context, for the redirection (الإضراب) occurring in His saying بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللهُ إِلَيْهِ (Rather, Allah raised him up toward Himself) is not fulfilled by the mere raising of the soul after death, which could coexist with being killed or dying naturally.

Thus, this raising is a type of deliverance with which Allah delivered him and saved him from their hands, whether he was taken fully (توفي) at that time by a natural death, or was not taken by natural death, killing, or crucifixion, but in another manner we do not know, or remained alive by Allah's preservation in a manner we do not know. All of this is possible.

(1) According to this researcher, the currently famous Gregorian calendar is of doubtful accuracy.

It is not impossible for Allah to take the Messiah fully and raise him to Himself and protect him, or for Allah to preserve his life in a manner that does not conform to our prevailing custom. This is no less significant than the other miracles of Jesus himself that the Noble Quran recounts regarding his birth and his life among his people, and what it recounts of the miracles of Abraham, Moses, Salih, and others. All of this follows a single course; the Mighty Book indicates their occurrence with a proof that cannot be repelled except by the forced interpretations (التأويل) some people resort to, out of fear of the necessity of breaking custom (خرق العادة) and the suspension of the general law of causality. A comprehensive discussion on miracles (الإعجاز) and the breaking of custom has already passed in the first volume of this book.

After all this, the following verse is not without an intimation or indication of his (عليه السلام) life and his not having died yet.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً — "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them" (4:159). The word إِنْ is negating, and the subject (المبتدأ) is omitted, indicated by the context of negation; the estimation is: "And there is not one (أحد) among the People of the Book but will surely believe." The pronoun in His saying بِهِ (in him) and His saying يَكُونُ (he will be) refers to Jesus. As for the pronoun in His saying قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ (before his death), there is disagreement.

Some have said: The pronoun refers to the estimated subject, which is "one" (أحد). The meaning would be: Every single one of the People of the Book believes in Jesus before his [the person's] death; that is, it becomes clear to him shortly before death, during the death-throe (الاحتضار), that Jesus was truly the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) and His servant, even if this faith is one that does not benefit him. Jesus will then be a witness against all of them on the Day of Resurrection, whether they believed in him with a beneficial faith or a non-beneficial faith, like the one who believes at the time of his death.

This is supported by the fact that referring the pronoun of قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ to Jesus would rely on what has been mentioned in some reports—that Jesus is alive and has not died, and that he will descend at the end of time, whereupon the People of the Book among the Jews and Christians will believe in him. This would necessitate specifying the generality of His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ (There is none among the People of the Book) without a specificator (مخصص), for the requirement of the verse on this estimation is that those who believe in Jesus at that descent from heaven are the existing People of the Book, rather than the collective of them, including those who lived between the ascension of Jesus and his descent, died, and did not reach the time of his descent. This would be a specification of the verse's generality without a manifest specificator.

Others have said: The pronoun refers to Jesus (عليه السلام), and what is meant is their believing in him at his descent from heaven at the end of time, based on the narration as you have heard.

This is what they have mentioned. What deserves contemplation and deep reflection is that the occurrence of His saying وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً (and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them) in the context of His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ is manifest in that Jesus is a witness over all of them on the Day of Resurrection, just as all of them believe in him before death. Allah the Glorified has recounted the words of Jesus regarding this specific testimony in a particular way, saying of him: وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنْتَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ — "I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me fully, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things" (5:117).

Thus, he (عليه السلام) restricted his testimony to the days of his life among them before his being taken fully. This verse—namely His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ (etc.)—indicates his testimony over all who believe in him. If the one who believes in him is everyone, it necessitates that he is not taken fully until after everyone. This yields the second meaning, which is his (عليه السلام) being alive still, and his returning to them a second time so that they may believe in him. Ultimately, it would be said: Whoever among them does not reach his return to them believes in him at his death, and whoever reaches that believes in him either by compulsion or choice.

Furthermore, it is more suitable for the occurrence of this verse وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ in the context it occupies—namely after His saying وَما قَتَلُوهُ وَما صَلَبُوهُ وَلكِنْ شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ until He said بَلْ رَفَعَهُ اللهُ إِلَيْهِ وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً—that the verse be in the position of clarifying that he did not die and that he is alive still, as there is no manifest purpose in clarifying their compulsory faith and his testimony over them in any other scenario.

So, what we have mentioned supports that what is meant by their believing in him before death is the belief of all of them in him before his (عليه السلام) death.

However, there are other verses here that are not without an intimation of the contrary, such as His saying: إِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسى إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرافِعُكَ إِلَيَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَجاعِلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ — "When Allah said, 'O Jesus, I shall take you fully and raise you up toward Myself, and I shall purify you of the faithless, and I shall set those who follow you above the faithless until the Day of Resurrection'" (3:55). This indicates that among those who disbelieve in Jesus are those who remain until the Day of Resurrection. Similarly, His saying: بَلْ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً (Rather, Allah has set a seal on them for their unfaith, so they do not believe except a few), as its apparent meaning is that it is a retribution decreed upon them; thus, their society, as the society of the Jews or the society of the People of the Book, will not believe until the Day of Resurrection.

Indeed, the apparent meaning of the end of His saying وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ is that they remain after the taking fully of Jesus (عليه السلام).

But in fairness, the verses do not contradict what has passed. For His saying وَجاعِلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ does not indicate their remaining until the Day of Resurrection in the capacity of being People of the Book.

Likewise, His saying بَلْ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ (the verse) only indicates that faith does not encompass them all; even if they were to believe at some point, faith would only include a few of the many. Furthermore, His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ, if it indicates their believing in him before his death, only indicates the essence of faith; as for it being an accepted, non-compulsory faith, there is no indication of that.

Similarly, in His saying فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ (the verse), the referent of the pronoun is "the people" (الناس) rather than the People of the Book or the Christians, as evidenced by His saying at the beginning of the discourse: وَإِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "And when Allah will say, 'O Jesus son of Mary! Was it you who said to the people, "Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah"?'" (5:116). This is also indicated by the fact that he (عليه السلام) is one of the Arch-Prophets (أولي العزم) — the five greatest messengers of Allah who possessed firm resolve and brought new divine laws — sent to all people, and his testimony over their deeds encompasses the Children of Israel, the believers in him, and others.

In short, what contemplation of the context of the verses and the related verses yields is that Jesus (عليه السلام) was not taken fully by killing or crucifixion, nor by a natural death in the way we know its instance—as previously indicated. We have discussed as much as was possible for us regarding His saying يا عِيسى إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرافِعُكَ إِلَيَّ (3:55) in the third volume of this book.

Among the strange statements in this regard is what al-Zamakhshari mentioned in al-Kashshaf: that it is possible to mean that not one of all the People of the Book remains but will surely believe in him, in that Allah will bring them to life in their graves at that time, inform them of his descent and what he was sent for, and they will believe in him when their faith does not benefit them. This is a statement of the Return (الرجعة) — the belief that certain individuals will be brought back to life before the Day of Resurrection.

There are other poor interpretations regarding the meaning of the verse:

Among them: what appears from al-Zajjaj, that the pronoun in His saying قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ refers to the person of the Book (الكتابي), and that the meaning of His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ is that all of them say: "The Jesus who appears at the end of time, we believe in him."

This is a nonsensical meaning, for the verses are aimed at explaining their claim of killing and crucifying Jesus (عليه السلام) and refuting them, not their disbelief in him; this has no connection to their acknowledgment of the appearance of a Messiah at the end of time who will revive the matter of the Children of Israel, such that the discourse would be concluded with it.

Furthermore, if that were the intended meaning, there would be no need to mention His saying قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ, as the need for it would be removed without it. Likewise, His saying وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً, for on this estimation, it would be superfluous speech for which there is no need.

Among them: what some have mentioned, that the meaning of the verse is: "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in Muhammad before the death of that person of the Book."

This is as nonsensical as the previous one, for Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was not mentioned in the preceding speech such that the pronoun would refer to him. Nor does the context indicate that; it is a statement without proof. Yes, this meaning has been mentioned in some narrations that will pass by you in the following traditional discussion, but that is by way of application (الجري) — the interpretive principle where a verse is applied to instances beyond its original context — as we shall indicate. This is a matter that occurs frequently in narrations, as is not hidden from one who tracks them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ — "So due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We forbade them certain good things that had been lawful for them" (4:160). The letter fa indicates a result. The word "wrongdoing" (ظلم) is indefinite, perhaps to indicate its magnitude or for ambiguity, as no important purpose is served by specifying it. It is a substitution (بدل) for what was previously mentioned of their atrocities, except that it is not a "total substitution" (بدل الكل من الكل) as has been said, but rather a "partial substitution" (بدل البعض من الكل). For He made this wrongdoing of theirs a reason for forbidding the good things to them, and they were only forbidden in the law of Moses revealed in the Torah, with which the law of Moses concludes. Yet, among their atrocities and injustices mentioned were matters that occurred after that, such as the slander against Mary and others.

So, what is meant by "wrongdoing" is some of their heinous injustices mentioned, which was the reason for forbidding what was forbidden to them of the good things after they had been lawful.

Then He joined to that His saying: وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ كَثِيراً — "and for their barring many from the way of Allah" (4:160), which is their repeated turning away from the path of Allah, وَأَخْذِهِمُ الرِّبَوا وَقَدْ نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَأَكْلِهِمْ أَمْوالَ النَّاسِ بِالْباطِلِ — "and for their taking usury though they were forbidden it, and for their consuming the wealth of the people wrongfully" (4:161). His saying, the Exalted: وَأَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً — "and We have prepared for the faithless among them a painful punishment" (4:161) is coordinated with His saying حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ. Thus, they deserved two punishments from Allah for their injustices: a general worldly punishment, which is the prohibition of the good things, and an otherworldly punishment specific to the disbelievers among them, which is the painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: لكِنِ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ مِنْهُمْ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ — "But as for those who are firmly grounded in knowledge among them, and the faithful, they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you" (4:162). This is an exception and a rectification (استدراك) regarding the People of the Book. "Those who are firmly grounded" (الرَّاسِخُونَ) and what is coordinated with it is the subject, and "they believe" (يُؤْمِنُونَ) is its predicate. His saying "among them" (مِنْهُمْ) is connected to "those who are firmly grounded," and the "from" (مِنْ) in it is partitive (تبعيضية).

It is apparent that "the faithful" (الْمُؤْمِنُونَ) shares with "those who are firmly grounded" the connection to His saying "among them" in meaning. The meaning is: But those firmly grounded in knowledge and the true believers among the People of the Book believe in you and in what was revealed before you. This is supported by the reasoning coming in His saying: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ (etc.), for the apparent meaning of the verse, as will be explained, is that they believed in you when they found that your prophethood and the revelation with which We honored you resemble the revelation brought to them by the predecessors among the prophets of Allah: Noah and the prophets after him, and the prophets from the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Jacob, and others whom We have not recounted to you, without any difference.

This meaning—as you see—is more suitable for the believers from the People of the Book to be described with, rather than the believers from the Arabs whom Allah the Glorified described with His saying: لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أُنْذِرَ آباؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غافِلُونَ — "so that you may warn a people whose fathers were not warned, so they are oblivious" (36:6).

His saying وَالْمُقِيمِينَ الصَّلاةَ — "and those who maintain the prayer" (4:162) is coordinated with "those who are firmly grounded" and is in the accusative case (منصوب) for praise (المدح). Similar in coordination is His saying وَالْمُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ — "and those who give the zakat" (4:162), and His saying وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — "and those who believe in Allah and the Last Day" (4:162) is a subject whose predicate is His saying: أُولئِكَ سَنُؤْتِيهِمْ أَجْراً عَظِيماً — "to such We shall give a great reward" (4:162). Had His saying وَالْمُقِيمِينَ الصَّلاةَ been in the nominative case (مرفوعاً), as reported from the codex of Ibn Mas'ud, it and what is coordinated with it would be a subject whose predicate is His saying أُولئِكَ.

It is said in al-Majma': There is disagreement regarding the accusative case of al-muqimin. Sibawayh and the Basrans held that it is in the accusative for praise, on the estimation of "I mean those who maintain the prayer." They said: If you say, "I passed by Zayd the Generous" (مررت بزيد الكريم) and you intend to distinguish the generous Zayd from a non-generous Zayd, the correct case is the genitive (الجر). But if you intend praise and commendation, you may use the accusative and say: "I passed by Zayd the Generous" (مررت بزيد الكريم), as if you said: "I mention the generous one." Or if you wish, you may use the nominative and say: "the Generous" (الكريم), on the estimation of "he is the generous one."

Al-Kisa'i said: The position of al-muqimin is genitive, and it is coordinated with the "what" (ما) in His saying بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ, meaning "and in those who maintain the prayer."

A group said: It is coordinated with the pronoun (the ha and mim) in His saying مِنْهُمْ, meaning: "But those firmly grounded in knowledge among them and among those who maintain the prayer." Others said: It is coordinated with the kaf in قَبْلِكَ, meaning: "from what was revealed before you and before those who maintain the prayer."

It was also said: It is coordinated with the kaf in إِلَيْكَ or the kaf in قَبْلِكَ. These latter views are not permissible according to the Basrans because a manifest noun cannot be coordinated with a genitive pronoun without repeating the preposition.

He said: As for what is narrated from 'Urwah from 'Aishah, who said: I asked her about His saying وَالْمُقِيمِينَ الصَّلاةَ, and His saying وَالصَّابِئِينَ, and His saying إِنْ هذانِ, and she said: "O my nephew, this is the work of the scribes; they made a mistake in the Book." And what is narrated from some: "In the Book of Allah are things that the Arabs will correct with their tongues." They said: "And in the codex of Ibn Mas'ud: 'and those who maintain the prayer' (المقيمون الصلاة) [in the nominative]." This is not to be heeded, for if it were so, the companions would not have taught the people an error while they were the exemplars who took it from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم). (End quote).

In short, His saying لكِنِ الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ is an exception from the People of the Book regarding the implication of their asking the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) to bring down a book from heaven. As previously mentioned, the implication of their asking that is that what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) brought of the Book and Wisdom—confirming the signs of Allah revealed before him to His prophets and messengers—is not sufficient in calling them to the truth and establishing it. This is despite the fact that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) only brought them the like of what the prophets before him brought, and he did not live among them nor associate with them except as they lived and associated, as He said: قُلْ ما كُنْتُ بِدْعاً مِنَ الرُّسُلِ — "Say, 'I am not a novelty among the apostles...'" (46:9), and He said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ وَما جَعَلْناهُمْ جَسَداً لا يَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَما كانُوا خالِدِينَ — "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. We did not make them bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal" (21:7-8), until He said: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتاباً فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ — "We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your Reminder. Will you not then exercise your reason?" (21:10).

Thus, Allah the Glorified mentioned in a decisive word: that these askers—the People of the Book—do not possess the disposition to follow the truth, nor do they have steadfastness, resolve, or sound judgment. How many a clear sign have they wronged, and how many a true call have they barred? However, the firmly grounded in knowledge among them, because they possess steadfastness in their knowledge and what has become clear of the truth to them, and likewise the true believers among them, because they possess the disposition to follow the truth, they believe in what was revealed to you and what was revealed before you, because they found that the revelation sent to you resembles what was revealed before you to the other prophets: Noah and those after him.

From here, several points become clear: (First) the reason for describing those who followed the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) from the People of the Book as "firmly grounded in knowledge" and "the faithful." For the previous verses recount that they are not firmly grounded in what they knew, nor are they stable upon any truth even if bound by the most solemn covenants, and that they are not believers in the signs of Allah but rather bar from them even when clear proofs come. So, those whom Allah excepted are truly firmly grounded in knowledge or truly faithful.

(Second) the reason for mentioning what was revealed previously along with the Quran in His saying "they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you," because the context is one of negating any difference between the two.

(Third) that His saying in the following verse: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا (etc.) is in the position of reasoning for the faith of these excepted ones.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ — "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163) is in the position of reasoning for His saying "they believe in what has been sent down to you," as you have just recognized. The sum of the meaning—and Allah knows best—is that they believed in what was revealed to you because We did not give you a novel matter (أمراً مبتدعاً) that is unique in its claims and aspects such that it is not found with other previous prophets. Rather, the matter follows a single path in which there is no difference. For We revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him—and Noah was the first prophet to come with a book and a law (شريعة) — the divinely ordained system of law and way of life — and as We revealed to Abraham and those after him from his progeny. They know them and know the manner of their being sent and their call. Among them were those given a book, like David who was given the Psalms (زبوراً), which is a prophetic revelation; and Moses was given the direct speech (التكليم), which is a prophetic revelation; and others like Ishmael, Isaac, and Jacob were sent without a book, and that also was through prophetic revelation.

What unites them all is that they are messengers bringing good news of Allah's reward and warning of His punishment. Allah sent them to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة) — the final establishment of divine evidence that leaves no excuse for people — over mankind by clarifying what benefits them and what harms them in their hereafter and their world, so that mankind may have no argument against Allah after the messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَسْباطِ — "and the Tribes" (4:163). It has preceded in His saying وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْباطِ (3:84) that they are prophets from the progeny of Jacob or from the tribes of the Children of Israel.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً — "and We gave David the Psalms" (4:163). It is said that it means "the written" (المكتوب) from their saying: زبره, meaning he wrote it; thus, the Zabur is in the sense of the Mazbur (the written).

His saying, the Exalted: رُسُلاً مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ — "apostles, as bearers of good news and warners" (4:165). These are three circumstantial qualifiers (أحوال), or the first is a circumstantial qualifier and the latter two are its descriptions. The comprehensive discussion on the meaning of sending messengers and the completion of the proof from Allah over mankind, and that reason alone does not suffice without the sending of prophets with divine laws, has already passed in the discussion on His saying: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً (2:213) in the second volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكانَ اللهُ عَزِيزاً حَكِيماً — "and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise" (4:165). Since He possesses absolute might and absolute wisdom, it is impossible for anyone to overcome Him with an argument; rather, to Him belongs the conclusive argument (الحجة البالغة). He said: قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ — "Say, 'To Allah belongs the conclusive argument...'" (6:149).

His saying, the Exalted: لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ — "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge—and the angels bear witness [too]" (4:166). This is another rectification in the sense of a "disconnected exception" (استثناء منقطع) from the refutation related to their asking the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) to bring down a book to them from heaven. For what Allah mentioned in refuting their request by His saying فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ (to the end of the verses) implies that their request is rejected, because what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) brought by revelation from his Lord does not differ in kind from the revelation brought by all other prophets. So, whoever claims to be a believer in what they brought must believe in what he brought without distinction.

Then He rectified this by stating that Allah, nevertheless, bears witness to what He revealed to His Prophet, and the angels bear witness, and Allah suffices as a witness.

The core of His testimony is His saying أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ (He sent it down with His knowledge). For mere descent does not suffice for the claim, because among the types of descent is the descent through the revelation of devils (الشياطين), whereby a devil corrupts the matter of divine guidance by placing a false path in place of Allah's true path, or by mixing some falsehood into the true divine revelation so that the matter becomes confused. This is what He refers to negating in His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُؤْمِنُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ وَأَحْصى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عَدَداً — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them, and He keeps count of all things" (72:26-28). And He said: وَإِنَّ الشَّياطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلى أَوْلِيائِهِمْ — "Indeed the devils inspire their friends..." (6:121).

In short, testimony to mere descent or sending down does not remove the claim from a state of ambiguity. However, qualifying it with His saying بِعِلْمِهِ (with His knowledge) clarifies the intent with all clarity, and signifies that Allah the Glorified sent it down to His Messenger while He knows what He is sending down, encompasses it, and protects it from the plot of the devils.

Since the testimony is to the sending down, and the sending down is only through the mediation of the angels—as indicated by His saying: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart..." (2:97)—and He said in describing this honored angel: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ — "it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger, powerful and established with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy" (81:19-21). This indicates that under his command are other angels, who are the ones He mentioned when He said: كَلَّا إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ — "No indeed! It is a reminder—so let him who wishes remember it—[recorded] in honored scrolls, exalted and purified, by the hands of scribes, noble and pious" (80:11-16).

In short, because the angels are intermediaries in the sending down, they are also witnesses, just as He is a witness, and Allah suffices as a witness.

The proof for His testimony is what He revealed in His Book of the verses of challenge, such as His saying: قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً — "Say, 'Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another'" (17:88), and His saying: أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it" (4:82), and His saying: فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — "then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can besides Allah..." (10:38).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ قَدْ ضَلُّوا ضَلالاً بَعِيداً — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah have certainly strayed into far error" (4:167). When He mentioned the conclusive argument regarding the message of His Prophet and the descent of His Book from Allah—and that it is of the same nature as the revelation revealed to the prophets before him, and that it is accompanied by His testimony and the testimony of His angels, and He suffices as a witness—He established the error of whoever disbelieves in it and turns away from it, whoever they may be among the People of the Book.

In the verse, there is a substitution of "the Book"—the descent of which from Allah was the subject of the discourse—with "the way of Allah" (سبيل الله), where He said: وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ. In this is a subtle conciseness (إيجاز), as if it were said: Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from this Book and the revelation it contains have disbelieved and barred from the way of Allah, and those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah (etc.).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَظَلَمُوا لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ — "Indeed those who disbelieve and do wrong, Allah will never forgive them..." (4:168). This is another establishment and confirmation in the position of emphasizing the previous verse. Accordingly, what is meant by "wrongdoing" (الظلم) is barring from the way of Allah, as is apparent.

It is possible that the verse is in the position of reasoning for the previous verse, explaining the nature of their far error, and the meaning is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, regarding His saying وَقَوْلِهِمْ عَلى مَرْيَمَ بُهْتاناً عَظِيماً, narrated from Ibn Babawayh with his chain from 'Alqamah from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a hadith, he said: ألم ينسبوا مريم بنت عمران إلى أنها حملت بصبي من رجل نجار اسمه يوسف؟ — "Did they not attribute to Mary daughter of 'Imran that she became pregnant with a boy from a carpenter named Joseph?"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ, he said: My father narrated to me, from al-Qasim ibn Muhammad, from Sulayman ibn Dawud al-Minqari, from Abu Hamzah, from Shahr ibn Hawshab, who said: al-Hajjaj said to me: "O Shahr, a verse in the Book of Allah has exhausted me." I said: "O Amir, which verse is it?" He said: "His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ. By Allah, I order a Jew or a Christian to be brought and his neck struck—then I watch him with my eyes, and I do not see him move his lips until he is extinguished." I said: "May Allah rectify the Amir, it is not as you have interpreted." He said: "How is it then?" I said: "Indeed Jesus will descend to the world before the Day of Resurrection, and there will not remain a person of any creed, Jew or otherwise, but will believe in him before his [Jesus'] death, and he will pray behind the Mahdi." He said: "Woe to you! From where did you get this? And from where did you bring it?" I said: "Muhammad ibn 'Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn 'Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) narrated it to me." He said: "By Allah, you have brought it from a pure spring."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from Shahr ibn Hawshab who said: al-Hajjaj said to me: "O Shahr, a verse from the Book of Allah—I never read it but something in my soul objects to it. Allah said: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ. And I am brought prisoners and I strike their necks—and I do not hear them say anything." I said: "It was presented to you in other than its proper way. Indeed, when the soul of a Christian departs, the angels strike him from his front and his back and say: 'O wicked one, indeed the Messiah whom you claimed was God, or the son of God, or the third of three, is the servant of Allah, His spirit, and His word.' So he believes when his faith does not benefit him. And when the soul of a Jew departs, the angels strike him from his front and his back and say: 'O wicked one, indeed the Messiah whom you claimed you killed is the servant of Allah and His spirit.' So he believes in him when faith does not benefit him. Then, when Jesus descends, the living among them will believe in him just as the dead among them believed in him." He said: "From where did you take this?" I said: "From Muhammad ibn 'Ali." He said: "You have indeed taken it from its source." Shahr said: "By Allah, none narrated it to me but Umm Salamah, but I wanted to enrage him."

I say: It was also narrated in summary from 'Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir, from Shahr ibn Hawshab, from Muhammad ibn 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, who is Ibn al-Hanafiyyah. It appears that he narrated from Muhammad ibn 'Ali, then the narrators differed in identifying him as Ibn al-Hanafiyyah or Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام). The narration—as you see—supports what we presented in explaining the meaning of the verse. In it, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma' wa al-Sifat recorded that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: كيف أنتم إذا نزل فيكم ابن مريم وإمامكم منكم؟ — "How will you be when the son of Mary descends among you while your leader is from among you?"

In it: Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Abu Hurayrah who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) said: يوشك أن ينزل فيكم ابن مريم حكما عدلا يقتل الدجال ، ويقتل الخنزير ، ويكسر الصليب ، ويضع الجزية ، ويقبض المال ، وتكون السجدة واحدة لله رب العالمين ، واقرءوا إن شئتم : وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ ـ موت عيسى بن مريم — "The son of Mary will soon descend among you as a just judge; he will kill the Dajjal, kill the swine, break the cross, abolish the jizyah, and collect wealth. Prostration will be one for Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. Read if you wish: 'There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death'—the death of Jesus son of Mary." Then Abu Hurayrah repeated it three times.

I say: The narrations regarding the descent of Jesus (عليه السلام) at the appearance of the Mahdi (عليه السلام) are widely transmitted (مستفيضة) through the paths of the Sunnis, and likewise through the paths of the Shi'a from the Prophet and the Imams from his household (عليهم الصلاة والسلام).

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from al-Harith ibn Mughirah: from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً, he said: هو رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله — "He is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم)."

I say: Although its apparent meaning contradicts the context of the verses addressing the matter of Jesus (عليه السلام), it is possible that it is intended to explain the "application" (الجري) of the Quran. This means that after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) was sent and brought a book and a law that abrogates the law of Jesus, it became incumbent upon every person of the Book to believe in him, and to believe in Jesus and those before him as part of believing in him. So, if it is disclosed to a person of the Book at the time of death, for example, the truth of the message of Jesus after the sending of the Messenger of Allah Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), it is only disclosed within the disclosure of the truth of the message of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم). Thus, the faith of every person of the Book in Jesus (عليه السلام) is only considered faith if he believes in Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) primarily and in Jesus (عليه السلام) secondarily. So, the one in whom every person of the Book truly believes and who will be a witness over them on the Day of Resurrection is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) after his being sent. Even if Jesus (عليه السلام) is also such, there is no contradiction. The following report is not without some manifestation of this meaning.

In it, from Ibn Sinan from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): regarding the saying of Allah concerning Jesus: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً, he said: إيمان أهل الكتاب إنما هو لمحمد صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله — "The faith of the People of the Book is only in Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم)."

And in it [al-Kafi], narrated from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death; and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them" (4:159). He عليه‌السلام said: "There is no one from any of the religions who dies except that he sees the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام—truly, from the first to the last."

I say: It is more apparent that the narration is an instance of conceptual flow (الجري) — the application of a verse to subsequent instances that fit its universal meaning. Furthermore, the narration is not explicit in that what he عليه‌السلام mentioned is intended as a direct exegesis (تفسير) or specific application (تطبيق) of the verse; it is possible that it was a statement mentioned in the course of discussing the verse, and there are similar examples of this in the narrations.

And in it, from al-Mufaddal ibn ‘Umar, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah: وَإِنْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِ "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death." He replied: "This was revealed specifically concerning us; indeed, there is no man from the progeny of Fatimah who dies or leaves this world until he acknowledges the Imam and his Imamate (الإمامة) — the divine office of leadership and spiritual guidance — just as the children of Ya‘qub acknowledged Yusuf when they said: تَاللهِ لَقَدْ آثَرَكَ اللهُ عَلَيْنا 'By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us' (12:91)."

I say: This narration is among the solitary reports (الآحاد) and is broken in its chain (مرسلة). Narrations with similar meanings have been reported regarding the saying of the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا مِنْ عِبادِنا فَمِنْهُمْ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سابِقٌ بِالْخَيْراتِ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ "Then We caused to inherit the Book those whom We have chosen of Our servants; and among them is he who wrongs himself, and among them is he who is moderate, and among them is he who is foremost in good deeds by permission of Allah. That [inheritance] is what is the great bounty" (35:32). We shall provide a full discussion of them, Allah the Exalted willing.

And in it, regarding the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163): From Zurarah and Humran, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I have revealed to you as I revealed to Noah and the prophets after him"—thus He gathered for him every revelation (وحي).

I say: It appears that the meaning is that nothing of the nature of revelation (سنخ الوحي) escaped him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that would necessitate a divergence of the path (السبيل) or a variation in the call (الدعوة). It does not mean that everything revealed to a prophet with its specific details was revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, for this would be meaningless; nor does it mean that what was revealed to you encompasses all previous religious laws (الشرائع), as the context of the verse is not set to convey this meaning. This interpretation is supported by the following report.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Salim, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: Allah said to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him," and He commanded every prophet with a path (السبيل) and a tradition (السنة).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "Between Adam and Noah there were prophets who were hidden (مستخفين) and others who were manifest (مستعلنين). For this reason, their mention remained hidden in the Qur'an and they were not named as the manifest prophets were named. This is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَرُسُلاً لَمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ وَكَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسى تَكْلِيماً 'and apostles We have not mentioned to you; and Allah spoke to Moses with a [direct] speech' (4:164)—meaning: I did not name the hidden ones as I named the manifest ones among the prophets."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Kafi from ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, from Abu Hamzah, from him عليه‌السلام. In it: "...among the prophets were hidden ones, and for this reason their mention remained hidden in the Qur'an; they were not named as the manifest prophets were named. This is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: رُسُلاً قَدْ قَصَصْناهُمْ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَرُسُلاً لَمْ نَقْصُصْهُمْ عَلَيْكَ 'apostles We have already mentioned to you before, and apostles We have not mentioned to you' (4:164)—meaning: I did not name the hidden ones as I named the manifest ones among the prophets" (the tradition).

The meaning of the narration in any case is that Allah the Exalted did not mention the story of the hidden ones at all, nor did He name them, just as He narrated some stories of the manifest ones and named those whom He named. It is possible that the phrase "meaning: I did not name..." etc., is the speech of the narrator.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, who said: I heard Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام saying: "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you concerning ‘Ali—He sent it down with His knowledge, and the angels bear witness, and Allah suffices as a witness."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir with a connected chain from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام. This is of the nature of conceptual flow (الجري) and application (التطبيق), for there are parts of the Qur'an revealed concerning his عليه‌السلام guardianship (الولاية) — the divinely appointed authority and leadership. This does not imply a corruption (تحريف) of the Book, nor is it a variant reading (قراءة) from him عليه‌السلام.

Similar to this is what was narrated in al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, and by al-Qummi in his Tafsir from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَظَلَمُوا "Indeed those who are faithless and do wrong" (4:168)—[meaning they wronged] the Progeny of Muhammad regarding their right—لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ "Allah will never forgive them." And what was narrated in al-Majma‘ from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: قَدْ جاءَكُمُ الرَّسُولُ بِالْحَقِّ "The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth" (4:170)—meaning: with the guardianship (الولاية) of the one whose guardianship Allah commanded.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِٱلْحَقِّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١٧٠)
O mankind! The Apostle has certainly brought you the truth from your Lord. So have faith! That is better for you. And if you are faithless, [you should know that] to Allah indeed belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (170)
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُۥٓ أَلْقَىٰهَآ إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ ۚ ٱنتَهُوا۟ خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا ٱللَّهُ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُۥ وَلَدٌ ۘ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (١٧١)
O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion, and do not attribute anything to Allah except the truth. The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah, and His Word that He cast toward Mary and a spirit from Him. So have faith in Allah and His apostles, and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Relinquish [such a creed]! That is better for you. Allah is but the One God. He is far too immaculate to have any son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee. (171)
لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا (١٧٢)
The Messiah would never disdain being a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will gather them all toward Him. (172)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَنكَفُوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٧٣)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, He will pay them in full their rewards, and He will enhance them out of His grace. But those who are disdainful and arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find besides Allah any guardian or helper. (173)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُم بُرْهَـٰنٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ نُورًا مُّبِينًا (١٧٤)
O mankind! Certainly, a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a manifest light. (174)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱعْتَصَمُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيُدْخِلُهُمْ فِى رَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلٍ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَيْهِ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا (١٧٥)
As for those who have faith in Allah, and hold fast to Him, He will admit them to His mercy and grace, and He will guide them on a straight path to Him. (175)

(Commentary)
